<a id="post-installation-steps-overview"></a>

#### Post-Installation Steps Overview

> 📄 Source: [Post-Installation_Steps_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Post-Installation_Steps_Overview.htm)

##### Post-Installation Steps Overview


This section contains the post-installation tasks that Edifecs recommends after you install Edifecs Application Manager and XEServer.


###### Migrate H2 databases


In XEServer 9.3.3 all H2 databases distributed with products have been upgraded to version 2.2.222. If you upgrade a pre-9.3.3 XEServer instance to version 9.3.3 or later, you have to [migrate H2 databases](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/H2_DB_Upgrade.htm) that were in use before the upgrade.


###### Replace default encryption keys


Some XEServer and EAM features that use SSL for secure communication come with default keystores that contain encryption keys and certificates. For these components, Edifecs recommends that you replace the default keystore entries with your own keys and certificates.


###### EAM


The connection between EAM Client and EAM Server is encrypted using SSL. To establish trustworthy channels between EAM Client and EAM Server, EAM uses a default key pair and a certificate (self-signed) distributed with EAM. To read the instructions on how to replace the default encryption keys for EAM, see [Replace Keys for EAM](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_EAM.htm).


###### XEServer


XEServer features that use default encryption keys and certificates distributed with XEServer are listed below. Edifecs recommends that you replace these keys to strengthen security. Follow the links below to read the instructions on how to replace the default encryption keys for a specific feature:

- [Jetty components](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_Jetty_Components.htm) (RESTful Web Service, Web Service, XEServer agent, and so on).
- [Scheduler Services](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_Features.htm#XEServer_Scheduler_Services)
- [XEServer SSH Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_Features.htm#XEServer_SSH_Server)
- [Agent Auto-discovery](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_Features.htm#XEServer_Auto-Discovery)
- [FHIR Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_FHIR_Service.htm)
- [XPM Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XPM_Service.htm)


###### Adjust custom Jetty XML configurations


In XEServer 9.3.3, the Jetty web server third-party library distributed with XEServer has been upgraded from version 9.4.51 to version 10.0.16. If you use customized Jetty configuration files (jetty.xml, jetty.http.connector.xml, and jetty.https.connector.xml), you have to [make them compatible](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm) with the version 10.0.16.

---

<a id="replace-default-encryption-keys"></a>

#### Replace Default Encryption Keys

> 📄 Source: [Book_Replace Default Encryption Keys.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Replace Default Encryption Keys.htm)

##### Replace Default Encryption Keys
[Redirect to: {Replace_Keys_for_EAM}](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_EAM.htm)

---

<a id="replace-the-keys-for-eam"></a>

##### Replace the Keys for EAM

> 📄 Source: [Replace_Keys_for_EAM.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_EAM.htm)

###### Replace the Keys for EAM


The connection between the EAM Client and EAM Server is encrypted using SSL. To establish trustworthy channels between EAM Client and EAM Server, EAM uses default self-signed certificates distributed with EAM. Edifecs strongly recommends that you replace these default EAM certificates. These certificates are located at the following keystore locations:


|   EAM Component |   Keystore Location | Default Passwords | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Keystore | Key Pair | | |
| EAM Server | ${EAMRoot}\Server\security\keystores\ EAMServer.jks | eamserver | eamserver |
| EAM Client | ${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\ EAMClient.jks | eamclient | eamclient |
| EAM Service | ${EAMRoot}\Server\security\keystores\ EAMService.jks | eamservice | eamservice |



Tip EAM Service is a process that starts EAM Server if the latter is configured to run as a Windows/Linux service.


In the context of SSL, EAM has three components that use SSL certificates for intercommunication (arrows indicate related keys and certificates):



This section shows you how to generate new keys with self-signed certificates and how to replace the default certificates when you want to and on expiry. However, you can similarly use certificates issued by a certificate Authority (CA) to secure the connection between EAM components.


###### Generate Keys and Replace Certificates


The main steps to replace certificates for all the three EAM components (EAM Client, EAM Server, and EAM Service) are below. The procedure is rather straightforward and at a high level comprises the following steps:

1. Remove the existing entries in all the three keystores.
2. Generate a new key pair in the EAM Server keystore.
3. Export the EAM Server certificate, and then import this certificate to the keystores used by EAM Client and EAM Service.
4. Generate a new key pair in the EAM Client keystore.
5. Export the EAM Client certificate, and then import this certificate to the EAM Server keystore.
6. Generate a new key pair in the EAM Service keystore.
7. Export the EAM Service certificate, and then import this certificate to the EAM Server keystore.


Below you can view the detailed step-by-step instructions on how to replace the certificates. In this article, the open source GUI tool [KeyStore Explorer](https://keystore-explorer.org/) is used to manipulate the keystore contents, however you can use any other keystore management utility. All screen shots and instructions refer to KeyStore Explorer 5.4.


###### EAM Server


First, clear the EAM Server keystore. To do this:

1. Go to ${EAMRoot}\Server\security\keystores\, and then open EAMServer.jks (the default password to the keystore is "eamserver"). The keystore contains three entries:
2. Delete all the three entries.


Then, generate a new key pair in the EAM Server keystore. To do that:

1. Click to create a new key pair.
2. Use the default algorithm RSA and 2048-bit key size and then, click OK.
3. Set the validity range for the new key pair, and then click to provide DN fields.
4. Enter your organization's details in the DN fields, and then click OK.
5. Enter an alias for the new key pair.


Tip Although you can use any alias name, Edifecs recommends that you use eamserverkey for consistency.

1. Enter eamserver as a password and then click OK. This is the default password that EAM Server uses to access the key pair eamserverkey. If you want to use a custom password for the key pair, see Use Custom Keystore Passwords.


Now, export the EAM Server certificate from the key pair. To do that:

1. Right-click the key pair entry you have just created, and then click Export Certificate Chain.
2. Enter the location where you want to store the EAM Server certificate file (eamserverkey.cer). Later, you will have to import this certificate to the truststores of EAM Client and EAM Service.
3. Import the certificates of EAM Client and EAM Service to the EAM Server keystore.


Note You should perform this step later after you create new certificates for EAM Client and EAM Service. Skip this step for now, and you can return to this step later in this scenario.


###### EAM Client

1. Go to ${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\, and then open EAMClient.jks (the default password to the keystore is "eamclient"). The keystore contains two entries:
2. Delete the two entries.


Then, import the EAM Server certificate (file eamserverkey.cer created at Step 10 in EAM Server section). To do this:

1. Click to import the certificate.
2. Locate the EAM Server certificate file (eamserverkey.cer), and then click Open.
3. Enter an alias for the new certificate entry.


Tip Although you can use any alias name, Edifecs recommends that you use eamserverkey for consistency.


The EAM Server trusted certificate has been imported. Now, generate a new key pair in the EAM Client keystore. To do that:

1. Click to create a new key pair.
2. Use the default algorithm RSA and 2048-bit key size, and then click OK.
3. Set the validity range for the new key pair, and then click to provide DN fields.
4. Enter your organization's details in DN fields, and then click OK.
5. Enter an alias for the new key pair.


Tip Although you can use any alias name, Edifecs recommends that you use eamclientkey for consistency.

1. Enter eamclient as a password, and then click OK. This is the default password that EAM Client uses to access the key pair eamclientkey. If you want to use a custom password for the key pair, see Use Custom Keystore Passwords.


This creates the key pair for EAM Client. Now, export the EAM Client certificate from the key pair. To do that:

1. Right-click the key pair entry you have just created, and then click Export Certificate Chain.
2. Enter the location where you want to store the EAM Client certificate file (eamclientkey.cer).
3. Import this certificate file eamclientkey.cer to the EAM Server keystore (${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\EAMServer.jks). Use "eamclientkey" as an alias.


###### EAM Service

1. Go to ${EAMRoot}\Server\security\keystores\, and then open EAMService.jks (the default password to the keystore is "eamservice"). The keystore contains two entries.
2. Delete the two entries.


Then, import the EAM Server certificate (file eamserverkey.cer created at Step 10 in EAM Server section). To do this:

1. Click to import the certificate.
2. Locate the EAM Server certificate file (eamserverkey.cer), and then click Open.
3. Enter an alias for the new certificate entry.


Tip Although you can use any alias name, Edifecs recommends that you use eamserverkey for consistency.


This imports the EAM Server trusted certificate. Now, generate a new key pair in the EAM Server keystore. To do that:

1. Click to create a new key pair.
2. Use the default algorithm RSA and 2048-bit key size, and then click OK.
3. Set the validity range for the new key pair, and then click to provide DN fields.
4. Enter your organization's details in the DN fields, and then click OK.
5. Enter an alias for the new key pair.


Tip Although you can use any alias name, Edifecs recommends that you use eamservicekey for consistency.

1. Enter eamservice as a password and then click OK. This is the default password that EAM Server uses to access the key pair eamservicekey. If you want to use a custom password for the key pair, see Use Custom Keystore Passwords.


This creates the key pair for EAM Service. Now, export the EAM Service certificate from the key pair. To do that:

1. Right-click the key pair entry you have just created, and then click Export Certificate Chain.
2. Enter the location where you want to store the EAM Service certificate file (eamservicekey.cer).
3. Import this certificate file eamservicekey.cer to EAM Server keystore (${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\EAMServer.jks). Use "eamservicekey" as an alias.


###### Test EAM Connection


To verify that the new certificates work as expected:

1. Start EAM Client and connect to EAM Server.
2. Ensure that the connection is established and no exceptions are logged.


###### Use Custom Keystore Passwords


This section shows you how to use non-default passwords for EAM keystores and keys. To access password-protected keystores and keys, EAM components use the passwords defined in the .properties files:

- EAM Server: ${EAMRoot}\Server\security\config\server.properties
- EAM Service: ${EAMRoot}\Server\security\config\service.properties
- EAM Client. To access EAMClient.jks and its keys, EAM Client uses passwords specified in two files: - ${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\client.properties - ${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\hide.properties


These .properties files have a similar structure and look like the following:


Client.properties


#timestamp


enabled.cipher.suites=SSL_RSA_WITH_NULL_MD5


keystoretype=JKS


keystorepass=XE\:5H+PljgCe5nBPdnCPLKV1Q\=\=


keypassword=XE\:5H+PljgCe5nBPdnCPLKV1Q\=\=


keystorepath=${EAMRoot}/Client/configuration/security/EAMClient.jks


contextprotocol=TLSv1


tls.profiles=TLS SASL/EDIFECS_LOGIN


The highlighted properties hold the keystore and key passwords in an encoded format. If you decide to use a non-default password for the EAM keystores or keys, you have to specify your custom password in the corresponding .properties file to enable EAM access to the keystore / key.


For example, if your EAM Client's keystore and the key in this keystore have the password "foobar1234", then:

1. Open the file ${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\client.properties.
2. Specify your custom password as a plain text:keystorepass=foobar1234keypassword=foobar1234
3. Save the file.
4. Repeat steps 2 and 3 for the file ${EAMRoot}\Client\configuration\security\hide.properties.


The next time that you start EAM Client and connect to the EAM Server, EAM uses the new password "foobar1234" to access the EAM Client keystore and its key.


Tip When you start EAM after changing a password in the .properties file, EAM overwrites plain text passwords with encoded values.

---

<a id="replace-the-keys-for-the-xeserver-features"></a>

##### Replace the Keys for the XEServer Features

> 📄 Source: [Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_Features.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_Features.htm)

###### Replace the Keys for the XEServer Features


Note When you use the instructions in this section, use the following password to access the present keystores and to protect new keystores and keystore entries: password.


###### Scheduler Services


XEServer services [Scheduler Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Server_Service.htm) and [Scheduler Client](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Client_Service.htm) use SSL to securely communicate with each other. The services use the following default keystores:


| Service |   Keystore Location | Passwords | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Keystore | Key Pair | | |
| Scheduler Server | ${XESRoot}/features/scheduler/security/ scheduler.server.keystore.jks | password | password |
| Scheduler Client | ${XESRoot}/features/scheduler/security/ scheduler.client.truststore.jks | password | - |



You have two options on how to replace the default key and certificate used by the two services:

- Replace the default key and certificate in the present keystores, -or-
- Create a new .jks keystore (with a new key pair for Scheduler Server) and a new .jks truststore (with Scheduler Server's certificate).


To replace the default entries in the present keystores:

1. Open the keystore scheduler.server.keystore.jks.
2. Delete the key pair entry scheduler-server.
3. Generate a new key pair. You can use any name as an alias. The only requirement is that your keystore must hold only one entry - the key pair. Note If you use a non-default password for the key entry (or if you change the keystore password), make sure you update the password on the [Scheduler Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Server_Service.htm) configuration page.
4. From the key pair you just created, export the certificate to a .cer file.
5. Open the truststore scheduler.client.truststore.jks.
6. Delete the certificate entry scheduler-server.
7. Import the .cer certificate from Step #4.
8. Restart the profile with Scheduler services.


###### XEServer SSH Server


XEServer's built-in SSH server uses SSL to secure the connection to SSH clients. The XEServer SSH server uses the following default key pair:


| Component |   Keystore Location | Passwords | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Keystore | Key Pair | | |
| SSH Server | ${XESRoot}\platform\security\ssh.server.keystore.jks | password | password |



To replace the default key pair:

1. Go to ${XESRoot}\platform\security\.
2. Open the keystore ssh.server.keystore.jks.
3. Delete the default key pair entry xes-sshserver.
4. In the keystore ssh.server.keystore.jks, generate a new key pair entry with the alias xes-sshserver.
5. Restart XEServer.


The next time you connect to the XEServer's SSH server using an SSH client (for example, PuTTY), you will receive a warning about the server's host key mismatch which indicates that the SSH server has picked up the new key. Accept the new host key to connect to the XEServer's SSH server.


###### XEServer Auto-discovery


XEServer agent can send registration requests to XESManager through a protected channel secured by SSL. During the registration phase, XEServer agent acts as a client, and XESManager represents the server side. To identify XESManager as a trusted party, XEServer agent uses the default certificate in the truststore ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.manager.truststore.jks.


To replace the default certificate:

1. On the computer with XESManager installed, replace the default XESManager key pair: 1. Go to ${ECRootPath}\XESManager\workspace\config\. 2. Open the keystore xes-agents.jks. The default password is "password". 3. Delete the key pair entry xes-manager. 4. Create a new key pair and use xes-manager as an alias. 5. From the key pair you just created, export a certificate to a .cer file. 6. Move the .cer file to the computer with XEServer.
2. Open the truststore ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.manager.truststore.jks and delete the default certificate entry xesmanager.
3. Import the .cer certificate to the truststore ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.manager.truststore.jks. Tip As an alternative, you can create a new .jks truststore, import the certificate to this truststore, and update the SSL settings on the [Auto-Discovery Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Autodiscovery_Tab.htm).
4. Restart XEServer and XESManager.

---

<a id="replace-the-keys-for-the-jetty-components"></a>

##### Replace the Keys for the Jetty Components

> 📄 Source: [Replace_Keys_for_Jetty_Components.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_Jetty_Components.htm)

###### Replace the Keys for the Jetty Components


Some of the XEServer components based on Jetty server (RESTful Web Service, Tracking and Duplicates Server Service, XEServer agent, and so on) use the default encryption keys distributed with XEServer. Edifecs recommends that you replace the default keys and certificates with your own entries to eliminate the risk of the man-in-the-middle attacks (MITM) for your organization.


Throughout this section, the CLI Java utility [keytool](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/tools/unix/keytool.html) is used, however you can use any other similar software to generate certificates and keystores or obtain certificates from a certificate authority.


###### Generate keys for platform Jetty server


A generic procedure to replace the encryption keys for Jetty-powered components comprises the following steps:

1. Go to ${XESRoot}\platform\security.
2. Delete the present key pair entry in KeyringClient.jks:keytool -delete -alias {entryToDelete} -keystore KeyringClient.jks -storepass password
3. Generate a new key pair entry:keytool -genkeypair -alias {newEntryAlias} -keyalg RSA -validity 365 -keysize 2048 -keystore KeyringClient.jks -storepass password -keypass password


###### Generate keys for dedicated Jetty server


Below is an example of how to generate certificates with a specified hostname for the [Sample RESTful Web Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Sample_RESTful_Web_Service_Profile.htm) profile that uses dedicated Jetty server:

1. Create a new directory where you will save generated certificates to.
2. In the directory you created, in the command prompt, do the following:
3. Copy the generated keystore files rest-server-keystore.jks and rest-client-keystore.jks to ${XESRoot}\profiles\Sample RESTful Web Service\config\security


For a sample scenario that shows you how to replace encryption keys for the XEServer agent (XES Agent runs in Jetty), see [Replace Keys for XEServer and XESManager](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_and_XESManager.htm).

---

<a id="replace-the-keys-for-xeserver-and-xesmanager"></a>

##### Replace the Keys for XEServer and XESManager

> 📄 Source: [Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_and_XESManager.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XEServer_and_XESManager.htm)

###### Replace the Keys for XEServer and XESManager


The XEServer agent and XESManager communicate with each other (the agent sends the profile statistics to XESManager, and XESManager sends commands to the agent) over a protected SSL-secured channel. To encrypt the network traffic between XEServer agent and XESManager, the following encryption keys are used:




XEServer agent uses the default key pair xes-httpserver located in the keystore ${XESRoot}\platform\security:


*XESManager uses the default certificate xes-httpserver located in the keystore: ${ECRootPath}\XESManager\workspace\config\xes-agents.jks: This keystore contains the certificates of all the XEServer agents that communicate with XESManager. Edifecs recommends that you replace the default certificate xes-httpserver with your own certificate to eliminate the risk of man-in-the-middle attacks (MITM). The instructions below show you how to replace keystore entries by using the command line utility Java Keytool. However, you can use other keystore management tools. Note When you use the instructions in this section, use the following password to access the present keystores and to protect new keystores and keystore entries: password. ## Create a new key pair in XEServer First, you have to replace the default keypair xes-httpserver in KeyRingClient.jks. Go to ${XESRoot}\platform\security\. On your terminal, run the following command to remove the existing key pair xes-httpserver from the keystore KeyringClient.jks: keytool -delete -alias xes-httpserver -keystore KeyringClient.jks -storepass password Run the following command to generate a new key pair with the alias xes-httpserver in KeyringClient.jks. keytool -genkeypair -alias xes-httpserver -keyalg RSA -validity 365 -keysize 2048 -keystore KeyringClient.jks -storepass password -keypass password Specify the DN values by answering each question when prompted: DN Field Description First & Last Name (CN) The domain name that you or the users of your organization use to connect to XESManager. For example, localhost. Organizational Unit (OU) (Optional) The name of your organization unit to differentiate your organization from other divisions. Organization Name (O) (Optional) The name of your organization. City / Locality (L) (Optional) The name of the city your organization is in. State / Province (ST) (Optional) The name of the place where your organization is physically located. Country Code (C) (Optional) A two-letter country / region code. For example, US. Review the DN fields that you entered and proceed. The new certificate entry xes-httpserver is available in KeyringClient.jks. ## Export the certificate You have to export the certificate to a .cer file. Go to ${XESRoot}\platform\security\. On your terminal, run the following command: keytool -exportcert -alias xes-httpserver -file xes-httpserver.cer -keystore KeyringClient.jks This exports the certificate xes-httpserver from the keystore KeyringClient.jks. The file xes-httpserver.cer appears at ${XESRoot}\platform\security. Move the file xes-httpserver.cer to the computer where XESManager is installed. You can skip this step if both XEServer and XESManager run on the same computer. ## Replace the default agent certificate in XESManager You have to import the new XEServer agent certificate file (xes-httpserver.cer) to the XESManager truststore. Move the file xes-httpserver.cer to ${ECRootPath}\XESManager\workspace\config\. Open your terminal, and then change the working directory to ${ECRootPath}\XESManager\workspace\config\. Delete the existing certificate entry: keytool -delete -alias xes-httpserver -keystore xes-agents.jks -storepass password Run the following command: keytool -importcert -trustcacerts -file xes-httpserver.cer -alias xes-httpserver -keystore xes-agents.jks Tip You can choose any name as an alias. ## Test the Connection between XEServer and XESManager To verify that the new certificates work as expected: Restart XEServer and XESManager. Ensure that the connection has been established (https://localhost:5643/xes-manager page opens and your XEServer agent is online and is authorized) and no exceptions are logged. Note If you observe that the agent has the status Unauthorized (you can verify the status on the XESManager Agents page), run the script ${XESRoot}\bin\agent\cleanup_security_key.bat* on the XEServer computer.

---

<a id="replace-the-certificate-for-the-fhir-service"></a>

##### Replace the Certificate for the FHIR Service

> 📄 Source: [Replace_Keys_for_FHIR_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_FHIR_Service.htm)

###### Replace the Certificate for the FHIR Service


The XEServer [FHIR Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmodulefhir/9.4/Content/FHIR_Server_Service_Settings.htm) (becomes available after the installation of XESModule for FHIR) uses a default certificate to identify the FHIR server that runs on localhost as trusted. The certificate is located in the truststore ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.fhir.truststore.jks.


Note When you use the instructions in this section, use the following password to access the present keystores and to protect new keystores and keystore entries: password.


To replace the default certificate for the FHIR Service:

1. Generate a new key pair and export the certificate from the XESModule for FHIR keystore. For more information, see [SSL Certificate Deployment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xesmodulefhir/9.4/Content/Renew_SSL_Certificates.htm) in XESModule for FHIR Help Center.
2. In the truststore ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.fhir.truststore.jks, delete the default certificate entry localhost.
3. Import the .cer certificate to ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.fhir.truststore.jks using localhost as an alias. Tip As an alternative, you can create a new .jks truststore, import the certificate to this truststore, and update the SSL settings on the [FHIR Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmodulefhir/9.4/Content/FHIR_Server_Service_Settings.htm) configuration page.
4. Restart XEServer and XESModule for FHIR.

---

<a id="replace-the-certificate-for-the-xpm-service"></a>

##### Replace the Certificate for the XPM Service

> 📄 Source: [Replace_Keys_for_XPM_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Replace_Keys_for_XPM_Service.htm)

###### Replace the Certificate for the XPM Service


The XEServer [XPM Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/XProcess_Service.htm) uses a default certificate to identify the XPM server that runs on localhost as trusted. This certificate is located in the truststore ${XESRoot}/features/route-engine/components/state-event/resources/ssl/keystore.jks.


Note When you use the instructions in this section, use the following password to access the present keystores and to protect new keystores and keystore entries: password.


To replace the default certificate for the XPM Service:

1. Generate a new key pair and export the certificate from the XProcess Management keystore. For more information, see the section [SSL Certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xprocessmanagement/9.2/Content/Install_XProcess_Management.htm#SSL_Certificates) in XProcess Management Help Center.
2. In the truststore ${XESRoot}/features/route-engine/components/state-event/resources/ssl/keystore.jks, delete the default certificate entry localhost.
3. Import the .cer certificate to ${XESRoot}/features/route-engine/components/state-event/resources/ssl/keystore.jks using localhost as an alias. Tip As an alternative, you can create a new .jks truststore, import the certificate to this truststore, and update the SSL settings on the [FHIR Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/XESModuleFHIR/9.4/Content/FHIR_Server_Service_Settings.htm) configuration page.
4. Restart XEServer and XProcess Management.

---

<a id="h2-databases-upgrade"></a>

#### H2 Databases Upgrade

> 📄 Source: [H2_DB_Upgrade.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/H2_DB_Upgrade.htm)

##### H2 Databases Upgrade


In XEServer 9.3.3 all H2 databases distributed with products have been upgraded to version 2.2.222. This was done to address the vulnerability reported in [CVE-2022-45868](https://cve.mitre.org/cgi-bin/cvename.cgi?name=CVE-2022-45868).


If you are upgrading XEServer from a version older than 9.3.3, you have to migrate all the H2 databases created by previous versions of the product. Below are some XEServer components that can use H2 databases:

- Checkpoint service. By default, uses the database ${XESWorkspace}/CP/data.mv.db,
- Scheduler Server service. By default, uses the database ${XESWorkspace}/scheduler/JobStorage.mv.db,
- Balance Counters database,
- Batcher database, and so on.


For easy and smooth migration of such databases, use [H2 Database Migration Tool](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Edifecs_Library/H2_Migration_Tool.htm) which is available in [Edifecs Library](https://repository.edifecsfedcloud.com/#/storage/library/Tools/Administration/Edifecs H2 Migration Tool). The tool allows you to locate outdated H2 databases in your file system and upgrade their version from 1.4 and 2.1 to 2.2 including database content migration.


###### Use H2 Migration Tool for XEServer


###### Migrate Checkpoint database

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)

```
run.bat -mode migrate -migration-report migration-report.csv -db "%ECRootPath%\XEServer\profiles\myProfile\workspace\CP\data.mv.db"
```






###### Migrate Scheduler Server database

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)

```
run.bat -mode migrate -migration-report migration-report.csv -db "%XESRoot%\profiles\myProfile\workspace\scheduler\JobStorage.mv.db"
```

---

<a id="known-issues"></a>

### Known Issues

> 📄 Source: [XES_Known_Issues.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XES_Known_Issues.htm)

#### Known Issues


###### Custom class loader warnings on RHEL


For some Java versions on RHEL OS you can observe the following warning:


Archived non-system classes are disabled because the property java.system.class.loader is set (value = "com.edifecs.utils.ECClassLoader"). To use archived non-system classes, this property must not be set.


This warning shows up due to the option `-Xshare` that is enabled on these Java versions by default but does not work for processes that use custom class loaders. You can safely ignore this warning as it does not affect the XEServer performance.


To disable the warning:

1. Edit the file ${XESRoot}\bin\system\exec.sh.
2. Add the command line argument `-Xshare:off` to the line where Java is invoked, for example:"%JAVA_HOME%\bin\java" -Xshare:off -Djava.system.class.loader=com.edifecs.etools.shared.classloader.XESClassLoader ....
3. Restart the profile that produced the warning.


###### H2 Database Warning in FIPS Mode


On systems with FIPS mode enabled, such as Red Hat Enterprise Linux, a warning may occur when using the H2 Database Engine with XEServer.


When a profile is started and an H2 connection is used, the system may log the following exception in the `std.out.log` file:

```
java.security.NoSuchAlgorithmException: SHA1PRNG SecureRandom not available
				at java.base/sun.security.jca.GetInstance.getInstance(GetInstance.java:159)
				at java.base/java.security.SecureRandom.getInstance(SecureRandom.java:381)
				at org.h2.util.MathUtils.getSecureRandom(MathUtils.java:73)
		at org.h2.util.MathUtils.secureRandomBytes(MathUtils.java:294)
```


This message is a warning and does not indicate a failure. It occurs because the legacy **SHA1PRNG** algorithm is not available in FIPS-enabled environments. When this algorithm is unavailable, the H2 Database Engine automatically uses the default **SecureRandom** implementation provided by the JVM.


This behavior does not affect the functionality or performance of XEServer. No action is required.


###### Unexpected logouts from the XESManager web UI


If the XEServer Config Service uses the same credentials as those used for logging into XESManager web interface (https://localhost:5643/xes-manager/login/), unexpected logouts from the XESManager web interface may happen. This happens when XEServer pulls data from XESManager Configuration Server while someone is using the XESManager web interface under the same user account.


###### Oracle Solaris is not supported


Starting from XEServer 9.1, the Oracle Solaris operating system is not supported.


###### Hanging route/profile status


If a route spawns more tasks than the number specified in the Maximum # of active tasks/instance route property (for example, you may have a route with many inbound endpoints, and each inbound endpoint is a task originator; or you may have components with asynchronous route connections), you may observe that when you stop this route or shut down the parent profile, the route/profile takes on the STOPPING status, however it never reaches the DISCONNECTED status. If this happens, increase the Maximum # of active tasks/instance route property value to match the real number of active tasks in the route.


###### MultiSetup gets interrupted on Windows 64-bit


The MultiSetup wizard gets interrupted while installing the standards database on Windows 64-bit. The problem can be resolved by removing the C:\Program Files (x86)\Common Files\InstallShield\Driver\1150 folder.


###### JMS ActiveMQ message re-delivery issue


If the JMS Service that is configured to work with the ActiveMQ JMS provider is stopped when a message is being received by the JMS Inbound endpoint, then the message is redelivered by the JMS Inbound endpoint upon the next start. This happens only when the transacted mode in the JMS Inbound component is set to 'None'. To avoid this, set the transacted mode in the JMS Inbound component to 'Local’. For more information, refer to the [ActiveMQ issue](https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AMQ-6454).


###### DSA certificates are not supported for SSL connection


As a result of migrating to JDK 11 that uses TLS (Transport Layer Security) 1.3 for SSL connections, DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm) certificates are not supported anymore. If you used DSA keys to establish SSL connections, you must generate new RSA-2048 certificates to replace the DSA certificates.


###### Failed calls to XEngine .NET API methods


When XEngine is running on Microsoft IIS Server, the calls to XEngine .NET API methods may fail with the message: “Entry point was not found”. These failures are caused by some internal issues in IIS (that cannot be fixed in the Edifecs product). Edifecs recommends that you implement the required logic in XEServer routes using the REST API instead of the XE .NET API.


###### XEServer cannot process files locked by antivirus


During transaction processing, you may encounter a situation when XEServer cannot acquire exclusive locks on files to be processed, because these files are being scanned by an antivirus software. To eliminate processing issues caused by an antivirus that locks the files, consider the following recommendations:

- Exclude folders used by XEServer profiles as endpoints (for example, FS Inbound/Outbound endpoints or the profile workspace directory) from antivirus scans, and then scan these folders separately when they are not used in processing.
- Schedule your antivirus scans for the time when XEServer profiles don't process files.


###### NoClassDefFoundError when starting OpenSearch on AIX operating system with IBM Java


[OpenSearch fails to start](https://github.com/opensearch-project/OpenSearch/issues/11893) on AIX platform with IBM Semeru Runtime Certified Edition 17.0.6.0 (build 17.0.6+10). If you have profiles with the Tracking and Duplication Server service, Scheduler Server service, or FTP Server service, Edifecs recommends that you use [Adoptium OpenJDK](https://adoptium.net/temurin/releases/?os=aix&package=jdk&version=17).


###### Batcher Cleaner Fails with Large Batcher Databases


The batcher cleaner tool is not optimized to clean very large batcher databases. When the database contains a large volume of data, running the tool may cause excessive SQL Server transaction log growth. This can result in a transaction log full error before the cleanup completes.


This issue commonly occurs in the following scenarios:

- Very large Buckets and DataItems tables
- Environments with limited transaction log disk space
- Frequent batcher cleanup during development or testing
- Use of the -delete-all-data or -clean-channels options


**Issue**: When run with the -delete-all-data or -clean-channels option, the batcher cleaner tool executes SQL DELETE FROM <TableName> statements. The tool:

- Uses DML operations only
- Does not perform batched deletes
- Does not limit delete size
- Does not recreate database tables


As a result, SQL Server transaction logs may grow excessively during cleanup.


**Workaround**: If the batcher cleaner tool fails due to transaction log exhaustion, you can:

- Stop all profiles and batcher-related processes.
- Manually drop and recreate the batcher database or its tables, such as Buckets and DataItems.
- Ensure the batcher database is no longer required before performing manual cleanup.
- Use an account with appropriate database permissions.


**Important** You must perform manual drop or recreate operations only when no profiles or services are using the batcher database.

---

<a id="manage-xeserver-licenses"></a>

### Manage XEServer Licenses

> 📄 Source: [Manage_XEServer_Licenses.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manage_XEServer_Licenses.htm)

#### Manage XEServer Licenses


You can manage your licenses without having to uninstall XEServer. There are two types of licenses that can be applied to XEServer:

- Evaluation – Distributed for evaluation purpose. After expiration date XEngine Server can not be started any more.
- Production – Distributed for production usage. After expiration date Information messages are logged, however all vital processes remain uninterrupted and XEngine Server can be successfully started and stopped.


Regardless of license type, the following license modifications are available:

- Standard – Provides limited functionality. Web Service Call, Rest Client, SOAP Call, and DB components are not accessible with this license.
- Professional - Provides full access to XEServer features.


To activate a new license:

1. On the System tab, expand XEServer, and then double-click Admin Console.
2. On the Outline tab, click XEServer.


The following information is displayed on the Admin Console tab.


*[Image: Admin Console tab]*


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| XEServer version | The current version of XEServer installation. |
| XESRoot | The path to XEServer installation. |
| XERoot | The path to XEngine installation. |
| ECRootPath | The path to Edifecs products root folder. |
| License | |
| Company name | The name of the company for which the license is issued. |
| Expiration date | The date after which the license becomes expired, and a user needs to purchase a new one. Expired product behavior depends on the license type. |
| Product version | The product version for which the license is issued. You can use the same license for new products in scope of same major version. For example, the 8.3.0 license can be used with all 8.x.x XEServer versions. |
| License type | The current license type. |
| Modification | The current license modification. |


1. Click Activate new license.
2. In the New License dialog box, click Browse (), and then provide a path to the license file (.lic).
3. Click OK. The license details are displayed.


*[Image: New License dialog box ]*

1. Click OK. A backup copy of your previous license is created.
2. Click Yes. Information about the backup copy is displayed.
3. Click OK. Your new license has been activated.


Note By default, EAM displays a notification 30 days before your XEServer license expires. If you want to set another time, add the property com.edifecs.eam.license.expiration.notification.days to the file eam.ini located at ${EAMRoot}\Client. For example, to get the notification 60 days before the expiration date, add -Dcom.edifecs.eam.license.expiration.notification.days=60

---

<a id="use-xeserver-as-windows-service"></a>

### Use XEServer as Windows Service

> 📄 Source: [Use_XEServer_as_Windows_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_XEServer_as_Windows_Service.htm)

#### Use XEServer as Windows Service


You can control XEServer profiles using Windows services. If you use a [Windows service account](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/identity-protection/access-control/service-accounts) to run the XEServer service, the account must have the [Log on as a service](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/it-pro/windows-2000-server/cc957141(v=technet.10)) privilege.


To install and control XEServer profiles as Windows services, use the following scripts located at ${XESRoot}\profiles\<profileName>\win-service\:


Note The scripts listed below must run under the [Log on as a service](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/it-pro/windows-2000-server/cc957141(v=technet.10))-privileged account or Administrator.

- install_service.bat - creates a new Windows service with the name Edifecs_XEServer_<profileName>.
- start_service.bat - starts the Windows service Edifecs_XEServer_<profileName>.
- stop_service.bat - stops the Windows service Edifecs_XEServer_<profileName>.
- uninstall_service.bat - uninstalls the Windows service Edifecs_XEServer_<profileName>.


Commands issued by the Windows Service differ somewhat from the commands issued by Edifecs Application Manager. The following table illustrates the differences between the commands:


| Windows Service Command | EAM Command | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Start Service | Start | Starts an XEServer process or connects to an already running process. |
| Stop Service | Shutdown | Stops all processes and shuts down the server. |
| Restart Service | Reset | Restarts the server following a Pause or Stop command. |



##### Configure XEServer Windows Service


By default, when you run install_service.bat script, the new Windows service is created with the following parameters:

- Startup type = manual. If you want the service to automatically start on the Windows startup, change the startup type to Automatic: *[Image: XEngine Server Properties]*
- Log on as = Local System account. With this option, the profile service can access only system environment variables. To allow the service to use custom Windows user's variables, set the service to log on under a specific Windows account. Warning When you set a profile's service to log as non-System Windows account, the service does not start until you log on to Windows under the corresponding account. For this reason, using the profile's service with the System account is a more reliable and automated option, as it does not require manual Windows log on in case of the computer restart. ## XEServer Permissions All XEServer functional modules (database, web, file system, jms endpoints) require the [Log on as a service](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/it-pro/windows-2000-server/cc957141(v=technet.10)) Windows account privilege at minimum. Click to see the XEServer functional modules list - Database interaction: DB components, Balance Counters, Batcher, and so on; - Cloud services (Amazon, MFT connectivity) - Embedded servers (ActiveMQ Server) - JMS, Kafka services - RESTful, Web service, REST Call, SOAP Call - Salesforce connectors - Groovy engine - Tracking and Duplication function - Timers - FTP services - File System endpoints - HDFS endpoints - Business Rules - Process Call - Crosswalk/ECL components - Claim Forms components - Regression Suite components - XPM/XESManager connectors
