<a id="terms"></a>

### Terms

> 📄 Source: [Terms.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Terms.htm)

#### Terms


###### Component


A basic route element which represents a particular data processing stage. Route components are inbound and outbound endpoints, processors, auxiliary elements. Exchanges are created within inbound components, then processed by processors and saved to the outbound endpoints. The full list of XEServer components is available on the EAM Palette.


###### Connection


Connections are used to bind components together and exchange data through these connections. There are two connection types supported in routes - the synchronous and asynchronous. Different connection types can be set for various parts of the route. This might be useful to balance the processing on the route.

- Sync connection: All the messages are sent successively. Only when the first message processing is complete on the component it was sent to, the second message is sent to this component.
- Async connection: All the messages are sent one by one. As soon as the processing starts on the first message, the second message is picked up and sent to the queue. You can set maximum number of messages to be sent to one component. When the number of allowed threads is reached, processor waits for at least one message to be processed and then picks up the next message.


By default, synchronous connection type is used.


###### Environment


A set of variables, constants, and additional parameters used for running XEServer processes. You can create, set, and edit XEServer environment properties both for the whole XEServer or for every profile individually.

- Profile Environment properties allow you to configure the work of XEServer components in a scope of a profile.
- XEServer Environment properties are set in the .properties file and can be viewed from Environment tab in XEServer section.


###### Message Exchange


A wrapper object that travels through route components and carries one or more XEServer messages. A message exchange consists of a set of message exchange properties and a body that holds one or more XEServer messages.


###### Message


An object that carries a single integral piece of data (for example, a file read from the file system or FTP server, a message from email box, a message from JMS queue, a record from a database, and so on) through routes. A message consists of a set of message properties and a body with the payload.


###### Message Properties


Key-value metadata used to pass extra information about XEServer messages. Properties can be referenced during the route run time to apply additional logic. Message properties can be updated and extended as the message passes through components on the route. Outbound components can store message properties in the form of .properties files.


###### Multimessage Exchange


A message exchange that contains more than one XEServer messages. It can be useful when it is necessary to start batching XData processing or it is necessary to send messages to JMS in one transaction.


###### Macro


XEServer supports a wide range of macros that allow you to dynamically reference useful XEServer properties, such as message properties, route properties, component-specific properties, dates, and so on. For the list of all supported macros, see [XEServer Route Macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm). Only macros have the capability to fully access the structure.


###### Profile


A profile is a java application with a custom environment that runs in a separate java virtual machine. When one profile is turned off, the others can continue working. Each profile can be configured to meet particular business needs.


###### Route


A route is a graphical representation of data process flow. Creating a route is an easy way to configure an XEServer process. The whole process with its components, connectors, and all other related items is displayed graphically on the Route Editor.


###### Service


XEServer supports services that can be used in different data processes.


###### Task


Tasks are created mostly in inbound components on the route or in other components that receive input data. Components create message exchanges and messages within a certain task. Tasks are transmitted between components with a set of message exchanges. The task is marked completed after all corresponding exchanges are processed. If one exchange fails, the entire task is marked as failed.

---

<a id="meet-edifecs-application-manager"></a>

### Meet Edifecs Application Manager

> 📄 Source: [Meet_EAM.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Meet_EAM.htm)

#### Meet Edifecs Application Manager


Edifecs Application Manager (EAM) is a design-time application that provides a convenient user interface to work with XEServer, XEngine, XEConnect, and other Edifecs products. EAM consists of two major components:

- EAM Server. Provides access to an XEServer instance for configuration, control, and monitoring. EAM Server must be installed on the same computer as XEServer. One EAM Server is used to control one XEServer instance.
- EAM Client. Provides access to one or more EAM servers. Also, provides a user interface to manage XEServer.


The following diagram illustrates the base Edifecs products stack (XEngine, XEServer, EAM, and XESManager) and how the products interact with each other to automate EDI data processing:

---

<a id="use-eam-to-work-with-xeserver"></a>

### Use EAM to work with XEServer

> 📄 Source: [Use_EAM_to_work_with_XEServer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_EAM_to_work_with_XEServer.htm)

#### Use EAM to work with XEServer


When XEServer, EAM server, and EAM client have been installed on the same computer (local XEServer installation), you can view the default local EAM server (service:jmx:jmxmp://localhost:5555/server) on the System tab of the EAM client:


*This local EAM server, in turn, establishes connectivity to XEServer. To start working with a local XEServer: Start the EAM client. On the EAM System tab, expand the local EAM server item (service:jmx:jmxmp://localhost:5555/server). Enter the credentials to access the EAM server. Expand the XEServer item. The local XEServer instance is ready for use. If XEServer and the EAM server have been installed on a remote computer (remote XEServer installation), you have to establish a connection to the remote EAM server first. The remote EAM server, in turn, establishes connectivity to XEServer. Note The remote EAM server must be running. To start working with a remote XEServer: Start the EAM client. Right-click the System tab, and then click New. The following dialog is displayed: Enter the Connection nickname. This nickname identifies the connection between the current EAM client and the remote EAM server and helps avoid confusion when you work with several XEServer instances. Enter the EAM server connection parameters. Select Simple mode to specify the host name (or IP address) and the port number of the computer with a running EAM server or use Advanced mode to enter the full URI to the remote EAM server. Click OK. The new EAM server item is added to the list of EAM servers on the EAM System tab. On the EAM System tab, expand the remote EAM server item. Enter the credentials required to access the remote EAM server. Expand the XEServer item. The remote XEServer is ready for use. Tip Once you establish a connection between the EAM client and the remote EAM server, the connection configuration is set and you do not have to repeat the steps each time you connect to XEServer. ## Connection Security Connection between the EAM client and the EAM server is secured with basic password authentication and encryption using the SSL channel to prevent random users from harming your environment. SSL ensures that the messages from both the sides remain unaltered from the source to the destination. The EAM client and the EAM server authenticate each other using the public key/certificate. If the client and the server certificate are not present in the truststore of the server and of the client respectively, the connection session is terminated at startup. The EAM client uses the keystore: ${EAMRoot}/Client/configuration/security/EAMClient.jks*. The basic authentication configuration file (that contains SSL properties) is *${EAMRoot}/Client/configuration/security/client.properties*. The EAM server uses the following keystore: *${EAMRoot}/Server/security/keystores/EAMServer.jks*. The configuration file is *${EAMRoot}/Server/security/config/server.properties*. ## EAM Server Connection Timeout When you click to connect to an EAM server, the EAM client has 15 seconds to establish a connection to the EAM server (EAM server, in turn, provides the connectivity to XEServer), if the connection fails, both the EAM client and the server log an error. When you connect to an EAM server that runs on a remote computer, these 15 seconds may be insufficient (for example, a high network latency, or a firewall hindering the connection traffic). You can override the default connection timeout value.


To increase the timeout within which the EAM client should connect to the remote EAM server, do the following:

- On the computer where EAM client is installed, go to the directory ${EAMRoot}/Client/.
- Open the file eam(64).ini and add the following property: -Deam.jmx.client.authentication.socket.wait.timeout=N where N is the desired timeout in seconds.


##### XEServer Multi-User Access


One EAM server can only serve one XEServer instance while several EAM clients can work with the same EAM server. When an EAM client edits the XEServer configuration, the XEServer configuration is locked for other EAM clients (or becomes available only in the read-only mode) while the edit session is active. When another EAM client attempts to access a locked XEServer configuration, you receive the following notification:



To view the locked profile configuration in the read-only mode, click View. You can also ask the user who has currently locked the configuration to release the lock - just type a message and click Try Disconnect - this message will pop up in the EAM client that is currently editing the XEServer instance.


##### Memory Consumption Tracing


EAM comes with an in-built memory tracing mechanism. You can configure EAM (both client and server) to log a corresponding entry when the usage of RAM in your system exceeds a predefined limit. By default, memory tracing is disabled.


To enable memory tracing:

1. Declare the property `-DXESMemTrace=true` in ${EAMRoot}\Client\eam(64).ini (EAM Client) and in ${EAMRoot}\Server\bin\eamserver.ini (EAM Server). This property enables the tracing mechanism.
2. Declare the property `-DMemoryTracer.Logger.Level={INFO|WARN|ERROR}` in ${EAMRoot}\Client\eam(64).ini (EAM Client) and in ${EAMRoot}\Server\bin\eamserver.ini (EAM Server). This property defines the severity level of memory tracer. The following severity levels are available: - INFO: creates a log entry with the current RAM usage. - WARN: creates a log entry only when the RAM usage exceeds 70%, - ERROR: creates a log entry only when the RAM usage exceeds 97%. Tip EAM runs a memory scan every two seconds.
3. Restart EAM Client and EAM Server.


You can view the memory tracer events in the following log files:

- EAM Client: ${EAMServer}\Client\workspace\.metadata\eam.client.memory.trace.log
- EAM Server: ${EAMServer}\Server\log\eam.server.memory.trace.log

---

<a id="run-your-first-profile"></a>

### Run Your First Profile

> 📄 Source: [Run_Your_First_Profile.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Run_Your_First_Profile.htm)

#### Run Your First Profile


The section shows you how to create your first XEServer profile from scratch and test. The single profile's route described in this section picks data from the file system, archives the data, and stores the archive to the specified location. For more sophisticated real-life EDI processing scenarios, visit [Edifecs Library](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Home_Edifecs_Library_Profiles.htm).


To run the profile, you must perform the following steps:

1. Create a new profile 1. Open Edifecs Application Manager. 2. On the EAM System tab, expand the EAM server item (by default, the local EAM server is available: service:jmx:jmxmp://localhost:5555/server ). 3. Enter credentials to access the EAM server. 4. Right-click XEServer, and click New. The following dialog is displayed:*[Image: New Profile]* 5. Enter a name for the new profile. 6. Select the Default template. This creates an empty profile. 7. Click OK. 8. On the EAM System tab, expand Profiles. The newly created profile is displayed in the list.
2. Create a new route 1. On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer, and then expand Profiles. 2. Expand the newly created profile. 3. Double-click Configuration. 4. On the EAM Outline tab, right-click Service Manager, and then click New Route. EAM Route Editor is displayed. 5. Click Save () or press **CTRL+S** to save the route.
3. Configure the route 1. In the Route Editor, on the Palette, in the Inbound Endpoints section, select File System, and then place the selected component on the Route Editor canvas. This component is used to pick up data files from the file system and pass the data to other XEServer components in the form of XEServer message exchange. 2. Configure the FS Inbound component: 1. Select the FS Inbound component and on the Route component properties tab, click Settings. 2. In the Source folder field, provide a path to the directory from which the component should pick up data files. For this scenario, use the default value. The specified source folder is created automatically when the profile starts. 3. In the Route Editor, on the Palette, in the Data Processors section, select Zip, and then place the selected component on the Route Editor canvas. This component is used to compress the data. 4. Configure the Zip component: 1. Select the Zip component and on the Route component properties tab, click Settings. 2. In the Compression level field, specify the level of data compression. For this scenario, use the default compression level 9 which provides maximum compression effectiveness. 3. In the File name template field, specify a template used to generate a name for the result archive. For this scenario, use the default template {E_ID}_{M_SID}.{M_Ext}. Here {E_ID} is a [macro](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm) that will be resolved to XEServer message exchange id at run time, {M_SID} - is the ordinal position of XEServer message within the message exchange, and {M_Ext} is the extension of resulting file. 5. In the Route Editor, on the Palette, in the Outbound Endpoints section, select File System, and then place the selected component on the Route Editor canvas. This component is used to store data from XEServer message exchange to a file on the file system. 6. Configure the FS Outbound component: 1. Select the FS Outbound component and on the Route component properties tab, click Settings. 2. In the Destination folder field, specify a path to the destination folder. For this scenario, use the default destination folder which is created automatically after the data is successfully processed and is located at ${XESRoot}\profiles\[ProfileName]\workspace\test-data\outbound. 3. In the File name template, specify a template used to generate a name for the result file. For this scenario, use the default template {E_ID}-{T_Cnt[RIdx]}-{E_CName}_{E_Out}.{M_Ext}. 7. Connect the components as shown in the image: 8. Click Save () or press CTRL+S to save changes.
4. Start the profile To start the profile: - On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer. - Double-click Admin Console. - On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need. - On the Admin Console tab, click Start. - Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started
5. Test the profile To process test data files: 1. Go to the ${XESRoot}\test\data\native directory. 2. Copy the 4010_270-ex1.dat, 4010_270-ex1_A1.dat, and 4010_271-ex1.dat data files to the source folder of the FS Inbound component (${XESRoot}\profiles\{ProfileName}\workspace\test-data\inbound). #### Expected results: Three .zip archives are available at ${XESRoot}\profiles\{ProfileName}\workspace\test-data\outbound.

---

<a id="profile-design-recommendations"></a>

### Profile Design Recommendations

> 📄 Source: [Profile_Design_Recommendations.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Design_Recommendations.htm)

#### Profile Design Recommendations


This topic describes recommendations to help you avoid possible issues that you may encounter when creating a profile configuration. Before you start designing your profile, learn the basic XEServer [terms](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Terms.htm) and usage recommendations provided below.


###### Async connection


With asynchronous [connection type](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Route_Connection_Types.htm), messages are sent one by one. As soon as the processing starts on the first message, the second message is picked up and sent to the queue, without waiting for the message processing to complete. This type of connection introduces asynchrony in route execution that may improve performance, but makes profile review and troubleshooting more complicated.


Usage recommendations

- Before you apply async connectors, make sure you have a clear understanding of their architecture.
- Use async connectors before time-consuming operations on leaf components (for example, before the DB Writer component if writing files to the database takes a lot of time).


###### File system components


Consider the following possible issues when using the [FS Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm) and [FS Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Outbound.htm) components in your profile logic.

- If the FS Inbound component working in the By command [activation mode](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Activation_mode) reports files to the [Checkpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint.htm) component, the processing after the Checkpoint component does not continue until the FS Inbound component reports all the files in a folder to the Checkpoint. Thus, if the source folder for the FS Inbound component contains a lot of files, processing on the route may become more time-consuming.
- If you run multiple instances of the route with the FS Inbound component working in the Route start [activation mode](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Activation_mode), all these instances read one and the same folder and compete with each other for files. This may lead to significant performance degradation or a deadlock on the FS Inbound components.
- FS Inbound component allows you to define [exclude/include masks](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Include_masks) with regular expressions, which affects performance.
- The rename [lock type](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Lock_type) allows the FS Inbound component to start working at the file if it managed to rename the file. Windows file systems ensure that the file cannot be renamed until it's writing is complete. However, Linux file systems allow to rename the file even when another process is writing it, which may cause issues when the files are being written by third-party applications other than by the FS Outbound components.
- The OS lock [lock type](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Lock_type) does not lock a file for the whole processing, but tries to acquire a lock on the file to make sure that it is not already locked by another process.
- The check for stable size [lock type](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Lock_type) is designed to overcome drawbacks of the rename lock type on Linux file systems, but it may be difficult to configure it properly.


###### Checkpoint component


[Checkpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint.htm) allows you to continue data processing in case of a hardware crash or a processing error. At a high level, Checkpoint stores data in a persistent storage and re-transmits data from the storage until the data is successfully processed by the route after the Checkpoint component, or until the error limit is achieved.


Note that the Checkpoint component does not report data to the next component until the XEServer task within which the inbound data was received is complete.


Usage recommendations

- Do not use the components that iteratively report data to Checkpoint within a single XEServer task (for example, the [Native Processing](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Native_Processing_Component.htm) component or the [FS Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm) component in By command activation mode).


###### Aggregation component


When using the [Aggregation](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Aggregation_Overview.htm) component, keep in mind the recommendations below.


Usage recommendations

- Do not use a constant as a [correlation ID](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Aggregation_Correlation_Properties.htm#Correlation), otherwise all the inbound XEServer messages are grouped into one aggregation, leading to unexpected [completion strategy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Aggregation_Completion_Messages.htm) behavior.
- Configure [aggregation timeout](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Aggregation_Common_Properties.htm) based on your business case specifics - the bigger timeout, the more resources are consumed. It is a good practice to define timeouts in XEServer [profile environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) properties.
- The [completion strategy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Aggregation_Completion_Messages.htm) by Task completion has been deprecated. This functionality was implemented before introducing route instances and sub-routes to scale message processing. Use the completion strategy by Last message contains property and scale a route by increasing the number of route instances.


###### Groovy scripts


The XEServer SDK for Groovy allows to get message body as a string using the message.bodyAsString function. This function returns the first 10 MB of message body and stores it in memory.


Usage recommendations

- To get the full message body, use the message.bodyAsSmartStream.inputStream function.


###### Subroute component

- The [Subroute](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Subroute.htm) and [Parent Route](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Parent_Route_Inbound.htm) components use XEServer tasks to share the processing context among the routes. The Parent Route component starts a new task, and after processing on the route is finished, this task is merged into the task on the parent route. This flow makes route review and troubleshooting complicated.
- In addition to the specifics related to XEServer tasks, the Subroute component has a specific error handling logic. If an error occurred on a sub-route, this error is available only on this sub-route. That is, even though the sub-route failure leads to the Subroute component failure on the parent route, the error occurred on the sub-route is not available on the parent route since this error is replaced with the java.lang.Exception “One or more sub tasks are failed.”


###### Balance Counters

- If a counter is not completed, the [BCounter Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/BCounter_Inbound.htm) component reports this counter as expired.
- If a counter is not updated due to errors on a route, which makes the counter incomplete, this counter will be reported later after its expiration. What should be considered is that a small expiration timeout may be triggered in case of a temporarily slow processing even if the processing is eventually successful, and a big expiration timeout does not allow you to react to the issues in a short timeframe.

- The [BCounter Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/BCounter_Outbound.htm) component has the Transacted functionality, meaning that all the counter updates are applied only after the XEServer task is complete. If the task fails, counters are not updated.

---

<a id="xeserver-in-containerized-environments"></a>

### XEServer in Containerized Environments

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_in_Containerized_Environments.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_in_Containerized_Environments.htm)

#### XEServer in Containerized Environments


You can run XEServer profile not only on your on-premise infrastructure, but also in lightweight containers managed by an orchestration tool like Kubernetes, Openshift, and so on. To adapt XEServer profiles to running in a containerized environment, container mode has been introduced. This mode prevents XEServer components from saving data to the local file system in favor of some persistent storage outside of a container, thus following best practices in containerized environments operation.


This help topic takes you through the following:

- How to enable/disable container mode for a profile and for a particular XEServer functionality,
- What XEServer components/services do or stop doing when you switch to container mode,
- Which XEServer settings are recommended for proper operation in containerized environment.


##### Enable container mode


You can enable/disable container mode for a profile and/or for a specific functionality.


###### To activate container mode for XEServer profile:

1. On the [global](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) or [profile](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) Environment tab, add a new environment properties category.
2. In the new category, add a new environment property EDFX_CONTAINER_MODE=true.


###### To activate/deactivate container mode for a specific XEServer functionality:

1. On the [global](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) or [profile](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) Environment tab, go to the environment properties category you created for container mode properties or add a new one.
2. Add one or more environment properties and set them to true or false. The following properties are supported:


##### XEServer settings in container mode


Some of the profile, route, and component settings need to be adjusted for proper operation in a containerized environment. Below are the changes applied automatically when you enable container mode, as well as recommendations you need to consider to customize your profiles for running in a container.


###### Profile and route settings:

- Profile recovery: The [profile restart settings](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_QoS_Tab.htm) are ignored and disabled since crash detection and handling must be performed externally on the orchestration tool side.
- Error Channel: By default, XEServer stores error messages to a directory within a profile. In the container mode, the error channel can redirect error messages to a custom directory, typically located on a shared file system. There are following properties to tune this behavior: - EDFX_DEFAULT_ERROR_CHANNEL_CONTAINER_MODE: activates the container mode for the error channel. - EDFX_DEFAULT_ERROR_CHANNEL_CONTAINER_MODE_PATH: defines a path to store error messages. This should be a directory on a shared file system. - EDFX_DEFAULT_ERROR_CHANNEL_CONTAINER_MODE_DISABLED: disables storing errors to the default error channel (to the directory within a profile) for all the profiles.
- Route Tracer: For tracer files not to be saved inside a container, you should mount an external folder to your container and specify a path to it by setting the property EDFX_TRACES_ROOT=<tracerFilesLocation> on the [global](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) or [profile](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) Environment tab. When you set this property with container mode enabled, XEServer ignores the Base location specified on the [Tracer tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Route_Properties.htm#Tracer) of route properties. To activate route tracing dynamically in a containerized environment, use [SSH console](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SSH_Console_Parameters_Overview.htm) or [HTTPS endpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_Using_HTTP(S)_Endpoints.htm#PUT_tracer).
- Communication channels: Due to security reasons, JMX and SSH channels can be started on your local host only. To communicate with XEServer profiles in containers, use [HTTPS endpoints](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_Using_HTTP(S)_Endpoints.htm).


###### Component/service settings:


Click the XEServer functionality below to see configuration recommendations and learn what is changed in container mode automatically.

 *Audit In container mode, all the data transfers from Audit service to Visualizer Server are handled through a Kafka channel. Audit service acts as a producer that sends messages to the Kafka topic for audit messages. Visualizer Server acts as a consumer that reads messages from the Kafka topic and processes them. What is changed automatically Since all the audit events and logs are sent to the Kafka channel, the settings on the Audit service's Log Configuration and Visualizer Server tabs become disabled. What you should configure For instructions on how to configure Kafka channel for communication with Visualizer Server in a containerized environment, view Kafka Configurations. Balance Counters What is changed automatically In container mode, Balance Counter service sends commands directly to a database without buffering them on the file system. File System components What you should configure For FS Inbound component, you should specify a location on a shared file system to store original files for failed routes. By default, if a route fails, XEServer stores the files picked by FS Inbound to a directory within the profile and this location can be overridden using the com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.fs.recovery.template property. In the container mode, use the EDFX_FILESYSTEM_CONTAINER_MODE_ROLLBACK_FILENAME_TEMPLATE property to define a path and a file name mask to store original files for failed tasks. This property is effective if EDFX_CONTAINER_MODE=true or EDFX_FILESYSTEM_CONTAINER_MODE=true and has a higher priority than com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.fs.recovery.template. To avoid orphaned locks left when a container dies, set the environment property EDFX_FILESYSTEM_CONTAINER_MODE_EXTRA_LOCK=true. This environment property for container mode has the same effect as the system property com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.fs.cluster.extralock=true. To enable extra locking for FS Inbound and Outbound components, your file system must support file locks. FTP Events In container mode, if FTP Event Router service fails to send events to a JMS queue, it temporarily saves these events to the local file system and attempts to re-send them from this location. What is changed automatically If you configured buffering to internal JMS, it becomes disabled. What you should configure On the Profile Startup tab, add the system property xes.docker.event.processor.fs.storage.path to define where to save the events before sending them to the JMS queue. Lineage (distributed with XES Module for FHIR) In container mode, all the data transfers from Lineage service to Visualizer Server are handled through a Kafka channel. Lineage service acts as a producer that sends messages to the Kafka topic for tracking messages. Visualizer Server acts as a consumer that reads messages from the Kafka topic and processes them. What is changed automatically Since all the tracking records and logs are sent to the Kafka channel, the settings on the Lineage service's Log Configuration and Visualizer Server tabs become disabled. What you should configure For instructions on how to configure Kafka channel for communication with Visualizer Server in a containerized environment, view Kafka Configurations. Scheduled Jobs What you should configure To prevent data loss in container mode, you should not use the default H2 database located on your file system. To store job configurations in containerized environment, set the JDBC URL that points to an external database. Sequencer Sequence Manager is not scalable by design. For proper functioning in container mode, consider using multiple external databases to avoid issues with the same Group IDs in different Sequence Manager services. What you should configure On the Configuration tab of Sequence Manager service, add connection to external database. Visualizer Reporter In container mode, all the data uploads from the Visualizer Reporter component to Visualizer Server is handled through a Kafka channel. Visualizer Reporter component acts as a producer that sends messages to the Kafka topic for tracking messages. Visualizer Server acts as a consumer that reads messages from the Kafka topic and processes them. What is changed automatically Since all data is sent to the Kafka channel and thus Visualizer Reporter service does not establish connection directly with Visualizer Server, the settings on the Visualizer Reporter service's Configuration and SSL Settings tabs become disabled. The Mode control on the Visualizer Reporter component's Common tab is disabled - the component uploads data to Visualizer Server synchronously. What you should configure For instructions on how to configure Kafka channel for communication with Visualizer Server in a containerized environment, view Kafka Configurations. ### XEServer Agent telemetry In container mode, XEServer profiles communicate with XEServer Agent using the telemetry protocol. This data exchange mode has been introduced to allow profiles to transmit their data such as statistics, events, and statuses in containerized environments. The interaction between XEServer profiles and XESManager in container mode looks as follows: The telemetry layer serves the bidirectional connection between XEServer profiles and the agents. The profiles send their data to the agents through HTTP along with the information for the agents on how to find these profiles and connect to them. What you should configure On the profile Environment tab, add the environment properties to define the following: The receiver type: xeserver.telemetry.sink=com.edifecs.etools.management.telemetry.sink.http.HttpSink The receiver address (the agent's HTTP URL) for the profile to connect to the agent: xeserver.telemetry.http.endpoint=https\://<xesagent_box_ip>\:<port>/telemetry You can enable/disable XES Agent telemetry using the environment property EDFX_TELEMETRY_CONTAINER_MODE=<true|false> both for the profile and for the agent. ## Containerized Environments Configuration This section explains how XEServer prioritizes environment variables and Java system properties, how it resolves macros during runtime, and how you can externalize sensitive configuration values using mounted files. Understanding these mechanisms helps ensure consistent behavior across environments and enhances the security and maintainability of your configuration setup. ### Environment Variables Priority By default, when you pass parameters to a container as both environment variables and Java system properties, XEServer gives priority to Java system properties. To override this behavior and prioritize environment variables, set the EDFX_ENV_VARIABLES_PRIORITY_MODE* environment variable to true in your operating system's environment variables.
When environment variable priority mode is enabled, XEServer resolves parameters in the following order:

1. It first searches for the parameter in the operating system's environment variables.
2. If the environment variable is not found, it searches for the parameter in the Java system properties.


XEServer recognizes environment variable names in both of the following formats:

- Uppercase with underscores (for example, *DB_CONNECTION_URL*)
- Lowercase with dots (for example, *xeserver.telemetry.http.timeout*)


###### Macro Resolution Behavior


By default, during macro resolution, XEServer searches for parameter names as-is, without converting their format. To enable automatic conversion of macro names to uppercase with underscores (for example, ${*java.home*} to ${*JAVA_HOME*}), set the environment variable *EDFX_ENV_MACRO_CANONICAL_NAME_ENABLED=true*.


With this setting enabled, XEServer resolves macros in the following order:

1. It searches for the environment variable *parameter.name.*
2. If not found, it converts the name to uppercase with underscores and searches for *PARAMETER_NAME.*
3. If still not found, it searches for the Java system property *parameter.name.*


###### Externalize Properties and Secrets


You can externalize properties and secrets by setting the *EDFX_PROPERTIES_CONTAINER_MODE_PATH* environment variable. This variable defines the path to a directory containing mounted files, where each file represents a property or secret.


When this path is configured, XEServer reads each file in the directory and treats:

- The **file name** as the property name.
- The **file content** as the property value.


These values can be referenced in your XES configuration using *${...}* placeholders, just like environment variables. Additionally, secrets stored in this directory are compatible with all XES-supported encoders.


###### Requirements:

- Place all files directly in the specified directory. Nested subfolders are not supported.
- Name each file exactly as the corresponding environment variable or property name. If *EDFX_ENV_MACRO_CANONICAL_NAME_ENABLED* is enabled, you may use canonical formatting.
- Do not use file extensions. Each file must contain only the UTF-8 encoded value of a single property or secret, without enclosing the property name.


**Note**: When this path is configured, the values from these files take precedence over both environment variables and Java system properties, regardless of the *EDFX_ENV_VARIABLES_PRIORITY_MODE* setting.

---

<a id="kafka-configurations"></a>

#### Kafka Configurations

> 📄 Source: [Kafka_Configurations.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Configurations.htm)

##### Kafka Configurations


In container mode, all the data transfers to Visualizer Server from Audit service, Lineage service, and Visualizer Reporter component are handled through a Kafka channel. Audit service, Lineage service, and Visualizer Reporter component act as producers that send messages to the Kafka topics for audit or tracking messages. Visualizer Server acts as a consumer that reads messages from the Kafka topics and processes them. Below are the instructions on how to configure Kafka channel for communication with Visualizer Server in a containerized environment.


What you should configure on the producer side


On the [profile Environment tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm), add the environment properties to define the following:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Topic settings | |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_AUDIT_TOPIC_NAME=<audit-topic-name> | Kafka topic name for audit messages. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_TRACKING_TOPIC_NAME=<tracking-topic-name> | Kafka topic name for tracking messages. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_AUDIT_PARTITION=<positive-integer-value> | (Optional) Partition for audit topic. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_TRACKING_PARTITION=<positive-integer-value> | (Optional) Partition for tracking topic. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_AUDIT_NUM_PARTITIONS=<positive-integer-value> | (Optional) Number of partitions of the Kafka topic specified in the EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_AUDIT_TOPIC_NAME property. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_TRACKING_NUM_PARTITIONS=<positive-integer-value> | (Optional) Number of partitions of the Kafka topic specified in the EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_TRACKING_TOPIC_NAME property. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_AUDIT_REPLICATION_FACTOR=<positive-integer-value> | (Optional) Replication factor for the Kafka topic specified in the EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_AUDIT_TOPIC_NAME property. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_TRACKING_REPLICATION_FACTOR=<positive-integer-value> | (Optional) Replication factor for the Kafka topic specified in the EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_TRACKING_TOPIC_NAME property. |
| Connectivity settings | |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP_ADDRESS=<host>:<port> | Kafka address (host and port of a container with the Kafka service). |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_SECURITY_PROTOCOL=<protocol>. | Protocol used for communication with brokers. Possible values are: PLAINTEXT, SSL, SASL_PLAINTEXT, SASL_SSL. This property is the Kafka's security.protocol parameter alias. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_TRUSTSTORE_LOCATION=<path> | Path to a Keystore with trusted certificates. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_TRUSTSTORE_PASSWORD=<password> | Password to access the Truststore. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_VALIDATE_HOSTNAME=<true\|false> | Enable/disable hostname validation. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_KEYSTORE_LOCATION=<path> | Path to the file that contains Keystore certificates. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD=<password> | Password to access the Keystore. |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_SSL_KEYSTORE_KEY_PASSWORD=<password> | Password to the private key. |
| Messaging settings | |
| EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_COMPRESSION_THRESHOLD=<limit-value> | Limit in megabytes after which the message will be compressed before being sent to Kafka. The default value is 1. |
| xes.docker.vs.audit.fs.storage.path=<path> | (Optional) The path to the file system where Audit data will be saved as files if Kafka is unavailable or doesn't accept messages. |
| xes.docker.vs.reporter.fs.storage.path=<path> | (Optional) The path to the file system where Visualizer Reporter and Lineage data will be saved as files if Kafka is unavailable or doesn't accept messages. |
| xes.docker.fs.storage.files.limit=<limit-value> | (Optional) The maximum number of files that can be saved in the file system if Kafka is unavailable or doesn't accept messages. Upon reaching the limit, old files are removed. The default value is 1000. |
| xes.docker.fs.storage.retransmission.timeout=<time> | (Optional) The time interval (in milliseconds) after which XEServer tries to send data to Kafka again, from the location specified in xes.docker.vs.audit.fs.storage.path and xes.docker.vs.reporter.fs.storage.path. The default value is 1000. |



To specify additional [Kafka client configurations](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#configuration), put these configurations to a .properties file as in the example below and specify the path to this file in EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_PROPERTIES=<path-to-file>.

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)


Example




```

##### properties to enable SASL mechanism:
sasl.jaas.config=org.apache.kafka.common.security.plain.PlainLoginModule required username="" password="
" sasl.mechanism=PLAIN security.protocol=SASL_SSL ``` What you should configure on the consumer side


To configure Kafka channel on the consumer (Visualizer Server) side, define the above properties as system environment variables or add them to the kafka.properties file. This file also allows [properties in Spring notation](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/docs/current/reference/html/application-properties.html#appendix.application-properties.integration) prefixed with `spring.kafka` as in the example below. You can set the path to the file by the property spring.cloud.kafka.config.location or use the default path ${VSRoot}/config/kafka/kafka.properties

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)


Example




```
spring.kafka.properties.security.protocol=SASL_SSL
spring.kafka.properties.sasl.mechanism=PLAIN
spring.kafka.properties.sasl.jaas.config=org.apache.kafka.common.security.plain.PlainLoginModule required username="$ConnectionString" password="…"
```






###### Authentication


For client connections, you can either use [SASL mechanism types supported by the XEServer Kafka Service 2](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_2_Service.htm#SASL), or upload other login modules by placing a .jar file with the authentication library to ${XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\kafka and mounting this directory to a container. For the library to be available in Visualizer Server, place the .jar file to ${VSRoot}\components\tomcat\lib\additional


To provide configuration properties, use the .properties file that you reference in EDFX_VS_CONTAINER_MODE_KAFKA_PROPERTIES=<path-to-file>. For exact list of properties and their values, refer to the documentation for the authentication mechanism you are going to use.

---

<a id="xeserver-structure"></a>

## XEServer Structure

> 📄 Source: [Book_XEServer_Structure.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_XEServer_Structure.htm)

### XEServer Structure


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="xeserver-agent"></a>

### XEServer Agent

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Agent.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Agent.htm)

#### XEServer Agent


Each XEServer installation includes an XEServer agent - a connectivity module that acts as a supervisor over XEServer profiles. The main tasks of an XEServer agent are the following:

- Monitors the structure and health status of XEServer profiles and reports this data to XESManager in real time.
- Gathers statistics from XEServer profiles and reports to XESManager in an aggregated form.
- Receives control commands from XESManager (START, STOP, and so on) and executes the commands on profiles.


By default, an XEServer agent automatically starts as a separate process as soon as you start any XEServer profile. You can disable automatic agent startup and use the scripts in the directory ${XESRoot}\bin\agent to start and stop the agent.


##### Agent Registration


Before an XEServer agent can communicate with XESManager, the agent must be [registered](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/XEServer_Agents_Overview.htm#AgentRegistration) in XESManager. By default, XESManager automatically registers the agent that belongs to the local XEServer installation (${ECRootPath}\XEServer).


Tip If you observe authorization errors when you register an agent (typically, this may happen after XEServer upgrade or re-installation), run the cleanup_security_key.bat(.sh) script to generate new encryption keys. The script is located in ${XESRoot}\bin\agent\.


###### Agent auto-discovery


**Note** When XEServer and XESManager are installed on the same machine, auto-discovery has to be set to *false*. If auto-discovery is required, configure manually the correct host and then enable auto-discovery. Enabling auto-discovery without setting the correct host works in case XEServer and XESManager are installed on different machines.


When you register a new agent, you have to manually provide the agent's host name and port number in the XESManager web interface. However, instead of manual registration, you can make use of the [Agent auto-discovery](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Agent_AutoDiscovery.htm) feature. With this feature, when an agent starts, it automatically submits a registration request to XESManager. You can configure auto-discovery settings in following ways:

- In EAM, on the [Auto-discovery](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Autodiscovery_Tab.htm) tab of the XEServer environment. This is the recommended and the most convenient way.
- By editing the discovery file under ${XESRoot}\platform\etc. This file lists all the supported properties with brief descriptions. Also, the file lists environment variables that you can declare in your OS/XES environment to configure auto-discovery behavior in systems without GUI (Docker).
- By editing startup.ini under ${XESRoot}\platform\etc. You can use the environment properties listed in the discovery file.


Note Auto-discovery settings have the following priority (from highest to lowest): - OS environment - XEServer environment - discovery file / EAM interface


##### Run agent as a Windows service


You can configure an XEServer agent to run as a Windows service. To do this:

1. Go to ${XESRoot}\platform\agent\win-service.
2. Run install_service.bat as administrator.
3. Run start_service.bat to start the service.


##### Enable/Disable XEServer agent


By default, an XEServer agent is enabled and automatically starts when you start any profile. To disable the agent, use the following properties in the startup.ini file located at ${XESRoot}\platform\etc.

- `-Dcom.edifecs.xeserver.controller.agent.start=[false|true]`: If set to `true` (default value), XEServer profiles try to start the agent process if they detect that the agent is offline. If set to `false`, the agent does not start with profiles. In this case, you can manually control the agent life cycle by using the start-agent.bat and stop-agent.bat scripts under ${XESRoot}\bin\agent.
- `-Dcom.edifecs.xeserver.controller.agentclient.start=[false|true]`: If set to `true` (default value), XEServer profiles try to connect to the agent to send and receive runtime metrics. If set to `false`, the agent will not receive runtime metrics from XEServer profiles. In this case, if the agent is still running, XESManager displays the agent as online, but all the profiles as offline.


##### XEServer agent memory consumption


By default, the XEServer agent process starts with the `-Xmx2g` JVM flag which means that it can consume up to 2 GB of RAM. This value is sufficient for most of the business cases, however, if you want to increase this value, you should specify a custom `-Xmx` value in ${XESRoot}\platform\agent\etc\startup.ini.


##### Container mode


If you run XEServer profile configurations in a containerized environment, read [this topic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_in_Containerized_Environments.htm) to learn about the required changes in settings.

<a id="xeserver-environment-overview"></a>

#### XEServer Environment Overview

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Environment.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Environment.htm)

##### XEServer Environment Overview


Using XEServer Environment, you can declare global variables that can be referenced from your routes at run time. Global variables are available for all XEServer profiles. To set a variable which is available only within a specific profile, use the [Profile Environment Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm).


Note If there is a profile configuration currently opened in EAM and you make an update to XEServer environment, such update is not reflected on the route [Effective Properties tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Effective_Tab.htm) until you close the profile configuration and opened it again.

---

<a id="environment-tab"></a>

#### Environment Tab

> 📄 Source: [Environment_Tab.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm)

##### Environment Tab


On this tab, you can define global XEServer environment properties available for all profiles.



###### Environment Properties Declaration


XEServer stores all environment properties in groups - Environment properties categories. These are containers used to group logically related properties. To define a new environment property, first you have to create a new Environment properties category and then you can add properties to this category. XEServer stores each properties category as a separate .properties file on the file system.


###### Environment Properties Usage


To reference a property defined on this tab, use the notation ${propertyName}. For example, when you configure an XEServer entity (a service, a route, or a route component), you can reference the environment properties as shown in the image below:



###### Encrypted Properties


You can make your Environment properties categories encrypted. This means that all the properties in such a category are stored in an encrypted form. To create an encrypted category, select the option Encrypted properties file when you create a new Environment properties category as shown in the image:



Note Once your properties are encrypted, you cannot bring your environment properties file (.properties) back to the decrypted form.


When you configure a route component and have to specify a password (or other sensitive data) in the component's settings, a best practice is to store your password as an encrypted environment property and then reference this property in the component configuration (for example, ${myPassword}).


You can also create and change encrypted properties using command-line [Crypto tool](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Encryption_Tool.htm).

---

#### Effective Tab

On this tab you can view the full list of XEServer and operating system variables in the read-only mode:

<a id="startup-tab"></a>

#### Startup Tab

On this tab, you can specify JVM parameters (-Xmx, -Xrs, Garbage Collection settings, and so on) to be used when starting XEServer profiles:

<a id="cloud-account-tab"></a>

#### Cloud Account Tab

> 📄 Source: [Cloud_Account_Tab.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Cloud_Account_Tab.htm)

##### Cloud Account Tab


On this tab, you can enter credentials to enable access to [Smart Trading Cloud services](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/SmartTradingCloud/Content/Home.htm) for the features that require authentication at Smart Trading Cloud (for example, [Cloud Archive components](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Cloud_Archive_Components_Overview.htm), [Cloud selector](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEngine_Service.htm#Authentication), and so on). Credentials specified on this tab can be used by these components in all profiles.



You can provide credentials of two types:

- User credentials: a username-password pair used to access your Smart Trading Account.
- Application credentials: a special token used for programmatic access to your Smart Trading Account. In this case, you can either feed credentials.stcc file to EAM or enter the token string. For more information on how to get application credentials, see [Application Users](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/SmartTradingCloud/Content/Application_Users.htm).


Note When you update ST credentials on this tab, the credentials are also updated on the [Profile Cloud Account Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Cloud_Account_Tab.htm).


###### Smart Trading Cloud domain


By default, XEServer connects to the Smart Trading Account service hosted on [https://edifecsfedcloud.com](https://edifecsfedcloud.com/). If you want to work with the Smart Trading Cloud services hosted on a different domain (for example, www.cоllablynk.com), uncomment the required domain name in ${ECRootPath}\Common\cloud.properties. After you update the file, EAM restart is required.

---

<a id="auto-discovery-tab"></a>

#### Auto-Discovery Tab

> 📄 Source: [Autodiscovery_Tab.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Autodiscovery_Tab.htm)

##### Auto-Discovery Tab


On this tab, you can define auto-discovery settings for an [XEServer agent](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Agent.htm). If the auto-discovery function is enabled for an agent, XESManager can automatically detect the agent when the agent starts.


By default, registering an XEServer agent in XESManager requires a manual setup. For example, if you want to distribute an XEServer configuration among different environments (docker, AWS cloud, desktop, and so on) and want to control all the XEServer instances through XESManager, then you have to register each XEServer agent in the XESManager web interface. The agent auto-discovery feature allows you to automate this step. With auto-discovery, you include the XESManager address into your XEServer configuration. When such a configuration is deployed and the agent starts, it notifies XESManager (using the address stored in the XEServer configuration) about the new agent location . This way, XESManager can automatically detect and register the agents with auto-discovery enabled. For more information, see the XESManager [Agent Auto-Discovery](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Agent_AutoDiscovery.htm) page.


###### Auto-Discovery Settings


The following settings are available on the Auto-Discovery tab:


*Field Description Enable auto-discovery Select to enable the auto-discovery function for your XEServer agent. **Note** When XEServer and XESManager are installed on the same machine, auto-discovery has to be set to false*. If auto-discovery is required, configure manually the correct host and then enable auto-discovery. Enabling auto-discovery without setting the correct host works in case XEServer and XESManager are installed on different machines.








XESManager connection settings


In this section, you specify the location of XESManager host.






 Host


 Enter the name of the host where XESManager is running.




 Port


 Enter the port number to be used for the connection.




 Base URL


 The base RESTful endpoint for sending requests. Use the default value unless you have changed XESManager settings that affect RESTful URL.




 HTTP timeout


 Set the time frame in seconds during which the connection to XESManager must be established.




 Authentication token


 Enter a token to authenticate the auto-discovery requests to XESManager. If the token authentication is enabled on the XESManager side, XEServer agents must provide a valid token to access the XESManager auto-discovery API. This token is generated on the XESManager [Agents](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Agent_AutoDiscovery.htm) page. If the value is empty, the token authentication is disabled.




 Enable SSL


 Select this option if you want your agent to send registration requests over the SSL-secured channel. During the registration phase, XEServer agent acts as a client, and XESManager represents the server side.






Trusted Store


The trusted store contains XESManager certificates to identify XESManager as a trusted party.






 Enable hostname validation


 If selected, XEServer compares XESManager's host name with the host name in the certificate (the certificate sent to XEServer by XESManager). If the names do not match, the SSL connection is not established.




 Path


 Enter the path to a truststore with trusted certificates.




 Password


 Enter the password to the truststore. The default password is "password".






Key store


If the client authentication is enabled on the server (XESManager), this key store kicks in. The key store contains XEServer agent's key pairs (a private key and a certificate with a public key) used by XESManager to validate the XEServer's identity.






 Path


 Enter the path to the keystore file that contains key pair entries.




 Password


 Enter the password to the keystore.




 Key password


 Enter the password to the key pair.






XEServer agent settings






 Heartbeat


 The time interval at which the agent notifies XESManager that the agent is up and running. XEServer shares the heartbeat value with XESManager, so if XESManager does not receive a heartbeat request within the expected interval from the agent, this agent is considered inaccessible.




 Description


 The description of the agent. This description will be visible in XESManager interface.




 XESManager Group


 The agent group. The agent will be displayed under the specified group in XESManager interface.







Agents auto-discovery can also be configured using the file [discovery](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Agent.htm#Discovery) available in the folder ${XESRoot}\platform\etc\discovery or the file [startup.ini](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Agent.htm#StartUpIni) available in the folder ${XESRoot}\platform\etc.

<a id="xeserver-environment-loading-order"></a>

#### XEServer Environment Loading Order

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Environment_Loading_Order.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Environment_Loading_Order.htm)

##### XEServer Environment Loading Order


When started, XEServer loads environment properties in the following order:

1. Profile environment
2. Global XEServer environment
3. Profile startup properties (${XESRoot}\profiles\<profileName>\etc\startup.ini)
4. Global XEServer startup properties (${XESRoot}\platform\etc\startup.ini)
5. Operating system environment
6. XEServer route environment


Note EAM ignores system properties defined in the startup.ini files using the "-D" parameter.

---

### Profiles

#### Profiles

<a id="xeserver-profiles-overview"></a>

#### XEServer Profiles Overview

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Profiles_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Profiles_Overview.htm)

##### XEServer Profiles Overview


An XEServer profile is an isolated processing environment within XEServer which typically performs one or more end-to-end data flow tasks. Splitting and grouping data orchestration tasks among multiple profiles makes your work with EDI more scalable and easy-to-maintain. Once you have configured a profile to perform a certain task, you can easily share it with other users or migrate the profile using in-build XEServer migration capabilities.


You can think of a profile as a sandbox that:

- Runs as a separate process in the operating system
- Can be controlled both locally and remotely
- Has a limited amount of resources allocated to it
- Has a private environment to store profile-private data
- Has an access to the XEServer global environment
- Has its own logger to trace the profile life cycle


XEServer profiles contain routes and services that make up actual data processing flow. One profile may contain one or more routes and services at a time. Each profile has a configurable built-in logging system that logs events that occur inside the profile.


You can control the execution of an XEServer profile in the following ways:

- By using Edifecs Application Manager Admin Console. This is the most common way to manage XEServer profiles through an intuitive user interface.
- By using Edifecs XESManager. Allows to control a profile from any location through the web interface. Also, provides detailed information about XEServer profiles and offers additional features such as alerts, event notifications, real-time and periodical statistics, and so on.
- By using an SSH client. You can connect to a profile using a traditional SSH client.


[Edifecs Library](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Home_Edifecs_Library_Profiles.htm) hosts a repository of preconfigured sample profiles that demonstrates you how to solve common EDI tasks and shows best practices on XEServer usage. Before you create a new profile from scratch, consult [Edifecs Library](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Home_Edifecs_Library_Profiles.htm). The number of sample profiles in the repository continuously increases and probably, the solution you are seeking for is already there.


For more information on how to create and start profiles, see Create and Start Profiles.

---

<a id="create-a-new-profile"></a>

#### Create a New Profile

> 📄 Source: [Create_a_New_Profile.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Create_a_New_Profile.htm)

##### Create a New Profile


To create a new profile:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager (EAM).
2. On the EAM System tab, right-click Profiles and then click New. The following dialog box displayed:*[Image: New Profile]*
3. Select a template you want to use for the new profile. You can select templates from following locations: - Local: Select to use a local template provided with XEServer installation. - Edifecs Library: Select to pull a template from the [Edifecs Library](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Create_and_Start_XEServer_Profiles.htm). Edifecs Library is a directory in your Smart Trading Cloud Repository that contains XEServer profile images ready to be deployed into your XEServer installation. To access Edifecs Library, you should have a Smart Trading Cloud Repository Account .

1. In the Profile Name, enter the name for your profile.
2. Click OK.


###### Set Profile Ports


By default, when you create a new profile, XEServer automatically generates TCP ports (JMX, SSH, HTTP, HTTPS) to be used by the profile.


Note If you choose to generate ports automatically, XEServer makes sure that the generated port does not conflict with other XEServer profiles. However, you must ensure that the generated port does not conflict with other processes in the operating system.


To change the ports for an existing profile, use [Admin Console](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Admin_Console_Overview.htm#In).


To reduce the chance of a conflict, you can explicitly ban specific ports (for example, those ports occupied by other running applications) so that these ports are never used to create a new profile. To do this, you should add the reserved port to the special black list file C:\Edifecs\XEServer\platform\etc\reserved-ports.config. When XEServer generates ports for a new profile, XEServer excludes the ports defined in this file.


As an alternative, you can specify custom port values for your profile. To do this, clear the Generate ports check box and specify corresponding port values. For all the four protocols, you can specify any port number that is within the range 1000 - 65535. If the port you have specified is occupied by another XEServer profile, you receive a warning.

---

<a id="manage-xeserver-profiles"></a>

#### Manage XEServer Profiles

> 📄 Source: [Manage_XEServer_Profiles.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manage_XEServer_Profiles.htm)

##### Manage XEServer Profiles


Using the Admin console, you can create several profiles for XEServer to work simultaneously with different servers. One profile is created for each server. You can create as many profiles as you want, but the number that can be started simultaneously is controlled by your license.


The Admin console also displays an activity chart that graphically shows activity for all started servers and general CPU usage. On the [Outline tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Outline_Tab.htm), click profiles and the chart is displayed on the [Detail tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Detail_Tab.htm).


*[Image: Profile chart]*


The Summary CPU loading chart shows activity for all XEngine servers and services and lists the tasks that are being performed. When some services are active and no tasks are started, Summary CPU loading is the only way to see that some actions are being performed.


The CPU usage diagram graphically displays loading of all the available profiles and general CPU loading. There are several options of what can be displayed on the CPU usage diagram.

- CPU usage. Shows general CPU usage by all the profiles in XEServer.
- Load. Shows profiles loading in scope of the XEServer.
- Time range. Sets the timeline range to be displayed.

---

<a id="use-log-viewer"></a>

#### Use Log Viewer

> 📄 Source: [Profile_Log_Viewer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Log_Viewer.htm)

##### Profile Log Viewer


With Log Viewer, you can view logs generated by an XEServer profile. By default, XEServer stores the profile logs at ${XESRoot}\profiles\<yourProfileName>\log (default XEServer environment property XESLog). However, you can select a custom location to store the profile logs.


To configure the logs location:

1. Declare the property XESLog on the profile [Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) tab so that the property value points to a new location for storing log files.
2. Save the configuration to override the default logs location.
3. Restart the profile for changes to take effect. Log Viewer automatically picks up log files from the new XESLog location


Note After you add the new property XESLogand save the configuration, reopen Log Viewer User Interface in Edifecs Application Manager to pick up logs from the updated XESLog path.


To view the profile logs:

1. On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer, and then expand the profile you want to work with.
2. Double-click Log Viewer. Logs saved for the current profile are displayed on the Outline tab:*[Image: XEServer.LogViewer]*
3. Select a log you need to view in the Details pane:*[Image: Log Details pane]*


If a log file is too large, Log Viewer splits the file into pages (~5MB each). You can navigate between the pages using the Home, Next, Previous, and End controls on the right. Use the Auto refresh option to refresh the log file contents automatically as the log files are constantly updated with new events.


For more information about the log files, see [Log Files Types](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Log_Files_Types.htm).

---

<a id="profile-workspace"></a>

#### Profile Workspace

> 📄 Source: [Profile_Workspace.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Workspace.htm)

##### Profile Workspace


Each XEServer profile has a workspace directory that serves multiple purposes. By default, the workspace is located in the folder ${XESRoot}\profiles\<YourProfileName>\workspace and can be used for:

- submitting data for processing on the routes of the profile;
- storing the data processing results or an interim state of data;
- storing start-up logs;
- storing temporary files and/or folders required during processing. The folder ${XESRoot}\XEServer\profiles\<YourProfileName>\workspace\temp is created once the profiles start and can contain the various temporary files and/or folders. Click to view possible temporary files and folders. - SmartStream files that correspond to exchange messages with a large amount of content (more than 64 Kb) transmitted between XEServer components and that are currently being processed. - XEngine auxiliary data used in a BDF (big data processing file) mode. - The .jppf folder that contains JMX library files used for communication between XEServer and Edifecs Application Manager. - The cache-<UUID> folder that contains OSGI Felix frameworks bundles used for faster processing. - The jetty folders that contain unpacked WAR files with information on XEServer web applications. You can set a certain cleanup action to clean up files from the temp folder each time XEServer starts.To set a cleanup action for the temp folder: 1. In Edifecs Application Manager, on the System tab, expand XEServer. 2. In Profiles, expand the profile for which you want to clean up temporary files/folders, and then double-click Configuration. 3. On the Cleanup tab, in Cleanup configuration, select ${XESWorkspace}/temp (the path to the folder that you want to clean up), and then click Edit. 4. In the Edit Cleanup Path dialog , in Cleanup action, select one of the following actions: 5. Recursive: removes all the files and folders from the temp folder each time XEServer starts. 6. Non-recursive: removes only the files stored in the temp folder each time XEServer starts. Folders remain untouched. 7. None: does not remove anything from the temp folder each time XEServer starts. 8. Click OK, and then press Ctrl+S to save the changes. ### Change the workspace folder location To change the default location of the workspace folder for all XEServer profiles: 1. In Edifecs Application Manager, on the tab System, expand XEServer. 2. Double-click Admin Console and shut down all the profiles that are currently running. 3. On the System tab, double-click Environment. 4. On the Environment tab, click New to add a new Environment property category. 5. Enter a name for the category (for example, Workspace) and click Finish. 6. Click New again, and, in the displayed dialog, select Environment property. 7. Click Next and then, in Name, enter XESWorkspace, and in Value, enter the path to the folder you want to use. The new location becomes effective once you start your XEServer profiles.To change the default location of the workspace folder for a specific profile: 1. In Edifecs Application Manager, on the tab System, expand XEServer. 2. Double-click Admin Console and shut down the profile you need if it is currently running. 3. On the System tab, expand Profiles, and then expand the profile you need. 4. Double-click Configuration. 5. On the profile Environment tab, click New to add a new Environment property category. 6. Enter a name for the category (for example, Workspace) and click Finish. 7. Click New again, and, in the displayed dialog, select Environment property. 8. Click Next and then, in Name, enter XESWorkspace, and in Value, enter the path to the folder you want to use. The new location becomes effective once you start your XEServer profile. ### Change the temp folder location During data processing, if a file being processed is large in size, the temporary files in the temp folder may occupy significant amount of space on your disk. This may lead to a task failure for a certain profile due to not having enough space available for processing. To avoid this issue, you can change the temp folder location (for example, to use a remote location). To do this, use one of the following options (in order of priority): - Set the [XEngine parameter](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xengine/9.4/Content/XEngine_Parameters.htm) XEngine.Settings.TemporaryStorageLocation in the XERegistry file and specify the required location as the value. - Use one of the following environment variables in a profile configuration: XETMP or TEMP or TMP. If none of the above are defined, the default folder location is used (${XESRoot}\XEServer\profiles\<YourProfileName>\workspace\temp).

---

<a id="create-profile-documentation"></a>

#### Create Profile Documentation

> 📄 Source: [Create_Profile_Documentation.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Create_Profile_Documentation.htm)

##### Create Profile Documentation


Creating Profile Documentation option gives the customers an opportunity to create a package with the profile documentation by using Edifecs Application Manager. The package contains:

- report in xml format with the list of the services and components used in the profile
- common report with the description of the profile, structure of the routes, and the list of the services used in the profile
- route reports, which consists of the structure of the route and the description of the route components


To create profile documentation:

1. Start Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Expand the profile that you need.
4. Double-click Configuration.
5. On the Outline tab, right-click the name of the profile, and then click Create Profile Documentation.
6. Provide the path to the folder where you want the package to be stored.
7. Click OK.


All the information about the routes and the route components is taken from the description provided as a note on the Overview tab in the Edifecs Application Manager.


*[Image: Route component properties Note]*


Note If this information is not provided, the corresponding sections of the route report are empty.


The package also contains the screen shots of the routes. They are stored in the resources folder. If you need only the screen shots of the routes without any other information, it is not necessary to create the whole profile documentation package.


To capture the screen shot of the route:

1. Right-click the route, and then click Save Route As Image.


*[Image: Save Route as Image]*

1. Provide the path to the folder where you want the images to be stored.
2. Choose the format in which you want to save the image. The available formats are .png, .jpeg and .bmp.
3. Click Save.

---

<a id="services"></a>

### Services

> 📄 Source: [Book_Services.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Services.htm)

#### Services
[Redirect TO: {XEServer_Services_Overview.htm}](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Services_Overview.htm)

---

<a id="xeserver-services-overview"></a>

#### XEServer Services Overview

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Services_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Services_Overview.htm)

##### XEServer Services Overview


XEServer has a service-oriented design where certain functional capabilities are enclosed in services. An XEServer service is a reusable module that encapsulates certain functionality such as:

- maintaining a connection to a database
- spinning up an embedded web server
- establishing a connection to third-party services
- providing communication with other Edifecs products, and so on.


XEServer comes with a wide range of services, to see the full list, click [here](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Supported_Services.htm).


Typically, a service is linked to one or more [route components](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Route_Components_Overview.htm), exposing its functionality to the linked component(s), so services are mostly used in combination with route components. This is very convenient since you have to provide configuration parameters in one place (on a service level) and have multiple dependent components that refer to the same configuration.


Tip When you add a new route component which requires a service, the corresponding service is automatically created and linked to the new component.
