<a id="work-with-amazon-queues"></a>

##### Work with Amazon Queues

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Amazon_Queues.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Work_with_Amazon_Queues.htm)

###### Work with Amazon Queues


###### Generic Workflow


This section describes the basic steps of data exchange through the Amazon SQS service.


Step 1: A producer establishes a connection with Amazon SQS service.


Step 2: The producer sends a message to the Q1 Amazon SQS queue.


Step 3: The message is received by Amazon SQS and is distributed across multiple Amazon SQS servers redundantly.


Step 4: A consumer makes a request to Amazon SQS service to read a message from Q1.


Step 5: Since there is an available message M1 in Q1, Amazon SQS gives M1 to the consumer.


From this moment, M1 gets the [Inflight](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-visibility-timeout.html#sqs-inflight-messages) status on Amazon servers: it is still kept in the queue but is not available to other consumers (Amazon Inbound components). Also, the [Visibility Timeout](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-visibility-timeout.html#configuring-visibility-timeout) starts at this moment. The message M1 has the Inflight status until the timeout expires. This means that the consumer is responsible for deleting M1 from Q1 within this Visibility Timeout. If the consumer fails to do this before Visibility Timeout expires (a processing error may occur, or the connection may fail), M1 loses the Inflight status and becomes available for other consumers.


Step 6: A consumer's back-end system processes M1. When processing is compete, the consumer sends an acknowledgment to Amazon SQS that the message was successfully read and processed and M1 is deleted from Q1


###### XEServer interaction with Amazon SQS


This section describes a basic end-to-end cycle of message exchange through the Amazon SQS service using XEServer.


###### Prerequisites


To perform the steps described below, you need to accomplish the following tasks first:

1. Create an [Amazon AWS account](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-setting-up.html).
2. Create an [Amazon IAM User](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-setting-up.html#sqs-creating-iam-user).
3. Get an [Access Key ID and a Secret Access Key](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-setting-up.html#sqs-getting-access-key-id-secret-access-key). The key is required to authenticate Java applications (XEServer) connecting to Amazon SQS.
4. Create a new XEServer profile.


###### Step 1. Connect XEServer to Amazon SQS

1. Add a new Amazon SQS service to your XEServer profile.
2. On the service Configuration tab, select the region that is closest to your location and enter Amazon Access Key ID and Secret Access Key. Leave the advanced settings unchanged, as the default configuration is suitable for this scenario.
3. Click Test Connection to verify the connection to Amazon.
4. Save the service configuration.


###### Step 2. Create a New Amazon Queue


The following procedure shows you how to create a new Amazon queue using XEServer.


Note You can also create a new queue using the [Amazon AWS Management Console](http://console.aws.amazon.com/sqs).

1. Create a new route (AmazonSQS_Write) in your profile.
2. Add the Amazon SQS Outbound component to the route.
3. On the component's Settings tab, on the Configuration tab, next to the Queue Name field, click New. The Add Amazon SQS <QueueType> Queue dialog is displayed:
4. Enter a name for the new queue. Leave the advanced settings unchanged.**Note** When **Queue type**=**FIFO**, there is no need to add the ".fifo" suffix to the name of the queue. It will be added dynamically in runtime.
5. Click OK.


Since Amazon SQS is a distributed system, it may take some time before you can view the newly created queue in your Amazon AWS Management Console.


Note The same delay may occur when you create a queue using AWS Console and try to update the list of available queues by clicking Refresh in Amazon SQS Inbound/Amazon SQS Outbound components.


However, this does not affect the functionality of the queue - you can start using the new queue right after it is created.


###### Step 3. Send message to the Amazon queue


The following procedure shows you how to send a message to an Amazon queue using XEServer.


Note You can also send a message to a queue using the [Amazon AWS Management Console](http://console.aws.amazon.com/sqs) to test the process.

1. On the AmazonSQS_Write route, select the Amazon SQS Outbound component.
2. Open the Configuration tab of the component's Settings tab.
3. Next to the Queue Name field, click Refresh.
4. From the Queue Name drop-down list, select the queue that you created in Step 2.
5. In the Message Group ID field, enter a constant value, for example "Group1".
6. In the Message Deduplication ID, enter ${E_ID}. This macro dynamically sets a deduplication Id for every message.
7. Leave the rest of the settings unchanged.
8. Add a new FS Inbound component to the AmazonSQS_Write route, and then connect it to the Amazon SQS Outbound component as shown in the image: This component is used as a source location for messages to be written.
9. Select the added FS Inbound component, and on the component's Settings tab, set the Source folder field to ${XESWorkspace}/test-data/src.
10. Save the route configuration.
11. Start the current profile from EAM Admin Console.
12. Drop several files to the FS Inbound component's source folder (${XESWorkspace}/test-data/src). The files are sent to the Amazon SQS queue.


###### Step 4. Read messages from the Amazon queue


The following procedure shows you how to receive a message from an Amazon queue using XEServer.


Note You can also view and delete a message from a queue using the [Amazon AWS Management Console](http://console.aws.amazon.com/sqs) to test the process.

1. Create a new route (AmazonSQS_Read) in your profile.
2. Add the Amazon SQS Inbound component to the route.
3. Open the Configuration tab of the component's Settings tab.
4. Set the component's Activation mode to By command to initiate queue reading using a trigger.
5. Next to the Queue Name field, click Refresh.
6. From the Queue Name drop-down list, select the queue to read messages from (the queue that you created in Step 2).
7. Add the FS Inbound component to the route. This component is used as a trigger to activate the Amazon SQS Inbound component.
8. Add the FS Outbound component to the route to store messages from the queue to the file system. Set the component's Data properties option to overwrite to store property files in the file system.
9. Connect the components as shown in the image:
10. Save the route configuration.
11. Start the current profile from EAM Admin Console.
12. Go to the FS Outbound's destination folder (${XESWorkspace}/test-data/outbound). The messages fetched from the queue are displayed.


Since this example uses the FIFO queue type, the messages are read in the strict order they were sent to the queue.


For a more complex real-life scenario of using Amazon SQS queues, see Amazon SQS sample profile.

---

<a id="create-a-new-queue"></a>

##### Create a New Queue

> 📄 Source: [Create_a_New_Queue.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Create_a_New_Queue.htm)

###### Create a New Queue


Before you start working with Amazon SQS service, you should create a new Amazon queue. You can do this by using the [Amazon AWS Management Console](http://console.aws.amazon.com/sqs) or by using the EAM user interface. The latter method is described further in this section.


Note After you delete a queue (for example, "q1"), there is a 60-second timeout during which you cannot create a queue with the same name ("q1"). For more information see, Amazon SQS [limitations](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-limits.html).


To open the Add Amazon Queue dialog, on the Settings tab of the Amazon SQS Outbound / Amazon SQS Inbound component, next to the Queue Name field, click New. The Add Amazon SQS <QueueType> Queue dialog is displayed:



###### Queue settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Queue Name | A queue name should adhere to the following requirements: Only alphanumeric characters, hyphens ('-'), and underscores ('_') . A maximum name length of 80 characters. Should be case-sensitive. **Note** When **Queue type**=**FIFO**, there is no need to add the ".fifo" suffix to the name of the queue. It will be added dynamically in runtime. |
| Visibility timeout | The timeout that starts ticking once a message is consumed from the queue. During this timeout, the consumed messages acquires the so-called [Inflight status](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-visibility-timeout.html#sqs-inflight-messages): it is still kept in the queue, but is not available to other consumers. This timeout is designed to ensure that back-end systems (XEServer route) complete reading and processing messages in time and none of the messages are lost due to connectivity/processing issues. For example, there is a queue that has Visibility Timeout set to 30 seconds. This means that once a consumer (Amazon SQS Inbound component) reads a message from this queue, the message becomes unavailable to other Amazon SQS Inbound components for 30 seconds. The current XEServer route is expected to complete the processing of the consumed message and remove it from the queue within these 30 seconds. If this does not happen (connection or processing errors may occur), the message is returned to the message pool and again becomes available to all consumers. You should set Visibility Timeout that is suitable for your particular route. This means that the timeout should be more than the estimated time required for processing a single message from the queue. For a better understanding of how Amazon queues work, see [Amazon SQS message life cycle](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-message-lifecycle.html). |
| Retention period | The maximum time frame during which a message can be kept in a queue. After expiration, the message is automatically deleted from the queue. |
| Maximum Message Size | The maximum size of the messages for the queue. Supported values: 1KB - 256KB. Note XEServer converts message exchange properties to [Amazon message attributes](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSSimpleQueueService/latest/SQSDeveloperGuide/sqs-message-attributes.html) and sends them together with the message body to Amazon queues. The 256 KB limit is applied to the whole message including the message body and attributes. |
| Delivery Delay | The delay between the moment when a message is added to the queue and the moment this message becomes available for reading by customers. This parameter allows you to postpone message delivery by 0 to 900 seconds. |
| Receive Message Wait time | The time interval during which XEServer waits for a message to be read from the queue. With this option, you can reduce the number of requests to Amazon SQS. |
| Content-Based Deduplication | This parameter is required for FIFO queues to identify a duplicate message in a queue based on the message body contents. |
| Use Dead Letter queue | Select whether you want to bind your queue to a [dead letter queue](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Amazon_SQS_Outbound.htm#DeadLetterQueue). |
| Queue Name | The name of the dead letter queue to send messages to. |
| Maximum Message Receive | The maximum number of unsuccessful attempts to read a message from a queue before this message is redirected to a dead letter queue. |



When you create a new queue using the EAM interface, it may take some time before you can view this queue in your [AWS Management Console](http://console.aws.amazon.com/sqs).

<a id="amazon-sqs-performance"></a>

##### Amazon SQS Performance

> 📄 Source: [Amazon_SQS_Performance.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Amazon_SQS_Performance.htm)

###### Amazon SQS Performance


This section contains the test results of exchanging data through Amazon SQS and ActiveMQ(FIFO) queues and demonstrates the inter-dependency between performance and queue types.


The tests were carried out on the computer with the following hardware:

- CPU: Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5-2630 v2 @ 2.60GHz 2.60GHz
- RAM: 16 GB


During the tests, the average ping time to Amazon SQS server was 10 milliseconds.


During each test, 1000, 5000, and 10000 messages (1KB each) were sent and read from the queue by a different number of producers and consumers. The total number (1000, 5000, 10000) of sent and read messages did not affect the performance significantly, so the number of sent and read messages has been ignored. You can view the performance changes on the following charts:


###### Message Sending


The speed of sending messages for both queue types grows as the number of producers increases:



###### Message Reading


The speed of reading messages grows almost linearly for Standard queue type as the number of consumers increases. FIFO queues demonstrate a constant reading speed as a result of FIFO queue discipline.

---

<a id="amazon-s3-service"></a>

##### Amazon S3 Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Amazon_S3_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Amazon_S3_Service.htm)

###### Amazon S3 Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="amazon-s3-service-overview"></a>

##### Amazon S3 Service Overview

> 📄 Source: [Amazon_S3_Service_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Amazon_S3_Service_Overview.htm)

###### Amazon S3 Service Overview


Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) is a cloud storage on the Internet. Using this storage, you can store and retrieve any amount of data, anytime from anywhere on the Web.


XEServer provides you with convenient and versatile inbound/outbound route components to allow you to interact with Amazon S3 from your XEServer routes at run time.


Before you proceed with this section, review the following key [Amazon S3](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/gsg/AmazonS3Basics.html) concepts:

- Object: Amazon S3 stores data in the form of objects. An object consists of a file and optional metadata that describes that file.
- Bucket: Buckets are containers for these objects. You can have one or more buckets at a time. You can control access to each bucket (create, delete, and list objects in the bucket), view access logs and its objects, and choose the geographical region where Amazon S3 stores the bucket and its contents.


Before you start working with Amazon S3, you have to complete the following steps:

1. [Sign up](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/gsg/SigningUpforS3.html) for Amazon S3.
2. To authorize Java applications (XEServer) connecting to Amazon S3:
3. Create a [Bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/gsg/CreatingABucket.html).


For more information, see [Amazon Simple Storage Service Documentation](https://aws.amazon.com/documentation/s3/).

---

<a id="amazon-s3-service"></a>

##### Amazon S3 Service

> 📄 Source: [Amazon_S3_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Amazon_S3_Service.htm)

###### Amazon S3 Service


With this service, you can establish a connection with an Amazon S3 server. The route components [Amazon S3 Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Amazon_S3_Inbound.htm) and [Amazon S3 Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Amazon_S3_Outbound.htm) use this service to read from and write data to Amazon S3 buckets.


Note Use of this component requires an XEServer Module for AWS license. Contact [Edifecs Customer Support](https://support.edifecs.com/) to obtain the XEServer Module for AWS license.


###### IAM User Permissions


The service can invoke the following [AWS actions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/API_Operations.html):

- GetObjectMetadata
- PutObject
- DeleteObject
- GetObject
- GetObjectTagging
- ListObjectsV2
- ListBuckets


To perform these actions, the corresponding IAM user must have sufficient permissions defined in the user policies. For more information, see [Amazon S3 actions and permissions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/service-authorization/latest/reference/list_amazons3.html#amazons3-actions-as-permissions).


###### Service Configuration


When you add the service to a profile, you can view the following settings:



###### General


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Import connection settings | Click to load the connection parameters from a file on your file system. |
| Export connection settings | Click to export the current connection parameters to a file. |
| Endpoint Region | Select the geographic region closest to your physical XEServer instance for optimal latency. |
| Access Key ID | (Optional) Enter your Amazon Access Key ID. You can [view your Access Key ID](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/home#/security_credential) in the AWS IAM console. If this field is empty, the system looks for AWS credentials in the following [sources](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/AWS_Default_Credentials_Provider_Chain.htm). |
| Secret Access Key | (Optional) Enter your Secret Access Key. You can [view your Access Key ID](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/home#/security_credential) in the AWS IAM console. The XEServer encrypted properties can be used in this field. If you have already declared encrypted properties in the current profile, you will see them in the drop-down list. If this field is empty, the system looks for AWS credentials in the following [sources](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/AWS_Default_Credentials_Provider_Chain.htm). |
| Advanced The settings in this section are used to fine-tune the connection between XEServer and Amazon S3 servers. The default values would probably satisfy most of your business needs. For more information about connection parameters, see [Amazon S3 API reference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/Welcome.html). | |
| Run with IAM role | The **Run with IAM role** option is used in combination with an Amazon EC2 instance. If the IAM role to assume is the same as the IAM role attached to the Amazon EC2 instance, then there is no need to select the **Run with IAM role** option. Because if selected, the IAM role would try to assume itself, which is not the correct expectation. If the IAM role to assume is different from the IAM role attached to the Amazon EC2 instance, select the **Run with IAM role** option and configure it as described below. When selected, XEServer assumes a [role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html) to access the Amazon S3 service. Specify the following: ARN: [Amazon resource name](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/aws-arns-and-namespaces.html) for the IAM role to assume. The **ARN** (IAM role to assume) should be added to the trust policy of the IAM role attached to the Amazon EC2 instance by modifying the trust relationships or policies using the **Trust Relationships** tab in the [AWS Management Console:](http://console.aws.amazon.com/sqs) External ID: (Optional) An unique identifier for [assuming the role by a third-party account](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_create_for-user_externalid.html). Session duration (min): The maximum duration of the role session in minutes. This parameter cannot exceed the [maximum session duration in IAM console](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use.html#id_roles_use_view-role-max-session). |
| Custom service endpoint | If you want to connect to a custom implementation of S3 service, enter its host name and port number (for example, `0.0.0.0:8000`). Make sure that you use a valid certificate, or add the certificate used on the S3 server side to the default Java truststore on the S3 client side. If you use a Dell S3 endpoint, to access the endpoint, in Endpoint Region, select any endpoint region or enter US-Standard as an endpoint region value. |
| Access style | Select the access style for requests in order to define how a bucket name must be fetched. Autodetect: The URL access style is detected based on the endpoint you specify and can be either [virtual-hosted-style](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/VirtualHosting.html#virtual-hosted-style-access) or [path-style](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/VirtualHosting.html#path-style-access). Note that if the host name is an IP address, or the bucket name is not a DNS-compliant name, then the URL is considered as of path-style. Path Style: Select to use [path-style access](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/VirtualHosting.html#path-style-access) for all requests. Note that this style requires a region-specific endpoint to access a bucket. |
| Connection max number | The maximum number of concurrent connections to an Amazon server. |
| Connection timeout | The timeout in milliseconds within which the connection should be established. |
| Connection max idle timeout | The maximum time period to keep connections in the connection pool. |
| Request retry attempt | The number of sequential request attempts to an Amazon S3 server if the server does not respond. |
| Request retry delay | The delay between attempts. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="azure-connection-service"></a>

##### Azure Connection Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Azure_Connection_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Azure_Connection_Service.htm)

###### Azure Connection Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="azure-connectivity-overview"></a>

##### Azure Connectivity Overview

> 📄 Source: [Azure_Connectivity_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Connectivity_Overview.htm)

###### Azure Connectivity Overview


[Azure Blob storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blobs-introduction) is a cloud solution optimized for storing massive amounts of unstructured data, such as text or binary data, up to about 190 TiB. [Azure Queue storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/queues/storage-queues-introduction) is a service aimed at storing large numbers of messages (up to 64 KB in size) which you can access via HTTP/HTTPS calls.


XEServer provides you with convenient and versatile inbound/outbound route components to allow you to interact with Azure Blob storage ([Azure Blob Storage Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Blob_Storage_Inbound.htm) and [Azure Blob Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Blob_Storage_Outbound.htm) components) and Azure Queue storage ([Azure Queue Storage Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Queue_Storage_Inbound.htm) and [Azure Queue Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Queue_Storage_Outbound.htm) components) from your XEServer routes at run time. The components use Azure Connection service to connect to your Azure storage account and interact with your Azure resources.


###### Prerequisites


Throughout this section, it is implied that you have an [Azure storage account](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-overview) with [container(s)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blobs-introduction#containers) and/or [queue(s)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/queues/storage-quickstart-queues-portal) to read/write your data from/to.


Before you configure Azure Connection service and the components, you need to do and to get the following:

1. [Register](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app) XEServer as an application with your Microsoft identity platform and [assign app roles](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/howto-add-app-roles-in-azure-ad-apps#assign-app-roles-to-applications) to it. For XEServer to interact with your Azure Blob storage, assign the [Storage Blob Data Contributor](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles#storage-blob-data-contributor) and/or [Storage Blob Data Reader](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles#storage-blob-data-reader) roles. To interact with Azure Queue storage, assign the [Storage Queue Data Contributor](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles#storage-queue-data-contributor) and/or [Storage Queue Data Reader](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/role-based-access-control/built-in-roles#storage-queue-data-reader) roles.
2. Depending on the authorization type you choose, you need to do the following:
3. For authorization with a client secret:
4. On the [page where you registered](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app#register-an-application) the XEServer application, copy the Application (client) ID that identifies XEServer application in the Microsoft identity platform and the Directory (tenant) ID of the [Azure Active Directory tenant](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-create-new-tenant#use-an-existing-azure-ad-tenant) associated with your Azure account.
5. Issue a [client secret](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app#add-a-client-secret) for XEServer in your Azure portal.
6. For authorization with a client certificate:
7. On the [page where you registered](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app#register-an-application) the XEServer application, copy the Application (client) ID that identifies XEServer application in the Microsoft identity platform and the Directory (tenant) ID of the [Azure Active Directory tenant](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-create-new-tenant#use-an-existing-azure-ad-tenant) associated with your Azure account.
8. Create a [self-signed certificate](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/howto-create-self-signed-certificate#option-2-create-and-export-your-public-certificate-with-its-private-key).
9. For Shared Key authorization type: Obtain a storage account [access key](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-keys-manage?tabs=azure-portal).
10. For SAS authorization type: Create a [shared access signature (SAS)](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/create-account-sas).


Now you are ready to [configure](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Connection_Service.htm) Azure Connection service and the route components.

---

<a id="azure-connection-service"></a>

##### Azure Connection Service

> 📄 Source: [Azure_Connection_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Connection_Service.htm)

###### Azure Connection Service


The Azure Connection Service establishes a connection with your Azure storage account. The route components [Azure Queue Storage Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Queue_Storage_Inbound.htm) and [Azure Queue Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Queue_Storage_Outbound.htm) use this service to read and write messages in your Azure Queue storage. The [Azure Blob Storage Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Blob_Storage_Inbound.htm) and [Azure Blob Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Azure_Blob_Storage_Outbound.htm) components use this service to read and write blobs in your Azure Blob storage.


###### Service Configuration


When you add the service to a profile, you can view and edit the following settings:



To see the list of macros supported by this service, see [XEServer supported macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm#AzureBlob).


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Account name | Enter the name of your [Azure storage account](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-overview). |
| Authentication type | Select how the HTTP requests made by XEServer to your Azure Storage must be authenticated. ACCOUNT_KEY: Specify your storage account [access key](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-account-keys-manage?tabs=azure-portal) generated by Azure. As the access key is passed with the request header, the Shared Key authorization option is not recommended for production environment. CLIENT_CERTIFICATE: Requests to Azure Storage are authorized by Azure Active Directory (Azure AD). Specify the following configurations: Tenant ID: The directory (tenant) ID of the [Azure Active Directory tenant](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-create-new-tenant#use-an-existing-azure-ad-tenant) associated with your Azure account. Client ID: The application (client) ID automatically generated when you [register](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app#register-an-application) XEServer as an application with your Microsoft identity platform. Certificate Path: The path to the private key for the self-signed certificate you [created](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/howto-create-self-signed-certificate#option-2-create-and-export-your-public-certificate-with-its-private-key). Certificate Password: The [password](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/howto-create-self-signed-certificate#option-2-create-and-export-your-public-certificate-with-its-private-key) for your private key if you store the private key in a .pfx file. CLIENT_SECRET: Requests to Azure Storage are authorized by Azure Active Directory (Azure AD). Specify the following configurations: Tenant ID: The directory (tenant) ID of the [Azure Active Directory tenant](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-create-new-tenant#use-an-existing-azure-ad-tenant) associated with your Azure account. Client ID: The application (client) ID automatically generated when you [register](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app#register-an-application) XEServer as an application with your Microsoft identity platform. Client Secret: A client secret that you can [issue](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app#add-a-client-secret) for XEServer in your Azure portal. MANAGED_IDENTITY: Requests to Azure Storage are authorized by Azure Active Directory (Azure AD). XEServer will use [managed identity](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/managed-identities-azure-resources/overview) to obtain Azure AD token. Use this option if XEServer instance is running from within an Azure entity. Optionally, you can specify the Resource ID of the Azure resource or Client ID that identifies XEServer application in the Microsoft identity platform. SAS_TOKEN: Specify a shared access signature (SAS) you [create](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/create-account-sas) in your Azure Storage account. Since the account SAS uses the storage account key which can be compromised, this authorization option is not recommended for production environment. |
| Advanced In this section, you can set Azure Storage connection preferences. The default values would probably satisfy most of your business needs. For more information about connection parameters, see [Retry guidance for Azure Storage](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/best-practices/retry-service-specific#azure-storage). | |
| Request timeout | The maximum allowed time for one operation. Includes connection and read timeouts. If set to -1, the request time is unlimited. |
| Retry policy | Select the retry pattern: FIXED: The linear retry policy. All the request attempts are made with the same back-off interval. EXPONENTIAL: The back-off interval is growing exponentially with each subsequent retry. You can limit the maximum interval in the Retry max delay property. |
| Retry attempts | The number of repetitive request attempts if Azure Storage does not respond. |
| Retry delay | The back-off interval between attempts. |
| Retry max delay | The maximum allowed interval between retry attempts. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="rabbitmq-connectivity"></a>

##### RabbitMQ Connectivity

> 📄 Source: [Book_RabbitMQ.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_RabbitMQ.htm)

###### RabbitMQ Connectivity


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="rabbitmq-overview"></a>

##### RabbitMQ Overview

> 📄 Source: [RabbitMQ_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RabbitMQ_Overview.htm)

###### RabbitMQ Overview


[RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/) is an open source message broker designed to accept and forward messages that carry a set of attributes and a binary blob of data. RabbitMQ implements many protocols including [AMQP](https://www.rabbitmq.com/protocol.html) (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) 0-9-1 that is a messaging protocol that helps to transport value-bearing messages across and between organizations in a timely manner.


Message queuing allows web services to respond to requests quickly instead of being forced to perform resource-consuming procedures there and then. Message queuing helps when you want to distribute a single message to multiple recipients for reading or for balancing loads between workers.


XEServer RabbitMQ provides an interface to the RabbitMQ message broker and allows you to leverage the benefits of AMQP.


Before you proceed with this section, review the following key [AMQP cocepts](https://www.rabbitmq.com/tutorials/amqp-concepts.html):

- Broker: A running RabbitMQ server.
- Producer: An application, for example, XEServer RabbitMQ Outbound component, that publishes (sends) messages to the broker.
- Consumer: An application, for example, XEServer RabbitMQ Outbound component, that reads messages from a broker.
- Message: Information that is sent from the producer to a consumer through RabbitMQ. Contains a set of headers and a binary blob of data.
- Queue: A buffer that stores messages.
- Exchange: Messages are not sent directly to a queue, instead, the producer sends messages to a message exchange that is responsible for routing the message to the corresponding queue using bindings and routing keys. There are several types of message exchanges: - Direct: Delivers messages to queues based on a message routing key. - Fanout: Delivers messages to all of the queues that are bound to it. - Topic: Performs a wildcard match between the routing key and the routing pattern specified in the binding. - Headers: Uses message header attributes for routing.
- Binding: An entity that creates a relationship between a message queue and a message exchange.
- Routing: The process of forwarding a message from the message exchange to the required queue.
- Acknowledgement: A callback from the receiver that notifies the broker that a message has been successfully received and was not lost by connection or application failure.


For more information, see [AMQP model](https://www.rabbitmq.com/tutorials/amqp-concepts.html).

---

<a id="rabbitmq-service"></a>

##### RabbitMQ Service

> 📄 Source: [RabbitMQ_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RabbitMQ_Service.htm)

###### RabbitMQ Service


With this service, you can establish a connection to a running RabbitMQ server and allow XEServer RabbitMQ connectors to interact with RabbitMQ queues.


When you add the service to a profile, you can view the following settings:



###### General


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Connection Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Host | Enter the name or IP address of the computer where RabbitMQ server is running. |
| Port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Username | Enter the name of the user to connect to the RabbitMQ server. |
| Password | Enter the user password.XEServer encrypted properties can be used in this field. If you have encrypted properties declared in the current profile, they are available in the drop-down list. |



###### SSL Settings


Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead.


|   |   |
| --- | --- |
| Enable SSL | Select to enable secure communication with the RabbitMQ server. |
| Trusted Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to a key store with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Truststore. |
| Key Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to the file that contains Keystore certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the private key. |



###### Advanced Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Namespace | Enter a namespace to logically group RabbitMQ entities, such as connections, message exchanges, queues, bindings, user permissions, policies, and so on. For example, you can have RabbitMQ message exchanges with the same name but belonging to different namespaces. |
| Connection timeout | Enter the timeout within which a connection to a RabbitMQ server should be established. |
| Automatic Connection Recovery Enabled | Select this option to automatically recover the connection to a RabbitMQ server when there is a connection failure. |
| Topology recovery enabled | Select this option to recover RabbitMQ topology (queues, message exchanges, bindings, and others) when there is a connection failure. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="database-services"></a>

##### Database Services

> 📄 Source: [Book_Database_Services.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Database_Services.htm)

###### Database Services


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="jdbc-connectivity-overview"></a>

##### JDBC Connectivity Overview

> 📄 Source: [JDBC_Connectivity_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JDBC_Connectivity_Overview.htm)

###### JDBC Connectivity Overview


XEServer components and services rely on JDBC to work with databases. By default, XEServer comes with the following JDBC drivers located at {XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\jdbc:

- h2-{version}.jar
- postgresql-{version}.jar
- jtds-{version}.jar


jTDS is an open source Java JDBC 3.0 (type 4) driver for Microsoft SQL Server. However, jTDS does not support the latest features of MS SQL Server. To work with MS SQL Server, Edifecs recommends that you use the Microsoft JDBC driver for SQL Server. To do this:

1. Download [Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/jdbc/download-microsoft-jdbc-driver-for-sql-server).
2. Put the driver JAR to {XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\jdbc.
3. Set the new XEServer environment property `jdbc.driver.sqlserver = com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver` either on the XEServer [Environment Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) (global environment) or on the [Profile Environment Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) (profile-level environment).
4. Restart the profile that uses the JDBC driver.


Note XEServer installation does not include the Oracle JDBC driver by default. To work with Oracle databases, you have to download the [Oracle JDBC driver](https://www.oracle.com/database/technologies/appdev/jdbc-downloads.html) and put the ojdbc{version}.jar to ${XESRoot}/platform/lib.user/jdbc/. Make sure that XEServer and XEngine use the same Oracle JDBC driver version.


###### Troubleshoot MS SQL driver connectivity


Starting [10.2](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/jdbc/release-notes-for-the-jdbc-driver?view=sql-server-ver16#changes-in-102), Microsoft JDBC driver for SQL Server has TLS encryption enabled by default. In case you have encryption misconfiguration, the driver raises the following error:


com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerException: The driver could not establish a secure connection to SQL Server by using Secure Sockets Layer (SSL) encryption.


To resolve this issue, perform one of the following procedures:

- disable TLS encryption. To be performed on testing/protected environments.
- configure an encrypted connection by managing certificates. Recommended for production environments.


###### Disable TLS encryption


To disable TLS encryption, add the `encrypt=false` property to the JDBC connection string.


For example:


jdbc:sqlserver://{host}:{port};databaseName=my_db;**encrypt=false**;.....


**Note** You should follow this approach only in a protected network.


###### Configure an encrypted connection


For production environments, you must ensure that a proper certificate is configured on the server side and is trusted by the client machine. This method is recommended for interaction with MS SQL databases.

 *Create a self-signed certificate To generate a self-signed certificate that works for encryption of connections to MS SQL Server, you can use the New-SelfSignedCertificate command for PowerShell as in the example below: Copy New-SelfSignedCertificate -Type SSLServerAuthentication -Subject "CN=$env:COMPUTERNAME" -DnsName "$env:COMPUTERNAME",'localhost' -KeyAlgorithm "RSA" -KeyLength 2048 -Hash "SHA256" -TextExtension "2.5.29.37={text}1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.1" -NotAfter (Get-Date).AddMonths(36) -KeySpec KeyExchange -Provider "Microsoft RSA SChannel Cryptographic Provider" **Note** For more information about the New-SelfSignedCertificate command, its syntax and parameters, follow this link. To set permissions for the certificate Find the Service Account Name for your SQL Server instance: Start SQL Server Configuration Manager Access **SQL Server Services** Right-click on the required Server Service from the list and select **Properties**: Copy the Account Name for later re-use (e.g., NT Service\MSSQLSERVER*) *Click **OK** Launch the Certificates snap-in Launch MMC (Microsoft Management Console) by typing mmc.exe in the Windows search bar. Go to: **File** – **Add / Remove Snap-in …**, select Certificates from the list of the Available snap-ins and click on **Add >**: Choose **Computer Account**, click **Next** and then click **Finish**. Click **OK** Grant SQL Server rights to read the private key for the certificate In MMC, in the left pane, go to: **Certificates (Local computer)** – **Personal** – **Certificates** Right-click on the created certificate and go to: **All Tasks** – **Manage Private Keys** Add the Service Account name for your instance of SQL Server (e.g., NT Service\MSSQLSERVER*) Grant the Service Account Read permissions and click **OK**. To configure SQL Server to use the certificate

- Open SQL Server Configuration Manager
- Go to: **SQL Server Network configuration** – **Protocols for {your_instance}**
- Right-click and go to: **Properties** – **Certificate** tab
- Choose the certificate from the drop-down certificate list and click **OK**.
- Restart the service





 To export the certificate to a file

- In MMC, in the left pane, go to: **Certificates (Local computer)** – **Personal** – **Certificates**
- Right-click on the created certificate and go to: **All Tasks** – **Export**. The Certificate Export Wizard is displayed.
- Click **Next** and select **No, do not export the private key**, and then click **Next**.
- Ensure the **DER encoded binary X.509 (.CER)** is selected and click **Next**.
- Specify a file name for the exported certificate, e.g., Example.cer.
- Click **Next** and then click **Finish**.





 To configure the client for encryption


You can use one of the following options when configuring the client for encryption:

- Inform the client driver to not validate the server certificate by adding the `trustServerCertificate=true` property to the JDBC connection string. For example: jdbc:sqlserver://{host}:{port};databaseName=my_db;encrypt=true;**trustServerCertificate=true**;..... **Note** Ensure that the `encrypt` property is set to `true`.
- Import the server certificate to client trust store. This option implies that `encrypt=true` and `trustServerCertificate=false` in the JDBC connection string. In this case the client driver will validate the server certificate. To import the trust material in the client computer's trust store for successful validation, use the JAVA "keytool" utility that is installed with the JRE (Java Runtime Environment). The following command prompt demonstrates how to use the "keytool" utility to import a certificate from a file, e.g., Example.cer: [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);) ``` keytool -import -v -alias myServer -cacerts -storepass changeit -file Example.cer ```

---

<a id="data-storage"></a>

##### Data Storage

> 📄 Source: [Book_Data_Storage.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Data_Storage.htm)

###### Data Storage
[Redirect to: {Data_Storage_Service}](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Data_Storage_Service.htm)

---

<a id="data-storage-service"></a>

##### Data Storage Service

> 📄 Source: [Data_Storage_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Data_Storage_Service.htm)

###### Data Storage Service


The Data Storage service is an internal hardware transactional memory (HTM) caching service used to store data during run-time operations. The service relies on a relational database and by default, uses an H2 database, however the service also supports MS SQL Server, Oracle, IBM DB2 databases, and PostgreSQL. On the first connecting to the database, the Data Storage service automatically performs all the required database initialization: creates tables, indices, and so on. If you want to initialize a database for Data Storage manually, use [preconfigured SQL scripts](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manual_DB_Initialization.htm).


The following route components use this service:

- [Data Storage Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Data_Storage_Inbound.htm) to read data from the storage,
- [Data Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Data_Storage_Outbound.htm) to write data to the storage.


###### Interaction with Other Profiles


You can set the service to work as a:

- Server: The service acts in the server mode when its TCP server 'on' option is set to true. In this mode, the service starts a TCP server that can be accessed by XEServer profiles including those running on remote computers. To access the Data Storage service running in this mode, you must specify a host name and a port number. For this mode, the JDBC connection URL must have the following format: jdbc:h2:tcp://<SampleHostName>:<TCPServerPortNumber>/<PathToDatabaseFile>. Example jdbc:h2:tcp://localhost:7500/${XESWorkspace}/DS/data
- Client: The service acts in the client mode when its TCP server 'on' option is set to false. In this mode, the service can access the storage only within a profile. For this mode, the JDBC connection URL should have the following format: jdbc:h2:file:<PathToDatabaseFile>. Example jdbc:h2:file:${XESWorkspace}/DS/data


###### Work with IBM DB2 Database


To allow the service to work with a DB2 database:

1. Download the **db2jcc** driver from [IBM website](https://www.ibm.com/support/pages/node/382667).
2. Extract the package and drop db2jcc.jar into the ${XESRoot}/platform/lib.user/jdbc/ directory.
3. To register the driver, on the EAM **System** tab, in XEServer, click Environment, and then on the tab Startup, add a new XEServer profile startup parameter -Djdbc.driver.DB2 with the value: com.ibm.db2.jcc.DB2Driver (-Djdbc.driver.DB2=com.ibm.db2.jcc.DB2Driver).
4. Restart the profile with the Data Storage service for changes to take effect.


When you have to write and read data within the same profile it may be preferable to use H2. If you are sure that you are operating with limited set of data, you can use the memory configuration for H2. However, when you write data on one server but have to read this data on another server, you may have to use MS SQL Server, Oracle, or PostgreSQL databases.


###### Work with MS SQL Server


To allow the service to work with MS SQL Server:

1. Download [Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/jdbc/download-microsoft-jdbc-driver-for-sql-server).
2. Put the driver JAR to {XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\jdbc.
3. To register the driver, on the EAM System tab, in XEServer, click Environment, and then on the tab Startup, add a new XEServer profile startup parameter -Djdbc.driver.sqlserver with the value com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver (-Djdbc.driver.sqlserver=com.microsoft.sqlserver.jdbc.SQLServerDriver).
4. Restart the profile with the Data Storage service for changes to take effect.


###### Work with PostgreSQL Database


To allow the service to work with PostgreSQL Server:

1. Download [PostgreSQL JDBC driver](https://jdbc.postgresql.org/download).
2. Put the driver JAR to {XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\jdbc.
3. To register the driver, on the EAM System tab, in XEServer, click Environment, and then on the tab Startup, add a new XEServer profile startup parameter -Djdbc.driver.postgresql with the value org.postgresql.Driver (-Djdbc.driver.postgresql=org.postgresql.Driver).
4. Restart the profile with the Data Storage service for changes to take effect.


###### Expiration Control


The service has an expiration control mechanism that prevents storage from growing excessively as a result of accumulated stale data items. A stale item is a data item that has been submitted to storage but cannot be fetched from storage (for example, an error in a reading route). You can activate the expiration control mechanism using the service parameter Expiry Control.


To clean up storage, each data item in storage has a property Expiration Timeout that defines the item's time-to-live. This property is set on the [Data Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Data_Storage_Outbound.htm#Expiration) when the component submits a data item to the storage and defaults to -1 (items never expire). When Expiration Control is activated, the service periodically scans the storage and removes expired data items.


To enable the expiration control mechanism, ensure that the following requirements are met:

- The Data Storage Outbound component's Expiration timeout is set to a positive value.
- The Expiry Control parameter of the Data Storage service is set to true.


As alternatives you can use the following:

- Shared file system location,
- Build your own component that communicates with designated storage.



###### Configuration Tab


The following parameters are available for the service:


| Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Connection check on start | Checks to see whether the connection can be established during the profile startup. If the value is set to "false", the check is skipped. **Note** If [jTDS](http://jtds.sourceforge.net/) driver is used and successful database connectivity has already been tested via other components, disable the **Connection check on start** setting to allow the DB Connection Service to start. This is due to the fact that the jTDS driver does not support the latest features of MS SQL Server. For more information, see [JDBC Connectivity Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JDBC_Connectivity_Overview.htm#jTDS). |
| Connection retry attempts | The number of attempts to restart the component when a failure occurs. |
| Connection retry timeout (ms) | Defines the time intervals between attempts. |
| Expiry control | Enables or disables the expiry control mechanism. If set to TRUE, the service periodically checks the Expiration Timeout property of data items in the storage and removes the expired items. This property is set on the [Data Storage Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Data_Storage_Outbound.htm#Expiration) component. If set to FALSE, the service does not remove items from the storage. To enable the expiration control mechanism, ensure that the following requirements are met: The Data Storage Outbound component's Expiration timeout is set to a positive value. The Expiry Control parameter of the Data Storage service is set to true. |
| JDBC connection URL | Enter a JDBC URL to the database to store data items. Note XEServer installation does not include the Oracle JDBC driver by default. To work with Oracle databases, you have to download the [Oracle JDBC driver](https://www.oracle.com/database/technologies/appdev/jdbc-downloads.html) and put the ojdbc{version}.jar to ${XESRoot}/platform/lib.user/jdbc/. Make sure that XEServer and XEngine use the same Oracle JDBC driver version. For H2 databases, you should use the absolute URL notation, for example jdbc:h2:file:${XESWorkspace}/DS/data, while other databases can be accessed by using relative URLs, for example jdbc:oracle:thin:<SampleHost>:<SamplePortNumber>:DS/data. Note If you are using Data Storage with MS SQL Server and jtds drivers, then you should use the following property: sendStringParametersAsUnicode=false. By default, the value of this parameter is TRUE, which negatively impacts the performance of SQL Server 2000 as it does not automatically cast types. For example, if an index column is Unicode and a string is submitted using the default character encoding (or the other way around), MS SQL Server performs an index scan instead of an index seek. So with the default value of this parameter, each query lasts longer and may end up with dead locks. |
| TCP server 'on' | Set the value to TRUE to provide an access to the service over TCP and allow other XEServer profiles to work with this service. |
| TCP server port | Enter the port for the connection to the service. |
| Zip data greater than | Zips data files whose size exceeds the value provided. |
| Pool max number of active connections | The maximum number of active connections available in the connection pool. |
| Pool max number of idle | The maximum number of idle connections available in the connection pool. |



Example:


You have "Pool max number of idle connections" set to 3. If you have 10 active connections and they are not used for some period of time, then the number of inactive connections will be reduced to 3.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="use-an-external-database-with-data-storage"></a>

##### Use an External Database with Data Storage

> 📄 Source: [Use_an_External_Database_with_Data_Storage.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_an_External_Database_with_Data_Storage.htm)

###### Use an External Database with Data Storage


All Data Storage services in various routes can be connected to the same shared database. With such an implementation you can ensure the failover and ability for another instance of the route to operate with the same data for the extensibility purpose. The database can be installed remotely and you need to make sure that connection properties are properly set. The supported database types are Oracle, MS SQL, and PostgreSQL.


###### Prerequisites

- Make sure that an empty production database is available in the network.
- Make sure that the database permissions allow users to access the database and create tables in it. Data Storage Service in XEServer creates all necessary tables.


Note Data Storage database creation is not performed automatically by Edifecs products but is a task for System DBA.


To connect Data Storage to the same database:

1. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
2. Expand a profile where you have Data Storage Service available and double-click Configuration.
3. On the Outline tab, expand Services.
4. Expand Connectivity and DS.
5. Double-click Data Storage Service.
6. On the Configuration tab, change the value of the TCP Server 'on' option to true.
7. Provide JDBC connection URL of the server where production database is created.
8. Type TCP server port to be used for the connection to the server.


*[Image: Data Storage Service Confuguration tab]*

---

<a id="h2-database-service"></a>

##### H2 Database Service

> 📄 Source: [H2_Database_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/H2_Database_Service.htm)

###### H2 Database Service


Settings Tab QoS Tab Dependencies Tab Service Information


With the H2 Database Service, you can deploy full featured H2 databases or connect to an already created H2 database.


Using an H2 database becomes an efficient solution when you expect low data loads. With the H2 Database Service you do not need to deploy a third-party database software, thus the process of profile migration becomes robust and convenient. For more information about H2 databases, see [H2 Database Tutorial](http://www.h2database.com/html/tutorial.html).


XEServer components can access embedded H2 databases through a jdbc URL which is displayed on the Settings tab of the H2 Database Service:

- jdbc:h2:tcp://<server>:<port>/<path>/<databaseName>


Note For the <path> value, use the absolute path notation. For example, jdbc:h2:file:${XESWorkspace}/DS/data.


For more information about H2 URL formats, see [H2 Database URL Overview](http://www.h2database.com/html/features.html#database_url).


If there is no database under the specified jdbc URL, a new database with no tables is created. The root user is "sa" and has no password.


When the service is added to a profile, you can view the following settings:



###### Settings Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Enable TCP server | Select to run a local database server controlled through the TCP protocol.Note The TCP connection using the jdbc:h2:tcp://<server>:<port>/<path>/<databaseName> URL is possible only if the Enable TCP server option is selected. |
| TCP port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Allow remote connection to TCP server | Select this option to grant remote access to the server. If the option is cleared, only processes on the same computer can access the server. |
| Enable WEB server | Select to run a local database server controlled through the HTTP protocol. If the option is selected, a link to the server is displayed. To open the H2 Database web console, click the displayed link.Note The web console is available only while the profile with the H2 Database Service is running. |
| WEB port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Allow remote connection to WEB server | Select this option to allow remote hosts to access the H2 Database web console. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="db-connection-service"></a>

##### DB Connection Service

> 📄 Source: [DB_Connection_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DB_Connection_Service.htm)

###### DB Connection Service


Configure a database connection service for each database to access when you are using database components on routes. The route components that require a DB Connection Service are DB Reader, DB Writer, DB Transaction Start, and DB Transaction Close.


*[Image: DB Connection Service General settings]*


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select the service status. The following options are available: Enabled: The service starts together with the profile.Using the [Expression Language](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Expression_Language.htm), you can specify a startup condition that allows you to start a service based on a condition. If the startup condition evaluates to `true`, the service starts together with the profile. The startup condition can use only system macros (`${propName}`). For example, you can enable the DB Connection service used by the [Locking service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Locking_Service.htm) by checking for the environment property`'${LockingType}' = 'locking.service'`. Disabled: The service does not start with the profile. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Connection Password | Enter the password of the database user. |
| Connection User Name | Enter the name of the database user. |
| Connection check on start | Select whether to verify that the connection is established. If the value is set to 'false', verification is not performed. **Note** If [jTDS](http://jtds.sourceforge.net/) driver is used and successful database connectivity has already been tested via other components, disable the **Connection check on start** setting to allow the DB Connection Service to start. This is due to the fact that the jTDS driver does not support the latest features of MS SQL Server. For more information, see [JDBC Connectivity Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JDBC_Connectivity_Overview.htm#jTDS). |
| Connection retry attempts | The number of attempts to restart the component when a failure occurs. |
| Connection retry timeout (ms) | Defines time intervals between the attempts. |
| JDBC connection URL | Specify a JDBC URL to connect to the database. Note XEServer installation does not include the Oracle JDBC driver by default. To work with Oracle databases, you have to download the [Oracle JDBC driver](https://www.oracle.com/database/technologies/appdev/jdbc-downloads.html) and put the ojdbc{version}.jar to ${XESRoot}/platform/lib.user/jdbc/. Make sure that XEServer and XEngine use the same Oracle JDBC driver version. For H2 databases, use the absolute URL notation. For example, jdbc:h2:file:${XESWorkspace}/DS/data. For other databases, you can use relative URLs. For example, jdbc:postgresql://<ip address>:<port>/<data base name>. For more information on supported JDBC drivers and troubleshooting recommendations, see [JDBC Connectivity Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JDBC_Connectivity_Overview.htm). |



###### Pool Tab


| Property name | Default value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Exhausted action | 2 | The behavior when the pool is exhausted. |
| Min evictable idle time (ms) | 60000 | The minimum amount of time an object may sit idle in the pool before it is eligible for eviction by the idle object evictor (if any). |
| Pool max number of active connections | -1 | The maximum number of active connections that can be allocated from this pool at the same time. The default value indicates that no limit is set. |
| Pool max number of idle connections | 8 | The maximum number of connections that are kept in the pool at all times. |
| Pool max wait (ms) | 3000 | The maximum number of milliseconds that the pool waits for a connection to be returned before throwing an exception, or <= 0 to wait indefinitely. |
| Pool min number of idle connections | 0 | The minimum number of connections that are kept in the pool at all times. |
| Pool number tests per eviction run | 3 | The number of objects to examine during each run of the idle object evictor thread (if any). |
| Pool soft min evictable idle time (ms) | -1 | The minimum amount of time an object may sit idle in the pool before it is eligible for eviction by the idle object evictor (if any). |
| Pool time between eviction runs (ms) | 10000 | The number of milliseconds to sleep between the runs of the idle object evictor thread. When non-positive, no idle object evictor thread is run. |
| testOnBorrow | True | The indication of whether objects are validated before being borrowed from the pool. If the object fails to validate, it is dropped from the pool, and we attempt to borrow another. |
| testOnReturn | False | The indication of whether objects are validated before being returned to the pool. |
| testWhileIdle | False | The indication of whether objects are validated by the idle object evictor (if any). If an object fails to validate, it is dropped from the pool. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |
