<a id="use-db-transaction"></a>

##### Use DB Transaction

> 📄 Source: [Use_DB_Transaction.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_DB_Transaction.htm)

###### Use DB Transaction


They only open and close transactions.


Two input connection nodes are present on the DB Transaction Close component:

1. A commit connection node that processes and closes a successful database transaction.
2. A rollback connection node to handle error streams and rollback any updates that the database transaction may have caused.


*[Image: DB Transaction Close component route]*

---

<a id="use-groovy-to-work-with-databases"></a>

##### Use Groovy to Work with Databases

> 📄 Source: [Book_Use_Groovy_to_Work_with_Oracle_DB.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Use_Groovy_to_Work_with_Oracle_DB.htm)

###### Use Groovy to Work with Oracle Database
[Redirect to: {Groovy_SQL_Overview}](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_SQL_Overview.htm)

---

<a id="groovy-sql-overview"></a>

##### Groovy SQL Overview

> 📄 Source: [Groovy_SQL_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_SQL_Overview.htm)

###### Groovy SQL Overview


This section shows you how to perform basic operations with a relational database from Groovy scripts. You can run Groovy scripts using XEServer Groovy components at the route's run time. The Groovy sql API supports a wide variety of databases, some of which are shown below:

- Oracle
- MS SQL Server
- H2


In this section, we are going to use Oracle database (Release 12c) as an example.


###### Prerequisites


Before you proceed with this section, make sure you have the following:

- The system variable ${JAVA_HOME} points to JDK 8 (1.8.0_181 is used in this scenario),
- Edifecs products: XEServer 9.0 or later, Edifecs Application Manager 9.0 or later,
- The XEServer route that has the following structure:
- Oracle JDBC driver ojdbc8.jar is installed. To work with an Oracle database from the Groovy code, you must download the driver ojdbc8.jar from [Oracle JDBC Download Page](https://www.oracle.com/database/technologies/jdbc-ucp-122-downloads.html) and put ojdbc8.jar to ${XESRoot}/features/core/lib/ext/.
- Test Oracle database In this scenario, 64-bit Oracle Database 12c Standard Edition (Release 12.2.0.1.0) is used.

---

<a id="connect-to-a-database"></a>

##### Connect to a Database

> 📄 Source: [Connect_to_a_Database_Groovy.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Connect_to_a_Database_Groovy.htm)

###### Connect to a Database


A base groovy script that simply establishes a connection to an Oracle database is below.

```
import groovy.sql.Sql;
import java.sql.*;
import oracle.jdbc.driver.*;

// Credentials to access the database:
def userName = "SYSTEM";
def password = "YourPasswordHere";
// JDBC URL to the database:
def url = "jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:ORCL";

// Output debug info to XEServer std.out.log:
System.out.println("Class: " + this.getClass());
try {
	System.out.println("BEGIN connection test");
	// Load Oracle JDBC driver:
	Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver");
	// Connect to the database:
	System.out.println("Establishing connection...")
	Sql sql = Sql.newInstance(url, userName, password)
	System.out.println("sql object: " + sql);
	// Close the connection:
	System.out.println("Connection established. sql object: " + sql);
	sql.close();
	System.out.println("END connection test");
} catch (Exception ex) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + ex);
throw ex;
}
```


###### Test the script

1. Replace the highlighted values with your parameters and feed this code to the Groovy component on your route.
2. Restart the test route and drop any data file into the route's inbound directory to activate the route processing.


###### Expected results


If the route processing goes through without errors, the XEServer log std.out.log contains the following entries:


Class: class Script3


BEGIN connection test


Establishing connection...


Connection established. sql object: groovy.sql.Sql@22582bcf


Closing connection for: groovy.sql.Sql@22582bcf


END connection test


Tip When you edit your Groovy code (either an in-memory script on the EAM user interface or an external .groovy file) use the Reset command on the EAM Admin Console for changes to take effect.

---

<a id="perform-crud-operations"></a>

##### Perform CRUD Operations

> 📄 Source: [Perform_CRUD_Operations.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Perform_CRUD_Operations.htm)

###### Perform CRUD Operations


This section shows you how to perform CRUD operations on a database from the Groovy code. The sample Groovy script below does the following:


Step 1. Connects to the database.


Step 2. Creates a new table XES_TEST.


Step 3. Populates the table with dummy data:



Step 4. Retrieves data from the table.


Step 5. Updates the table XES_TEST:



Note When you define an SQL statement within the Groovy code, for example sql.query ("SELECT * FROM XES_TEST"), do not insert a semicolon at the end of the SQL expression as it may cause the exception: ORA-00922: missing or invalid option.

```
import groovy.sql.Sql;
import groovy.sql.OutParameter;
import java.sql.*;
import oracle.jdbc.driver.*;
import java.time.LocalTime;
import oracle.jdbc.OracleTypes;

// Credentials to access the database:
def userName = "SYSTEM";
def password = "YourPasswordHere";
// JDBC URL to the database:
def url = "jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:ORCL";
// Test table name:
def tableName = "XES_TEST";

System.out.println("Class: " + this.getClass());
try {
System.out.println("BEGIN CRUD test");
// Load Oracle JDBC driver:
Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver");
// Connect to the database:
System.out.println("Establishing connection..");
Sql sql = Sql.newInstance(url, userName, password);
System.out.println("Connection established. sql object: " + sql);
try {
	// CREATE table:
	sql.execute ("""
		CREATE TABLE ${tableName} (
		id INTEGER,
		data VARCHAR(64))
		""".toString());
	// INSERT data:
	sql.execute ("""
		INSERT INTO ${tableName} (id, data)
		VALUES (0, 'foo')
		""".toString());
	sql.execute ("""
		INSERT INTO ${tableName} (id, data)
		VALUES (1, 'bar')
		""".toString());
	// SELECT data:
	sql.query ("SELECT * FROM ${tableName}".toString()) { resultSet ->
		// Iterate through the result set returned by SELECT:
		while (resultSet.next()) {
			// Get value by index:
			def id = resultSet.getString(1);
			// Get value by column name:
			def data = resultSet.getString('data');
			System.out.println("Fetched row: ID ${id}, Data: ${data};");
		}
	}

	// UPDATE data
	System.out.println("Updating table...");
	sql.execute ("""
		UPDATE ${tableName}
		SET data='updated'
		WHERE id=0
		""".toString());
	sql.query ("SELECT * FROM ${tableName}".toString()) { resultSet ->
		while (resultSet.next()) {
			def id = resultSet.getString(1);
			def data = resultSet.getString('data');
			System.out.println("Fetched row: ID ${id}, Data: ${data};");
		}
	}
	// DROP table:
	sql.execute("DROP TABLE ${tableName}".toString());
} catch(Exception e) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + e);
	throw e;
}
// Close the connection:
System.out.println("Closing connection for: " + sql);
sql.close();
System.out.println("END CRUD test");
} catch (Exception ex) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + ex);
	throw ex;
}
```


###### Test the script

1. Replace the highlighted values with your parameters and copy this code to the Groovy component on your route.
2. Restart the test route and drop any data file into the route's inbound directory to activate the route processing.


###### Expected results


If the route processing goes through without errors, the XEServer log std.out.log contains the following entries:


Class: class Script2


BEGIN CRUD test


Establishing connection...


Connection established. sql object: groovy.sql.Sql@3bb3f30d


Fetched row: ID 0, Data: foo;


Fetched row: ID 1, Data: bar;


Updating table...


Fetched row: ID 0, Data: updated;


Fetched row: ID 1, Data: bar;


Closing connection for: groovy.sql.Sql@3bb3f30d


END CRUD test


Tip When you edit your Groovy code (either an in-memory script on the EAM user interface or an external .groovy file), use the Reset command on the EAM Admin Console for changes to take effect.

---

<a id="call-stored-procedures"></a>

##### Call Stored Procedures

> 📄 Source: [Call_Stored_Procedures.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Call_Stored_Procedures.htm)

###### Call Stored Procedures


This section show you how to create and call an SQL stored procedure from the Groovy code. First, the script creates a new stored procedure XES_TEST_PROC.

 Click to view stored procedure SQL

```
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE XES_TEST_PROC
	(
		PARAM0_IN IN NUMBER,
		PARAM1_OUT OUT VARCHAR2
	) IS
BEGIN
	SELECT DATA
	INTO PARAM2
	FROM XES_TEST
	WHERE ID=PARAM1;
END;
```






The procedure has the following parameters:

- PARAM0_IN: a NUMERIC input parameter that accepts row ID,
- PARAM1_OUT: a VARCHAR output parameter that carries the corresponding value of the DATA column from the table XES_TEST.


The table XES_TEST that has the following structure:


```
import groovy.sql.Sql;
import groovy.sql.OutParameter;
import java.sql.*;
import oracle.jdbc.driver.*;
import java.time.LocalTime;
import oracle.jdbc.OracleTypes;

// Credentials to access the database:
def userName = "SYSTEM";
def password = "YourPasswordHere";
// JDBC URL to the database:
def url = "jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:ORCL";
// Test table/proc name:
def test_tableName = "XES_TEST";
def test_procName = "XES_TEST_PROC";

System.out.println("Class: " + this.getClass());
try {
System.out.println("BEGIN STORED_PROC test");
// Load Oracle JDBC driver:
Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver");
// Connect to the database:
System.out.println("Establishing connection..");
Sql sql = Sql.newInstance(url, userName, password);
System.out.println("Connection established. sql object: " + sql);
try {
	// CREATE table:
	sql.execute ("""
		CREATE TABLE ${test_tableName} (
		id INTEGER,
		data VARCHAR(64)
		)
		""".toString());
	// INSERT data:
	sql.execute ("""
		INSERT INTO ${test_tableName} (id, data)
		VALUES (0, 'foo')
		""".toString());
	sql.execute ("""
		INSERT INTO ${test_tableName} (id, data)
		VALUES (1, 'bar')
		""".toString());
	// CREATE procedure:
	sql.execute("""
		CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE ${test_procName}
		(
		PARAM0_IN IN NUMBER, PARAM1_OUT OUT VARCHAR2
		) IS
		BEGIN
		SELECT DATA
		INTO PARAM1_OUT
		FROM ${test_tableName}
		WHERE ID=PARAM0_IN;
		END;
		""".toString());
	System.out.println("Stored procedure created. Calling ${test_procName}...");
	sql.call("{call XES_TEST_PROC(?, ?)}", [1, Sql.VARCHAR]) {
	// Get the output parameter PARAM1_OUT returned by the stored procedure:
	PARAM1_OUT ->
		// You can process returned param here...
		System.out.println("Stored procedure returned: " + PARAM1_OUT);
	};

	System.out.println("Dropping stored procedure: ${test_procName}");
	sql.execute("DROP PROCEDURE ${test_procName}".toString());
	System.out.println("Dropping table: ${test_tableName}");
	sql.execute("DROP TABLE ${test_tableName}".toString());
} catch(Exception e) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + e);
	throw e;
}
// Close the connection:
	System.out.println("Closing connection: " + sql);
	sql.close();
	System.out.println("END test");
} catch (Exception ex) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + ex);
	throw ex;
}
```


###### Test the script

1. Replace the highlighted values with your parameters and copy this code to the Groovy component on your route.
2. Restart the test route and drop any data file to the route's inbound directory to activate the route processing.


###### Expected results


If the route processing completes without errors, the XEServer log std.out.log contains the following entries:


Class: class Script3


BEGIN STORED_PROC test


Establishing connection..


Connection established. sql object: groovy.sql.Sql@57d60b19


Stored procedure created. Calling XES_TEST_PROC...


Stored procedure returned: bar


Dropping stored procedure: XES_TEST_PROC


Dropping table: XES_TEST


Closing connection: groovy.sql.Sql@57d60b19


END test


Notice the highlighted entry which indicates that the stored procedure has returned the expected value from the table XES_TEST.


Tip When you edit your Groovy code (either an in-memory script in EAM UI or an external .groovy file) use the Reset command on the EAM Admin Console for changes to take effect.

---

<a id="work-with-cursors"></a>

##### Work with Cursors

> 📄 Source: [Work_with_Cursors.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Work_with_Cursors.htm)

###### Work with Cursors


In Oracle databases, a [Cursor](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/B28359_01/appdev.111/b28370/cursor_variable.htm#LNPLS01312) (REF CURSOR) is a PL/SQL data type that points to a memory area where the database stores processing information while executing a multiple-row query. Using such a pointer to the "work area", you can access its information and process the rows returned by a query individually without loading the entire result set at once. Cursors are extremely powerful when used with stored procedures. In short, Cursors reduce the data traffic between the database and a client: when a database returns a REF CURSOR to the client, the database does not return all the data immediately. Instead, the database returns only an exact row of the Cursor when the client opens the REF CURSOR and requests the next row. For more advantages of using Cursors, see Oracle's [Introduction to REF CURSOR](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/B28359_01/appdev.111/b28370/cursor_variable.htm#LNPLS01312).


The script below shows how to handle Cursors returned as an output parameter from a stored procedure. First, the script creates a new stored procedure XES_TEST_PROC_CURSOR.

 Click to view stored procedure SQL

```
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE XES_TEST_PROC_CURSOR
	(
		PARAM0_IN IN NUMERIC,
		PARAM1_OUT OUT VARCHAR2,
		PARAM2_OUT OUT SYS_REFCURSOR
	) IS
BEGIN
	PARAM1_OUT := 'testString';
	OPEN PARAM2_OUT FOR
	SELECT * FROM XES_TEST;
END;
```






The procedure has the following parameters:

- PARAM0_IN: a NUMERIC input parameter that accepts row ID,
- PARAM1_OUT: a VARCHAR output parameter. The procedure explicitly sets this parameter's value to 'testString',
- PARAM2_OUT: the SYS_REFCURSOR that returns a Cursor with multiple data rows.


The table XES_TEST has the following structure:


```
import groovy.sql.Sql;
import groovy.sql.OutParameter;
import java.sql.*;
import oracle.jdbc.driver.*;
import java.time.LocalTime;
import oracle.jdbc.OracleTypes;

// Credentials to access the database:
def userName = "SYSTEM";
def password = "YourPasswordHere";
// JDBC URL to the database:
def url = "jdbc:oracle:thin:@localhost:1521:ORCL";
// Test table name:
def test_tableName = "XES_TEST";
def test_proc_cursorName = "XES_TEST_PROC_CURSOR";
System.out.println("Class: " + this.getClass());

// Anonymous OutParameter definition for type CURSOR:
OutParameter CURSOR_PARAMETER = new OutParameter() {
	public int getType() {
		return OracleTypes.CURSOR;
	}
};
try {
System.out.println("BEGIN test");

// Load Oracle JDBC driver:
Class.forName("oracle.jdbc.driver.OracleDriver");

// Connect to the database:
System.out.println("Establishing connection..")
Sql sql = Sql.newInstance(url, userName, password)
System.out.println("Connection established. sql object: " + sql);
try {
	// CREATE table:
	sql.execute ("""
		CREATE TABLE ${test_tableName} (
		id INTEGER,
		data VARCHAR(64)
		)
		""".toString());

	// INSERT data:
	sql.execute ("""
		INSERT INTO ${test_tableName} (id, data)
		VALUES (0, 'updated')
		""".toString());
		sql.execute ("""
		INSERT INTO ${test_tableName} (id, data)
		VALUES (1, 'bar')
		""".toString());

	// CREATE procedure:
	System.out.println("Creating stored procedure...");
	sql.execute("""
		CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE ${test_proc_cursorName}
		(
		PARAM0_IN IN NUMERIC,
		PARAM1_OUT OUT VARCHAR2,
		PARAM2_OUT OUT SYS_REFCURSOR
		) IS
		BEGIN
		PARAM1_OUT := 'testString';
		OPEN PARAM2_OUT FOR
		SELECT * FROM ${test_tableName};
		END;
		""".toString());

	System.out.println("Stored procedure created. Calling..");
	def parametersList = [1, Sql.VARCHAR, CURSOR_PARAMETER];
	sql.call("{call XES_TEST_PROC_CURSOR(?, ?, ?)}", parametersList) {
		PARAM1_OUT,PARAM2_OUT ->
		System.out.println("PARAM1_OUT is: " + PARAM1_OUT);
		System.out.println("Iterating through Cursor..")
		while (PARAM2_OUT.next()) {
			System.out.println("\t id: " 	+
			PARAM2_OUT.getString('ID') 		+
			", "							+
			"data: " 						+
			PARAM2_OUT.getString('DATA')	+ ";");
		}
	};
	// Cleanup DB:
	System.out.println("Dropping stored procedure: ${test_proc_cursorName}");
	sql.execute("DROP PROCEDURE ${test_proc_cursorName}".toString());
	System.out.println("Dropping table: ${test_tableName}");
	sql.execute("DROP TABLE ${test_tableName}".toString());
} catch(Exception e) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + e);
	throw e;
}
// Close the connection:
System.out.println("Closing connection: " + sql);
sql.close();
System.out.println("END test");
} catch (Exception ex) {
	System.out.println("GROOVY Exception: " + ex);
	throw ex;
}
```


Notice the anonymous definition of CURSOR_PARAMETER type. This type is required to work with Cursors.


###### Test the script

1. Replace the highlighted values with your parameters and copy this code to the Groovy component on your route.
2. Restart the test route and drop any data file to the route's inbound directory to activate the route processing.


###### Expected results


If the route processing completes without errors, the XEServer log std.out.log contains the following entries:


Class: class Script8


BEGIN test


Establishing connection..


Connection established. sql object: groovy.sql.Sql@f3617de


Creating a stored procedure:


CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE XES_TEST_PROC_CURSOR


(


PARAM0_IN IN NUMERIC,


PARAM1_OUT OUT VARCHAR2,


PARAM2_OUT OUT SYS_REFCURSOR


) IS


BEGIN


PARAM1_OUT := 'testString';


OPEN PARAM2_OUT FOR


SELECT * FROM XES_TEST;


END;


The stored procedure created. Calling procedure..


PARAM1_OUT is: testString


Iterating through Cursor..


id: 0, data: updated;


id: 1, data: bar;


Closing connection: groovy.sql.Sql@f3617de


END test

<a id="ms-sql-server-windows-authentication"></a>

##### MS SQL Server Windows Authentication

> 📄 Source: [MS_SQL_Server_Windows_Authentication.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/MS_SQL_Server_Windows_Authentication.htm)

###### MS SQL Server Windows Authentication


This section discusses a scenario that shows you how to configure XEServer to connect to an MS SQL Server using Windows authentication.

1. Verify that the SQL Server certificate is properly installed on XEServer and on MS SQL Server computers. To create a new certificate: 1. Create a new P12 certificate (for example, using Java Keytool). 2. On both SQL Server and on the XEServer computer, install the P12 certificate to the Personal and Trusted Root Certification Authorities certificate stores.
2. Verify that the master and other databases to be accessed by XEServer are configured with the domain user account. To create a new user account: 1. Create a new login that uses Windows Authentication. To create a new login: | Microsoft SQL Server Management Studio | T-SQL | | --- | --- | | In Object Explorer, expand Security. Right click Logins and then click New Login. Create a new login with the following parameters: Login name: [domain\username], Authentication: Windows authentication, Default database: dbName, Default language: us_english. | ``` USE [master] GO CREATE LOGIN [domain\username] FROM WINDOWS WITH DEFAULT_DATABASE=[dbName], DEFAULT_LANGUAGE=[us_english] GO ``` | 2. b. For each database that XEServer has to access, add the domain user account using the login created on the previous step. To add a domain user account: | Microsoft SQL Server Management Studio | T-SQL | | --- | --- | | In Object Explorer, navigate to Databases/[dbName]/Security/Users/. Right click Users, and then click New User. Create a new user using the login [domain\username] from the previous step. | ``` USE [dbName] GO CREATE USER [domain\username] FOR LOGIN [domain\username] WITH DEFAULT_SCHEMA=[dbo] GO ``` |
3. Download the [Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/jdbc/download-microsoft-jdbc-driver-for-sql-server?view=sql-server-ver15). In this scenario, Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server 7.4 is used. Run sqljdbc_{version}_enu.exe to extract the driver.
4. In the downloaded JDBC driver location, copy the corresponding 64-bit or 32-bit DLL: Microsoft JDBC Driver 7.4 for SQL Server\sqljdbc_7.4\enu\auth\{x64|x86}\sqljdbc_auth.dll. Drop this DLL into {XESRoot}\bin\x64\ for each XEServer instance that connects to MS SQL Server using Windows authentication.
5. In the downloaded JDBC driver location, navigate to Microsoft JDBC Driver 7.4 for SQL Server\sqljdbc_7.4\enu\ and copy the correct JAR mssql-jdbc-7.4.1.jre{version}.jar that matches the JDK version of the XEServer's computer. For example, use mssql-jdbc-7.4.1.jre8.jar when XEServer uses JDK 8.
6. On the XEServer computer, go to {XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\jdbc\ and replace all existing files mssql-jdbc*.jar (if any) with the one from Step #5.
7. Open the profile that connects to the target MS SQL Server. In the database connection settings, enter the following JDBC URL:


###### Test the Connection


A quick way to test the connection to your MS SQL Server below.

1. Start the EAM Client, and then connect to the EAM Server.
2. Expand XEngine, right-click Database Selector, and then click Properties.
3. In Connection, enter your JDBC URL and the credentials from Step #7.
4. Click Check Connection. If everything has been configured correctly, EAM displays a notification on a successful connection test.

<a id="activemq-server-service"></a>

##### ActiveMQ Server Service

> 📄 Source: [ActiveMQ_Server_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/ActiveMQ_Server_Service.htm)

###### ActiveMQ Server Service


Configure ActiveMQ Server to Work with Other ProfilesMigration to XEServer 9.2 and later


The service allows you to deploy your own JMS server based on the open source messaging server [Apache ActiveMQ](https://activemq.apache.org/) (version 5.17.1). Once the profile which has this service starts, the JMS server can be accessed by other XEServer profiles.


When you add the service to a profile, you can view the following settings:


*[Image: ActiveMQ Server General settings]*


To configure the broker:

1. On the EAM System tab, expand the profile with the ActiveMQ Server service.
2. Double-click Configuration.
3. On the Outline tab, expand Services, expand Servers, and then click ActiveMQ Server.
4. On the Configuration tab, click Broker configuration.
5. Click Configure, make the required changes to the XML file, and then click Save.


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Broker configuration | Specify the path to the [broker configuration file](https://activemq.apache.org/configuring-brokers) used to run an ActiveMQ broker. By default, the service creates a default broker configuration file at ${XESRoot}/profiles/<ProfileName>/config/activemq/broker.xml. You can provide a custom location for the broker configuration file. |
| TCP connector | The link to the TCP connector. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |



###### Configure ActiveMQ Server to Work with Other Profiles


To allow other profiles to interact with your ActiveMQ Server service, edit your broker configuration file as follows:

1. Select an ActiveMQ Server you want to configure.
2. On the Configuration tab, select the Broker configuration property, and then click Configure.
3. Append the attribute useJmx="true" to the <broker> node to [enable JMX](https://activemq.apache.org/jmx.html): <broker ... useJmx="true" >
4. For an insecure connection, in the <managementContext /> node, set the attribute createConnector="true": <managementContext> <managementContext createConnector="true" /> </managementContext>


-Or-

1. For a secure connection, replace the <managementContext> </managementContext> node with the following: <managementContext> <managementContext createConnector="true" jmxDomainName="org.apache.activemq"> <property xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans" name="environment"> <map xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans"> <entry xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans" key="jmx.remote.x.password.file" value="${XESProfileConfig}/activemq/jmx.password"/> <entry xmlns="http:-//www.springframework.org/schema/beans" key="jmx.remote.x.access.file" value="${XESProfileConfig}/activemq/jmx.access"/> </map> </property> </managementContext> </managementContext>
2. In the directory${XESRoot}/profiles/<profileName>/config/activemq/ , create the file jmx.access and specify the read/write permissions for a user in the following format: <userName> readwrite <userName1> readonly


In the directory ${XESRoot}/profiles/<profileName>/config/activemq/, create the file jmx.password and specify the user password in the following format:


<UserName> <UserPassword>


The userName-userPassword entries declared in this file are required to access your ActiveMQ Server.


###### Configuring CLI Console Access for ActiveMQ


The CLI console provides terminal-based access with the same features as the web console.


Note: Use the ActiveMQ server (managed via the web or CLI consoles) only for testing purposes. For production environments, prefer standalone or managed solutions to ensure reliability and stability.


Steps to enable CLI Console Access:

1. In the EAM System tab, expand the profile with the ActiveMQ Server service.
2. Double-click Configuration.
3. In the Outline tab, expand Services, then Servers, and select ActiveMQ Server.
4. In the Configuration tab, click Broker configuration, then click Configure.
5. Enable JMX (Java Management Extensions) to allow CLI console access. By default, ActiveMQ disables JMX ports for security. Add the following attribute to the <broker> node: <broker ... useJmx="true" >
6. In the <managementContext /> node, set the createConnector="true":<managementContext><managementContext createConnector="true" /></managementContext>Optionally, specify the connector port.createConnector= “true” connectorPort= “1099”
7. Click Save.


###### Launch the CLI Console


Use the following command to start the CLI console. You can reuse this command in scripts or automation tools:


@echo off


setlocal


set CP=%XESRoot%/features/core/lib/bundles/resolve/*;


set CP=%CP%;%XESRoot%/platform/lib/*;


set CP=%CP%;%XESRoot%/features/core/lib/activemq/*;>


"%JAVA_HOME%/bin/java" -cp "%CP%" org.apache.activemq.console.Main %*


exit /B 0


To explore available CLI tasks, options, and advanced configurations, refer to the official [ActiveMQ Classic Command Line Tools Reference](https://activemq.apache.org/components/classic/documentation/activemq-classic-command-line-tools-reference) page.


###### Migration to XEServer 9.2 and later


In XEServer 9.2, the service has been upgraded to a newer version of the Apache ActiveMQ engine: 5.15.9. Compared to pre-9.2 XEServer versions, this upgrade affects the configuration of the default broker.xml file created by the service. The major change is that the persistence adapter [amqPersistenceAdapter](https://activemq.apache.org/amq-message-store) (used by default in pre-9.2 XEServer versions) is replaced with [kahaDB](https://activemq.apache.org/kaha-persistence) since the former is not supported by the new ActiveMQ version.


When you use Configuration Manager to deploy a pre-9.2 profile to XEServer 9.2, Configuration Manager checks the broker.xml file inside the profile image and automatically replaces the unsupported <amqPersistenceAdapter /> adapter with <kahaDB />. However, there are two limitations you should keep in mind:

- The file broker.xml used by the ActiveMQ Server service in the profile image must also reside within the image.
- In broker.xml, the adapter's backing database directory (directory attribute) must be under {$XESWorkspace}. See the following sample adapter definition created by a pre-9.2 XEServer instance: Example...<persistenceAdapter><amqPersistenceAdapter directory="${XESWorkspace}/activemq/" maxFileLength="50mb" /></persistenceAdapter>...


If these two conditions are observed, Configuration Manager deploys the profile image and the deployed broker.xml contains the updated adapters <kahaDB /> that are ready to work. Otherwise, the profile deployment fails and the corresponding warning goes to the deployment report.


Note The difference between KahaDB and AMQ Message Store adapters implementation does not allow you to migrate the database contents using Configuration Manager.

---

<a id="ftp-command"></a>

##### FTP Command

> 📄 Source: [Book_FTP_Command.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_FTP_Command.htm)

###### FTP Command
[Redirect to: {FTP_Connection_Service}](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Connection_Service.htm)

---

<a id="ftp-connection-service"></a>

##### FTP Connection Service

> 📄 Source: [FTP_Connection_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Connection_Service.htm)

###### FTP Connection Service


This service is used to establish a connection with an FTP server (FTP/FTPS/SFTP) and share the connection with multiple [FTP Command](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Command.htm) components that perform FTP operations through this connection. When the service opens a connection to an FTP server, the service creates a new connection object identified by an ID. FTP Command components associated with the service can request the service to provide a specific connection by ID.


Using multiple components FTP Command in combination with this service significantly improves the performance as the service opens the physical connection only once and shares this connection among several FTP Command components, thus reducing the overhead on opening the TCP/IP connection each time.


###### Service Settings


The service has the following settings:



###### Connection Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Connection Protocol** | Select the protocol to be used by the service. The following options are available:[FTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc959): both the command and data channels are unencrypted.[FTPS](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2228.txt): an SSL-protected version of FTP, where data exchange through the data channel is protected by SSL certificates.[SFTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-secsh-filexfer-02): a protected version of FTP based on SSH. Both the data and commands are transferred in the form of specially formatted packets through a single connection. |
| **Host** | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| **Port** | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| **Retry attempt** | The number of retry attempts to establish a connection with the FTP server. |
| **Retry delay** | The delay between retry attempts in milliseconds. |
| Inactivity timeout | The timeout that defines the time-to-live for connection objects. If the service does not receive a connection request from FTP Command components during this timeout, the service closes the corresponding FTP connection making it unavailable for reuse. Each connection request from FTP Command components resets the timeout. |
| Keep connection alive FTP servers have an idle timeout to close idle connections. Typically, this timeout is rather short - about one minute, depending on the FTP server. The service can keep the connection alive by sending meaningless commands (such as NOOP) to the FTP server at regular intervals. | |
| Refresh connection every | Specify the time interval in seconds to send the keep alive command to the FTP server. |
| Refresh operation | Select the FTP operation to send. |
| Login | You can select whether the service should perform the login operation when opening a connection to the FTP server. You can disable logging in if, for example, you want to perform some operations (such as SITE) on the server before the login. In this case, you have to perform the login operation using `ftp.login()` in your Groovy code on the corresponding FTP Command component. |

---

<a id="ftp-services"></a>

##### FTP Services

> 📄 Source: [Book_FTP.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_FTP.htm)

###### FTP Services


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="ftp-service"></a>

##### FTP Service

> 📄 Source: [FTP_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Service.htm)

###### FTP Service


With this service, you can establish a connection with an FTP server. The components [FTP Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Inbound.htm) and [FTP Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Outbound.htm) use this service to download and upload data from/to an FTP server.


The service has the following settings:


*[Image: FTP Service general settings]*


To see the list of macros supported for this component, see [XEServer supported macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm#Restful).


###### FTP Logging


By default, the service logs FTP events in xes.ftp.client.log and in xes.system.log with the level WARNING. If you set the FTP Client logging level to DEBUG, the service directs all FTP/FTPS/SFTP logs to xes.ftp.client.log. To get the most verbose FTP interaction logs, set the logging level to TRACE - in this case, XEServer creates the folder ftptracer under {XESRoot}\profiles\<profileName>\log\ that contains detailed FTP logs split into individual sessions.


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Connection Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Connection Protocol** | Select the protocol to be used by the service. The following options are available:[FTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc959): both the command and data channels are unencrypted.[FTPS](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2228.txt): an SSL-protected version of FTP, where data exchange through the data channel is protected by SSL certificates.[SFTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-secsh-filexfer-02): a protected version of FTP based on SSH. Both the data and commands are transferred in the form of specially formatted packets through a single connection. |
| **Host** | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| **Port** | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| **User name** | Enter the user name to log in to the FTP Server. |
| **Password** | Enter the password to log in to the FTP Server. |
| **Transfer mode** | Select the data transfer mode. You can select between: BINARY: the service creates a byte-for-byte identical copy of the transferred file. ASCII: intended for transferring files that contain plain text. This option is not available for the SFTP protocol. |
| **Retry attempt** | The number of retry attempts to establish a connection with the FTP server. |
| **Retry delay** | The delay between retry attempts in milliseconds. |
| Waiting time threshold | Specify the maximum time (in seconds) that the service waits until the FTP server completes an operation. If the FTP server does not respond that the operation has been completed within this timeout, the service interrupts the current operation and closes the connection to the FTP server. If the value is set to 0, the service does not interrupt the current operation and waits until the FTP server returns the operation completion response. |
| Enable active mode | When the active mode is enabled, the XEServer FTP service sends the connection details to an FTP server and the FTP server initiates the data exchange session that the FTP service accepts. By default, the data exchange session is established in the passive mode and is initiated by the FTP service. |



###### Security Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Server Certificate Store In this section, you specify a keystore that contains the FTP server's security certificates. | |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used in the Server keystore. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The address of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to work with the key from the keystore. |
| User Certificate Store In this section, you specify a keystore with user certificates to allow the FTP server to authenticate user identity. | |
| **Type** | The type of keystore used in the User keystore. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The address of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to work with the key from the keystore. |
| **Primary key password** | The password to the private key. |



###### Pool Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can specify the connection pool parameters.


| Parameter Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| pool.maxPoolCapacity | The maximum number of groups of connections that can be managed by the pool. Each connection group is identified by the key combined from the FTP protocol, FTP server host, FTP server port, and username. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxActive | The maximum number of objects that can be allocated by the pool at a time. If value < 0 the number of objects is unlimited. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxIdle | The maximum number of idle objects in the pool at a time. If value < 0 the number of objects is unlimited. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxWait | The maximum amount of time to wait for getting an object from the pool. The default value is 1000. |
| pool.minEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum amount of time for an idle object in the pool before it is eligible for eviction due to idle state. The default value is 60000. |
| pool.minIdle | The minimum number of allowed objects in the pool before new objects are spawned. The default value is 0. |
| pool.numTestsPerEvictionRun | The number of objects to be examined during each run of idle eviction thread. The default value is 3. |
| pool.softMinEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum amount of time an object may remain idle in the pool before it is eligible for eviction by the idle evictor, with the extra condition that at least "minIdle" object remains in the pool. The default value is 1. |
| pool.testOnBorrow | Defines whether objects pass a validation before being borrowed from the pool. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. The default value is true. |
| pool.testOnReturn | Defines whether objects pass a validation before being returned to the pool. The default value is false. |
| pool.testWhileIdle | Defines whether objects pass a validation by the idle object evictor. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. The default value is true. |
| pool.timeBetweenEvictionRunsMillis | The time interval at which idle object evictor runs. The default value is 10000. |
| pool.whenExhaustedAction | Select the behavior of getting an object from the pool if the pool is exhausted (the list number matches the selected value): - throws "NoSuchElementException". - blocks according to the pool.maxWait value and throws "NoSuchElementException" after timeout exceeds. - creates a new object and returns it. The pool.maxActive parameters becomes meaningless for such behavior. The default value is 1. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="ftps-service"></a>

##### FTPS Service

> 📄 Source: [FTPS_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTPS_Service.htm)

###### FTPS Service


File Transfer Protocol Secure (FTPS) is a network protocol that is used to transfer files from one host to another over a TCP-based network by using negotiated authentication that requires a known security mechanism, such as SSL or TLS. FTPS can be used to provide a location from which data files can be picked up and then processed by a route or a routing profile in XEServer.


*[Image: FTPS Service General settings]*


###### FTP Logging


By default, the service logs FTP events in xes.ftp.client.log and in xes.system.log with the level WARNING. If you set the FTP Client logging level to DEBUG, the service directs all FTP/FTPS/SFTP logs to xes.ftp.client.log. To get the most verbose FTP interaction logs, set the logging level to TRACE - in this case, XEServer creates the folder ftptracer under {XESRoot}\profiles\<profileName>\log\ that contains detailed FTP logs split into individual sessions.


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Connection Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Connection Protocol** | Select the protocol to be used by the service. The following options are available:[FTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc959): both the command and data channels are unencrypted.[FTPS](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2228.txt): an SSL-protected version of FTP, where data exchange through the data channel is protected by SSL certificates.[SFTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-secsh-filexfer-02): a protected version of FTP based on SSH. Both the data and commands are transferred in the form of specially formatted packets through a single connection. |
| **Host** | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| **Port** | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| **User name** | Enter the user name to log in to the FTP Server. |
| **Password** | Enter the password to log in to the FTP Server. |
| **Transfer mode** | Select the data transfer mode. You can select between: BINARY: the service creates a byte-for-byte identical copy of the transferred file. ASCII: intended for transferring files that contain plain text. This option is not available for the SFTP protocol. |
| **Retry attempt** | The number of retry attempts to establish a connection with the FTP server. |
| **Retry delay** | The delay between retry attempts in milliseconds. |
| Waiting time threshold | Specify the maximum time (in seconds) that the service waits until the FTP server completes an operation. If the FTP server does not respond that the operation has been completed within this timeout, the service interrupts the current operation and closes the connection to the FTP server. If the value is set to 0, the service does not interrupt the current operation and waits until the FTP server returns the operation completion response. |
| Enable active mode | When the active mode is enabled, the XEServer FTP service sends the connection details to an FTP server and the FTP server initiates the data exchange session that the FTP service accepts. By default, the data exchange session is established in the passive mode and is initiated by the FTP service. |



###### Security Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Server Certificate Store** | A keystore containing Server security certificates. |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used in the Server keystore. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The address of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to work with the key from the keystore. |
| **User Certificate Store** | A keystore containing user security certificates. |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used in the User keystore. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The address of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to work with the key from the keystore. |
| **Primary key password** | The password to the private key. |
| **Connection start mode** | The start mode defines when the SSL negotiation is started upon the connection to server. Auto. If the remote port is set to the standard plaintext port of the protocol where applicable, the client behaves the same way as with explicit start mode. In all other cases, SSL negotiation is implicit . Explicit. The client first connects in plaintext, and then explicitly start SSL negotiation through a protocol command. Implicit. The SSL negotiation starts immediately after the connection is established. None. There is no SSL negotiation and SSL security. All communication is in plaintext mode. |
| **Connection secure mode** | Select the needed protocol that provides connection security. |



###### Pool Configuration Tab


On this tab you can specify connection pool parameters.


| Parameter Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| pool.maxPoolCapacity | The maximum number of groups of connections that can be managed by the pool. Each connection group is identified by the key combined from the FTP protocol, FTP server host, FTP server port, and username. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxActive | The maximum number of objects that can be allocated by the pool at a time. If value < 0 the number of objects is unlimited. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxIdle | The maximum number of idle objects in the pool at a time. If value < 0 the number of objects is unlimited. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxWait | The maximum amount of time to wait for getting an object from the pool. The default value is 1000. |
| pool.minEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum amount of time for an idle object in the pool before it is eligible for eviction due to idle state. The default value is 60000. |
| pool.minIdle | The minimum number of allowed objects in the pool before new objects are spawned. The default value is 0. |
| pool.numTestsPerEvictionRun | The number of objects to be examined during each run of idle eviction thread. The default value is 3. |
| pool.softMinEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum amount of time an object may remain idle in the pool before it is eligible for eviction by the idle evictor, with the extra condition that at least "minIdle" object remains in the pool. The default value is 1. |
| pool.testOnBorrow | Defines whether objects pass a validation before being borrowed from the pool. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. The default value is true. |
| pool.testOnReturn | Defines whether objects pass a validation before being returned to the pool. The default value is false. |
| pool.testWhileIdle | Defines whether objects pass a validation by the idle object evictor. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. The default value is true. |
| pool.timeBetweenEvictionRunsMillis | The time interval at which idle object evictor runs. The default value is 10000. |
| pool.whenExhaustedAction | Select the behavior of getting an object from the pool if the pool is exhausted (the list number matches the selected value): - throws "NoSuchElementException". - blocks according to the pool.maxWait value and throws "NoSuchElementException" after timeout exceeds. - creates a new object and returns it. The pool.maxActive parameters becomes meaningless for such behavior. The default value is 1. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab

| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |

| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |

<a id="sftp-service-deprecated"></a>

##### SFTP Service (Deprecated)

> 📄 Source: [SFTP_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Service.htm)

###### SFTP Service (Deprecated)


Secure File Transfer Protocol (SFTP) is a network protocol that is used to transfer files from one host to another over a TCP-based network by using SSH encryption. FTP can be used to provide a location from which data files can be picked up and then processed by a route or a routing profile in XEServer.


Note This service is deprecated and will be removed from XEServer in 2023 (Q3). Use the new [SFTP Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Service_Dedicated.htm) to interact with FTP servers over SFTP.


*[Image: SFTP Service General settings]*


###### FTP Logging


By default, the service logs FTP events in xes.ftp.client.log and in xes.system.log with the level WARNING. If you set the FTP Client logging level to DEBUG, the service directs all FTP/FTPS/SFTP logs to xes.ftp.client.log. To get the most verbose FTP interaction logs, set the logging level to TRACE - in this case, XEServer creates the folder ftptracer under {XESRoot}\profiles\<profileName>\log\ that contains detailed FTP logs split into individual sessions.


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Connection Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Connection Protocol** | Select the protocol to be used by the service. The following options are available:[FTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc959): both the command and data channels are unencrypted.[FTPS](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc2228.txt): an SSL-protected version of FTP, where data exchange through the data channel is protected by SSL certificates.[SFTP](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-secsh-filexfer-02): a protected version of FTP based on SSH. Both the data and commands are transferred in the form of specially formatted packets through a single connection. |
| **Host** | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| **Port** | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| **User name** | Enter the user name to log in to the FTP Server. |
| **Password** | Enter the password to log in to the FTP Server. |
| **Transfer mode** | Select the data transfer mode. You can select between: BINARY: the service creates a byte-for-byte identical copy of the transferred file. ASCII: intended for transferring files that contain plain text. This option is not available for the SFTP protocol. |
| **Retry attempt** | The number of retry attempts to establish a connection with the FTP server. |
| **Retry delay** | The delay between retry attempts in milliseconds. |
| Waiting time threshold | Specify the maximum time (in seconds) that the service waits until the FTP server completes an operation. If the FTP server does not respond that the operation has been completed within this timeout, the service interrupts the current operation and closes the connection to the FTP server. If the value is set to 0, the service does not interrupt the current operation and waits until the FTP server returns the operation completion response. |
| Enable active mode | When the active mode is enabled, the XEServer FTP service sends the connection details to an FTP server and the FTP server initiates the data exchange session that the FTP service accepts. By default, the data exchange session is established in the passive mode and is initiated by the FTP service. |



###### Security Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Server Certificate Store** | A keystore containing Server security certificates. |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used in the Server keystore. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The address of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to work with the key from the keystore. |
| **User Certificate Store** | A keystore containing user security certificates. |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used in the User keystore. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The address of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to work with the key from the keystore. |
| **Primary key password** | The password to the private key. |
| **Authentication mode** | The authentication is used when the client connects to the server. None. No authentication is performed. The current User value is ignored, and the connection is logged in as anonymous. Certificate. The client uses the User and Certificate values to authenticate the user. Certificate value must have a private key available for this authentication method to succeed. Mixed. Using this the client is able to attempt a multi-step authentication process. The client sends authentication data to the server based on the list of methods allowed for the current user and the authentication property values supplied. The client continues to send authentication data until the server acknowledges authentication success. If the server rejects an authentication step, the an exception is thrown. Password.The client uses the User and Password values to authenticate the user. |



###### Pool Configuration Tab


On this tab you can specify connection pool parameters.


| Parameter Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| pool.maxPoolCapacity | The maximum number of groups of connections that can be managed by the pool. Each connection group is identified by the key combined from the FTP protocol, FTP server host, FTP server port, and username. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxActive | The maximum number of objects that can be allocated by the pool at a time. If value < 0 the number of objects is unlimited. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxIdle | The maximum number of idle objects in the pool at a time. If value < 0 the number of objects is unlimited. The default value is 4. |
| pool.maxWait | The maximum amount of time to wait for getting an object from the pool. The default value is 1000. |
| pool.minEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum amount of time for an idle object in the pool before it is eligible for eviction due to idle state. The default value is 60000. |
| pool.minIdle | The minimum number of allowed objects in the pool before new objects are spawned. The default value is 0. |
| pool.numTestsPerEvictionRun | The number of objects to be examined during each run of idle eviction thread. The default value is 3. |
| pool.softMinEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum amount of time an object may remain idle in the pool before it is eligible for eviction by the idle evictor, with the extra condition that at least "minIdle" object remains in the pool. The default value is 1. |
| pool.testOnBorrow | Defines whether objects pass a validation before being borrowed from the pool. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. The default value is true. |
| pool.testOnReturn | Defines whether objects pass a validation before being returned to the pool. The default value is false. |
| pool.testWhileIdle | Defines whether objects pass a validation by the idle object evictor. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. The default value is true. |
| pool.timeBetweenEvictionRunsMillis | The time interval at which idle object evictor runs. The default value is 10000. |
| pool.whenExhaustedAction | Select the behavior of getting an object from the pool if the pool is exhausted (the list number matches the selected value): - throws "NoSuchElementException". - blocks according to the pool.maxWait value and throws "NoSuchElementException" after timeout exceeds. - creates a new object and returns it. The pool.maxActive parameters becomes meaningless for such behavior. The default value is 1. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |

| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |

<a id="sftp-service"></a>

##### SFTP Service

> 📄 Source: [SFTP_Service_Dedicated.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Service_Dedicated.htm)

###### SFTP Service


This service is used to establish a connection with an FTP server using the SFTP protocol. The route components [SFTP Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Inbound.htm) and [SFTP Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Outbound.htm) use this service to download and upload data onto the server. This service was designed as a replacement for the [SFTP Service (Deprecated)](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Service.htm) which under certain conditions could have performance issues. Compared to the old SFTP Service, this service uses more efficient and secure libraries and provides more stable SFTP connection.


Note The legacy [SFTP Service (Deprecated)](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SFTP_Service.htm) is deprecated and will be removed in 2023 (Q3). Edifecs recommends that you update your FTP components that work with SFTP to use this new implementation.


###### Service settings


Apart from generic service settings, the service has the following settings:



###### Connection tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Host** | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| **Port** | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| **User name** | Enter a user name to log in to the FTP server. |
| **Password** | Enter a password to log in to the FTP server. |
| **Retry attempt** | Specify the number of retries in case a connection with the FTP server cannot be established. |
| **Retry delay** | The delay between retry attempts in milliseconds. |
| Waiting time threshold | Specify the maximum time (in seconds) for the service to wait until an FTP server completes a requested operation. If the FTP server does not acknowledge that the operation has been completed within this timeout, the service interrupts the current operation and closes the connection with the FTP server. If the value is set to 0, the service does not interrupt the operation and waits until the FTP server returns the operation completion response. |



###### Security tab


Note SSH keys must be [generated](https://www.thedigitalcatonline.com/blog/2018/04/25/rsa-keys/#generating-key-pairs-with-openssh) using RSA key generation algorithm.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Server certificate store A server certificate store acts as a truststore - it holds certificates of FTP servers to which XEServer can connect. If you do not specify a certificate store, XEServer can connect to any FTP server. | |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used as a server certificate store. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The location of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to access the keystore. |
| User certificate store A user certificate store acts as a regular keystore - it holds a private key used to connect to secured FTP server. | |
| **Type** | The type of the keystore used as a user certificate store. |
| **Path** | The keystore location. |
| **Subject** | The location of the key in the keystore. |
| **Password** | The password used to access the keystore. |
| **Primary key password** | The password to the private key. |
| **Authentication mode** | Select the type of authentication for XEServer to use when connecting to an FTP server. None. No authentication. The User value is ignored, and XEServer attempts to log in anonymously. Certificate. XEServer uses the User and Certificate values to authenticate the connection. The Certificate must have a private key available for this authentication method to succeed. Mixed. Allows XEServer to perform a multi-step authentication process. The client sends authentication data to the server based on the list of methods allowed for the current user and the authentication property values supplied. The client continues to send authentication data until the server acknowledges authentication success. If the server rejects an authentication step, an exception is thrown. Password. XEServer uses the User and Password values to authenticate the user session. |



###### Pool tab


On this tab, you can define connection pooling parameters. Opening and closing a physical connection with an FTP server is a costly operation. With connection pooling, the service opens several connections and then reuses these connections when needed keeping them in the pool. If disabled, the service opens a new physical connection each time an FTP operation is requested.


| Parameter Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| pool.maxActive | The maximum number of connection objects that can be allocated by the pool at a time. If value < 0, the number of objects is unlimited. |
| pool.maxIdle | The maximum number of idle objects in the pool at a time. If value < 0, the number of objects is unlimited. |
| pool.maxWait | The maximum time to wait for getting an object from the pool. |
| pool.minEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum time for an idle object to remain in the pool before it is eligible for eviction due to idle state. |
| pool.minIdle | The minimum number of allowed idle objects in the pool before new objects are spawned. |
| pool.numTestsPerEvictionRun | The number of objects to be examined during each run of idle eviction thread. |
| pool.softMinEvictableIdleTimeMillis | The minimum time an object may remain idle in the pool before it is eligible for eviction by the idle evictor, with the extra condition that at least `pool.minIdle` objects remains in the pool. |
| pool.testOnBorrow | Defines whether objects should pass a validation before being borrowed from the pool. If the validation fails, the object is removed from the pool. |
| pool.testOnReturn | Defines whether objects should pass a validation before returning to the pool. |
| pool.testWhileIdle | Defines whether objects pass a validation by the idle object evictor. If the validation fails, the object is dropped from the pool. |
| pool.timeBetweenEvictionRunsMillis | The time interval at which the idle object evictor runs. |
| pool.whenExhaustedAction | Select the behavior when the service attempts to get a connection object from the pool and the pool is exhausted (the list number matches the selected value): throws "NoSuchElementException". blocks according to the pool.maxWait value and throws "NoSuchElementException" after timeout exceeds. creates a new object and returns it. The pool.maxActive parameter becomes meaningless for such behavior. |



###### Manage SFTP Algorithms


You can enable (disable) cipher, key exchange, message authentication code (MAC), compressions, and signatures algorithms used by the SFTP Service to work with the SFTP protocol. For this, you have to define the supported algorithms as system properties on the [profile Startup tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) or on the [global Startup tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/Startup_Tab.htm) for the [FTP Server Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Manage_SFTP_Algorithms.htm) and SFTP Inbound / SFTP Outbound components.


The properties are the following:

- com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.component.sftp.ciphers
- com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.component.sftp.mac
- com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.component.sftp.kex
- com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.component.sftp.compressions
- com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.component.sftp.signatures


The following algorithms are supported by the SFTP Service:


Ciphers: none, 3des-cbc, aes128-ctr (default), aes192-ctr (default), aes256-ctr (default), aes128-cbc (default), aes192-cbc (default), aes256-cbc (default), aes128-gcm@openssh.com (default), aes256-gcm@openssh.com (default), arcfour128, arcfour256, blowfish-cbc, chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com (default)


MACs: hmac-md5, hmac-md5-96, hmac-sha1 (default), hmac-sha1-etm@openssh.com (default), hmac-sha1-96, hmac-sha2-256 (default), hmac-sha2-256-etm@openssh.com (default), hmac-sha2-512 (default), hmac-sha2-512-etm@openssh.com (default)


Key exchange algorithms: curve25519-sha256 (default), curve25519-sha256@libssh.org (default), curve448-sha512 (default), diffie-hellman-group1-sha1, diffie-hellman-group14-sha1, diffie-hellman-group14-sha256 (default), diffie-hellman-group15-sha512 (default), diffie-hellman-group16-sha512 (default), diffie-hellman-group17-sha512 (default), diffie-hellman-group18-sha512 (default), diffie-hellman-group-exchange-sha1, diffie-hellman-group-exchange-sha256 (default), ecdh-sha2-nistp256 (default), ecdh-sha2-nistp384 (default), ecdh-sha2-nistp521 (default)


Compressions: none (default), zlib, zlib@openssh.com


Signatures: ssh-dss, ssh-dss-cert-v01@openssh.com, ssh-rsa (default), ssh-rsa-cert-v01@openssh.com, rsa-sha2-256 (default), rsa-sha2-256-cert-v01@openssh.com (default), rsa-sha2-512 (default), rsa-sha2-512-cert-v01@openssh.com (default), ecdsa-sha2-nistp256 (default), ecdsa-sha2-nistp256-cert-v01@openssh.com (default), ecdsa-sha2-nistp384 (default), ecdsa-sha2-nistp384-cert-v01@openssh.com (default), ecdsa-sha2-nistp521 (default), ecdsa-sha2-nistp521-cert-v01@openssh.com (default), sk-ecdsa-sha2-nistp256@openssh.com (default), ssh-ed25519 (default), ssh-ed25519-cert-v01@openssh.com (default), sk-ssh-ed25519@openssh.com (default)


To define the algorithms to be used:

1. On the [Startup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) tab of the profile with the SFTP Service or on the global [Startup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeserver/9.4/Content/Startup_Tab.htm) tab, set a system property with the values of algorithms you want to be used as on the example below:
2. Save the profile configuration.
3. Restart the profile.


To check which algorithms are enabled:

1. On the [Logger](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xeserver/Content/Logger_Tab.htm) tab of the profile with the SFTP Service, expand Advanced logger configuration.
2. In the Logger Name column, select FTPClient.
3. In the Log Level column, select Debug from the drop-down list.
4. Save the profile configuration and restart the profile.
5. On the System tab, expand your profile and double-click Log Viewer.
6. On the Outline tab, select \xes.ftp.client.log.
7. View the lists of enabled algorithms on the Log Viewer tab as on the example below:

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)


Example




```
...
| List of selected ciphers: chacha20-poly1305@openssh.com,aes128-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes256-ctr,aes128-gcm@openssh.com,aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-cbc,aes192-cbc,aes256-cbc
...
| List of selected KEX algorithms: curve25519-sha256,curve25519-sha256@libssh.org,curve448-sha512,ecdh-sha2-nistp521,ecdh-sha2-nistp384,ecdh-sha2-nistp256,diffie-hellman-group-exchange-sha256,diffie-hellman-group18-sha512,diffie-hellman-group17-sha512,diffie-hellman-group16-sha512,diffie-hellman-group15-sha512,diffie-hellman-group14-sha256
...
| List of selected MAC algorithms: hmac-sha2-256-etm@openssh.com,hmac-sha2-512-etm@openssh.com,hmac-sha1-etm@openssh.com,hmac-sha2-256,hmac-sha2-512,hmac-sha1
...
| List of selected compression algorithms: none,zlib,zlib@openssh.com
...
| List of selected signature algorithms: ecdsa-sha2-nistp256-cert-v01@openssh.com,ecdsa-sha2-nistp384-cert-v01@openssh.com,ecdsa-sha2-nistp521-cert-v01@openssh.com,rsa-sha2-512-cert-v01@openssh.com,rsa-sha2-256-cert-v01@openssh.com,ecdsa-sha2-nistp256,ecdsa-sha2-nistp384,ecdsa-sha2-nistp521,sk-ecdsa-sha2-nistp256@openssh.com,rsa-sha2-512,rsa-sha2-256,ssh-rsa
```






Note Only the algorithms that are considered safe to use are enabled by default. To find which algorithms have been deprecated, refer to the [Apache MINA SSHD Documentation](https://github.com/apache/mina-sshd#implementedavailable-support). If you still want to include deprecated algorithms, you can define them using system properties.
