<a id="salesforce-service"></a>

##### Salesforce Service

> 📄 Source: [Configure_Salesforce_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Salesforce_Service.htm)

###### Salesforce Service


With this service, you can establish a connection to Salesforce and select the API version to work with.


When the service is added to a profile, you can view the following settings:



###### General


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| API version | Select the Salesforce API version you are going to work with. For more information on getting the latest API version, see the [official documentation](https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/dome_versions.htm). |
| Authorization type | Select the type of authorization. For the OAuth type, fill the Client ID and the Client secret fields. For more information, see [Username-Password OAuth Authentication Flow](https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/intro_understanding_username_password_oauth_flow.htm). |
| Environment | Select the Salesforce environment you want to work with. Select Production to work with the default Salesforce environment or select Sandbox to connect to a [Salesforce Sandbox](https://www.salesforce.com/ap/products/platform/products/sandbox/). |
| Username | Enter your account login. If you select to work with a Salesforce sandbox, you must append ".<sandboxName>" string to your user name, for example user@company.com.testSandbox where "testSandbox" is the name of the Salesforce sandbox. |
| Password | Enter a password for your account. |
| Security token | Enter your account security token. This token is issued by Salesforce to allow third-party software to connect to the Salesforce REST API. You can reset the token on the Personal Settings page at the Salesforce portal. |
| Client ID | Required for the OAuth authorization type. Enter your Salesforce consumer key. |
| Client secret | Required for the OAuth authorization type. Enter your Salesforce consumer secret. |
| Load Credential | Click to select the Salesforce account that is used by the component. By default, the configuration file with available accounts is located at ${ECRootPath}/Common/SalesforceConfig.json. For more information, see [Import Salesforce Credentials](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Import_Credential.htm). Note Loading credentials overwrites all values on the Connection tab. |
| Check Authentication | Click to verify the correctness of the entered credentials. |



For more information on getting your Salesforce security token, consumer key, and consumer secret, click the [link](https://help.salesforce.com/apex/HTViewSolution?id=000205876&language=en_US).


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="kafka-service"></a>

##### Kafka Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Kafka_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Kafka_Service.htm)

###### Kafka Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="kafka-service-overview"></a>

##### Kafka Service Overview

> 📄 Source: [Kafka_Service_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Service_Overview.htm)

###### Kafka Service Overview


Apache Kafka is a distributed commit log service. It provides the functionality of a messaging system, like JMS, but has several advantages over traditional JMS. By using the Kafka service, you can exchange data through Kafka clusters. Producers submit their data to Kafka cluster topics - logical categories, to which all messages are published. The data is stored in the cluster for a configurable amount of time and is available for consumers for reading.


By using the XEServer Kafka services, you can interact with Kafka clusters, submit, and read data from them. XEServer has two services to work with Kafka brokers:

- [Kafka Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Service.htm): Implements Kafka API version 0.8.2 and can be used to work with Kafka broker version 0.8.2.0 and later. This service does not support SSL, SASL, or any other feature implemented in Kafka broker versions later than 0.8.2.0.
- [Kafka Service 2](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_2_Service.htm): Implements the Kafka API version 2.1.0 and can be used to work with Kafka broker version 0.10.2 and later. The service supports the SSL and SASL mechanisms to provide a secure connection with Kafka clusters.


The XEServer Kafka services support [Apache Kafka 3.6.0](https://archive.apache.org/dist/kafka/3.6.0/RELEASE_NOTES.html). To start exchanging data through Kafka clusters, configure XEServer [Kafka services](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Service.htm) and [Kafka endpoints](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Inbound.htm).

---

<a id="kafka-service"></a>

##### Kafka Service

> 📄 Source: [Kafka_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Service.htm)

###### Kafka Service


With the XEServer Kafka service, you can interact with Kafka brokers through the XEServer Kafka endpoints.


Note This service implements the Kafka API version 0.8.2 and can be used to work with Kafka broker version 0.8.2.0 and later. This service does not support SSL, SASL, or any other feature implemented in Kafka broker versions later than 0.8.2.0. To use the SSL and SASL mechanisms, use XEServer [Kafka 2](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_2_Service.htm) service.


When you add the service to a profile, you can view the following settings:



To see the list of macros supported for this component, see [XEServer supported macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm#Kafka).


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Consumer Settings Tab


This tab allows you to configure the Kafka Consumer side. Here you can specify connectivity parameters to allow the Kafka Inbound components to read messages from Kafka clusters.


All connectivity parameters are specified as <propertyName> - <propertyValue> pairs. To find the list of all available Kafka consumer properties with their possible values, see [Kafka Consumer Properties](http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#consumerconfigs). You can specify these properties in a separate file in the form of pairs and specify the path to this file in the Consumer properties file field. Alternatively, you can define the consumer properties in the Name - Value EAM interface.


Note that the default consumer settings contain the property read.properties.from.key = true which means that Kafka message keys are going to be mapped to XEServer message properties.


The following consumer properties are mandatory for XEServer Kafka Service:


| Property Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| zookeeper.connect | A string that contains the host name and port number of the ZooKeeper server to connect in the following format: <HostName>:<PortNumber> . You can specify multiple host-port pairs to allow the XEServer Kafka Service to connect through other ZooKeeper nodes if one of ZooKeeper machines is down. Example hostName1:portNumber1,hostName2:portNumber3,hostName3:portNumber3 |



###### Producer Settings Tab


This tab allows you to configure the Kafka Producer side. Here you can specify connectivity parameters to allow the Kafka Outbound components to publish messages to Kafka clusters.


All connectivity parameters are specified as <propertyName> - <propertyValue> pairs. To find the list of all available Kafka producer properties with their possible values, see [Kafka Producer Properties](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#producerconfigs). You can specify these properties in a separate file in the form of pairs and specify the path to this file in the Producer properties file field. Alternatively, you can define the producer properties in the Name - Value EAM interface.


Note that the default producer settings contain the property write.properties.to.key = true which means that XEServer message properties are going to be serialized to Kafka message keys.


The following producer configuration properties are mandatory for XEServer Kafka Service:


| Property Name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| bootstrap.servers | A list of Host:Port pairs required to establish a connection to the Kafka cluster. Your data is distributed evenly among all the available cluster servers, irrespective of the servers specified in the list - this list affects only the initial hosts to discover the full set of servers. The list should have the following format: host1:port1,host2:port2,hostN:portN,.... Since the specified servers are only used for the initial connection to discover all the cluster members (which may change dynamically), you do not have to specify the full set of servers in this list. However, we recommend that you specify more than one cluster server in this list as the server may become temporary unavailable. If none of the specified servers is available, the data is not sent until at least one server from the list becomes available. The default value is "localhost:9092." |



Note The property values, defined in the EAM Name-Value interface override the values defined in an external file.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="kafka-service-2"></a>

##### Kafka Service 2

> 📄 Source: [Kafka_2_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_2_Service.htm)

###### Kafka Service 2


With the XEServer Kafka Service 2, you can interact with Kafka brokers through the XEServer Kafka endpoints.


Note This service implements the Kafka API version 2.1.0 and can be used to work with Kafka broker version 0.10.2 and later. The service supports the SSL and SASL mechanisms to provide a secure connection with Kafka clusters.


When you add the service to a profile, you can view the following settings:



To see the list of macros supported for this component, see [XEServer supported macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm#Kafka).


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select the service status. The following options are available: Enabled: The service starts together with the profile.Using the [Expression Language](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Expression_Language.htm), you can specify a startup condition that allows you to start a service based on a condition. If the startup condition evaluates to `true`, the service starts together with the profile. The startup condition can use only system macros (`${propName}`). For example, you can enable a service by checking for the environment property (`${testStartupProperty}='foo'`). Disabled: The service does not start with the profile. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Consumer Settings Tab


This tab allows you to configure the Kafka Consumer side. Here you can specify connectivity parameters to allow the Kafka Inbound components to read messages from Kafka clusters.


All connectivity parameters are specified as <propertyName> - <propertyValue> pairs. To find the list of all available Kafka consumer properties with their possible values, see [Kafka Consumer Properties](http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#consumerconfigs). You can specify these properties in a separate file in the form of pairs and specify the path to this file in the Consumer properties file field. Alternatively, you can define the consumer properties in the Name - Value EAM interface.


Note that the default consumer settings contain the property read.properties.from.key = true which means that Kafka message keys are going to be mapped to XEServer message properties.


###### Producer Settings Tab


This tab allows you to configure the Kafka Producer side. Here you can specify connectivity parameters to allow the Kafka Outbound components to publish messages to Kafka clusters.


All connectivity parameters are specified as <propertyName> - <propertyValue> pairs. To find the list of all available Kafka producer properties with their possible values, see [Kafka Producer Properties](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#producerconfigs). You can specify these properties in a separate file in the form of pairs and specify the path to this file in the Producer properties file field. Alternatively, you can define the producer properties in the Name - Value EAM interface.


Note that the default producer settings contain the property write.properties.to.key = true which means that XEServer message properties are going to be serialized to Kafka message keys.


The following property is mandatory for both Consumer and Producer settings of XEServer Kafka 2 service:


| Configuration Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| bootstrap.servers | A list of Host:Port pairs required to establish a connection to the Kafka cluster. Your data is distributed evenly among all the available cluster servers, irrespective of the servers specified in the list - this list affects only the initial hosts to discover the full set of servers. The list should have the following format: host1:port1,host2:port2,hostN:portN,.... Since the specified servers are only used for the initial connection to discover all the cluster members (which may change dynamically), you do not have to specify the full set of servers in this list. However, we recommend that you specify more than one cluster server in this list as the server may become temporary unavailable. If none of the specified servers is available, the data is not sent until at least one server from the list becomes available. The default value is "localhost:9092." |



Note The properties defined in the EAM Name-Value interface override the properties defined in an external file.


###### SSL Tab


Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead.


On this tab, you can enable the SSL mechanism for secure communication between XEServer and Kafka clusters.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Enable hostname validation | Select this option to verify the specified host name for validity. |
| Key Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to the file that contains Keystore certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the private key. |
| Trusted Store |   |
| Path | Enter the path to a Keystore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Truststore. |



###### SASL Tab


On this tab, you can enable the [SASL](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc4422) mechanism for secure communication between XEServer and the Kafka clusters. The following authentication types are supported:

- GSSAPI: Performs authentication using the Kerberos protocol and the [Krb5LoginModule](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/jre/api/security/jaas/spec/com/sun/security/auth/module/Krb5LoginModule.html) login module.
- OAUTHBEARER: An OAuth-based authentication mechanism that performs authentication using the [OAuthBearerLoginModule](https://docs.confluent.io/5.0.1/clients/javadocs/org/apache/kafka/common/security/oauthbearer/OAuthBearerLoginModule.html). For more information, see [Use OAUTHBEARER](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#security_sasl_oauthbearer_prod).
- PLAIN: Performs authentication using plain text user name and password.Note If you select to use this authentication type, we recommend that you use it in combination with the enabled SSL option (see SSL tab).
- SCRAM-SHA-256, SCRAM-SHA-512: Perform authentication using an encrypted user name and password.


The following table lists the configuration properties available for each authentication type:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| GSSAPI | |
| sasl.jaas.config | A complex property that consists of several [JAAS](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/technotes/guides/security/jaas/tutorials/GeneralAcnOnly.html) configuration properties:Login Module: The fully qualified Java class name of the [Krb5LoginModule](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/jre/api/security/jaas/spec/com/sun/security/auth/module/Krb5LoginModule.html) module.Login Module Success is: Defines the login module behavior depending on login success.Login Module optionsuseKeyTab: Set this property to true if you want the module to get the principal's key from the keytab. If the keytab is not set, the module locates the keytab from the Kerberos configuration file. If the keytab is not specified in the Kerberos configuration file, the module attempts to use the file {user.home}{file.separator}krb5.keytab.storeKey: Set this property to true if you want the keytab or the principal's key to be stored in the Subject's private credentials. If the isInitiator property is set to false, and the principal is "*", the stored keytab can be used by anyone. Otherwise, the keytab is restricted to be used only by the specified principal.keyTab: The name of the keytab file used to get the principal's secret key.principal: The name of the principal that should be used. This may be a simple user name (for example, "testuser") or a service name (for example, "host/testhost.eng.sun.com"). You can use the principal property to set the principal when there are credentials for several principals in the keytab, or when you want a specific ticket cache. The principal can also be set using the system property sun.security.krb5.principal. If this property is not set, then the principal name from the configuration file is used. The principal name can be set to "*" (only if the isInitiator property is set to false). In this case, the acceptor is not bound to a single principal. |
| sasl.kerberos.service.name | The Kerberos principal name that Kafka runs as. |
| sasl.kerberos.kinit.cmd | The Kerberos [kinit](https://web.mit.edu/kerberos/krb5-1.12/doc/user/user_commands/kinit.html) command path. |
| sasl.kerberos.min.time.before.relogin | The time interval between login attempts. |
| sasl.kerberos.ticket.renew.jitter | The percentage of random jitter added to the renewal time. |
| OAUTHBEARER | |
| sasl.jaas.config | A complex property that consists of several [JAAS](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/technotes/guides/security/jaas/tutorials/GeneralAcnOnly.html) configuration properties:Login Module: The fully qualified Java class name of the [OAuthBearerLoginModule](https://docs.confluent.io/5.0.1/clients/javadocs/org/apache/kafka/common/security/oauthbearer/OAuthBearerLoginModule.html) login module.Login Module Success is: Defines the login module behavior depending on the success of the login operation. |
| sasl.login.callback.handler.class | The fully qualified name of an SASL login callback handler class that implements the AuthenticateCallbackHandler interface. |
| PLAIN | |
| sasl.jaas.config | A complex property that consists of several [JAAS](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/technotes/guides/security/jaas/tutorials/GeneralAcnOnly.html) configuration properties:Login Module: The fully qualified Java class name of the [PlainLoginModule](https://kafka.apache.org/21/javadoc/index.html?org/apache/kafka/common/security/plain/PlainLoginModule.html) module.Login Module Success is: Defines the login module behavior depending on the success of the login operation.Login Module optionsusername: The user name required for authentication.password: The user password. |
| sasl.client.callback.handler.class | The fully qualified name of the SASL client callback handler class that implements the AuthenticateCallbackHandler interface. |
| SCRAM-SHA-256, SCRAM-SHA-512 | |
| sasl.jaas.config | A complex property that consists of several [JAAS](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/technotes/guides/security/jaas/tutorials/GeneralAcnOnly.html) configuration properties:Login Module: The fully qualified Java class name of the [ScramLoginModule](https://kafka.apache.org/20/javadoc/index.html?org/apache/kafka/common/security/scram/ScramLoginModule.html) module.Login Module Success is: Defines the login module behavior depending on the success of the login operation.Login Module optionsusername: The user name required for authentication.password: The user password. |



###### Other authentication types


You can use SASL mechanism types not supported by the Kafka Service 2 by default. To do this, put a .jar file with the authentication library to ${XESRoot}\platform\lib.user\kafka and provide configuration properties like `sasl.mechanism` and `sasl.jaas.config` on the SASL tab. For exact list of properties and their values, refer to the documentation for the authentication mechanism you are going to use.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="kafka-configuration-properties"></a>

##### Kafka Configuration Properties

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Kafka_Configuration_Properties.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Kafka_Configuration_Properties.htm)

###### XEServer Kafka Configuration Properties


The full list of supported properties is available at [Apache Kafka official documentation](http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#configuration) page. You can define these properties on the [Kafka Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_Service.htm) and [Kafka Service 2](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Kafka_2_Service.htm) pages.

<a id="restful-web-service-overview"></a>

##### Restful Web Service Overview

> 📄 Source: [RESTful_Web_Service_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RESTful_Web_Service_Overview.htm)

###### Restful Web Service Overview


The XEServer RESTful Web Service allows you to spin up a [Jetty](https://www.eclipse.org/jetty/)-powered HTTP web service to handle HTTP requests. You can decide which HTTP methods you would like your server to support. When the service receives an HTTP request, the service directs the request data to a route for processing, and after the data is processed, the service returns an HTTP response to the caller. For this, the [RESTful Web Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Restful_Web_Service.htm) uses the following route components:

- [RESTful Web Service Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RESTful_Web_Service_Inbound.htm). Used to accept request data on a route. When the service receives an HTTP request, the request data goes to the corresponding inbound component identified by the relative path of the request URL. The component creates a new XEServer message populating the message with data from the HTTP request. If an HTTP request has a body (for example, POST), the request body is mapped to the XEServer message body. You can [configure the mapping](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RESTful_Web_Service_Inbound.htm#RequestMapping) of the request parameters (query parameters, cookies, request headers) to XEServer message properties. Tip You can have a single Restful Web Service associated with several RESTful Web Service Inbound route components. Using the Relative Path option, each inbound component represents a separate RESTful endpoint (for example, {BaseURL}/getAllItems, {BaseURL}/insertItem, {BaseURL}/deleteItem and so on).
- [RESTful Web Service Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RESTful_Web_Service_Outbound.htm). Used to form an HTTP response. When the route processing reaches the outbound component, the component returns the corresponding HTTP response to caller.


The [RESTful Web Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Restful_Web_Service.htm) supports SSL-protected communication over HTTPS and allows you to use the HTTP Basic, HTTP Digest, and OAuth 2.0 authentication mechanisms.

---

<a id="restful-web-service"></a>

##### Restful Web Service

> 📄 Source: [Restful_Web_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Restful_Web_Service.htm)

###### RESTful Web Service


Apart from generic service settings, Restful Web Service has the following settings:


*[Image: Restful Web Service]*


###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Path | Specify a base RESTful URL to access the web service. |
| Session Timeout | Specify the timeout in milliseconds within which the service must return an HTTP response to the caller. If a route does not provide a response within this timeout time, the service returns the response 503 Service Unavailable. |
| Allowed HTTP Methods | Select the HTTP methods that you want your web service to support. |



To see the list of macros supported by Restful Web Service and Restful route components, see [XEServer Macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Route_Macros.htm#Restful).


###### Jetty Tab


The service is built on top of the [Jetty](https://www.eclipse.org/jetty/) web server (version 10.0.16) and has the following Jetty settings:


Note In XEServer 9.3.3, Jetty server library has been updated to the version 10.0.16 to address security vulnerabilities. If you used XEServer components based on Jetty server, view the [backward compatibility instructions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm) you might need to follow when upgrading to XEServer 9.3.3 and later.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Jetty Server | Select the type of Jetty server to use. In the Platform mode, the service uses a default instance of a Jetty web server that can be used by several XEServer services. This Jetty server is preconfigured and most of the settings are hidden. In the Dedicated mode, a separate instance of a Jetty web server starts together with the service. This approach is more flexible as it allows you to provide specific settings for your web service (HTTP host and port, SSL settings, HTTP Authentication, and so on). |
| HTTP options | |
| Host | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| Port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Thread pool size | The maximum number of process threads the service can handle simultaneously to process incoming requests. |
| SSL | |
| Enable SSL | Select to true/false to enable/disable the Secure Sockets Layer to provide secure access to your web service. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. You can also use macro expressions to enabled/disable connections through the SSL protocol. |
| Enable SNI host check | When the hostname check is disabled (false) in Server Name Indication (SNI), you will not receive a 400 Invalid SNI response if the `Host` header from the request does not match the Common Name (CN) or Subject Alternative Name (SAN) values in the Jetty server certificate. For more information, click [here](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm#Host_check). Default value: false. Note Starting with EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value has been changed to false. Prior to EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value was true. |
|  KeystoreA keystore contains cryptographic keys (private and public key pairs) used to verify the identity of parties that send requests to your web service. | |
| Path | Enter the path to the KeyStore file that contains the key pair entries. |
| Password | Enter the password to the KeyStore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the key pair. |
|  TruststoreA truststore contains digital certificates of the connecting parties to verify their identity. | |
| Enable client authentication | Select to enable the client authentication. If the client authentication is enabled, for incoming request Jetty asks the client to provide a digital certificate, and then compares the provided certificate with those stored in the truststore. If the provided certificate is present in the truststore, the client authentication succeeds. |
| Path | Enter the path to the TrustedStore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the TrustedStore. |
| Check certificate expiration | If set to True, the expiration date of the trust certificate is checked and an exception is thrown if an expired certificate is encountered. |
|  Authentication | |
| Type | The service supports the following authentication options: [Basic](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7617): In this mode, the Base64-encoded user name and password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. [Digest](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7616): In this mode, the plain text user name and a hashed password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. OAuth: In this mode, RESTful Web Service authenticates users by verifying JWT tokens within the Authorization header. **Note** The Authentication Type can also be defined as an environment property in the profile's Environment tab and used as a macro for specifying the authorization type. For example, create the environment property AuthType=Basic, and use the ${AuthType} macro as option for the Type field. |
| Audit | In this section, you can enable the generation of audit events by the Jetty web server. In Service name, specify the name of the XEServer [Audit Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Service.htm) responsible for handling the audit events. |



###### OAuth Authentication Type

 Click to view OAuth Settings


| Field | Option |
| --- | --- |
| Confirmation (cnf) claim XEServer implementation of the Jetty server supports [OAuth 2.0 mutual Transport Layer Security (TLS) authentication with X.509 certificates](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705). Mutual TLS (mTLS) is an authentication type where both the server and client have a certificate and both authenticate each other using the TLS protocol. Such an authentication method provides an additional layer of security and helps prevent various kinds of attacks, such as credential stuffing, malicious API requests, and others. For the Jetty server (resource server) to verify the legitimacy of the client that sends a token, authorization server embeds the certificate hash within a JWT-formatted access token as a cnf ("confirmation") claim when client connects to authorization server. The following is the cnf claim example: Example of "cnf" claim in certificate-bound token ``` { ... "client_id": "6344df61-2foo-4429-9557-6b2979a138f7", "cnf": { "x5t#S256": "Avc6NtXrtcjJsf0zZacl2MCETnUOMu59cz3H4ohh4-o" } ... } ``` The resource server then compares this certificate hash to a hash of the client certificate used for authentication. If the hashes do not match, the request is rejected. Note [Mutual-TLS endpoint aliases](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705#name-metadata-for-mutual-tls-end) are not supported in this release of XEServer. In the Confirmation (cnf) claim section of OAuth authentication type settings, you can configure the Jetty-based XEServer service to request the cnf claim in access tokens for verification. | |
| Confirmation claim verification | Select Disabled to disable verification of the cnf claim on the resource server (Jetty server). Select Enabled to force verification of the cnf claim. If token does not contain the cnf claim or certificate hashes do not match, access is rejected. Select If present to verify the cnf claim only if it is present in access token. |
| Certificate source | Connection: For successful authentication, the resource server matches the client certificate used in the access token request during the connection's handshake TLS session against the client certificate configured on the resource server. HTTP header: If a client performs the access token request through a proxy server or load balancer, the resource server uses the request header as the source of client certificate for matching against the configured client certificate. You must provide the name of the Header and the Certificate encoding (URL-encoded, Base64, Thumbprint) of the certificate sent in the request. |
| Token validation method | Select how you want the service to verify JWT tokens: By signature: The service calculates its own signature and compares it with the signature in the token. Service endpoint: Delegates the token verification to XEConnect Authorization service. |
| Token validation method: By signature By using a public key (for RSA) or a secret (for HMAC), the service creates its own signature from the token header and payload. If this signature matches the signature in the token, the token is considered valid. For this method, you should specify a signature algorithm to use and provide a public key or a secret. You can provide a public key from the following sources: you can import a PEM public key from a file. you can specify a JWKS URL that points to the authorization server (for example, XEConnect Authorization service) that issued the token. To access the authorization server, you have to specify your client ID and client secret. As a response, the authorization server returns a public key to verify the token. | |
| Token clock skew | The JWT verification process requires time synchronization on the computer that issued the token and the computer that verifies the token. This option specifies a time leeway to compensate for the possible clock skew on the authorization server (XEConnect Authorization service) and on the computer that verifies the token (XEServer RESTful Web service). For example, an authorization server issues a JWT token at 12:45 UTC, however the clock on the computer that verifies the token is out of sync and shows 12:47 UTC. In this case, the token fails the validation and is marked expired. However, you can compensate for this timing mismatch by setting the property to 180 (3*60s) or more. Tip For security reasons, Edifecs recommends that you do not use a large (hours, days) token skew intervals. |
| Signature algorithm | Select an algorithm to use to verify the token signature. |
| Token validation method: Service endpoint In this mode, XEServer delegates token verification to the XEConnect Authorization service. The service requests XEConnect to verify the token and passes the client ID and the client secret as parameters. The connection to XEConnect must be secured with SSL. | |
| Introspection URL | Specify the [introspection](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7662) URL of the XEConnect Authorization service that performs token verification. You have to specify the corresponding client ID and the client secret as only clients registered in XEConnect [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) can access the introspection endpoint. |
| Client ID | The ID of the client registered in [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) for whom the token has been issued. |
| Secret key | The secret key to protect the client's identity. |
| Required claims | In this section, you can specify the [claims](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4) to be verified in the token. If a claim specified in this pane is not available in the token, the request authorization fails.Tip When you define a new token claim to be verified, you can select from the drop down list of three claims: [Audience](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), [Issuer](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), and [Subject](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.2). However, you can specify custom claims to be verified. The claim's name and value are case-sensitive and should be the same as they appear in the token. |







###### HTTP Headers Size


By default, Jetty-based XEServer services can handle HTTP requests and responses with headers size up to 8KB. To change this limit, you can declare the following properties on the [Profile Startup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) or [Profile Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) tabs. The property values must be specified in bytes.

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`: Use to set the same limit for both request and response HTTP headers.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.request.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP request headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.response.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP response headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.


For example, the following property sets the maximum request/response HTTP headers size to 64KB:

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)

```
com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size=65536
```






###### Custom HTTP Headers


XEServer Jetty-based services can return custom HTTP headers. For this, set the following XEServer environment properties and restart the corresponding profile:

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.headers.enabled=true`: enables the custom headers feature.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.header.<headerName>=<headerValue>`: forces Jetty web server to return <headerName>=<headerValue> HTTP header in each response.


Tip Use [global environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) to force all Jetty-based services in all profiles to send the specified HTTP headers. Alternatively, you can use [profile-level environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) to affect Jetty behavior only within a specific profile.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="task-reporter"></a>

##### Task Reporter

> 📄 Source: [Book_Task_Reporter.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Task_Reporter.htm)

###### Task Reporter


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="select-a-report-template"></a>

##### Select a Report Template

> 📄 Source: [Select_a_Report_Template.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Select_a_Report_Template.htm)

###### Select a Report Template


When configuring Task Reporter output, you should select the template for the output report.


To select the report template:

- In the Route Viewer, click Task Reporter component you want to configure.
- On the Route component properties tab, click Outputs.
- In the Output properties section, click Browse next to the Report template box. The File Path dialog box is displayed.
- In the File box, enter the corresponding file path, or select the one from the List of files box.
- Click OK. The report template is selected.

---

<a id="web-service"></a>

##### Web Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Web_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Web_Service.htm)

###### Web Services


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="web-services-overview"></a>

##### Web Services Overview

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_WebService_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_WebService_Overview.htm)

###### Web Services Overview


XEServer Web Services are used to get data from remote users, process them inside the XES route, and return processing results. This is done with the help of new WS connectors feature.


6x profiles are not supported. Web services use only one method, which processes the message containing a header and a body.


XEServer Web Service technology supports the following features:

- Web Service connectivity service. For more information, see [Configure Web Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Web_Service.htm) and [Web Service Endpoints](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Endpoints.htm).
- Web Service Call route component. For more information, see [Web Service Call](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Call_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="web-service-interface"></a>

##### Web Service Interface

> 📄 Source: [Web_Service_Interface.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Interface.htm)

###### Web Service Interface


The Web Service interface sample is following:


*[Image: Web Service Interface]*


The following example is the WSDL file sample that is needed for the user to build his own client:


Example


<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>


<wsdl:definitions name="XESProcessorService" targetNamespace="http://ws.xeserver.etools.edifecs.com"


xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/wsdl/soap/" xmlns:soap12="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/wsdl/soap12/"


xmlns:tns="http://ws.xeserver.etools.edifecs.com" xmlns:wsdl="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/wsdl/"


xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">


<wsdl:types>


<xs:schema attributeFormDefault="unqualified" elementFormDefault="unqualified"


targetNamespace="http://ws.xeserver.etools.edifecs.com" xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/wsdl/soap/"


xmlns:soap12="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/wsdl/soap12/" xmlns:tns="http://ws.xeserver.etools.edifecs.com"


xmlns:wsdl="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/wsdl/" xmlns:xs="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema"


xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">


<xs:element name="process" type="tns:process" />


<xs:element name="processResponse" type="tns:processResponse" />


<xs:complexType name="process">


<xs:sequence>


<xs:element maxOccurs="unbounded" minOccurs="1" name="message" type="tns:message" />


</xs:sequence>


</xs:complexType>


<xs:complexType name="message">


<xs:sequence>


<xs:element name="body" ns1:expectedContentTypes="application/octet-stream"


type="xs:base64Binary" xmlns:ns1="http://www.w3.org/2005/05/xmlmime" />


<xs:element maxOccurs="unbounded" minOccurs="0" name="headers" type="tns:header" />


</xs:sequence>


</xs:complexType>


<xs:complexType name="header">


<xs:sequence>


<xs:element name="name" type="xs:string" />


<xs:element name="value" type="xs:string" />


</xs:sequence>


</xs:complexType>


<xs:complexType name="processResponse">


<xs:sequence>


<xs:element maxOccurs="unbounded" minOccurs="0" name="message" type="tns:message" />


</xs:sequence>


</xs:complexType>


<xs:complexType name="faultDetail">


<xs:sequence>


<xs:element name="reason" type="xs:string" />


</xs:sequence>


</xs:complexType>


<xs:element name="ProcessingFault" type="tns:faultDetail" />


</xs:schema>


</wsdl:types>


<wsdl:message name="ProcessingFault">


<wsdl:part element="tns:ProcessingFault" name="ProcessingFault">


</wsdl:part>


</wsdl:message>


<wsdl:message name="processResponse">


<wsdl:part element="tns:processResponse" name="parameters">


</wsdl:part>


</wsdl:message>


<wsdl:message name="process">


<wsdl:part element="tns:process" name="parameters">


</wsdl:part>


</wsdl:message>


<wsdl:portType name="XESProcessor">


<wsdl:operation name="process">


<wsdl:input message="tns:process" name="process">


</wsdl:input>


<wsdl:output message="tns:processResponse" name="processResponse">


</wsdl:output>


<wsdl:fault message="tns:ProcessingFault" name="ProcessingFault">


</wsdl:fault>


</wsdl:operation>


</wsdl:portType>


<wsdl:binding name="XESProcessorServiceSoap12" type="tns:XESProcessor">


<soap12:binding style="document" transport="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/http" />


<wsdl:operation name="process">


<soap12:operation soapAction="" style="document" />


<wsdl:input name="process">


<soap12:body use="literal" />


</wsdl:input>


<wsdl:output name="processResponse">


<soap12:body use="literal" />


</wsdl:output>


<wsdl:fault name="ProcessingFault">


<soap12:fault name="ProcessingFault" use="literal" />


</wsdl:fault>


</wsdl:operation>


</wsdl:binding>


<wsdl:service name="XESProcessorService">


<wsdl:port binding="tns:XESProcessorServiceSoap12" name="XESProcessorPort">


<soap:address location="http://localhost:9094/ws" />


</wsdl:port>


</wsdl:service>


</wsdl:definitions>

---

<a id="configure-web-service"></a>

##### Configure Web Service

> 📄 Source: [Configure_Web_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Web_Service.htm)

###### Configure Web Service


XEServer Web Service allows you to spin up a Jetty-based web server that can receive requests from remote client applications and return responses through HTTP(S). When the service receives a client request, the service passes the request data to an XEServer route using the component [Web Service Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Inbound.htm). After the route processes the request data, the processed data can be returned to the caller using the route component [Web Service Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Outbound.htm).


The service has the following settings:


*[Image: Web Servise General settings]*


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can set the following properties:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Enable MTOM(Message Transmission Optimization Mechanism) | When the property is set to true, you can work in the mode that allows you to process large volumes of data. |
| Processing timeout | The time limit for processing. |
| WS path | The path of the Web Service, to which the request should be sent. If the Web Service value is ws, the path is http://localhost:{http port}/ws |
| Target Namespace | Use this property to specify a custom target namespace for the web service. Note You should use the same target namespace if you have multiple XEServer Web Service services within one XEServer profile. By default, the property is hidden and the default target namespace "https://ws.xeserver.etools.edifecs.com" is used. To add the property to the service, click New and select the property from the drop-down list. |



###### Security Tab


You can configure security settings for synchronous processing by using Web Service. They are used to verify the login and password located in the UsernameToken field of the SOAP message header.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Enable WS security** | Select to enable UsernameToken authentication for the Web Service. |
| Password Type | Select the type of data format for sending a password to a server. The following options are available: Digest: The hashed password to be used for the Web Service Security. Text: The password in plaintext to be used for the Web Service Security. |
| **Name** | Enter the login name to be used for the Web Service. |
| **Value** | Enter the password to be used for the Web Service. |



###### Jetty Tab


The service is built on top of the [Jetty](https://www.eclipse.org/jetty/) web server (version 10.0.16) and has the following Jetty settings:


Note In XEServer 9.3.3, Jetty server library has been updated to the version 10.0.16 to address security vulnerabilities. If you used XEServer components based on Jetty server, view the [backward compatibility instructions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm) you might need to follow when upgrading to XEServer 9.3.3 and later.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Jetty Server | Select the type of Jetty server to use. In the Platform mode, the service uses a default instance of a Jetty web server that can be used by several XEServer services. This Jetty server is preconfigured and most of the settings are hidden. In the Dedicated mode, a separate instance of a Jetty web server starts together with the service. This approach is more flexible as it allows you to provide specific settings for your web service (HTTP host and port, SSL settings, HTTP Authentication, and so on). |
| HTTP options | |
| Host | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| Port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Thread pool size | The maximum number of process threads the service can handle simultaneously to process incoming requests. |
| SSL | |
| Enable SSL | Select to true/false to enable/disable the Secure Sockets Layer to provide secure access to your web service. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. You can also use macro expressions to enabled/disable connections through the SSL protocol. |
| Enable SNI host check | When the hostname check is disabled (false) in Server Name Indication (SNI), you will not receive a 400 Invalid SNI response if the `Host` header from the request does not match the Common Name (CN) or Subject Alternative Name (SAN) values in the Jetty server certificate. For more information, click [here](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm#Host_check). Default value: false. Note Starting with EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value has been changed to false. Prior to EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value was true. |
|  KeystoreA keystore contains cryptographic keys (private and public key pairs) used to verify the identity of parties that send requests to your web service. | |
| Path | Enter the path to the KeyStore file that contains the key pair entries. |
| Password | Enter the password to the KeyStore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the key pair. |
|  TruststoreA truststore contains digital certificates of the connecting parties to verify their identity. | |
| Enable client authentication | Select to enable the client authentication. If the client authentication is enabled, for incoming request Jetty asks the client to provide a digital certificate, and then compares the provided certificate with those stored in the truststore. If the provided certificate is present in the truststore, the client authentication succeeds. |
| Path | Enter the path to the TrustedStore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the TrustedStore. |
| Check certificate expiration | If set to True, the expiration date of the trust certificate is checked and an exception is thrown if an expired certificate is encountered. |
|  Authentication | |
| Type | The service supports the following authentication options: [Basic](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7617): In this mode, the Base64-encoded user name and password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. [Digest](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7616): In this mode, the plain text user name and a hashed password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. OAuth: In this mode, RESTful Web Service authenticates users by verifying JWT tokens within the Authorization header. **Note** The Authentication Type can also be defined as an environment property in the profile's Environment tab and used as a macro for specifying the authorization type. For example, create the environment property AuthType=Basic, and use the ${AuthType} macro as option for the Type field. |
| Audit | In this section, you can enable the generation of audit events by the Jetty web server. In Service name, specify the name of the XEServer [Audit Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Service.htm) responsible for handling the audit events. |



###### OAuth Authentication Type

 Click to view OAuth Settings


| Field | Option |
| --- | --- |
| Confirmation (cnf) claim XEServer implementation of the Jetty server supports [OAuth 2.0 mutual Transport Layer Security (TLS) authentication with X.509 certificates](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705). Mutual TLS (mTLS) is an authentication type where both the server and client have a certificate and both authenticate each other using the TLS protocol. Such an authentication method provides an additional layer of security and helps prevent various kinds of attacks, such as credential stuffing, malicious API requests, and others. For the Jetty server (resource server) to verify the legitimacy of the client that sends a token, authorization server embeds the certificate hash within a JWT-formatted access token as a cnf ("confirmation") claim when client connects to authorization server. The following is the cnf claim example: Example of "cnf" claim in certificate-bound token ``` { ... "client_id": "6344df61-2foo-4429-9557-6b2979a138f7", "cnf": { "x5t#S256": "Avc6NtXrtcjJsf0zZacl2MCETnUOMu59cz3H4ohh4-o" } ... } ``` The resource server then compares this certificate hash to a hash of the client certificate used for authentication. If the hashes do not match, the request is rejected. Note [Mutual-TLS endpoint aliases](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705#name-metadata-for-mutual-tls-end) are not supported in this release of XEServer. In the Confirmation (cnf) claim section of OAuth authentication type settings, you can configure the Jetty-based XEServer service to request the cnf claim in access tokens for verification. | |
| Confirmation claim verification | Select Disabled to disable verification of the cnf claim on the resource server (Jetty server). Select Enabled to force verification of the cnf claim. If token does not contain the cnf claim or certificate hashes do not match, access is rejected. Select If present to verify the cnf claim only if it is present in access token. |
| Certificate source | Connection: For successful authentication, the resource server matches the client certificate used in the access token request during the connection's handshake TLS session against the client certificate configured on the resource server. HTTP header: If a client performs the access token request through a proxy server or load balancer, the resource server uses the request header as the source of client certificate for matching against the configured client certificate. You must provide the name of the Header and the Certificate encoding (URL-encoded, Base64, Thumbprint) of the certificate sent in the request. |
| Token validation method | Select how you want the service to verify JWT tokens: By signature: The service calculates its own signature and compares it with the signature in the token. Service endpoint: Delegates the token verification to XEConnect Authorization service. |
| Token validation method: By signature By using a public key (for RSA) or a secret (for HMAC), the service creates its own signature from the token header and payload. If this signature matches the signature in the token, the token is considered valid. For this method, you should specify a signature algorithm to use and provide a public key or a secret. You can provide a public key from the following sources: you can import a PEM public key from a file. you can specify a JWKS URL that points to the authorization server (for example, XEConnect Authorization service) that issued the token. To access the authorization server, you have to specify your client ID and client secret. As a response, the authorization server returns a public key to verify the token. | |
| Token clock skew | The JWT verification process requires time synchronization on the computer that issued the token and the computer that verifies the token. This option specifies a time leeway to compensate for the possible clock skew on the authorization server (XEConnect Authorization service) and on the computer that verifies the token (XEServer RESTful Web service). For example, an authorization server issues a JWT token at 12:45 UTC, however the clock on the computer that verifies the token is out of sync and shows 12:47 UTC. In this case, the token fails the validation and is marked expired. However, you can compensate for this timing mismatch by setting the property to 180 (3*60s) or more. Tip For security reasons, Edifecs recommends that you do not use a large (hours, days) token skew intervals. |
| Signature algorithm | Select an algorithm to use to verify the token signature. |
| Token validation method: Service endpoint In this mode, XEServer delegates token verification to the XEConnect Authorization service. The service requests XEConnect to verify the token and passes the client ID and the client secret as parameters. The connection to XEConnect must be secured with SSL. | |
| Introspection URL | Specify the [introspection](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7662) URL of the XEConnect Authorization service that performs token verification. You have to specify the corresponding client ID and the client secret as only clients registered in XEConnect [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) can access the introspection endpoint. |
| Client ID | The ID of the client registered in [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) for whom the token has been issued. |
| Secret key | The secret key to protect the client's identity. |
| Required claims | In this section, you can specify the [claims](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4) to be verified in the token. If a claim specified in this pane is not available in the token, the request authorization fails.Tip When you define a new token claim to be verified, you can select from the drop down list of three claims: [Audience](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), [Issuer](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), and [Subject](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.2). However, you can specify custom claims to be verified. The claim's name and value are case-sensitive and should be the same as they appear in the token. |







###### HTTP Headers Size


By default, Jetty-based XEServer services can handle HTTP requests and responses with headers size up to 8KB. To change this limit, you can declare the following properties on the [Profile Startup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) or [Profile Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) tabs. The property values must be specified in bytes.

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`: Use to set the same limit for both request and response HTTP headers.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.request.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP request headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.response.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP response headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.


For example, the following property sets the maximum request/response HTTP headers size to 64KB:

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)

```
com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size=65536
```






###### Custom HTTP Headers


XEServer Jetty-based services can return custom HTTP headers. For this, set the following XEServer environment properties and restart the corresponding profile:

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.headers.enabled=true`: enables the custom headers feature.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.header.<headerName>=<headerValue>`: forces Jetty web server to return <headerName>=<headerValue> HTTP header in each response.


Tip Use [global environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) to force all Jetty-based services in all profiles to send the specified HTTP headers. Alternatively, you can use [profile-level environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) to affect Jetty behavior only within a specific profile.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="configure-ws-web-sample-service"></a>

##### Configure WS Web Sample Service

> 📄 Source: [Configure_WS_Web_Sample_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_WS_Web_Sample_Service.htm)

###### Configure WS Web Sample Service


General Settings Configuration Tab QoS Tab Dependencies Tab Service Information Tab


The WS Web Sample service supports a sample client web application and profile that can be found in the ${XESRoot}\samples folder.


*[Image: WS Web Sample Service General settings]*


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Name** | An additional property name. |
| **Value** | An additional property value. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |
