<a id="web-service-endpoints"></a>

##### Web Service Endpoints

> 📄 Source: [Web_Service_Endpoints.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Endpoints.htm)

###### Web Service Endpoints


This section provides overview information on Web Services endpoints. The following Web Service endpoints are available in routes:

- [Web Service Inbound Endpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Inbound.htm). Used to receive data from a client application that is located outside of XEServer.
- [Web Service Outbound Endpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Outbound.htm). Used to return data to a client application that is located outside of XEServer.


*[Image: Web Service Endoint]*

---

<a id="web-service-outbound"></a>

##### Web Service Outbound

> 📄 Source: [Web_Service_Outbound.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Web_Service_Outbound.htm)

###### Web Service Outbound


The Web Service outbound endpoint uses the XEServer Web Service to return data to a client application that is located outside of XEServer. The Web Service that is used by this component must be the XEServer Web Service.


*[Image: Web Service Outbound Settings tab]*


###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


The following fields are available for the Web Service Outbound Endpoint component:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service Name** | The name of the XEServer Web Service. |
| **Full URL** | The URL for the XEServer Web Service. For example: http://localhost:9080/ws?wsdl. This setting is read-only. |



###### Attributes tab


On this tab, you can manage key-value metadata transferred to the endpoint destination: for example, to a directory on a file system, web server, JMS queue, and so on. Depending on the endpoint destination, metadata are stored in the format that is suitable for a certain destination environment. For example, for FS components attributes are stored in the form of key-value pairs in .property files, for JMS components, attributes are mapped to queue message metadata, and so on.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Include exchange properties** | Select this option to send exchange properties for further processing. |
| **Include message properties** | Select this option to send message properties for further processing. |
| Attribute parameters | In this pane, you can specify a custom name-value pair to be appended to the metadata. |



###### Outputs tab


The following output options are available for the outbound Web Service endpoint:


| Output | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **error** | Error is a routing component error output that streams error data to another component in the route. When errors are not passed to another component, use the Error Channel Inbound endpoint to collect "spare" errors for the route. |
| **original** | Original is a routing component output that streams data which was originally input to the routing component. For example, an EDI file in the state that it was originally received from a trading partner can be passed to another component in addition to a result stream that is some permutation of the original file. |

---

<a id="web-service-monitoring"></a>

##### Web Service Monitoring

> 📄 Source: [Book_Web_Service_Monitoring.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Web_Service_Monitoring.htm)

###### Web Service Monitoring


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="monitor-the-xeserver"></a>

##### Monitor the XEServer

> 📄 Source: [Monitor_the_XEServer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Monitor_the_XEServer.htm)

###### Monitor the XEServer


There are several ways of monitoring XEServer Web Service behavior and get information about the errors that occur while data processing. Use the following techniques for monitoring the Web Service:

- WSDL Check: Perform periodic checks to verify that WSDL is running.
- [XEServer Logs:](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Analyze_XEServer_Log_Files.htm) Analyze XEServer log files to get information about errors related to the Web Service.
- [Routes for Monitoring:](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Add_Monitoring_Properties_to_Route_Components.htm)Add monitoring properties to the route components to perform the Web Service check.
- [Startup Properties:](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_Route_Startup_Properties.htm)Use route startup properties to store log files to a location in your file system and get notified when errors occur.


For general information about Web Services, refer to [Web Services Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_WebService_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="verify-that-the-web-service-is-running"></a>

##### Verify that the Web Service is Running

> 📄 Source: [Verify_that_the_Web_Service_is_Running.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Verify_that_the_Web_Service_is_Running.htm)

###### Verify that the Web Service is Running


You can perform periodic checks to see if the Web Service running. To do so, check the URL address being used by the Web Service inbound component on your route.


To check the URL being used by the Web Service inbound component:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand the profile for the Web Service route that you want to verify.
3. On the Outline tab, locate the desired route, and double-click WS Inbound component.
4. On the Route component properties tab, click Settings.
5. In the Full url field, copy the URL address.


Example


http://localhost:9081/ws?wsdl.

1. Paste the copied URL in the address bar to check whether it is accessible.

---

<a id="analyze-xeserver-log-files"></a>

##### Analyze XEServer Log Files

> 📄 Source: [Analyze_XEServer_Log_Files.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Analyze_XEServer_Log_Files.htm)

###### Analyze XEServer Log Files


XEServer log files can be used to pick up information about the Web Service and its status. XEServer log files contain information about all events that happen in the data processing. You can analyze the logs and get the information related to the Web Service you are running.


XEServer log files are located at ${XESRoot}\profiles\{profile name}\log. The xes.error.log file contains information about all the events of the current profile.

---

<a id="add-monitoring-properties-to-route-components"></a>

##### Add Monitoring Properties to Route Components

> 📄 Source: [Add_Monitoring_Properties_to_Route_Components.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Add_Monitoring_Properties_to_Route_Components.htm)

###### Add Monitoring Properties to Route Components


You can set route components to check if the Web Service is up. Special properties should be added to the route component when sending data to the Web Service. These properties are then checked with the data received from the Web Service. If the corresponding property file exists, this means that Web Service is working.


Generally, this schema works as a regular ping. You need to send some message to the Web Service or set timer functionality to perform this action according to a schedule. For more information about setting timers, see [Timers.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Timers_Services.htm)


We use our sample Web Service route, to perform the check described earlier. For the detailed information about this route, see Sample Web Service.


To create a sample Web Service profile:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Double-click Admin Console.
4. On the Outline tab, right-click Profiles, and then click New Child.
5. In the New Profile dialog box, in Profile name, type a name for the route.
6. In the Template list, click Sample WebService.
7. Click OK.


To perform a Web Service check:

1. On the Outline tab for the sample Web Services profile you created in the preceding procedure, expand Routes, and then double click Test route.
2. In the Route editor, add FS Inbound component and name it Web Service Ping. This component is used to send a message to the Web Service with the following specified properties to check if the Web Service is running.

1. On the created route component properties tab, click Outputs tab.
2. On the right side of the tab, click New.
3. In the New Output Property dialog box, in Name, type mode.
4. In Value, type check.
5. Click OK. A new property is sent with a data file to the Web Service route.
6. On the Outline tab, expand Route, and then double-click Router.
7. On the Route component properties tab, click Outputs.
8. Under Component output names, in the middle of the tab, click New.
9. In the New Output Property dialog box, in Output, type ping.
10. In Filter, type {Prp[mode]} = 'check'.
11. Click OK. The following image shows the ping output name and its properties.*[Image: Route component properties]*
12. On the Palette, in the Outbound Endpoints section, click Web Service and in the Route Viewer, click the place where you want to locate the selected component.
13. Connect Web Service outbound component to the Ping outbound on the Router component: *[Image: Web Service Check Route]*


To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started


If a message is received from the Check outbound endpoint, this means that the Web Service is running.

---

<a id="use-route-startup-properties"></a>

##### Use Route Startup Properties

> 📄 Source: [Use_Route_Startup_Properties.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_Route_Startup_Properties.htm)

###### Use Route Startup Properties


You can use route startup properties to save error events of this profile to a separate file in your file system.


To access and change startup properties:

1. Open the route you want to use.
2. On the Outline tab, click the route name on top.


*[Image: Outline Web Service Monitoring]*

1. Remove comments from the following line:


-Dcom.edifecs.etools.xeserver.log.errors.path=${XESLog}/errors.

1. Provide the path to the location where you want to store error events of this profile. For example:


-Dcom.edifecs.etools.xeserver.log.errors.path=C:\Errors


All the events that happen in this profile are stored at the provided location.

---

<a id="xesmanager-service"></a>

##### XESManager Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_XESManager_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_XESManager_Service.htm)

###### XESManager Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="config-service"></a>

##### Config Service

> 📄 Source: [Config_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Config_Service.htm)

###### Config Service


The Config Service service is XEServer service that allows you to manage the configuration settings of your applications from a central location. The service watches all the changes made to the configuration settings in order to fetch and implement the changes in the applications. You can set up the configuration settings of all the applications at some directory and these applications can retrieve the settings from that directory saving you the trouble to manually set up the configuration settings in each application. As soon as the configuration settings are saved, the Config Service detects the changes, fetches the configuration settings from the specified directory, and automatically restarts the applications/profiles.


The Config Service can upload the configuration settings from the following directories:

- A folder
- [XESManager Configuration Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Configuration_Server.htm)


This service can be used by the profiles. The service comes preconfigured and disabled within XEServer profiles. However, if you want to use this service, you have to enable the service in the profiles and to add or to import the configuration settings of the profiles to a folder directory or to XESManager Configuration Server.


###### Service Settings


The service has the following settings:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Service | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| Type | The type of the XEServer service. This value cannot be changed. |
| Status | You can select to enable the service. |
| Path | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange the services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop the services in the folders. |
| Auto start | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100, where 1 starts first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can define from where to fetch the configuration settings for the profiles.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Applications | Specify the applications whose configuration settings you require for a profile. Use comma as a delimiter between application names. For example, Clinical,Common,Financial. |
| Polling interval | The time interval in seconds at which the Config Service requests the configuration settings for the profile and fetches them, if any. |
| Fail on service start | Select this option to fail the service if any of the enabled configuration sources is unavailable at the start. |
| Load configuration from folder | |
| Status | You can select to enable the upload of the configuration settings from a folder. |
| Path | Specify the path to a location where the configuration settings are stored. |
| Load configuration from XES Manager | |
| Status | You can select to enable the upload of the configuration settings from XESManager. |
| Credentials | |
| Username | The name of an XESManager user who has the permissions to read the contents of the XESManager Configuration Server. |
| Password | The password of the XESManager user who has the permissions to read the contents of the XESManager Configuration Server. |
| Connection Settings | |
| Use auto-discovery connection settings | Select to use the XESManager connection settings that have been specified on the tab Auto-discovery in Environment of XEServer for auto-discovery configuration settings of an XEServer agent to be automatically detected by XESManager. |
| Address | Specify the location of the XESManager Configuration Server. The name of the host where XESManager is running. Alternately, you can use the default value. The port number to be used for the connection. Alternately, you can use the default value. The base RESTful endpoint of the XESManager Configuration Server. Use the default value unless you have changed the XESManager settings that affect RESTful URL. |
| Use default SSL Settings | Select this option if you want your agent to send registration requests over the SSL-secured channel. During the registration phase, XEServer agent acts as a client, and XESManager represents the server side. |
| Enable hostname validation | Select this option for XEServer to compare the XESManager's host name with the host name in the certificate (the certificate sent to XEServer by XESManager). If the names do not match, the SSL connection is not established. |
| Trusted Store The trusted store contains XESManager certificates to identify XESManager as a trusted party. | |
| Path | Enter the path to a truststore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the truststore. The default password is "password". |
| Key store If client authentication is enabled on the server (XESManager), this key store comes into effect. The key store contains XEServer agent's key pairs (a private key and a certificate with a public key) used by XESManager to validate the XEServer's identity. | |
| Path | Enter the path to the keystore file that contains the key pair entries. |
| Password | Enter the password to the keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the key pair. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="work-with-config-service"></a>

##### Work with Config Service

> 📄 Source: [Work_With_Config_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Work_With_Config_Service.htm)

###### Work with Config Service


This section shows you how to use the XEServer Config Service in combination with XESManager to dynamically fetch the values of properties (that can change) from XESManager during the route's run time.


###### XESManager Setup


To configure XESManager, do the following:

1. Start XESManager.
2. On the XESManager web interface, open the Configuration Server page.
3. Add a new Application entry.
4. For the new Application entry, create a new Property Category with a single sample property as shown in the image:
5. Click Save Changes.


###### XEServer Setup

1. Create a new XEServer profile.
2. In the profile, create a new XEServer Config Service.
3. On the service configuration page, specify the following settings as shown in the image below: - Applications: Specify the name of the Application as defined on the XESManager Configuration Server page. - Load configuration from XESManager: Set Status to Enabled to allow XEServer to pull in the property updates from XESManager.
4. Create a sample route that has the following structure: 1. In this route, select the FS Inbound component, and on the component's Outputs tab, define the output property as shown in the image:Tip When this route starts, XEServer pulls in the value from the property ${foo} from XESManager. 2. Select the FS Inbound component, and on the component's Settings tab, set the option Data properties to overwrite.
5. Save the route configuration.


###### Run Sample Scenario


To test the scenario:

1. Start the sample XEServer profile.
2. Drop a file into the source location of the FS Inbound component (${XESRoot}\profiles\test\workspace\test-data\inbound\).
3. When the processing is complete, a .properties file appears in the outbound location (${XESRoot}\profiles\test\workspace\test-data\outbound\). Open the .properties file. The file contains the following property:fetchedFromConfigServer=bar
4. On the XESManager web interface, go to Configuration Server page, and then change the value of the property foo. For example, foo=bar123.
5. Click Save Changes. When XEServer detects the property modification, XEServer also updates the property within the profile and restarts the profile.
6. Repeat Step 2 to trigger the route processing again.
7. When the processing is complete, open the .properties file in the route outbound directory. The file contains the updated property:
8. fetchedFromConfigServer=bar123


Tip The sample profile creates the file ${XESRoot}profiles\test\workspace\Services\Config Service\test_application\test_category.properties that stores key-value properties fetched from XESManager.

---

<a id="groovy-service"></a>

#### Groovy Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Groovy_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Groovy_Service.htm)

##### Groovy Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="groovy-service"></a>

##### Groovy Service

> 📄 Source: [Groovy_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_Service.htm)

###### Groovy Service


Groovy service allows executing any custom Groovy code on service start and stop. The service uses the Groovy engine version 2.5.19.


When you add the service to a route, you can view and edit the following settings:



For more information, see [Configure Groovy Components.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_Component.htm)


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Script | Provide a groovy script in the value field. Configuring def _onGroovyServiceStart() and def _onGroovyServiceStop() methods enables controlling the lifecycle of Groovy. Note In case none of these methods is defined service startup is failed. You can edit a groovy script. To do this, click Edit next to the Scriptfield. |
| Script path | Provide a path to a file with a groovy script. You can also edit a file from the repository. To do this, you need to provide a path to the file from the repository first. Click Browse next to the Script path box. In the File Path dialog box, select Cloud repository, and then navigate to the file you need. The selected file is pulled from the repository to the Script path field. To edit a file from the repository, click Edit next to the Script path field. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="monitor-services"></a>

#### Monitor Services

> 📄 Source: [Book_XEServer_Monitoring_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_XEServer_Monitoring_Service.htm)

##### Monitoring Services


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="ftp-event-router"></a>

##### FTP Event Router

> 📄 Source: [FTP_Event_Router.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FTP_Event_Router.htm)

###### FTP Event Router


This service allows you to collect FTP events from an [XEConnect FTP Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Configure_FTP_Server_Service.htm) and send the events to a JMS queue. You can configure the service to report all FTP events or you can select specific events to report.


###### Service Configuration


Apart from generic service settings, the service has the following settings:



| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| FTP Event Source | Select an [XEConnect FTP Server Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Configure_FTP_Server_Service.htm) instance to monitor. One FTP Event Router can monitor one FTP server. |
| Target JMS Service | Select a [JMS service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_JMS.htm) through which the service submits FTP events to a JMS server. |



In the Queues pane, define FTP events you want to report to a specific JMS queue. For this, click New and select the types of events to be reported.


Tip You can use system macros (${myQueueName}) to specify queue names as XEServer environment variables.


###### Events buffering


To improve reliability and eliminate delivery issues, XEServer can save all events to a persistent local storage before sending them to the target JMS server.


To enable events buffering, set the property -Dcom.edifecs.etools.xeserver.event.processor.broker.enabled=true on the [Profile Startup Tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm). With this feature enabled, all events are first saved to ${XESWorkspace}/ftp-events, and then sent to the external JMS server. If the server becomes unresponsive, XEServer waits until the connection is restored and then sends all the queued events to the target server.


###### Container mode


If you run XEServer profile configurations in a containerized environment, read [this topic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_in_Containerized_Environments.htm) to learn about the required changes in settings.

---

<a id="monitoring-services-overview"></a>

##### Monitoring Services Overview

> 📄 Source: [Monitoring_Services_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Monitoring_Services_Overview.htm)

###### Monitoring Services Overview


XEServer Monitoring services allow you to gather statistics about a particular system and receive notifications on the system performance flow. You can monitor file systems and JMS servers, such as Active MQ, IBM MQ, or Oracle WebLogic.


The information gathered about a monitored system is reported in the form of metrics. Metrics are abstract pieces of data that contain specific information about a system. For example, the Free Disk Space metric reports on free disk space in gigabytes and percentages.


With [XESManager](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XESManager_Overview.htm), you can receive notifications on events that occur in a monitored system. For example, you can set a trigger to fire a notification when the monitored system starts accumulating excessive data. Excessive data accumulation can be a consequence of obstructions in the data flow. By tracking the number of files or disk space usage, you can detect data accumulation as soon as it starts to pile up and get instant notification to recover and correct the data flow quickly.

---

<a id="monitoring-active-mq-service"></a>

##### Monitoring Active MQ Service

> 📄 Source: [Monitoring_ActiveMQ_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Monitoring_ActiveMQ_Service.htm)

###### Monitoring ActiveMQ Service


With this service, you can monitor [Apache ActiveMQ](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/ActiveMQ_Server_Service.htm) JMS servers. To allow the service to connect to ActiveMQ JMS servers, see configuration of standalone or embedded ActiveMQ server. The following settings are displayed on the Detail tab when the Monitoring ActiveMQ service is created:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can set connectivity parameters of your ActiveMQ server and queues or topics to be monitored.


To start monitoring an Active MQ JMS server, queue or topic:

1. Enter connectivity parameters to establish a connection to an ActiveMQ server:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Polling interval | The time interval in milliseconds at which the ActiveMQ server is scanned. |
| Server JMX URL | The JMX URL to an ActiveMQ server. For standalone JMS servers, specify the URL in the following format: service:jmx:rmi:///jndi/rmi://<host>:1099/jmxrmi The 1099 port number should be used for standalone JMS servers. For embedded ApacheMQ servers, specify the URL in the following format: service:jmx:jmxmp://<host>:<port>/server The JMX port number, specified for Monitoring Active MQ service, should be used. You can view the JMX port number in the Admin Console. |
| Broker Alias | The logical name of an Active MQ broker to be monitored. The statistics that have been gathered are displayed in XESManager under this name. |
| Broker Name | The name of a broker to be monitored. This name must match the name, defined in the Active MQ XML configuration file. |
| User Name | The user name used for accessing the JMS server. |
| User Password | The user password used for accessing the JMS server. |


1. Click New to define the ActiveMQ topics or queues to be monitored. The Destination dialog box is displayed:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Destination Alias | The logical name of a queue or topic to be monitored. The statistics that have been gathered are displayed in XESManager under this name. |
| Destination Name | The name of the topic or queue to be monitored. |
| Destination Type | The type of destination to be monitored. Select either QUEUE or TOPIC to monitor the corresponding storage. |


1. Save the configuration.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |



###### Configuring Standalone ActiveMQ Server


To allow Monitoring Active MQ service to connect to a standalone Active MQ server:

1. Open the activemq.xml located at %ApacheActiveMQServer%/conf.
2. Append the useJmx="true" attribute to the <broker> node to enable using JMX: <broker useJmx="true">
3. For insecure connection, in the <managementContext /> node, set the createConnector="true": <managementContext> <managementContext createConnector="true"/> </managementContext> -or- For secure connection, replace the <managementContext></managementContext> node with the following code: <managementContext> <managementContext createConnector="true" jmxDomainName="org.apache.activemq"> <property xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/ beans" name="environment"> <map xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans"> <entry xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans" key="jmx.remote.x.password.file" value="${activemq.base}/conf/jmx.password"/> <entry xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans" key="jmx.remote.x.access.file" value="${activemq.base}/conf/jmx.access"/> </map> </property> </managementContext> </managementContext>
4. Open the jmx.access file located at %ApacheActiveMQServer%/conf and provide read/write permissions for the user: <UserName> readwrite By default, the value is set to "admin readwrite".
5. Open the jmx.password file located at %ApacheActiveMQServer%/conf and specify the user password: <UserName> <UserPassword> By default, the value is set to "admin activemq".


For more information about ApacheMQ JMX support, see [Apache official documentation](http://activemq.apache.org/jmx.html).


###### Configuring Embedded ActiveMQ Server


To allow Monitoring Active MQ service to connect to an embedded XEServer ActiveMQ Server:

1. On the EAM Outline tab, expand Services, then expand Servers, and click ActiveMQ Server.
2. On the Configuration tab, select the Broker configuration property, and click Configure.
3. Append the useJmx="true" attribute to the <broker> node to enable using JMX: <broker ... useJmx="true">
4. For insecure connection, in the <managementContex /> node, set the createConnector="true": <managementContext> <managementContext createConnector="true"/> </managementContext> -or- For secure connection, replace the <managementContext></managementContext> node with the following code: <managementContext> <managementContext createConnector="true" jmxDomainName="org.apache.activemq"> <property xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans" name="environment"> <map xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans"> <entry xmlns="http://www.springframework.org/schema/beans" key="jmx.remote.x.password.file" value="${XESProfileConfig}/activemq/jmx.password"/> <entry xmlns="http:-//www.springframework.org/schema/beans" key="jmx.remote.x.access.file" value="${XESProfileConfig}/activemq/jmx.access"/> </map> </property> </managementContext> </managementContext>
5. Under the ${XESRoot}/profiles/%ProfileName%/config/activemq/ directory, create the jmx.access file and provide read/write permissions for the user in the following format: <UserName> readwrite
6. Under the ${XESRoot}/profiles/%ProfileName%/config/activemq/ directory, create the jmx.password file and specify the user password in the following format: <UserName> <UserPassword> The specified user name and password are now required for accessing the embedded server.

---

<a id="monitoring-file-system-service"></a>

##### Monitoring File System Service

> 📄 Source: [Monitoring_File_System_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Monitoring_File_System_Service.htm)

###### Monitoring File System Service


With this service, you can track a file system directory. The following settings are displayed on the Detail tab when the Monitoring File System service is created:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can define the file system directories to be monitored and metrics to be reported.


To start monitoring a file system directory:

1. On the Configuration Tab, click New.
2. In the Folder dialog box, follow the instructions:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Performance statistics collection period | The time interval in minutes, at which statistics is gathered and reported. By default, the value is set to 60 minutes. |
| Folder Alias | The logical name of a folder to be monitored. The statistics that have been gathered are displayed in XESManager under this name. |
| Folder Path | The path to the file system directory to be monitored. |
| Collect File Metrics | Select this option to report File metrics that have information about number of files and directory size. |
| Collect Disk Free Space Metrics | Select this option to report Disk Free Space metrics that have information about the amount of free disk space in percentages and gigabytes. |
| Collect Disk Performance Metrics | Select this option to report Disk Performance metrics that have information about read, write, and delete speeds. |


1. Save the configuration. The statistics about the specified directory is gathered and the selected metrics are reported.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

###### Monitoring IBM MQ Service


With this service, you can track [IBM Websphere MQ](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_IBM_WebSphere_MQ_(Edifecs_ContextFactory)_Service.htm) servers. The following settings are displayed on the Detail tab when the Monitoring IBM MQ service is created:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can define connectivity parameters of your IBM MQ server and queues or topics to be monitored.


To start monitoring an IBM MQ JMS server, queue or topic:

1. Enter connectivity parameters to establish a connection to an IBM MQ server:


| Field | Description |

| IBM Server Queue Manager | The name of your IBM MQ queue manager. |
| CCSID | The 16-bit code, representing the coded character set. |
| IBM Server host | The host name or IP address of your IBM MQ server. |
| IBM Server port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| IBM Server Channel name | The name of the IBM MQ channel used to connect to the queue manager. |
| User ID | The user name used for accessing the server. |
| User Password | The user password used for accessing the server. |


1. Click New to define the IBM MQ topics or queues to be monitored. The Destination dialog box is displayed.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Destination Alias | The logical name of a queue or topic to be monitored. The statistics that have been gathered are displayed in XESManager under this name. |
| Destination Name | The name of the topic or queue to be monitored. |
| Destination Type | The type of destination to be monitored. Select either QUEUE or TOPIC to monitor the corresponding storage. |


1. Save the configuration.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |

On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.
