###### Monitoring Weblogic Service


With this service, you can track [Oracle WebLogic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_Oracle_BEA_WebLogic_Service.htm) JMS servers. The following settings are displayed on the Detail tab when the Monitoring Weblogic service is created:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab you can define connectivity parameters of your Oracle WebLogic server and queues or topics to be monitored.


To start monitoring an Oracle WebLogic JMS server queue or topic:

1. Enter connectivity parameters to establish a connection to an Oracle WebLogic server:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Polling interval | The time interval in milliseconds at which the WebLogic server is scanned. |
| Server JMX URL | The JMX URL to the WebLogic server. By default, the 7001 port number is used. |
| Manage Server Name | The name of the server. This name is defined on the WebLogic server side. |
| JMS Server Name | The name of the JMS server. This name is defined on the WebLogic server side. |
| User Name | The user name used for accessing the server. |
| User Password | The user password used for accessing the server. |


1. Click New to define the Oracle WebLogic topics or queues to be monitored. The Destination dialog box is displayed:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Destination Alias | The logical name of a queue or topic to be monitored. The statistics that have been gathered are displayed in XESManager under this name. |
| Destination Name | The name of the topic or queue to be monitored. Note This name must include a WebLogic JMS server module name and have the following format: <JMSModuleName>!<QueueName/TopicName> For more information on how to retrieve a correct destination name, see Weblogic Modules. |
| Destination Type | The type of destination to be monitored. Select either QUEUE or TOPIC to monitor the corresponding storage. |


1. Save the configuration.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |



###### WebLogic Modules


To allow XEServer Monitoring WebLogic service to track a WebLogic queue or topic, ensure that the name of the topic or queue is correctly specified in the Destination Name.


To learn the required name of the queue or topic to be monitored:

1. Open your Oracle WebLogic Server administration console.
2. In the Domain Structure pane, go to %YourServerName%/Services/Messaging/JMS Modules. The JMS Modules table is displayed:
3. In the JMS Modules table, select the module that contains the queue/topic you need.
4. In the Summary of Resources table, select the queue/topic you need and go to the Monitoring tab. The destination name displayed in the table is the correct name of the destination and must be used in Destination Name field. Note that this destination name can be different from the JNDI Name. For more information about WebLogic servers, see [Oracle official documentation](http://www.oracle.com/technetwork/middleware/weblogic/documentation/index.html).

---

<a id="xprocess-service"></a>

##### XProcess Service

> 📄 Source: [XProcess_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XProcess_Service.htm)

###### XProcess Management Service


With this service, you can establish a connection with Edifecs [XProcess Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/xprocessmanagement/Content/Home.htm) (XPM) and submit XPM events (generated by [route components](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Events_Tab.htm)) to an XPM server.


When you add the service to a profile, you can view and edit the following settings:



In addition to generic service settings, the service has the following settings:


###### Settings Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Connection Hosts | Enter the address of the XPM server to send events to in the following format: <{HostName}\|{IP}>:<Port>/sm/api/1.0 If you use several XPM servers (see XPM High Availability mode), specify all the server addresses separated by a comma. |
| User name | Enter the XPM user name to connect to the server.Note If you work with several XPM servers (see XPM High Availability mode), all the XPM servers must have an identical user name - password pair to allow XEServer to connect to all the XPM instances. |
| Password | Enter your XProcess Management password. |
| Domain | Enter a user domain to work with. In XPM, a domain is an independent storage area used to isolate different users' processes and prevent naming collisions. If you are an authorized user, you can access multiple domains (assuming an XPM administrator grants you the permissions to access these domains). When you select a domain from this list, you can submit events that only relate to the processes of the selected domain. |



###### SSL Tab


On this tab, you can configure the secure SSL connection to an XPM server. XPM is distributed with a self-signed certificate to encrypt all the network communications with XEServer. Edifecs recommends that you replace the default certificate with your own certificate to protect your organization's users who use XProcess Management from the man-in-the-middle attacks (MITM). If your certificate is signed by an untrusted authority, you have to install the certificate to the truststores on all the users' computers to avoid unsecured connection warnings in a web browser.


Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Enable hostname validation | If you select this option, the service compares the name of XPM server host with the host name from the certificate that XPM server sends to the XEServer XPM Service during the SSL handshake. |
| Truststore | |
| Path | Enter the path to a keystore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the truststore. The default password is password. |
| Keystore | |
| Path | Enter the path to the file that contains keystore certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the private key. |



To set up the SSL connection between XEServer and XPM, you have to perform the following steps:

 1. Generate a certificate


First, you have to create a new keystore and generate a new key pair (for example, by using the Java Keytool utility at the following location: ${JAVA_HOME}\bin\keytool.exe). To do this, run the following command:


keytool -genkeypair -alias xpm -keyalg RSA -validity 365 -keysize 2048 -keystore keystore.jks


where:

- -genkeypair: The command to generate a key pair.
- -alias: The name of the keystore entry (a key pair), for example, xpm.
- -keyalg: The algorithm to be used to generate the key pair. The recommended value is RSA.
- -validity: The number of days during which the certificate is valid.
- -keysize: The key size. The recommended value is 2048.
- -keystore: The location of the keystore that will be created along with the certificate or an existing keystore to where the certificate will be imported.


After you run the command, you will be prompted to provide the information about your organization to populate certificate DN fields (CN, O, OU, L, ST, C).





 2. Import the generated certificate to XPM


To use the newly generated certificate on the XPM server side, open the file ${ECRootPath}\XProcessManagement\config\settings.properties, and set the following properties:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| server.ssl.key-alias | The name of the key pair entry inside the keystore used by XPM. For example, server.ssl.key-alias=xpm. |
| server.ssl.key-password | The password to the keystore entry being used (with the name xpm). For example, server.ssl.key-password=password or server.ssl.key-password= if you did not set a password when you created the certificate. |
| server.ssl.key-store-password | The password to the keystore file. For example, server.ssl.key-store-password=password. |
| server.ssl.key-store | The location of the keystore file. For example, server.ssl.key-store=file:/D:/Edifecs/XProcessManagement/config/new-keystore.jks. |



For these properties to take effect, restart the XPM server.





 3. Import the generated certificate to XEServer


To use the newly generated certificate on the XEServer side and allow the XPM Service to communicate with an XPM server through an SSL-secured channel, you have to install the certificate you have generated (see previous steps) to the truststore of XPM Service. To do this:

1. In the XPM truststore location (${ECRootPath}\XProcessManagement\config\keystore.jks), run the following command to export the certificate you generated (xpm.cer):
2. If you set a password when you created the certificate, enter the password to the keystore entry.
3. Save the file xpm.cer to any location on the computer where XEServer is installed.
4. In the XEServer truststore location (${XESRoot}/features/route-engine/components/state-event/resources/ssl/keystore.jks), run the following command to import the certificate xpm.cer to the truststore used by XPM Service (keystore.jks):
5. Enter the password to the XEServer keystore entry (the default password is password), and then confirm the import.






###### Internal Storage


When a route component generates an XPM event, the component passes the event to the XPM Service that, in turn, submits the event to an XPM server. However, the XPM Service does not send the event immediately - instead, the service puts the event to a local buffer (a database), and only then the service sends the event to the target XPM server and clears the buffer. This is done to preserve the event flow if, for example, an XPM server goes down. When the failed XPM server recovers, the XPM Service sends all the accumulated events to that server.


On the Internal Storage tab, you can configure the connection to the database used as a buffer. You can use the following databases engines:

- MS SQL Server
- Oracle
- PostgreSQL
- H2


The tab has the following options:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Connection URL | The database connection URL. |
| Login | The user login to connect to the database. |
| Password | The user password to connect to the database. Tip For this field, you can use system macros, for example, ${dbPassword}, where dbPassword is the name of the encrypted profile environment variable defined on the [Profile Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) tab. The recommended approach is to specifying a password as it allows you to keep the password encrypted during the profile migration. |
| Connection check on start | Select this option to check the database connection when the service starts. If the connection cannot be established, the service throws an error. |
| Connection pool settings The service uses database connection pooling to speed up the database operations. You can use the following properties to fine-tune the database performance. | |
| Exhausted action | Select the connection pool behavior when the pool gets exhausted: throws NoSuchElementException. blocks for Pool max wait value and throws NoSuchElementException after the timeout expires. creates a new connection object and returns it. The property Pool max number of active connections is ignored for such behavior. |
| Min evictable idle time (ms) | The minimum amount of time that a connection object may remain idle in the pool before the object is eligible for eviction by the idle object evictor (if any). |
| Pool max number of active connections | The maximum number of active connections that can be allocated from this pool simultaneously. The default value -1 indicates that no limit is set. |
| Pool max number of idle connections | The maximum number of connection objects that persistently remain idle in the pool. |
| Pool max wait | The maximum amount of time (in milliseconds) that the pool waits for a connection object to be returned before the pool throws an exception. If set to a negative value, the pool waits indefinitely. |
| Pool min number of idle connections | The minimum number of connections that persistently remain idle in the pool. |
| Pool number tests per eviction run | The number of connection objects to analyze during each run of the idle object evictor thread (if any). |
| Pool soft min evictable idle time | The minimum amount of time (in milliseconds) that an object may remain idle in the pool before the object is eligible for eviction by the idle object evictor (if any). |
| Pool time between eviction runs | The time interval (in milliseconds) between the runs of the idle object evictor thread. If set to a negative value, the idle object evictor thread does not run. |
| testOnBorrow | Indicates whether connection objects are validated before borrowing from the pool. If an object fails the validation, the object is removed from the pool, and another object is borrowed. |
| testOnReturn | Indicates whether connection objects are validated before returning to the pool. |
| testWhileIdle | Indicates whether connection objects are validated by the idle object evictor (if any). If an object fails the validation, the object is removed from the pool. |



###### XPM High Availability Mode


To improve the reliability of XPM, you can have several XPM servers that share a common Elasticsearch storage with events (or several Elasticsearch nodes combined into a cluster). Using this approach, if one of the XPM servers goes down, the XEServer XPM Service evenly distributes events among other remaining XPM servers. This configuration has the following structure:



To allow the XEServer XPM service to work with several XPM servers, you should list all the XPM servers in the field Connection Hosts using comma as a separator, for example:


localhost:8443, 192.168.0.27:8343


XPM service evenly distributes all the events among the available XPM servers using the round-robin algorithm: each subsequent event goes to the next available XPM server in the pool (the list of XPM servers that you specify in the field Connection Hosts) in rotation. This greatly improves the performance since the overall event processing effort is split among multiple XPM servers, especially when you deal with a large number of events. For the diagram above, the order in which the events are sent is the following:

1. Event 1 to XPM Server 1
2. Event 2 to XPM Server 2
3. Event 3 to XPM Server 3
4. Event 4 to XPM Server 1, and so in rotation.


If one of the XPM servers becomes unavailable (for example, due to connectivity issues), the service ignores this XPM server and attempts to send the event to the next XPM server. Take the following diagram as an example:



If XPM Server 2 becomes temporary unavailable, the order in which the events are sent is the following:

1. Event 1 to XPM Server 1
2. Event 2 to XPM Server 3
3. Event 3 to XPM Server 1(XPM Server 2 recovers at this point)
4. Event 4 to XPM Server 2
5. Event 5 to XPM Server 3
6. Event 6 to XPM Server 1
7. Event 7 to XPM Server 2, and so on, in rotation.


When one of XPM servers becomes unavailable, the service adds this XPM server to the dead servers list, and no events are directed to this XPM server. In the background, XPM Service continues to ping the XPM servers that have been added to the dead servers list at regular intervals. If an XPM server recovers, the service removes this XPM server from the dead servers list, and the events can go to this XPM server again.


If none of the XPM servers from the list are available, the service accumulates events in a local database and periodically checks to see if the any of the XPM servers are online. When at least one XPM server becomes available, the service moves the accumulated events to that server and purges the database.

---

<a id="sequence-manager-service"></a>

#### Sequence Manager Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Sequence_Manager_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Sequence_Manager_Service.htm)

##### Sequence Manager Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="sequence-manager-service-overview"></a>

##### Sequence Manager Service Overview

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Sequence_Manager_Service_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Sequence_Manager_Service_Overview.htm)

###### Sequence Manager Service Overview


The Sequence Manager Service picks up XEServer messages that arrive in random order, performs a stream sorting by using special IDs, and sends the messages for further processing in the proper order. The messages are split into abstract groups, defined by Group ID, and sorted by Sequence IDs within each group. The Sequence Manager Service consists of three basic elements:

- The XEServer [Sequencer](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Sequencer.htm) component: A route component that picks up incoming messages from a route, resolves the message Group ID, generates and assigns the corresponding Sequence ID to a message within a group.
- The XEServer [Sequence Manager Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Sequence_Manager_Service.htm): Provides a persistent layer for generated and sent message Sequence ID within a message group.
- The XEServer [Resequencer](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Resequencer.htm) component: A route component that performs stream sorting of messages according to the Sequence ID assigned by the Sequencer component.


The service is available for monitoring in the [XEServer Manager](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XESManager_Overview.htm).


The following scenario illustrates the Sequence Manager workflow:

1. The Sequencer component receives the first MESSAGE1 with Group ID = "G1" and asks the Sequence Manager to retrieve the last stored Sequence ID for this group. Since the message is the first one received for this group, the Sequence Manager responses with Last Sequence ID = "0".
2. Based on the Last Sequence ID, Sequencer generates a new Sequence ID value 01 (Sequence ID = "01") for MESSAGE1 and reports this value to Sequence Manager, thus updating the Last Sequence ID. In this way all subsequent messages with Group ID = "G1" are assigned unique Sequence IDs.
3. Then, the Resequencer arranges the messages within each message group in the order of their Sequence IDs to form a sequence, like .., 3, 4, 5, .. and sends them for further processing through the in order output. Messages, which have Sequence ID = PreviousMessageSequence ID + 1 are called expected.
4. But if the expected message is not received during the expiration timeout, the system searches the storage for the message with the closest Sequence ID. For example, if there are 3 messages in the storage with Group ID = "G1" : "4", "5", and "7". Last received message was "1", so the system expects for the message "2". The timeout expires, but the expected message "2" is not received, so the system asks the storage to search for the "closest" message, which is the message "4".
5. In this case, Resequencer generates fake empty messages "2" and "3", and then sends them to the out of order output.
6. Then, the messages "4" and "5" retrieved from the storage fit the sequence, so they are sent to the in order output.
7. The storage is not empty, although there is no message "6" received - a new timeout is started and once it expires, a fake message "6" is generated and sent to the out of order output. Note that every time there is a gap in a sequence, the timeout is started again.
8. The subsequent message "7" fits the sequence, so it is directed to the in order output. All subsequent messages are handled in the same described way.

---

<a id="configure-sequence-manager-service"></a>

##### Configure Sequence Manager Service

> 📄 Source: [Sequence_Manager_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Sequence_Manager_Service.htm)

###### Sequence Manager Service


With the Sequence Manager Service, you can store generated Sequence IDs for each message group. When the Sequence Manager Service is created, the following settings are displayed on the Detail tab:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can define a path to the database.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Connection URL | Select the database on the computer running XEServer and provide the IP address, port, and database name according to the template. If you don't select any database, XEServer uses the default H2 database located at ${XESWorkspace}/sequencer/data with the default settings. |
| Login | The user login to connect to the database. |
| Password | The user password to connect to the database. Tip For this field, you can use system macros, for example, ${dbPassword}, where dbPassword is the name of the encrypted profile environment variable defined on the [Profile Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) tab. The recommended approach is to specifying a password as it allows you to keep the password encrypted during the profile migration. |
| Connection pool settings The service uses database connection pooling to speed up the database operations. You can use the following properties to fine-tune the database performance. | |
| Exhausted action | Select the connection pool behavior when the pool gets exhausted: throws NoSuchElementException. blocks for Pool max wait value and throws NoSuchElementException after the timeout expires. creates a new connection object and returns it. The property Pool max number of active connections is ignored for such behavior. |
| Min evictable idle time (ms) | The minimum amount of time that a connection object may remain idle in the pool before the object is eligible for eviction by the idle object evictor (if any). |
| Pool max number of active connections | The maximum number of active connections that can be allocated from this pool simultaneously. The default value -1 indicates that no limit is set. |
| Pool max number of idle connections | The maximum number of connection objects that persistently remain idle in the pool. |
| Pool max wait | The maximum amount of time (in milliseconds) that the pool waits for a connection object to be returned before the pool throws an exception. If set to a negative value, the pool waits indefinitely. |
| Pool min number of idle connections | The minimum number of connections that persistently remain idle in the pool. |
| Pool number tests per eviction run | The number of connection objects to analyze during each run of the idle object evictor thread (if any). |
| Pool soft min evictable idle time | The minimum amount of time (in milliseconds) that an object may remain idle in the pool before the object is eligible for eviction by the idle object evictor (if any). |
| Pool time between eviction runs | The time interval (in milliseconds) between the runs of the idle object evictor thread. If set to a negative value, the idle object evictor thread does not run. |
| testOnBorrow | Indicates whether connection objects are validated before borrowing from the pool. If an object fails the validation, the object is removed from the pool, and another object is borrowed. |
| testOnReturn | Indicates whether connection objects are validated before returning to the pool. |
| testWhileIdle | Indicates whether connection objects are validated by the idle object evictor (if any). If an object fails the validation, the object is removed from the pool. |



###### Purge Settings Tab


On this tab you can define database cleanup parameters to exclude unused data accumulation in the database.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Enable purge service | Enable purge service to delete unused records from a database. |
| Purge timeout | The timeout in days to mark database records as unused. |
| Purge period | The time interval in hours at which the database is scanned for unused messages. |
| Start purge delay | The timeout in seconds after which the database is scanned for unused messages for the first time. |



###### Jetty Tab


The service is built on top of the [Jetty](https://www.eclipse.org/jetty/) web server (version 10.0.16) and has the following Jetty settings:


Note In XEServer 9.3.3, Jetty server library has been updated to the version 10.0.16 to address security vulnerabilities. If you used XEServer components based on Jetty server, view the [backward compatibility instructions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm) you might need to follow when upgrading to XEServer 9.3.3 and later.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Jetty Server | Select the type of Jetty server to use. In the Platform mode, the service uses a default instance of a Jetty web server that can be used by several XEServer services. This Jetty server is preconfigured and most of the settings are hidden. In the Dedicated mode, a separate instance of a Jetty web server starts together with the service. This approach is more flexible as it allows you to provide specific settings for your web service (HTTP host and port, SSL settings, HTTP Authentication, and so on). |
| HTTP options | |
| Host | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| Port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Thread pool size | The maximum number of process threads the service can handle simultaneously to process incoming requests. |
| SSL | |
| Enable SSL | Select to true/false to enable/disable the Secure Sockets Layer to provide secure access to your web service. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. You can also use macro expressions to enabled/disable connections through the SSL protocol. |
| Enable SNI host check | When the hostname check is disabled (false) in Server Name Indication (SNI), you will not receive a 400 Invalid SNI response if the `Host` header from the request does not match the Common Name (CN) or Subject Alternative Name (SAN) values in the Jetty server certificate. For more information, click [here](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm#Host_check). Default value: false. Note Starting with EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value has been changed to false. Prior to EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value was true. |
|  KeystoreA keystore contains cryptographic keys (private and public key pairs) used to verify the identity of parties that send requests to your web service. | |
| Path | Enter the path to the KeyStore file that contains the key pair entries. |
| Password | Enter the password to the KeyStore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the key pair. |
|  TruststoreA truststore contains digital certificates of the connecting parties to verify their identity. | |
| Enable client authentication | Select to enable the client authentication. If the client authentication is enabled, for incoming request Jetty asks the client to provide a digital certificate, and then compares the provided certificate with those stored in the truststore. If the provided certificate is present in the truststore, the client authentication succeeds. |
| Path | Enter the path to the TrustedStore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the TrustedStore. |
| Check certificate expiration | If set to True, the expiration date of the trust certificate is checked and an exception is thrown if an expired certificate is encountered. |
|  Authentication | |
| Type | The service supports the following authentication options: [Basic](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7617): In this mode, the Base64-encoded user name and password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. [Digest](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7616): In this mode, the plain text user name and a hashed password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. OAuth: In this mode, RESTful Web Service authenticates users by verifying JWT tokens within the Authorization header. **Note** The Authentication Type can also be defined as an environment property in the profile's Environment tab and used as a macro for specifying the authorization type. For example, create the environment property AuthType=Basic, and use the ${AuthType} macro as option for the Type field. |
| Audit | In this section, you can enable the generation of audit events by the Jetty web server. In Service name, specify the name of the XEServer [Audit Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Service.htm) responsible for handling the audit events. |



###### OAuth Authentication Type

 Click to view OAuth Settings


| Field | Option |
| --- | --- |
| Confirmation (cnf) claim XEServer implementation of the Jetty server supports [OAuth 2.0 mutual Transport Layer Security (TLS) authentication with X.509 certificates](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705). Mutual TLS (mTLS) is an authentication type where both the server and client have a certificate and both authenticate each other using the TLS protocol. Such an authentication method provides an additional layer of security and helps prevent various kinds of attacks, such as credential stuffing, malicious API requests, and others. For the Jetty server (resource server) to verify the legitimacy of the client that sends a token, authorization server embeds the certificate hash within a JWT-formatted access token as a cnf ("confirmation") claim when client connects to authorization server. The following is the cnf claim example: Example of "cnf" claim in certificate-bound token ``` { ... "client_id": "6344df61-2foo-4429-9557-6b2979a138f7", "cnf": { "x5t#S256": "Avc6NtXrtcjJsf0zZacl2MCETnUOMu59cz3H4ohh4-o" } ... } ``` The resource server then compares this certificate hash to a hash of the client certificate used for authentication. If the hashes do not match, the request is rejected. Note [Mutual-TLS endpoint aliases](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705#name-metadata-for-mutual-tls-end) are not supported in this release of XEServer. In the Confirmation (cnf) claim section of OAuth authentication type settings, you can configure the Jetty-based XEServer service to request the cnf claim in access tokens for verification. | |
| Confirmation claim verification | Select Disabled to disable verification of the cnf claim on the resource server (Jetty server). Select Enabled to force verification of the cnf claim. If token does not contain the cnf claim or certificate hashes do not match, access is rejected. Select If present to verify the cnf claim only if it is present in access token. |
| Certificate source | Connection: For successful authentication, the resource server matches the client certificate used in the access token request during the connection's handshake TLS session against the client certificate configured on the resource server. HTTP header: If a client performs the access token request through a proxy server or load balancer, the resource server uses the request header as the source of client certificate for matching against the configured client certificate. You must provide the name of the Header and the Certificate encoding (URL-encoded, Base64, Thumbprint) of the certificate sent in the request. |
| Token validation method | Select how you want the service to verify JWT tokens: By signature: The service calculates its own signature and compares it with the signature in the token. Service endpoint: Delegates the token verification to XEConnect Authorization service. |
| Token validation method: By signature By using a public key (for RSA) or a secret (for HMAC), the service creates its own signature from the token header and payload. If this signature matches the signature in the token, the token is considered valid. For this method, you should specify a signature algorithm to use and provide a public key or a secret. You can provide a public key from the following sources: you can import a PEM public key from a file. you can specify a JWKS URL that points to the authorization server (for example, XEConnect Authorization service) that issued the token. To access the authorization server, you have to specify your client ID and client secret. As a response, the authorization server returns a public key to verify the token. | |
| Token clock skew | The JWT verification process requires time synchronization on the computer that issued the token and the computer that verifies the token. This option specifies a time leeway to compensate for the possible clock skew on the authorization server (XEConnect Authorization service) and on the computer that verifies the token (XEServer RESTful Web service). For example, an authorization server issues a JWT token at 12:45 UTC, however the clock on the computer that verifies the token is out of sync and shows 12:47 UTC. In this case, the token fails the validation and is marked expired. However, you can compensate for this timing mismatch by setting the property to 180 (3*60s) or more. Tip For security reasons, Edifecs recommends that you do not use a large (hours, days) token skew intervals. |
| Signature algorithm | Select an algorithm to use to verify the token signature. |
| Token validation method: Service endpoint In this mode, XEServer delegates token verification to the XEConnect Authorization service. The service requests XEConnect to verify the token and passes the client ID and the client secret as parameters. The connection to XEConnect must be secured with SSL. | |
| Introspection URL | Specify the [introspection](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7662) URL of the XEConnect Authorization service that performs token verification. You have to specify the corresponding client ID and the client secret as only clients registered in XEConnect [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) can access the introspection endpoint. |
| Client ID | The ID of the client registered in [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) for whom the token has been issued. |
| Secret key | The secret key to protect the client's identity. |
| Required claims | In this section, you can specify the [claims](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4) to be verified in the token. If a claim specified in this pane is not available in the token, the request authorization fails.Tip When you define a new token claim to be verified, you can select from the drop down list of three claims: [Audience](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), [Issuer](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), and [Subject](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.2). However, you can specify custom claims to be verified. The claim's name and value are case-sensitive and should be the same as they appear in the token. |







###### HTTP Headers Size


By default, Jetty-based XEServer services can handle HTTP requests and responses with headers size up to 8KB. To change this limit, you can declare the following properties on the [Profile Startup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) or [Profile Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) tabs. The property values must be specified in bytes.

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`: Use to set the same limit for both request and response HTTP headers.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.request.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP request headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.response.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP response headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.


For example, the following property sets the maximum request/response HTTP headers size to 64KB:

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)

```
com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size=65536
```






###### Custom HTTP Headers


XEServer Jetty-based services can return custom HTTP headers. For this, set the following XEServer environment properties and restart the corresponding profile:

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.headers.enabled=true`: enables the custom headers feature.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.header.<headerName>=<headerValue>`: forces Jetty web server to return <headerName>=<headerValue> HTTP header in each response.


Tip Use [global environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) to force all Jetty-based services in all profiles to send the specified HTTP headers. Alternatively, you can use [profile-level environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) to affect Jetty behavior only within a specific profile.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |



###### Container mode


If you run XEServer profile configurations in a containerized environment, read [this topic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_in_Containerized_Environments.htm) to learn about the required changes in settings.

<a id="recovery"></a>

##### Recovery

<a id="checkpoint-service"></a>

##### Checkpoint Service

> 📄 Source: [Checkpoint_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint_Service.htm)

###### Checkpoint Service


The Checkpoint service provides connectivity to a database for [Checkpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint.htm) components. The service is also responsible for managing transaction rollbacks when there are errors.


The default service configuration uses a lightweight H2 database that is sufficient for testing and for basic operations. However, you should use a more comprehensive database engine (Oracle, MS SQL, MySQL, or PostgreSQL) in your production environment to achieve maximum reliability and security. On first connecting to the database, the Checkpoint service automatically performs the required database initialization: creates required tables, indices, and so on. When you have to initialize a database for Checkpoint manually, use [preconfigured SQL scripts](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manual_DB_Initialization.htm).


When the service has been added to the route, you can view the following settings:


*[Image: Checkpoint Service General settings]*


###### Configuration tab


Apart from generic service settings, the following properties are available on the Configuration tab:


Tip If some of the properties listed below are not displayed, click New, and from the drop-down list, select this property.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Connection Retry Attempts** | Enter the maximum number of repetitive attempts to connect to a database. |
| Connection Retry Timeout | Enter the timeout time in milliseconds between the retry attempts. |
| JDBC connection URL | Enter the JDBC URL to a database. The service creates two tables in the database: Sessions. The table contains information on active [Checkpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint.htm) components, components' heartbeat, components' GroupIDs (Checkpoint components are grouped by a route), the number of recoveries done on the component, and a Session ID that increments on profile start / restart. DBSchemaVersion: A system table. Session information is deleted from the database on a successful profile shutdown or when the session recovers successfully after a crash. The message payload data and the relative task ID are stored on the file system in Storage path. |
| Perform flush on task commit | When the XEServer task is completed (the XEServer message exchange reaches an outbound destination) and this value is set to TRUE, the service forcibly dumps the XEServer message exchange to the file system. This option improves the checkpoint's reliability, however the option is disabled by default as disk write operations lower the overall performance. |
| Storage path | Enter the path to the data storage directory. The service creates two files in this directory (a binary file {x}_{x} and a log file {x}_log) and whenever an XEServer message exchange passes through the [Checkpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint.htm) component associated with the service, the service appends the following information to the binary file: data from all the messages within the exchange, all the message and exchange properties message descriptors. Click to view sample data that is appended to the file 1_1 (hex-decoded): #Fri Jul 03 14:15:27 test=123   // message exchange property __CPDIScope.InputName=checkpoint __CPDIScope.MessageCount=1 checkpoint_inbound_task_id=17DF59C9-0DD7-41DC-9A68-10CCD24024F4 #Fri Jul 03 14:15:27 _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_Name=testData _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_Ext= _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_LastModified=1593774444475 _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_AbsolutePath=C\:\\Edifecs\\XEServer\\profiles\\Checkpoint_test\\workspace\\test-data\\inbound _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_RelativePath= xes_msg_srcname=FS Inbound _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_Size=6 _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_Created=1593774926822 _MESSAGE_DESCRIPTOR_File_NameNoExt=testData 123456    // message body 16e1e4b3a-fd39-4cb4-ac14-b5ca66190dea*0*Checkpoint 6a5f4cec-3708-4182-8ddb-f434f621f473 ##### Storage directory size The size of this directory grows as more and more messages pass through the Checkpoint component. The overhead to store message payload data gets lower with the payload size increase. For example, to store a message with 1 KB of payload, the service allocates ~1.5 KB of disk space (50%); to store 32 KB of payload, the service uses ~33.5 KB of disk space (5%). ##### Storage directory cleanup When the binary file ({x}_{x}) exceeds 10MB, it gets rolled over. It means that the service creates a new file - {x}_{x+1} and writes data to this file. The service performs a cleanup of the storage directory in the following cases: when all data stored in the file {x}_{x} has been processed,-and- if no data is appended to the file {x}_{x} anymore (as a result of a graceful system shutdown, or because the service already writes data to a new file ({x}_{x+1}). If the XEServer JVM process terminates unexpectedly, the data is retained in the storage directory and can be recovered. Note Do not remove files in the checkpoint storage manually as this makes it impossible to recover a failed processing session. ##### Storage cleanup delay When a profile with a Checkpoint service crashes and then gets restarted, the profile waits10 minutes before it can pick up files and clean up the storage. This is an expected behavior and you can see the reason below. Why does this happen? The Checkpoint mechanism supports a high availability mode - when several instances of the same profile work in parallel and use the same checkpoint database and the same storage directory. To identify which file belongs to a certain profile instance, each profile stores information about its files as session records in a shared database. Each profile instance updates the sessions at regular intervals (heartbeats) which by default is 10 minutes. If some checkpoint session does not receive a heartbeat update within 10 minutes, this session is considered as dead and the "abandoned" checkpoint files become available for pickup by other profile instances. If there is only one profile instance that crashes (checkpoint session is destroyed) and gets restarted immediately, the profile has to wait 10 minutes until the files from the previous processing session can be picked up due to being abandoned. To override the default delay of 10 minutes (600 secs), you can set the JVM system property com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.checkpoint.session_timeout_sec to a value in seconds. This property must be declared on the [Profile Startup tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm). |
| Connection User Name | Enter the database user name to access the database. |
| Connection Password | Enter the password to access the database. |
| DB objects prefix | Enter a prefix that is prepended to all the table names created by this Checkpoint service. This option is useful when you have two or more Checkpoint services writing data to the same database. Using different prefixes, you can avoid overwriting data in tables. You can set a unique DB objects prefix value for each service to be able to distinguish the entities in the database. |

---

<a id="use-checkpoint"></a>

##### Use Checkpoint

> 📄 Source: [Use_Checkpoint.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_Checkpoint.htm)

###### Use Checkpoint


With the Checkpoint component, you can save the interim data. To do this:

- Place the Checkpoint in the start and in the end of the route.


*[Image: Checkpoint route]*

- The first Checkpoint on the route receives the data from the inbound component and saves it.
- The task is transferred from the first Checkpoint to the processing components of the route, and the task is processed.
- The processed data goes to the second Checkpoint and is being saved there till the task is complete.
- When the task is complete and all the data is saved in the second Checkpoint, all data saved in the first Checkpoint is deleted.


If some error occurs during the task processing, the parts of the data already saved in the second Checkpoint are deleted. The inbound data is still saved in the first Checkpoint, and the task restarts from this point as many times as it is indicated by the Retry limit parameter in the Checkpoint settings.


*[Image: Checkpoint Retry Limit]*


When the retry limit is reached, the error data is saved or transferred to the corresponding outbound component.

<a id="timer-client-service"></a>

##### Timer Client Service

> 📄 Source: [Timer_Client_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Timer_Client_Service.htm)

###### Timer Client Service


General Settings Configuration Tab QoS Tab Dependencies Tab Service Information Tab


The Timer service provides the timer functionality used by the Timer endpoints (Activator, Deactivator, and Reporter). Use a Timer service client when the Activator and Deactivator route endpoints need to connect to a remote Timer service server to activate or deactivate schedulers. The Reporter endpoint can only connect to a local Timer service server. All listed service settings are required, and additional settings are allowed for the configuration.


*[Image: Timer Service Client General settings]*


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can set the following properties:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.client.jmx.reconnection.attempts | The number of tries the reconnection should be stated in case of loss of connection with Scheduler. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.client.jmx.reconnection.timeout | The number between the tries |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.client.name | Specifies the name of Scheduler Service to which you want to connect. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.client.type | Specifies the type of client, either VM or JMX. Clients of VM type are connected to Scheduler Service running in the same JVM. Clients of JMX type are connected to Scheduler Service through JMX. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.client.jmx.connector | Specifies JMX service URL to use for connecting to local or remote Scheduler Service. At least one of jmx.connector or jmx.discovery parameters should be specified. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.client.jmx.discovery | Specifies that SLP discovery should be used for locating of Scheduler Services. If this parameter is specified, then jmx.connector parameter is ignored. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="timer-server-service"></a>

##### Timer Server Service

> 📄 Source: [Timer_Server_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Timer_Server_Service.htm)

###### Timer Server Service


General Settings Configuration Tab QoS Tab Dependencies Tab Service Information Tab


The Timer service provides the timer functionality used by the Timer endpoints (Activator, Deactivator, and Reporter). Use a Timer service server when the Timer endpoints connect to a local Timer service. All listed service settings are required, and additional settings are allowed for the configuration.


*[Image: Timer Service Server General settings]*


###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


On this tab, you can set the following properties:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| org.quartz.threadPool.threadCount | Can be any positive integer. This is the number of threads that are available for concurrent execution of jobs. |
| org.quartz.threadPool.threadPriority | Can be any integer between Thread.MIN_PRIORITY and Thread.MAX_PRIORITY. The default is Thread.NORM_PRIORITY. |
| org.quartz.jobStore.misfireThreshold | The number of milliseconds the scheduler uses a trigger to pass its message, before being considered outdated. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.service.name | Specifies the name of Scheduler Service which you want to create. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.service.jmx.discovery | Specifies whether Scheduler should register itself as SLP service to enable SLP discovery. Has an effect only if jmx.enable = true. SLP discovery allows to Scheduler client to locate Scheduler service within network by name. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.service.jmx.enable | Specifies whether Scheduler Service should expose itself through MBean for allowing remote access. |
| org.quartz.jobStore.class | JobStore is responsible for keeping track of all the work data that you give to the scheduler: jobs, triggers, calendars, and the rest. Declare which JobStore your scheduler should use and its configuration settings in the properties file or object that you provide to the SchedulerFactory that you use to produce your scheduler instance. |
| org.quartz.threadPool.class | The name of the ThreadPool implementation you want to use. The threadpool delivered with Quartz is org.quartz.simpl.SimpleThreadPool. It provides a fixed-size pool of threads that have the lifetime of the Scheduler. |
| com.edifecs.etools.scheduler.service.jmx.connector | Specifies service URL to use for creating new JMX Connector. Takes effect when property jmx.enable is set to true. If this parameter is not specified and jmx.discovery is set to true, then URL service:jmx:jmxmp://localhost is be used, some available port is chosen and resulting JMX Service URL is registered through SLP to enable Discovery. |
| org.quartz.threadPool.threadsInheritContextClassLoaderOfInitializingThread | Can be true or false. The default value is false. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |
