<a id="job-scheduler"></a>

##### Job Scheduler

> 📄 Source: [Book_Job_Scheduler.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Job_Scheduler.htm)

###### Job Scheduler


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="scheduled-jobs-overview"></a>

##### Scheduled Jobs Overview

> 📄 Source: [Scheduled_Jobs_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Scheduled_Jobs_Overview.htm)

###### Scheduled Jobs Overview


Note Starting XEServer 9.3.1, due to the change of licensing model of newer versions of Elasticsearch and necessity to include latest security patches in Edifecs products, Elasticsearch backend used by Job Scheduler Server service has been replaced with OpenSearch. Please visit [this topic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Migrate_from_Elasticsearch_to_OpenSearch.htm) to get more details about performing the necessary data migration.


With the Scheduled Jobs feature, you can create schedules to send commands to XEServer profiles. These commands can be used to control XEServer profiles (START, STOP, RESET, SHUTDOWN) or to initiate data processing on a specific XEServer route. The functionality is similar to the Timer Service and Timer Reporting component, however the main difference is that Job Scheduler can run outside of the profile that carries the main business logic whereas Timer Services must run within the same profile. This fact allows Job Scheduler to work continuously and not be interrupted even if the profile with main business logic stops.


Note You can have only one Job Scheduler Server and Job Scheduler Client in one profile at a time.


###### Jobs and Commands


The core concept of the Scheduled Jobs feature is a Job - a process thread that runs in the Job Scheduler Server service, generates special commands and sends them to an outer system on a timely basis. You can manage jobs on the XESManager [Scheduled Jobs](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Scheduled_Jobs_Page.htm) page. A job can generate commands of two types:

- Trigger: Used to initiate data processing in a specific profile.
- Control: Used to START, STOP, SHUTDOWN, or RESET an XEServer profile.


You can also trigger the out-of-schedule job manually. This can be useful when you have to restart an XEServer route without waiting for an automated planned restart. In this case, you do not have to change the schedule of the day for an unplanned trigger of the route.


###### Scheduled Jobs Components


The Scheduled Jobs feature includes the following components:

- XEServer services: - [Job Scheduler Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Server_Service.htm): Deploys a server that handles all time-related logic and sends commands to other XEServer profiles. - [Job Scheduler Client](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Client_Service.htm): Listens for incoming commands of the type Trigger. When a command is received, the service activates the corresponding route component [Job Scheduler](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler.htm) - the intended recipient of the command.
- Route component [Job Scheduler](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler.htm): Creates an XEServer message exchange on a route based on the received command.
- XESManager [Scheduled Jobs](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Scheduled_Jobs_Page.htm) page: Provides a user interface to manage the Scheduled Jobs settings.
- XESManager [Scheduled Jobs Monitoring](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Job_Monitoring_Page.htm) page: Provides a user interface for monitoring the execution of jobs in several Job Scheduler Servers.
- XESManager Scheduler Alerts: An XESManager alert type used for notification about job execution.

---

<a id="job-scheduler-server-service"></a>

##### Job Scheduler Server Service

> 📄 Source: [Job_Scheduler_Server_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Server_Service.htm)

###### Job Scheduler Server Service


The service Job Scheduler Server performs two major functions:

- Provides a storage for job configurations (schedules) and tracking data. To manage jobs, use the XESManager [Scheduled Jobs](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Scheduled_Jobs_Page.htm) page. Job Scheduler Server polls XESManager at regular intervals for a configuration update, so when you change a job configuration in XESManager, the Job Scheduler Server service pulls the updates on the next poll. The service uses a database to store job configurations data and performs all the necessary database initialization on the first service startup. If you want to initialize a database for this service manually, use the preconfigured SQL scripts. The service stores job tracking data in an OpenSearch node storage. The service can use either an embedded instance of OpenSearch (an instance that runs within the profile process) or a standalone OpenSearch installation.
- Sends commands to Job Scheduler Client services (or to XEServer profiles). These commands can be of two types: - Trigger: A command that is sent from Job Scheduler Server to a profile with the Job Scheduler Client service. Such a command can be sent directly to the route component Job Scheduler to initiate data processing on an XEServer route. - Control: A command that is sent from Job Scheduler Server to any XEServer profile or route. Used to send commands such as START, STOP, SHUTDOWN, and RESET to XEServer. The service uses the system time of the computer where the corresponding profile runs.


Note When you use Job Scheduler Servers in the cluster mode (when several Job Scheduler Servers work with the same database) and one of the Job Scheduler Servers is running on the same computer along with XESManager, make sure that in the Job Scheduler Server configuration settings, the name of the host in XES Manager host has been specified as an external IP of this computer and not as "localhost".


When the service is added to a profile, you can view the following settings:



###### General


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Job provider | |
| XESManager host | Enter the XESManager host to pull job configurations. |
| Access Token | Specify an access token to access XESManager. This token is required to secure the access to jobs data and can be generated on the XESManager [Scheduler Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Scheduled_Jobs_Page.htm#Server_Authentication) page. |
| Update interval | Set a time interval in seconds at which the service requests XESManager for jobs configuration change. If a job configuration changes in XESManager, the service pulls the updates on the next interval tick. |
| SSL Settings In this section, you can provide SSL settings to allow your Job Scheduler Server to communicate with XESManager through the SSL-protected channel. To establish the SSL connection, the XESManager's digital certificate must be present in the truststore used by the Job Scheduler Server service. | |
| Use default SSL settings | If you select this option, the service uses the default Java truststore to search for XESManager's certificate. |
| Enable hostname validation | If you select this option, the service compares the name of XESManager host with the host name from the certificate that XESManager sends to Job Scheduler Server. If the names do not match, the connection can not be established. |
| Trusted Store | Specify a path and a password to the truststore with the XESManager's certificate. Tip By default, the service uses the truststore ${XESRoot}/platform/security/xes.manager.truststore.jks. This truststore already contains the certificate of XESManager that is installed on the local computer. |
| Job storage | |
| URL | Enter a JDBC URL to the database that stores latest job configurations. |
| User name | Enter the database user name. |
| Password | Enter the database user password. |



###### Callback Server Tab


On this tab, you provide connection settings to allow Job Scheduler Clients to notify the Job Scheduler Server about jobs execution outcome. For more information, see [Scheduled Jobs Callback Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Callback_Server.htm).


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| HTTP Options | |
| Host | Enter the IP address of the Job Scheduler Server to which Job Scheduler Clients should send callbacks after job execution. |
| Port | Enter the port number used for receiving callbacks. |
| SSL | |
| Enable SSL | Select to enable the Secure Sockets Layer to provide secure access to your web server. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. |
| Keystore | |
| Path | Enter the path to a java key store file. |
| Password | Enter the password to a java key store file. |
| Key Password | Enter the password to a private key. |
| Enable client authentication | Select to enable client authentication for accessing the server. |
| Trusted store | |
| Path | Enter the path to a key store with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to a key store with trusted certificates. |
| Check certificate expiration | If set to True, the expiration date of the trust certificate is checked and an exception is thrown if an expired certificate is encountered. |



###### Tracking Tab


On this tab, you can define which OpenSearch storage to use for storing job tracking data and define purge strategy for your storage.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Storage type | Job Scheduler Server stores all job tracking data in OpenSearch storage. By default, such storage is deployed locally, however a standalone OpenSearch storage can be used. Select Embedded to deploy a default OpenSearch storage inside the Job Scheduler Server. Select Remote if you want to work with a standalone OpenSearch storage installation. When you work with a standalone OpenSearch storage, your storage should have the preconfigured OpenSearch cluster "opensearch". The Job Scheduler Server uses the default "opensearch" cluster name when connecting to a storage. This value cannot be changed and is used for creating a default cluster during OpenSearch installation. |
| Host | Enter the host name with running standalone OpenSearch storage. |
| Port | Enter the HTTP port for accessing the standalone OpenSearch storage. Note To connect to a standalone OpenSearch storage, you should use the HTTP port which is 9200 by default, but not the TCP (9300) port. |
| History cleanup | |
| Enable cleanup | Select this option to perform regular cleanups of your OpenSearch storage. |
| Start time | Select the time of the day to start storage cleanup process. |
| Stop time | Select the time of the day to stop storage cleanup process. |
| Store period (days) | Enter the number of days that should pass before a record can be removed from the storage during regular cleanups. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |



###### Use external database


By default, the Job Scheduler Server service uses an embedded H2 database engine to store job configurations. This database is sufficient for testing purposes, however, you have to connect to an enterprise-level database for the production environment or if you run XEServer profile configurations in [container mode](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_in_Containerized_Environments.htm).


You can configure the Job Scheduler Server service to store job configurations in the following external databases:

 MS SQL Database


Follow the instructions below if you want to connect the Job Scheduler Server service to an MS SQL database.


To connect to MS SQL database:

1. In the Job storage section of the Job Scheduler Server service configuration, specify the JDBC URL `jdbc:sqlserver://${HOST}:${PORT};databaseName=${DBName};encrypt=true;trustServerCertificate=true;` as well as the user name and password of the database user.
2. Click Save to save the changes.
3. Drop the driver JAR [mssql-jdbc-{version}.jre11.jar](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/jdbc/download-microsoft-jdbc-driver-for-sql-server?view=sql-server-2017) into the following directory: - for Windows: %XESRoot%\platform\lib.user\jdbc - for Linux: ${XESRoot}/platform/lib.user/jdbc
4. Restart the Job Scheduler Server service.





 Oracle Database


Follow the instructions below if you want to connect the Job Scheduler Server service to an Oracle database.


###### To connect to Oracle database:

1. In the Job storage section of the Job Scheduler Server service configuration, specify the JDBC URL `jdbc:oracle:thin:@${HOST}:${PORT}:scheduler` as well as the user name and password of the database user.
2. Click Save to save the changes.
3. [Download](https://www.oracle.com/database/technologies/appdev/jdbc-downloads.html) the Oracle JDBC driver (version 19.7 or later) and put the ojdbc{version}.jar to:
4. Restart the Job Scheduler Server service.





 PostgreSQL Database


Follow the instructions below if you want to connect the Job Scheduler Server service to a PostgreSQL (Postgres) relational database management system (RDBMS).


###### To connect to PostgreSQL RDBMS:

1. In the Job storage section of the Job Scheduler Server service configuration, specify the JDBC URL `jdbc:postgresql://${HOST}:${PORT}/scheduler` as well as the user name and password of the database user.
2. Click Save to save the changes.
3. Drop the driver [PostgreSQL JDBC 4.1 Driver, {version}.jar](https://jdbc.postgresql.org/download/) into the following directory: - for Windows: %XESRoot%\platform\lib.user\jdbc - for Linux: ${XESRoot}/platform/lib.user/jdbc
4. Restart the Job Scheduler Server service.






###### Initialize the database


The Job Scheduler Server service creates database structures on the first service startup. However, if the database user you specified does not have permissions to create database structures, you need to run the script tables_<db-name>.sql from the following directory as a database administrator:

- for Windows: %XESRoot%\sql-scripts\scheduler
- for Linux: ${XESRoot}/sql-scripts/scheduler


The script creates all the required tables in an existing database.


Note Before you run the script, make sure that the role of the user on whose behalf you are going to run this script has access to the schema where you are going to create tables. Also, you have to set a search path to the required schema using the query `SET search_path TO schema_name`.

---

<a id="job-scheduler-client-service"></a>

##### Job Scheduler Client Service

> 📄 Source: [Job_Scheduler_Client_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Client_Service.htm)

###### Job Scheduler Client Service


The service is used as an intermediate layer for the Job Scheduler component for receiving commands of the Trigger type from Job Scheduler Server services. One Job Scheduler Client can be used by multiple Job Scheduler components.


The Job Scheduler Client service does not require any explicit configuration as all the orchestration and management are done through XES Manager and the XEServer Job Scheduler Server service. Please refer to the [Job Scheduler Server service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Server_Service.htm) manual for more details about configuration.


When the service is added to a profile, you can view the following settings:



###### General


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Callback Tab


A callback is a response from the Job Scheduler Client used to notify the Job Scheduler Server that the job task has been completed or failed (if some errors occurred). On this tab, you can enable the SSL connection for sending callbacks to the Job Scheduler Server.


Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Trusted Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to a key store with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password for accessing the trusted store. |
| Key Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to the file which contains key store certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password for accessing the keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to a private key. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="job-scheduler-callback-server"></a>

##### Job Scheduler Callback Server

> 📄 Source: [Job_Scheduler_Callback_Server.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Job_Scheduler_Callback_Server.htm)

###### Job Scheduler Callback Server


When a Job Scheduler Server job sends a Trigger or Control type command to a Job Scheduler Client, the latter responds with a callback to notify the server whether the job task has been successfully performed. As soon as the Job Scheduler Server receives the callback, it can mark the job with corresponding status. However, you should pay attention at using proper network addresses for sending callbacks when working in multiple-network environment or using multiple network interfaces. When deploying the Job Scheduler Server and the Job Scheduler Client in different networks, you should ensure that IP addresses used for callback server are available for all Job Scheduler Clients.


For better understanding of the potential issue and to avoid one in your business environment, consider the following sample workflow:



There are 2 separate networks: N1, and N2. There is a Computer 1 in the network N1. This computer has XESManager and XEServer with running Job Scheduler Server service (Profile 1).


The Computer 1 has a VPN connection to the network N2. Thus, XESManager on the Computer 1 can control XEServer instances in both N1 and N2 networks. The Computer 1 IP address in the network N1 is N1P1, and in the network N2 is N2P2.


There is a Computer 2 in the network N1 that has installed XEServer with the Scheduled Client Service (Profile 2).


Step 1 A job in the Scheduled Server Service (Profile 1) sends a command message to Scheduler Client Service (Profile 2).


Step 2 The Scheduler Client Service (Profile 2) receives the command, successfully performs the job task, and attempts to send a callback to the Scheduler Server Service (Profile 1).


Step 3 However, the callback cannot be sent successfully, since the Scheduler Server Client attempts to send the callback to the IP address of the VPN interface (N2P2 ), which is available only for computers in the N2 network.


The reason of such behavior is following:


When you use the default callback server (0.0.0.0) in the Scheduler Server Service, Java API dynamically picks an IP address from the list of currently available network interfaces during the run-time. Thus, if you have two or more network interfaces or VPN connections on your computer, the callback server IP address can be resolved with a priority that may not be suitable for you. For this reason, you should set the IP address of your callback server explicitly, and ensure that your Scheduler Server Client and Scheduler Server Server can communicate through this IP address.


To do this, enter an IP address that should be used for sending callbacks on the Callback Server tab of the Job Scheduler Server:



The Host field has two purposes:

1. It is used as a part of the callback URL to which Job Scheduler Clients send callbacks.
2. The Job Scheduler Server listens the entered IP address for callbacks from Job Scheduler Clients.


If you use the default IP address (0.0.0.0), the actual IP is resolved by Java API and can be probably unavailable for the current network. Also, the Scheduler Server will listen IP addresses of all interfaces, available on the computer.


If you use an explicit IP address (for example, 127.0.0.1), Job Scheduler Clients send callbacks to localhost and the Job Scheduler Server listens only to localhost's interface.


If you enter an IP address that is not in the list of available network interfaces, a warning is displayed.

---

<a id="high-availability-mode"></a>

##### High Availability Mode

> 📄 Source: [High_Availability_Mode.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/High_Availability_Mode.htm)

###### High Availability Mode


By default, all job configurations and tracking data are stored in a single separate Job Scheduler Server service. A failure in such Job Scheduler Server can lead to malfunction of all processes associated with business tasks performed by this Job Scheduler Server. You can handle this issue by restarting the service and applying suitable Recovery Strategy. In this case, the only harm to your business environment is a minor time delay required for restarting the service. This typical solution covers the majority of business needs.


However, if thorough accuracy and precision is vital in your business environment and operations triggered by the Job Scheduler Server must be performed accurate up to a second, a good practice to follow is running Scheduled Job Servers in high availability mode (HA mode). The High Availability Mode is a special disposition of XEServers, XESManager, standalone database and OpenSearch storage, which in combination provide high reliability for the scheduled operations to be performed.


The following diagram illustrates basic concept of high availability mode:



In this mode, there can be several computers with Job Scheduler Servers configured to use a standalone database (MS SQL or Oracle) for storing job configuration and standalone OpenSearch storage for storing job tracking data. In this case, if one Scheduler Server fails, for example, Scheduler Server 1, the other (Scheduler Server 2) still has access to both database and OpenSearch storage and continues to work while the failed server is restarting.


Consider the following recommendations when working with Job Scheduler Server in HA mode:

- All Job Scheduler Server services should run in different XEServer profiles.
- Use OpenSearch v. 2.5. OpenSearch should be started in the standalone mode.
- For connecting to a standalone OpenSearch storage, use a TCP port number. Ensure that Job Scheduler Server can access your OpenSearch installation.
- XEServer profiles with Job Scheduler Servers should have [Restart mode](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Route_Component_QoS_Settings.htm) set to Crash recovery to guarantee persistent job execution.
- To compensate failed job firings (for example, all Job Scheduler Servers were down for some period of time), set corresponding Recovery strategy for your jobs.
- Use XESManager alerts to get instant notifications about unexpected errors.
- Consider time difference if your computers are located in different time zones. System time should be synchronized on all computers with Job Scheduler Server.
- Scheduler Server configurations distributed among different computers should be identical. They should have identical and explicit (not "localhost") addresses to the OpenSearch storage, to the H2 database, and to XESManager.


The listed recommendations grant high level of reliability for your system, however even observing all these recommendations, you should still consider potential network or database failures.


If XESmanager fails or becomes unavailable, the Job Scheduler Server uses the last successfully cached configuration.

---

<a id="tracking-and-duplication-service"></a>

#### Tracking and Duplication Service

> 📄 Source: [Book_Tracking_And_Duplicates_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Tracking_And_Duplicates_Service.htm)

##### Tracking and Duplication Service


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="tracking-and-duplication-overview"></a>

##### Tracking and Duplication Overview

> 📄 Source: [Tracking_and_Duplicates_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Tracking_and_Duplicates_Overview.htm)

###### Tracking and Duplicates Overview


Note Starting XEServer 9.3.1, due to the change of licensing model of newer versions of Elasticsearch and necessity to include latest security patches in Edifecs products, Elasticsearch backend used by Tracking and Duplication Server service has been replaced with OpenSearch. Please visit [this topic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Migrate_from_Elasticsearch_to_OpenSearch.htm) to get more details about performing the necessary data migration.


With the Tracking and Duplicates functionality, you can check to see if incoming business data items (for example, claims) have been previously processed. The Tracking and Duplicates feature includes the following basic components:

- The route component [Tracking and Duplicates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Tracking_and_Duplicates.htm): Used to pick up data from a route and pass the data onto [Tracking and Duplicates Client Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Tracking_and_Duplicates_Client_Service.htm). When the check for duplication is complete, the component sends the results back to the route through one of the outputs based on the verification results.
- XEServer Tracking and Duplicates services. There are two major XEServer services: 1. [Tracking and Duplicates Server Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Tracking_and_Duplicates_Server_Service.htm): The core processing unit that performs actual data verification. The service stores information about incoming data items in the form of a unique set of keys and attributes in [OpenSearch storage](https://opensearch.org/docs/latest/about/). If a data item passes through the service repeatedly, the service finds an existing key-attributes record and marks the data as a duplicate. Considering potentially large volumes of data to be stored by the service, the service can run in a cluster mode or can use multiple OpenSearch instances to split processing and storage efforts among multiple physical workstations. The service exposes the RESTful API that you can use to directly interact with the service without using the route component. 2. [Tracking and Duplicates Client Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Tracking_and_Duplicates_Client_Service.htm): This service acts as a bridge between the route component and the Tracking and Duplicates Server Service. The service is associated with one Tracking and Duplicates route component and passes data from the component to Tracking and Duplicates Server Service using the HTTP(S) protocol.
- The Tracking and Duplicate Monitoring Page in XESManager: A monitoring facility that allows you to track processed data and to search for registered or duplicate data items.

---

<a id="configure-tracking-and-duplication-client-service"></a>

##### Configure Tracking and Duplication Client Service

> 📄 Source: [Configure_Tracking_and_Duplicates_Client_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Tracking_and_Duplicates_Client_Service.htm)

###### Configure Tracking and Duplicates Client Service


With Tracking and Duplicates Client Service, you can connect one of the Tracking and Duplicates route components with Tracking and Duplicates Server Service using the HTTP(S) protocol. To configure the Tracking and Duplicates Server Service, see[Configure Duplication Check Server Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Tracking_and_Duplicates_Server_Service.htm).


When you add the service to a profile, the following settings are displayed:



###### General settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| RESTful WebService URL | Enter the URL that points to the required Tracking and Duplicates Server service. |
| Enable hostname validation | Select this option to verify the specified host name for validity. |
| SSL Mode | Select the authentication mode. Note that the Server Authentication mode is complex and secure, because your data is transferred in an encrypted form. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. |
| Trusted Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to a key store with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Truststore. |
| Key Store | |
| Path | Enter the path to the file that contains Keystore certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the private key. |



###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies Tab


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information Tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

---

<a id="configure-tracking-and-duplication-server-service"></a>

##### Configure Tracking and Duplication Server Service

> 📄 Source: [Tracking_and_Duplicates_Server_Service.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Tracking_and_Duplicates_Server_Service.htm)

###### Tracking and Duplicates Server Service


With Tracking and Duplicates Server Service, you can store information about business items that have been processed and verify the incoming data for duplication. The server registers incoming data if the data has not been processed earlier or fires a notification if the data has been processed already.


The information that identifies a data item is stored in the OpenSearch storage as a combination of key, attribute, and source properties. The key is used for message ID calculation, attributes describe the details of the message. The source properties contain information about the source location of the received data.


When you add the service to a profile, the following settings are displayed:



###### General Settings


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Service** | Enter a name for the service. This name is used to display the service in the EAM Outline tab. |
| **Type** | The type of the XEServer service. This cannot be changed. |
| **Status** | Select to enable or disable the service. If enabled you can use a condition (written in Expression language) that evaluates to TRUE/FALSE and defines whether the corresponding route or service must start together with the profile (TRUE) or not (FALSE). The expression can use only system macros. |
| **Path** | The read-only location of the XEServer service within a profile. Tip: Use the EAM Outline tab to arrange services within a profile. You can create new folders and drag-and-drop services into folders. |
| **Auto start** | Select Off if you do not want the service to start automatically on profile startup. If you want the service to start together with the profile, select On with startup level and specify the startup priority index which is a value between 1 and 100. The lower the value, the higher the priority. Services with a higher priority are started first. You cannot use the startup level "0" as it is a reserved value used for system services. |



###### Configuration Tab


All data that has been checked for duplication is stored in the OpenSearch storage. The Tracking and Duplicates Server can use the storage in three modes:

- Embedded: Use to store data in a local OpenSearch storage deployed within XEServer.
- Remote: Use to store data in a standalone OpenSearch storage deployed on a remote computer. If you have a running OpenSearch instance and an XEServer instance on different computers, ensure that you have the same Java versions installed on all the computers.
- Cluster: Use to store data on more than one computer that runs XEServer Tracking and Duplicates Server services.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| RESTful WebService name | Enter a relative path that is appended to the base URL of the Tracking and Duplicates Server RESTful web service. To connect Tracking and Duplicates Client Service to Tracking and Duplicates Server Service and to submit data, the Server URL of the corresponding [Tracking and Duplicates Client Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configure_Tracking_and_Duplicates_Client_Service.htm) should point to this address. |
| Reporting | |
| Store processing statistics | Select to store statistic information (details about the number of registered items, number of duplicates, storage size, and so on) from this Tracking and Duplicates Server Service in the ${XESRoot}/statistics directory. This statistic is used for further analysis in XESManager. |
| Save storage status to log | Select this option to enable logging of cluster events to the ${XESRoot}/profiles/${YourProfileName}/log/dupcheck.status.log file that contains information about cluster name, cluster status, and garbage collector execution. In the Remote mode, the garbage collector execution within OpenSearch is not logged. |
| Storage mode: Embedded | |
| Node name | Enter a name for the embedded OpenSearch node storage. |
| Data folder | Enter the path to the folder that contains the OpenSearch database with all the information about registered items. |
| Storage mode: Remote In this mode, you can connect to already deployed and preconfigured local and remote OpenSearch installations. | |
| Cluster name | Enter a name for a group of computers that have the running OpenSearch storage you plan to connect to. |
| SSL settings Cluster nodes can communicate with each other through a secure SSL connection that you can configure in this section. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. | |
| Enabled | Select to enable the SSL connection for this Tracking and Duplicates Server Service. |
| Authenticate cluster nodes | Select this option to perform the authentication of nodes in the cluster. Note If a certificate of some node is not validated and a secure connection is not established, then that node will not be able to act as a part of the cluster and data will not be backed up. |
| Keystore Path | Unlike Tracking and Duplicates Client Service that has a TrustedStore and a KeyStore, here the Keystore should contain a private key, a certificate of the current node, and the certificates of all the other nodes in the cluster. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the private key. |
| Cluster nodes In this pane, you can specify the addresses of remote computers that have a running OpenSearch storage so that this instance of Tracking and Duplicates Server Service is able to access them on the network. To do this, specify a host name or IP address and an HTTP port number of the remote computers in the Cluster Nodes pane. | |
| Load the nodes list from the cluster | Select this option to get a list of the available remote computers from the cluster. This option is intended to be used in the following way: Assume there are four interconnected remote computers with running OpenSearch storage nodes that you want to use to store Tracking and Duplicates data. All four storage nodes are linked and can communicate with each other. In the Cluster Nodes pane, you specify the IP address and the HTTP port number of only one of the four computers (For example, 10.30.xx.xxx:9200, later referred as PC1) to access one OpenSearch storage. In this case, during initialization, XEServer Tracking and Duplicates Server Service makes a call to the OpenSearch storage node running on PC1 and this storage node returns a list of running OpenSearch nodes (a map of IP addresses and port numbers) which are currently visible to PC1. When data starts to flow, XEServer Tracking and Duplicates Server Service loops through the returned list of nodes and sends each new storage request to the next node in the list. This way, the disk storage load is evenly distributed among OpenSearch nodes. If some of the nodes go down or recover, OpenSearch automatically reorganizes the cluster and you do not have to handle the list of available nodes manually. The main requirement in this configuration is that PC1 should always be available, otherwise, the system does not function. However, you must pay special attention to remote computers located in an isolated network, for example a VPN, or a local network. When Tracking and Duplicates Server Service makes a call to the OpenSearch node on PC1 in order to obtain a list of OpenSearch nodes, the PC1 node responds with a list of addresses which are only accessible only to PC1, but not to Tracking and Duplicates Server Service (for example, a list of internal network addresses 192.168.xxx.xxx), then Tracking and Duplicates Server Service will not be able to send storage requests to these addresses, so this option does not work for this particular environment. |
| Storage mode: Cluster In this mode, you can group multiple Tracking and Duplicates Server services running on different computers into a cluster. | |
| Node settings | |
| Node name | Enter a name for the cluster node represented by this Tracking and Duplicates Server service. The name must be unique within a cluster. |
| Cluster name | Enter a name for the cluster that uses several Tracking and Duplicates Server services. |
| Data folder | Enter the path to the folder that has the OpenSearch database with all the information about registered items. |
| Host | Enter a host name or IP address using which other nodes in the cluster can access this node. For example, if you use the 192.168.0.11 value for a node, this node can be accessed only through a network interface that has the 192.168.0.11 IP address assigned to it and all the other nodes in the cluster must connect to this node using the same 192.168.0.11 IP address. If you use the 0.0.0.0 (default value) for a node, this node can be accessed through any network interface (if there are more than one installed on the computer) installed on the computer that hosts this node. All other nodes can connect to this node using the IP address of any network interface. |
| Port | The port number used for the connection. |
| Number of replicas | OpenSearch replicas are independent storage areas that allow the storing of data redundantly. Replicas are used to provide a failover mechanism in case a node goes offline or becomes unavailable. Use this parameter to provide failover mechanism for your cluster. For example, if there are two nodes in the cluster and the number of replicas is set to "1", then each node contains a complete copy of the data from another node. The OpenSearch cluster continues working if one node becomes unavailable. Note The more replicas there are in your cluster, the larger the size of data stored in OpenSearch storage. |
| Index distribution factor | Specifies the maximum number of nodes among which you can distribute the data. For example, if the OpenSearch cluster contains two nodes and the index distribution factor is set to "2", the data is distributed evenly between the two nodes. If the OpenSearch storage contains 2 nodes and the index distribution factor is set to "3", the data is organized in the following way: one node stores 66% of data, and the other stores 34%. Note Use this parameter in combination with the Number of replicas parameter, otherwise the whole OpenSearch cluster looses its failover capabilities. This parameter can only be set when you create a new node (during the first start of Tracking and Duplicates Server service) and cannot be changed later. If you ever decide to change the data distribution strategy later, you will have to re-create the node. |
| SSL settings The communication between cluster nodes can be performed through a secure SSL connection that you can configure in this pane. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. | |
| Enabled | Select to enable the Secure Sockets Layer to establish secure access to the node. |
| Authenticate cluster nodes | Select this option to allow the authentication of nodes in the cluster. Note If the certificate of a node is not validated and secure connection not established, that node cannot act as a part of the cluster and data cannot be backed up. |
| Keystore Path | Unlike Tracking and Duplicates Client Service that has a Trustedstore and a Keystore, here the Keystore contains a private key, a certificate of the current node, and the certificates of all the other nodes in the cluster. |
| Password | Enter the password to access the Keystore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the private key. |
| Cluster Nodes | |
| In this pane, you can specify the addresses of remote computers with a running XEServer Tracking and Duplicates Server Service storage so that multiple Tracking and Duplicates Server Services can interact in the cluster mode. To do this, specify a host name or IP address and a port number for each of the remote computers. For each node in the cluster, for Host and Port fields, use the values specified in the Node settings section of the corresponding Tracking and Duplicates Server service as shown in the image: Note If a cluster node has its Host value set to 0.0.0.0 (default value), you must use an external IP address of the computer that runs the corresponding Tracking and Duplicates Server service. If the value is set to localhost, this node cannot be accessed from a remote computer because in this case the OpenSearch node uses the loopback interface available only from the same computer. We recommend that you specify the full list of cluster nodes for each node, linking the nodes in a many-to-many model for maximum reliability. For example, if your cluster contains four nodes, you should specify the addresses of the other three nodes in each Tracking and Duplicates Server service: | |



###### Namespaces Tab


Namespaces are used for aggregating items in groups based on a certain parameter. You can manage these parameters by specifying the corresponding fields in the Namespaces pane.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Namespace | Enter a name for your namespace. |
| Description | A brief description to describe the namespace. |
| Expiration | Set a timeout after which registered items with the specified namespace are deleted. |



Warning You cannot have two same named attributes of different data types that belong to different Tracking and Duplicates namespaces (this is also applicable for keys and sources). If you appear to have such attributes as a result of the migration from a previous XEServer version (prior to 9.1), you will get the following exception: com.edifecs.common.dupcheck.exceptions.DupCheckException.


###### Mapping settings


Each item to be registered or verified includes three lists of properties: Key, Attribute, Source. In this section, you can specify which data types to use for storing these properties in the OpenSearch storage. Note that appropriate data type choice significantly compresses data and saves disk space.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Category | Select the category (Key, Attribute, or Source) that contains the field to be mapped. |
| Field | Enter the name of the field to be mapped. |
| Type | Select an appropriate data type to store the field. |



###### Purge Settings


On this tab, you can set the schedule to delete expired items.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Purge schedule | Select Always to set Tracking and Duplicates Server to continuously scan for expired items and delete them on detection. Select Custom to define a time frame within which the service scans OpenSearch storage for expired items. |
| Purge start time | The time for the purge service to start scanning the storage for expired items. |
| Purge stop time | The time for the purge service to stop scanning the storage for expired items. |



###### Storage Optimization


On this tab, you can optimize the currently used OpenSearch storage. All data in the storage is distributed among segments and the more segments there are in the storage, the poorer the search engine performance is likely to be. You can optimize the OpenSearch storage segmentation by merging segments.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Storage optimization | Select to enable/disable the optimization of your storage. |
| Cron schedule | Set a schedule for running the storage optimization process. Click to open [Time Expression Editor](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Time_Expression_Editor.htm). |
| Optimize only storage segments containing items marked for deletion. | Select this option to merge only those OpenSearch segments that contain expired records. |



Note The optimization process is very time-consuming and can take several days. Plan the optimization of OpenSearch storage to avoid an adverse impact on your business environment.


###### Jetty Tab


The service is built on top of the [Jetty](https://www.eclipse.org/jetty/) web server (version 10.0.16) and has the following Jetty settings:


Note In XEServer 9.3.3, Jetty server library has been updated to the version 10.0.16 to address security vulnerabilities. If you used XEServer components based on Jetty server, view the [backward compatibility instructions](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm) you might need to follow when upgrading to XEServer 9.3.3 and later.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Jetty Server | Select the type of Jetty server to use. In the Platform mode, the service uses a default instance of a Jetty web server that can be used by several XEServer services. This Jetty server is preconfigured and most of the settings are hidden. In the Dedicated mode, a separate instance of a Jetty web server starts together with the service. This approach is more flexible as it allows you to provide specific settings for your web service (HTTP host and port, SSL settings, HTTP Authentication, and so on). |
| HTTP options | |
| Host | Enter the host name or the IP address of the server. |
| Port | Enter the port number to be used for the connection. |
| Thread pool size | The maximum number of process threads the service can handle simultaneously to process incoming requests. |
| SSL | |
| Enable SSL | Select to true/false to enable/disable the Secure Sockets Layer to provide secure access to your web service. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. You can also use macro expressions to enabled/disable connections through the SSL protocol. |
| Enable SNI host check | When the hostname check is disabled (false) in Server Name Indication (SNI), you will not receive a 400 Invalid SNI response if the `Host` header from the request does not match the Common Name (CN) or Subject Alternative Name (SAN) values in the Jetty server certificate. For more information, click [here](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Jetty_Server_Upgrade.htm#Host_check). Default value: false. Note Starting with EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value has been changed to false. Prior to EAM 9.3.3.1, the property’s default value was true. |
|  KeystoreA keystore contains cryptographic keys (private and public key pairs) used to verify the identity of parties that send requests to your web service. | |
| Path | Enter the path to the KeyStore file that contains the key pair entries. |
| Password | Enter the password to the KeyStore. |
| Key password | Enter the password to the key pair. |
|  TruststoreA truststore contains digital certificates of the connecting parties to verify their identity. | |
| Enable client authentication | Select to enable the client authentication. If the client authentication is enabled, for incoming request Jetty asks the client to provide a digital certificate, and then compares the provided certificate with those stored in the truststore. If the provided certificate is present in the truststore, the client authentication succeeds. |
| Path | Enter the path to the TrustedStore with trusted certificates. |
| Password | Enter the password to the TrustedStore. |
| Check certificate expiration | If set to True, the expiration date of the trust certificate is checked and an exception is thrown if an expired certificate is encountered. |
|  Authentication | |
| Type | The service supports the following authentication options: [Basic](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7617): In this mode, the Base64-encoded user name and password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. [Digest](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7616): In this mode, the plain text user name and a hashed password must be sent to the server within the Authorization header. OAuth: In this mode, RESTful Web Service authenticates users by verifying JWT tokens within the Authorization header. **Note** The Authentication Type can also be defined as an environment property in the profile's Environment tab and used as a macro for specifying the authorization type. For example, create the environment property AuthType=Basic, and use the ${AuthType} macro as option for the Type field. |
| Audit | In this section, you can enable the generation of audit events by the Jetty web server. In Service name, specify the name of the XEServer [Audit Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Service.htm) responsible for handling the audit events. |



###### OAuth Authentication Type

 Click to view OAuth Settings


| Field | Option |
| --- | --- |
| Confirmation (cnf) claim XEServer implementation of the Jetty server supports [OAuth 2.0 mutual Transport Layer Security (TLS) authentication with X.509 certificates](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705). Mutual TLS (mTLS) is an authentication type where both the server and client have a certificate and both authenticate each other using the TLS protocol. Such an authentication method provides an additional layer of security and helps prevent various kinds of attacks, such as credential stuffing, malicious API requests, and others. For the Jetty server (resource server) to verify the legitimacy of the client that sends a token, authorization server embeds the certificate hash within a JWT-formatted access token as a cnf ("confirmation") claim when client connects to authorization server. The following is the cnf claim example: Example of "cnf" claim in certificate-bound token ``` { ... "client_id": "6344df61-2foo-4429-9557-6b2979a138f7", "cnf": { "x5t#S256": "Avc6NtXrtcjJsf0zZacl2MCETnUOMu59cz3H4ohh4-o" } ... } ``` The resource server then compares this certificate hash to a hash of the client certificate used for authentication. If the hashes do not match, the request is rejected. Note [Mutual-TLS endpoint aliases](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8705#name-metadata-for-mutual-tls-end) are not supported in this release of XEServer. In the Confirmation (cnf) claim section of OAuth authentication type settings, you can configure the Jetty-based XEServer service to request the cnf claim in access tokens for verification. | |
| Confirmation claim verification | Select Disabled to disable verification of the cnf claim on the resource server (Jetty server). Select Enabled to force verification of the cnf claim. If token does not contain the cnf claim or certificate hashes do not match, access is rejected. Select If present to verify the cnf claim only if it is present in access token. |
| Certificate source | Connection: For successful authentication, the resource server matches the client certificate used in the access token request during the connection's handshake TLS session against the client certificate configured on the resource server. HTTP header: If a client performs the access token request through a proxy server or load balancer, the resource server uses the request header as the source of client certificate for matching against the configured client certificate. You must provide the name of the Header and the Certificate encoding (URL-encoded, Base64, Thumbprint) of the certificate sent in the request. |
| Token validation method | Select how you want the service to verify JWT tokens: By signature: The service calculates its own signature and compares it with the signature in the token. Service endpoint: Delegates the token verification to XEConnect Authorization service. |
| Token validation method: By signature By using a public key (for RSA) or a secret (for HMAC), the service creates its own signature from the token header and payload. If this signature matches the signature in the token, the token is considered valid. For this method, you should specify a signature algorithm to use and provide a public key or a secret. You can provide a public key from the following sources: you can import a PEM public key from a file. you can specify a JWKS URL that points to the authorization server (for example, XEConnect Authorization service) that issued the token. To access the authorization server, you have to specify your client ID and client secret. As a response, the authorization server returns a public key to verify the token. | |
| Token clock skew | The JWT verification process requires time synchronization on the computer that issued the token and the computer that verifies the token. This option specifies a time leeway to compensate for the possible clock skew on the authorization server (XEConnect Authorization service) and on the computer that verifies the token (XEServer RESTful Web service). For example, an authorization server issues a JWT token at 12:45 UTC, however the clock on the computer that verifies the token is out of sync and shows 12:47 UTC. In this case, the token fails the validation and is marked expired. However, you can compensate for this timing mismatch by setting the property to 180 (3*60s) or more. Tip For security reasons, Edifecs recommends that you do not use a large (hours, days) token skew intervals. |
| Signature algorithm | Select an algorithm to use to verify the token signature. |
| Token validation method: Service endpoint In this mode, XEServer delegates token verification to the XEConnect Authorization service. The service requests XEConnect to verify the token and passes the client ID and the client secret as parameters. The connection to XEConnect must be secured with SSL. | |
| Introspection URL | Specify the [introspection](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7662) URL of the XEConnect Authorization service that performs token verification. You have to specify the corresponding client ID and the client secret as only clients registered in XEConnect [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) can access the introspection endpoint. |
| Client ID | The ID of the client registered in [Client Management](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xeconnect/9.4/Content/Authorization_Clients_Overview.htm) for whom the token has been issued. |
| Secret key | The secret key to protect the client's identity. |
| Required claims | In this section, you can specify the [claims](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4) to be verified in the token. If a claim specified in this pane is not available in the token, the request authorization fails.Tip When you define a new token claim to be verified, you can select from the drop down list of three claims: [Audience](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), [Issuer](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.3), and [Subject](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7519#section-4.1.2). However, you can specify custom claims to be verified. The claim's name and value are case-sensitive and should be the same as they appear in the token. |







###### HTTP Headers Size


By default, Jetty-based XEServer services can handle HTTP requests and responses with headers size up to 8KB. To change this limit, you can declare the following properties on the [Profile Startup](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) or [Profile Environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) tabs. The property values must be specified in bytes.

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`: Use to set the same limit for both request and response HTTP headers.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.request.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP request headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.response.header.size`: Use to set the limit only for HTTP response headers. If set, this property has a higher priority than `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size`.


For example, the following property sets the maximum request/response HTTP headers size to 64KB:

 [Copy](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/javascript:void(0);)

```
com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.header.size=65536
```






###### Custom HTTP Headers


XEServer Jetty-based services can return custom HTTP headers. For this, set the following XEServer environment properties and restart the corresponding profile:

- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.headers.enabled=true`: enables the custom headers feature.
- `com.edifecs.etools.xeserver.jetty.custom.response.header.<headerName>=<headerValue>`: forces Jetty web server to return <headerName>=<headerValue> HTTP header in each response.


Tip Use [global environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Environment_Tab.htm) to force all Jetty-based services in all profiles to send the specified HTTP headers. Alternatively, you can use [profile-level environment](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Environment_Tab.htm) to affect Jetty behavior only within a specific profile.


###### QoS Tab


On this tab, you can specify the behavior of a service by setting an additional Action when the service encounters a critical error. The following additional actions are available:


| Action | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Restart | Restarts the current service after a critical error. Use the Restart delay field to set a time delay in milliseconds before restarting the service. |
| Restart services | Restarts certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be restarted. |
| Stop services | Stops certain services after a critical error. Enter the path to the service(s) that has to be stopped. |
| None | No action is performed after a critical error. |



###### Dependencies


On this tab, you can view the route components associated with the current service. Double-click a component to view the component in the Route Editor.


###### Service Information


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **User name** | The last user to make changes to the service settings. |
| **License owner** | The name of the license owner. |
| **Modified** | The date and time of the last route modification. |
| **Note** | Notes about the service. You can edit this field any time while working on a route. |

<a id="suggested-configurations-and-hardware-requirements"></a>

##### Suggested Configurations and Hardware Requirements

> 📄 Source: [Suggested_Configurations_and_Hardware_Requirements.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Suggested_Configurations_and_Hardware_Requirements.htm)

###### Suggested Configurations and Hardware Requirements


In order to achieve the best performance rates for the Tracking and Duplicates Service, consider the following recommendations:


###### Hardware recommendations:


###### Storage size


The following storage requirements are expected:


| Number of stored items | Required space on HDD |
| --- | --- |
| 100 000 000 | 70 GB* |
| 200 000 000 | 140 GB* |
| 300 000 000 | 210GB* |



* - Storage size is calculated for the 512 byte size items (3 keys and 10 attributes).


###### Memory size


We strongly recommend that you provide a separate computer to run a profile with the Tracking and Duplicates Server Service. The following memory requirements are expected:


| Items processed per day | Recommended RAM size |
| --- | --- |
| 1 000 000 | 8 GB* |
| 10 000 000 | 16 GB* |



* - RAM size is calculated for the 512 byte size items (3 keys and 10 attributes).


The minimum amount of RAM that needs to be allocated to the profile process is 2GB for processing of 1 000 000 items per day and 3 GB for processing of 10 000 000 items per day. On the Startup tab of the profile that contains Tracking and Duplicates Server Service, under the -vmargs flag, set the equal -Xms and -Xmx parameters to define initial and maximum java heap memory pools respectively.


Example


// 1 000 000 items per day(2GB):


-Xms2g


-Xmx2g


 


// 10 000 000 items per day(3GB):


-Xms3g


-Xmx3g


Note Although the -Xms and -Xmx parameters limit the amount of java heap space allocated to the profile process, the actual RAM consumption may exceed the index of allocated memory. To improve performance of some operations OpenSearch loads data from hard drive to a separate RAM area, which is out of java heap memory space allocated to the profile process.


###### Software recommendations


By default, OpenSearch loads Lucene segments to the virtual memory, so it is recommended that you disable file paging in order to minimize the number of swap operations.


To disable file paging on Windows:

1. On Windows, go to Computer/Properties/Advanced system settings/Advanced Tab/Performance Settings/Advanced Tab/Virtual Memory Change.
2. Clear the Automatically manage paging file size for all drives option.
3. Select the No paging file option.


To disable swap on Linux:

1. To disable swap temporarily, execute the following command:sudo swapoff -a


-or-

1. To disable swap permanently, edit the /etc/fstab file and comment out the lines, containing the "swap" string.


In this case, OpenSearch loads data to the RAM so it is limited only by the size of RAM available.

---

<a id="tracking-and-duplication-restful-api"></a>

##### Tracking and Duplication RESTful API

> 📄 Source: [Tracking_and_Duplicates_Restful_API.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Tracking_and_Duplicates_Restful_API.htm)

###### Tracking and Duplicates RESTful API


Apart from working with the Tracking and Duplicates Server Service through the route component you can also make calls and pass commands to the Tracking and Duplicates RESTful API. This may be required if you don't want to use an XEServer route in your business environment. In this case, you can use any REST client application that submits calls to the Tracking and Duplicates Restful API and receives a response with verification results.


To incorporate such strategy in your business, ensure that following requirements are met:

- Your REST calls are sent through the HTTP(s) protocol to the Restful Service, defined on the Configuration tab of the Tracking and Duplicates Server Service.
- The passed command matches the URL pattern and HTTP method. All commands are listed in the following table:


| Command | URL pattern | Method | Processed HTTP status |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *Check | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/namespaces/Default Namespace/items?check | POST | 200 / 409 |
| *Register | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/namespaces/Default Namespace/items | POST | 201 / 409 |
| Clear namespace | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/namespaces/Default Namespace?user= | DELETE | 200 |
| *Get duplicates statistics | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/statistics/duplicates?request= | GET | 200 |
| Get actions history | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/history?limit= | GET | 200 |
| Get namespaces | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/namespaces | GET | 200 |
| Get namespaces statistics | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/statistics/namespaces | GET | 200 |
| Get original item | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/namespaces/<Default Namespace>/items/<ID> | GET | 200 / 410 |
| *Validate search request | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/validate | POST | 200 / 400 |
| *Search | http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/search | POST | 200 |



*Note The marked request entries should be converted to the JSON format and added to a request body.


Below is an example of the Search request body.


Example


{


"searchString":"*",


"namespaces":["Default Namespace"],


"original":false,


"duplicates":true,


"includeOriginalDuplicate":true,


"dateRange":"C",


"fromTimestamp": 1434545430398,


"toTimestamp": 1434631830398,


"start":0,


"limit":5,


"sort":{


"sorts":[


{


"field":"TIMESTAMP",


"dir":"ASC"


}


]


}


}


Tip You can use [OpenSearch Query DSL](https://opensearch.org/docs/latest/query-dsl/) in the `searchString`.


For example, to perform a search query using several filters (an attribute and a key at the same time), your `searchString` might look as follows:


"searchString":"attributes.Amount:123 AND key.ClaimID:foo"


The following is an example of the Register request body:


Example


{


"key":


{


"KeyProperty1":"KeyProperty1Value",


"KeyProperty2":"KeyProperty2Value",


"KeyProperty3":"KeyProperty3Value"


},


"attributes":


{


"Attribute1":"Attribute1Value",


"Attribute2":"Attribute2Value",


"Attribute3":"Attribute3Value",


"Attribute4":"Attribute4Value",


"Attribute5":"Attribute5Value",


"Attribute6":"Attribute6Value",


"Attribute7":"Attribute7Value",


"Attribute8":"Attribute8Value",


"Attribute9":"Attribute9Value",


"Attribute10":"Attribute10Value"


},


"source":


{


"ClientModule":"REST Client DupCheck Console test tool",


"ClientApplication":"TestTool"


}


}


The following sample request attempts to register a new entry in the OpenSearch storage. Since the entry is unique, it is registered, and the true status is returned in the response along with the generated entryId.


Request:


curl -XPOST http://localhost:9059/dupcheck/namespaces/Default%20Namespace/items -H "Content-Type: application/json" --data "{ key:{KeyProperty1:KeyValue1}, attributes:{Attribute1:AttributeValue1}, source:{SourceProperty1:SourceValue1}}"


Response:


{


"entryId": "c9b3f56387990ddf513395b8d2c47e78",


"status": true


}


Note The previous sample request is designed to be submitted through the cURL tool, thus it contains several cURL-specific command line arguments, such as curl -XPOST, -H, and --data. However, you can use any other tool for submitting requests.

<a id="time-expression-editor"></a>

##### Time Expression Editor

> 📄 Source: [Time_Expression_Editor.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Time_Expression_Editor.htm)

###### Time Expression Editor


With Time Expression Editor, you can predefine a schedule to trigger an XEServer process. For example, you can define a time for marking Batcher strategies as complete, schedule the OpenSearch storage optimization process, create a timetable for the Timer Reporter component to fire an alarm, and so on.


The following options are available in the Time Expression Editor:


*[Image: Time Expression Editor dialog box]*


| Field | Entry Format | Description | Examples | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Year | 1970 - 2099 | Enter a year. | * | Any Year |
| 2001 | Only in 2001. | | | |
| 2001,2010 | In 2001 and 2010. | | | |
| 2001-2010 | From 2001 to 2010. | | | |
| 2001-2010/2 | From 2001 to 2010, every 2 years. | | | |







 Day-of-Week




1-7


-or-


SUN - SAT



 Enter a day of the week.
Note Your time expression should have either the Day-of-Week or Day-of-Month field specified, but not both. If you specify one value, the other is set to '?'.




| * | Any day of the week. |
| --- | --- |
| MON | Only Monday. |
| MON,FRI | Monday and Friday. |
| MON-FRI | From Monday to Friday. |
| MON-FRI/2 | Every two days from Monday to Friday (Monday, Wednesday, Friday). |
| 6#3 | Day 6 = Friday and "#3" = the third Friday in the month. |
| 6L | The last Friday of the month. |
| ? | Either one day in a week or one day in a month, but not both. If you specify one value, set the other to '?'. |







 Months



1 - 12


-or-


JAN - DEC



 Enter a month.



| * | Any month. |
| --- | --- |
| MAR | Only March. |
| MAR,JUN | March and June. |
| MAR-JUN | From March to June inclusively. |







 Day-of-Month


 1 - 31

 Enter a day of the month.
Note Your time expression should have either the Day-of-Week or Day-of-Month field specified, but not both. If you specify one value, the other is set to '?'.




| * | Any day of month. |
| --- | --- |
| 2 | The 2nd day of the month. |
| 2, 10 | The 2nd and the 10th days of the month. |
| 2-10/2 | From the 2nd to the 10th day of the month, every 2 days. |
| L | The last day of the month. |
| 15W | The closest weekday to the 15th of the month. |
| ? | Either one day in a week or one day in a month, but not both.. If you specify one value, set the other to '?'. |







 Hours


 0 - 23

 Enter an hour.



| * | Any hour. |
| --- | --- |
| 2 | Only 02:00. |
| 2, 10 | 02:00 and 10:00. |
| 2-10/2 | From 02:00 to 10:00, every 2 hours. |







 Minutes


 0 - 59

 Enter a minute.



| * | Any minute. |
| --- | --- |
| 7 | The 7th minute. |
| 2, 10 | The 2nd and 10th minutes. |
| 7-20/5 | From the 7th to the 20th minute, every 5 minutes. |







 Seconds


 0-59

 Enter a second.



| * | Any second. |
| --- | --- |
| 2 | The 2nd second. |
| 2, 10 | The 2nd and 10th seconds. |
| 10-40/10 | From the 10th to the 40th second, every 10 seconds |







 Number of firings


 1-1000

 Enter the number of alarms, generated on completion of your time expression. Click Test to view the generated schedule.




 TimeExpressionResult


 Displays the resulting time expression mask.

 0-50/10 15 06 10 3 ? 2019 - Generates 5 consecutive firings at 10 seconds intervals on March 11, 2019 at 6:13:45 PM .
