<a id="claim-forms"></a>

### Claim Forms

> 📄 Source: [Book_Claim_Forms.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Claim_Forms.htm)

#### Claim Forms Components


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="xes-module-for-claim-forms-overview"></a>

#### XES Module for Claim Forms Overview

> 📄 Source: [Xes_Module_for_Claim_Forms_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Xes_Module_for_Claim_Forms_Overview.htm)

##### XES Module for Claim Forms Overview


In businesses across industries, an audit is a challenging process that organizations have to subject themselves to regularly. In the healthcare industry, an external medical claims audit is especially painful and laborious. Payers have to make the required medical claims available to external auditors in a human-readable format to let them analyze and evaluate the claim processing and payment procedures, verify billing accuracy, and ensure adherence to rules and regulations. External auditors do not have access to the payers' electronic database. This means that payers have to open up the required electronic claims to auditors in a standardized format that is usually used for hard paper claims.


With XES Module for Claim Forms, you can convert your electronic claims to a PDF document that represents the required claim in a standard hard copy format to make them easy for human analysis. The following forms are available:

- [CMS-1500](https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Billing/ElectronicBillingEDITrans/1500.html): The Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) 1500 is a universal insurance claim form created for submitting claims and used by non-institutional healthcare service providers for reimbursements from Payers. The form is approved by the American Medical Association Council on Medical Services and is adopted by all government healthcare programs. This form is submitted as a HIPAA 837P transaction it its electronic form.
- [UB-04](https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Billing/ElectronicBillingEDITrans/15_1450.html): The Form UB-04 (also known as CMS-1450) is the industry-standard claim form used to submit institutional claims for inpatient and outpatient services and to bill various government and private insurers. In addition, the Form UB-04 is also used to bill Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACs). This form is submitted as a HIPAA 837I transaction in its electronic form.
- [ADA-2012](https://www.ada.org/en/publications/cdt/ada-dental-claim-form): The American Dental Association (ADA) Dental Claim Form is a standard format used to report dental services to a patient's dental benefit plan. This form is submitted as a HIPAA 837D transaction in its electronic form.


XES Module for Claim Forms creates an appropriately-filled form in a PDF format based on the required HIPAA-compliant 837 transaction. XES Module for Claim Forms introduces three components that perform conversion to the corresponding standard claim form:

- CMS-1500 Generator: Creates a CMS-1500 claim form. Tips By default, the component inserts the Billing Address into the Box 33. However, you can force the component to insert the Pay-To Address (if it exists) into the Box 33. For this, you should declare the property -Dconverter.xdata.to.cms1500.f33.prefer.paytoaddress=true on the profile [Startup tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm). You can use the environment property ReportProviderNameAsSOF=true/false to specify whether you want to populate the field 31 (Signature of Physician or Supplier) with the provider's name as the signature.
- UB-04 Generator: Creates a UB-04 claim form Note You can use the environment property UB04.UseBillingAddressInsteadOfPayToAddress to insert either Billing Address or Pay-To Address into the Box 2 of a printed 837I claim. If this property is set to true, the component inserts Billing Address into the Box 2. If this property is not declared or is set to false, the component populates the Box 2 with the Pay-To Address value.
- ADA-2012 Generator: Creates an ADA-2012 claim form


Note Starting from XEServer 9.2.2 hotfix 1 and EAM 9.2.2 hotfix 1, the components CMS-1500 Generator and UB-04 Generator can generate an Addendum page in the output PDF. For this, enable the option Generate Addendum Page on the Settings tab of the corresponding component.

---

<a id="sample-case-study"></a>

#### Sample Case Study

> 📄 Source: [XES_Module_Sample_Case_Study.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XES_Module_Sample_Case_Study.htm)

##### Sample Case Study


This sample case study demonstrates how to provide external auditors or other regulatory agencies with the required electronic medical claim in a human-readable (hard paper) format.


The following procedure helps you configure a route that converts an EDI 837P transaction to a PDF format that represents the claim in the filled CMS-1500 form.


Before you go through this scenario, you should do the following:

- Create a new profile. For more information, see Create a New Profile in the XEServer user guide.
- Create a new route. For more information, see Create a New Route in the XEServer user guide.


In this scenario, the Claim Processing component processes raw EDI data and creates XData 2.0 that is then converted to a PDF format by the CMS-1500 Generator component.



Note The XES Module for Claim Forms component expects to receive claims in the Edifecs XData 2.0 format in the separate input files (one file must contain only one claim). This format is natively produced by the XE Native component of XEngine Server. In most cases, XData 2.0 is automatically produced for your partner data. However, if you need to force the 2.0 version of an XData format, you can either update the guideline by [exporting it to the XData 2.0 format](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/specbuilder/9.4/Content/Export_to_XDATA_2.0_XML_Schema.htm) in SpecBuilder, or specify the property Guideline.XSDNames.X12=EDI2XSD2 in the [XEngine Service configuration](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEngine_Service.htm) of your route.


To configure the route:

1. Connect the data output of the Claim Intake component with the Claim Processing component.

1. Connect the output xdata of the component Claim Processing with the CMS-1500 Generator component.
2. Connect the output data of the component CMS-1500 Generator with the Forms PDF Archive component.
3. Click Save () or press CTRL+S to save changes.


To start the profile:

- On the System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.


Note Shut down the other XEServer profiles.

- On the Outline tab, expand Profiles.
- Click the name of the profile you want to start.
- On the toolbar, click Start.

- On the Admin Console tab, verify that all the services have been started.


To convert an electronic claim to the .pdf format:


Navigate to the source folder of the Claim Intake component and drop the required raw EDI 837P file there. The default location is ${XESRoot}\profiles\[ProfileName]\workspace\test-data\inbound. You can change the default location from the Common tab of the Claim Intake component.


Note Since the XES Module for Claim Forms component expects to receive XData 2.0 claims in individual files, if your inbound file contains more than one claim, you have to split that file into the split files that contain one claim each before you send them to the component.


###### Expected results:


Navigate to the destination folder of the Forms PDF Archive component to get the generated .pdf file. By default, the result is located at ${XESRoot}\profiles\[ProfileName]\workspace\test-data\outbound. You can change the default location from the Common tab of the Forms PDF Archive component.

---

<a id="xesmanager-components"></a>

### XESManager Components

> 📄 Source: [Book_XESManager_Components.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_XESManager_Components.htm)

#### XESManager Components


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="alert"></a>

#### Alert

> 📄 Source: [Alert.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Alert.htm)

##### Alert


The Alert route component is designed to trigger an alert once a specific XEServer event occurs and send a notification to XESManager.


###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Alert qualifier | Enter the alert qualifier string used to identify the alert in XESManager. |



###### Attributes tab


On this tab, you can manage key-value metadata transferred to the endpoint destination: for example, to a directory on a file system, web server, JMS queue, and so on. Depending on the endpoint destination, metadata are stored in the format that is suitable for a certain destination environment. For example, for FS components attributes are stored in the form of key-value pairs in .property files, for JMS components, attributes are mapped to queue message metadata, and so on.


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| **Include exchange properties** | Select this option to send exchange properties for further processing. |
| **Include message properties** | Select this option to send message properties for further processing. |
| Attribute parameters | In this pane, you can specify a custom name-value pair to be appended to the metadata. |



###### QoS tab


To use the information gathered by the Alert component you must establish connection between the route component and the event-based Alert created by using XESManager. In this case, create a new Alert and choose the XES Alert Event as the event type, indicating the alert qualifier property, matching the one provided for the component. For more information, see XESManager help.

---

<a id="statistic-reporter"></a>

#### Statistic Reporter

> 📄 Source: [Statistic_Reporter.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Statistic_Reporter.htm)

##### Statistic Reporter


The Statistic Reporter route component is designed to report specific route-related information to XESManager.


To use the information gathered by the Statistic Reporter component you must establish connection between the route component the statistical Alert created by using XESManager. In this case, create a new Alert and choose the Statistics Registry as the system type, indicating the mask, matching your Registry Name. For more information, see [XESManager help](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/Statistic_Reporter.htm#Create_a_statistical_alert).


###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Registry name | Use to provide a registry name that is used to refer to a particular Statistic Reporter component when using XESManager. |
| By Partner | Select the check box and use the field to provide the Partner name. If this option is selected, the statistics is collected only for those messages that are received from a particular Partner. |
| By message type | If this option is selected, the statistics is collected only for messages of a particular type. |
| Message count | Use to collect statistics about the number of received messages. The default value {E_MsgCnt} macro counts messages that go through the component. You can also use other macros. |
| Message size | Use to collect statistics about the size of received messages. The default value {E_Size} macro counts the size of every message that goes through the component. You can also use other macros. |
| Processing time | Use to collect statistics about the processing time of received messages. The default macro {T_Duration} counts the time from the task start till the moment a message reaches the Statistic Reporter component. You can also use other macros. |
| User parameter | Use to track any processing characteristics that are specific for the processing implementation. This parameter can be used for future Alert configuration or Performance investigation by using XESManager. The parameter syntax supports macros, simple arithmetic operations, and regular expressions. |



###### QoS tab


You can set [Quality of service (QoS)](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_QoS_Settings.htm) individually for each component that supports this option.

---

<a id="audit-reporter"></a>

#### Audit Reporter

> 📄 Source: [Audit_Reporter.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Reporter.htm)

##### Audit Reporter


With this component, you can generate [Audit Events](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Events_Overview.htm). When an XEServer message passes through the component, the component generates an audit event based on either the message contents or the parameters specified in the component's settings. The component depends on an [Audit Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Service.htm) that can write events to log files or submit events to Visualizer Server.



###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Service name | Select the XEServer [Audit Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Audit_Service.htm) to store events generated by the component. |
| Source | Select the data source to compose events. The following sources are available:Message body: In this mode, the component retrieves all the properties required to compose events (Correlation ID, Session ID, namespace, and so on) from the message body.Properties: In this mode, the component creates events based on the properties specified on the component's configuration page. |
| Correlation ID | An ID used to track an XEServer message as it travels down a route and passes through several Audit Reporter components that generate audit events. For example, a request with Correlation ID "foo" passes through several processing stages on a route. After each stage, FHIR components create audit events using the same Correlation ID "foo" that links the events together. This way, you can view the processing stages the XEServer message has currently passed through in the Visualizer Server interface. |
| Session ID | Specify an ID to correlate an audit event with the XEServer message exchange that produced this event. On the XESManager [Audit Events](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/VisualizerServer/VS_View_Audit_Events.htm) page, you can use this ID to: filter out events, identify the current processing phase of a specific request. Tip If the route processing starts from an HTTP request, the request header "x-edfx-request-id" can be used as a Session ID. This header is generated automatically by XEServer and contains a unique value. |
| Namespace | Enter a string that logically groups related events. You can use namespaces on the XESManager [Audit Events](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xesmanager/9.4/Content/VisualizerServer/VS_View_Audit_Events.htm) page to filter and search for events generated by different routes and profiles. |
| Source Name | The name of the source system that produced the event. |
| Severity | Indicates the severity of the event. The possible values are "INFO," "WARNING," and "ERROR." |
| Payload Size | Specify the payload size of the XEServer message that produced the event. |
| Custom Attributes | Use this pane to pass custom key-value pairs within an event.Tip In Visualizer Server, you use custom attributes as a filter to search for events. |

---

<a id="regression-suite"></a>

### Regression Suite

> 📄 Source: [Book_Regression_Suite.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Regression_Suite.htm)

#### Regression Suite Profiles


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="overview"></a>

#### Overview

> 📄 Source: [RS_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RS_Overview.htm)

##### Overview


The following three Regression Suite components meet all your testing requirements by allowing you to compare any files or folders that could be the output of a software or an application, and to produce comprehensive summary reports to highlight the differences between the two versions. If you have upgraded your routes and you want to compare the output of a previous and a later version of routes, you can use the Regression Suite components to compare the output files of the two versions, and view the comparison report. The Regression Suite components support the EDI and XML file formats.


The following Regression Suite components are available:

- **TS (Test Script) Processor**: Executes test scripts and creates execution reports.
- **Compare**: Compares any two files or folders and generates comparison reports to highlight the differences and variations between the two.
- **Result Watcher**: Waits for a pre-defined number of files/folders or a pre-defined time before the execution of a subsequent component on a route.

---

<a id="ts-processor"></a>

#### TS Processor

> 📄 Source: [TS_Processor.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/TS_Processor.htm)

##### TS Processor


The TS Processor component allows you to execute your test script(s) through a route and to generate execution reports. To create a test script, see Sample Test Script.


When you add the component to a route, you can view the following settings:



###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Execution run identifier | (Optional ) Set a test run name to group test scripts. For example, if you set the identifier as Execution1, the name of test run is displayed as Execution1 on the TS Processor Summary Report. If you do not set an identifier, the default test run name format is Test run mmddyyyy. |
| Report location | (Optional ) The location where TS Processor generates the test script execution report. The default location is ${XESWorkspace}\test-data\reports. Note When you execute several test scripts through a route to multiple instances, Execution Report may not link with its relative Comparison Reports as the execution is asynchronous. However, if the report location is the same for all the scripts, the reports are generated and linked correctly for each test script. If the report locations are different, the reports may not get linked. |
| Testcase name exclude masks | Set a filter/mask (regular expression) to exclude only those test cases that match your naming condition. For exclude masks that are applicable, see Regular Expressions. |
| Testcase name include masks | Set a filter/mask (regular expression) to include only those test cases that match your naming condition. For include masks that are applicable, see Regular Expressions. |



###### Regular Expressions for TestCase Include and Exclude Masks


The following regular expressions (regex) can be used to include or exclude test cases from a test script execution:

- **"Expression"**: Matches the given expression with any subsequence in the string.
- **"^Expression"**: Matches the given expression with a subsequence at the beginning of the string.
- **"$Expression"**: Matches the given expression with a subsequence at the end of the string.
- **"^((?!Expression).)*$"**: If the expression is present in the string, it excludes the string.
- **"Expression 1|Expression 2"**: Matches either expression 1 or expression 2 as a subsequence in the string.


You can view the following example:


| Expression | Strings Matched | Strings Not Matched |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 837 | "TestCase 837 Validation", "TestCase837Validation" | "TestCase 835 Validation" |
| 834\|837 | "TestCase 837 Validation", "TestCase 834 Validation" | "TestCase 835 Validation" |
| ^837 | "837 Validation" | "TestCase 837 Validation", "TestCase 834 Validation" |
| ^837\|^834 | "837 Validation", "834 Validation" | "TestCase 837 Validation" |
| ^((?!837).)*$ | "TestCase 835", "TestCase 834 Validation" | "837 Validation", "TestCase 837 Validation" |
| 837^834 | "837 Validation" | "TestCase 834 Validation" |
| Test837Validation | "Test837Validation" | "TestCase 837 Validation". "Test834Validation" |


 TS Processor Summary Report


When you execute a test script using the TS Processor component, the component creates a set of result reports that allow you to view the result of your execution. Each time you execute a test script, a subfolder is created in the Reports folder (at the path specified in the test script or TS Processor).


Summary Report displays the results of all the executions, along with their test scripts executed in each execution run. From Summary Report, you can drill down to different levels, and view the reports for each test script (when there are multiple test scripts) and test cases.



The following attributes are available:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Total Test Scripts | The total number of scripts that have been executed. |
| Processed | The count of test scripts that have been executed successfully. |
| Skipped | The count of test scripts that have been excluded from processing. |
| Passed | The count of test scripts that have passed during the execution. |
| Failed | The count of test scripts that have failed during the execution. |
| Status | The status of a test script execution.**Processed**: Indicates that the test script has been executed successfully.**Skipped**: Indicates that the test script has been excluded from processing. |
| Result | The cumulative status of comparison steps and test cases with comparison steps within a test script. **Passed**: The status if the result of all the test cases is "Passed."**Failed**: The status if the result of any of the test cases is "Failed." |
| Test Cases | The total number of test cases executed within each test script. |
| Execution Start Time | The date and time when a test script execution has started. |
| Execution End Time | The date and time when a test script execution has been completed. |
| Duration | The total time taken for the execution of a test script. |






 Detailed Execution Reports


When you drill down Summary Report, you can view the following reports:


**Script result report**: Provides a summarized result of all the test cases executed within a test script. When you click a script name from Summary Report, you can view the following report.



| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Total Test Cases | The total number of cases that have been executed within a test script. |
| Processed | The count of test cases that have been executed successfully. |
| Skipped | The count of test cases that have been left out of processing. |
| Passed | The count of test cases where compared data is the same in all the expected and actual files/folders. |
| Failed | The count of test cases where compared data is not the same in all the expected and actual files/folders. |
| Status | The status of a test case execution. **Processed**: Indicates that the test case has been executed successfully.**Skipped**: Indicates that the test case has been excluded from processing. |
| Result | The status of a test case containing comparison steps. **Passed**: The compared data is the same in all the expected and actual files/folders.**Failed**: The compared data is not the same in all the expected and actual files/folders. If a test case does not have any comparison steps, the result is "-". |
| Execution Start Time | The date and time when a test case execution has started. |
| Execution End Time | The date and time when a test case execution has been completed. |
| Duration | The total time taken for the execution of a test case. |



**Test case result report**: Provides a summarized result of all the comparisons made within a test case. When you click a test case name from the Script result report, you can view the following report:



The following attributes are available:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Total Comparisons | The total number of comparisons that have been made within a test case. |
| Result | The status of a test case containing comparison steps. The possible values are:**Passed**: The compared data is the same in all the expected and actual files/folders.**Failed**: The compared data is not the same in all the expected and actual files/folders. |
| Expected Files | The count of files/folders/sub-folders that are picked up first for a comparison. |
| Actual Files | The count of files/folders/sub-folders that are picked up next to be compared to Expected Files. |
| Files with no data difference | The count of **Expected Files** compared with the corresponding **Actual Files** without any data difference. |
| Files with data difference | The count of **Expected Files** compared with the corresponding **Actual Files** with data differences. |



Note The values for **Total Comparisons**, **Expected Files**, **Actual Files**, **Files with no data difference**, and **Files with data difference** are extracted from the corresponding "Comparison Report" generated for each test case.





 Sample Test Script


A test script includes several actions and test cases. You can create a test script file in an XML or JSON format. You can use this file to define all your test cases, and you can introduce setup and tearDown actions. All the actions and test cases are executed in a sequence. You do the setup action once before execution of all the test cases, and the tearDown action after the execution of all the test cases.


To create a test script:

1. Create an .xml or .json file, for example testscript.xml, and save it anywhere on your computer.
2. Add the following lines to the file. You can add as many test cases as you want based on your testing requirements.Note If you want to use variables in the scripts, you can use the Environments tab of the route to define variables, as displayed in the following example.XML<testScript name="Good 271 response" description="Test the route for good 271 response"><setup name="cleanupActualResult"><property name="actualResultLocation" value="${270actualResultPath}/Good270"/></setup><testCase name="TC_Good_270" description="Validating generation of good 271 response"><property name="inputFileLocation" value="${270inputDataPath}"/><property name="inputFileIncludeMask" value="Good270*"/><property name="actualResultLocation" value="${270actualResultPath}/Good270"/><property name="expectedResultLocation" value="${270expectedResultPath}/Good270"/></testCase><tearDown name = "cleanupTracers"></tearDown></testScript>JSON"name": "Good 271 response""description": "Test the route for good 271 response","testCases": [{"name": "TC_Good_270","description": "Validating generation of good 271 response","properties": {"inputFileLocation": "${270inputDataPath}","inputFileIncludeMask": "Good270*","actualResultLocation": "${270actualResultPath}/Good270","expectedResultLocation": "${270expectedResultPath}/Good270"}}],"setup":{"name": "cleanupActualResult","properties":{"actualResultLocation": "${270actualResultPath}/Good270"}},"tearDown":{"name": "cleanupTracers"}}Note If you copy from the code snippet above, ensure that the copied path is in the same line without breaks or spaces. The sample snippet has breaks to accommodate the lines for reading.
3. Enter the required values, and then save the file.


Note Ensure that the properties set in your routes are the same as those configured in your test script.

---

<a id="compare"></a>

#### Compare

> 📄 Source: [Compare.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Compare.htm)

##### Compare


The Compare component compares any two files or folders and generates comparison reports to highlight the differences between them at the data element level. The comparison reports are generated in an HTML format.


When you add the component to a route, you can view the following settings:


*[Image: Compare]*


###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Actual result exclude masks | Set a filter/mask to exclude only those expected files that match your naming condition. For more information, see [File Include and Exclude Masks.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm) |
| Actual result include masks | Set a filter/mask to include only those expected files that match your naming condition. For more information, see [File Include and Exclude Masks.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm) |
| Actual result location | The location where the expected files/folders are available. Note The file/folder that is picked up next to be compared to the first file/folder is the actual file/folder. For example, when you upgrade your routes and want to compare the output of the old and new version of routes, the expected files/folders are what you already have, and the actual files/folders are those coming in from a newer version of routes. This field is mandatory. |
| Content comparison configuration file | The location of the configuration file that contains a list of ignored fields for each file type and the file extensions. To create a configuration file, see Sample Content Comparison Configuration File. |
| Expected result exclude masks | Set a filter/mask to exclude only those expected files that match your naming condition. For more information, see [File Include and Exclude Masks.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm) |
| Expected result include masks | Set a filter/mask to include only those expected files that match your naming condition. For more information, see [File Include and Exclude Masks.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm) |
| Expected result location | The location where the expected files/folders are available. Note The file/folder that is picked up first (as a base) for comparison is the expected file/folder. |
| Matcher configuration file | This property allows you to select a configuration file that defines a strategy to match files. If a file from an expected location and a file from an actual location have the same relative path, then it is Match. For more information, see Sample Matcher Configuration file. If you do not select a configuration file, the Compare component uses the default matching logic. |
| Report location | (Optional) The location where the Compare component generates the comparison reports. The default location is ${XESWorkspace}\test-data\reports, but you can change the location. Note To link Execution Report to Comparison Report, in the Compare component, keep the following report location: {Prp[testcase.report.folder]}. |



###### Outputs Tab


The following output options are available for the Compare component :


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| result | The result of a comparison between the expected result and the actual result files/folders. This output channel returns an empty message with the following message exchange properties:compare.status: The status of the Compare component. The possible values are Processed and Skipped.compare.result: This is the result of a comparison. The possible values are Passed and Failed.compare.time.start: The date and time when a comparison has started.compare.time.end: The date and time when a comparison has been completed.compare.report.path: The path where the comparison result report is available.compare.expected.files: The count of the total files/folders available at the expected location.compare.actual.files: The count of the total files/folders available at the actual location.compare.diff.files: The count of Expected files compared with the corresponding Actual files with data differences.compare.nodiff.files: The count of Expected files compared with the corresponding Actual files without any data difference.compare.notcompared.files: The count of files that have not been compared because of unsupported file formats. compare.expected.notfound.files: The count of missing files/folders at the Actual location that are available at the Expected location.compare.actual.notfound.files: The count of missing files/folders at the Expected location that are available at the Actual location. |
| original | The original input that is received by the component, that is, the component relays the same input message(s) that it receives as input. |
| error | The output channel relays any exceptions that occur during the execution of the Compare component. |


 Sample Content Comparison Configuration File


If you want to use a custom configuration, you can do the configuration before you perform a comparison. You can also select to merge or override your configuration with the default configuration. The following two types of configurations are available:

- Fields to be excluded for comparison
- File extensions for EDI, XML, CSV, and Properties files

 A comparison-configuration JSON file


{


"x12Configuration": {


"overrideMergeMode": "merge",


"extensionList": [


"dat",


"txt",


"edi",


"x12",


""


],


"ignoreList": [


"ISA09",


"ISA10",


"ISA13",


"GS04",


"GS05",


"GS06",


"GE02",


"IEA02",


"ST02",


"SE02",


"BGN03",


"BGN04",


"BHT04",


"BHT05",


"HL01",


"HL02",


"LX01",


"IT101"


]


},


"xmlConfiguration": {


"overrideMergeMode": "merge",


"extensionList": [


"xml"


],


"ignoreList": [


"//@GUID",


"//@Guid",


"//ExecutionDate",


"//ExecutionTime",


"//LastModified",


"//FilePath",


"//FileSize",


"//DataFile",


"//DataURL",


"//TaskID",


"/DataReport/Fields/Field[@Name='TaskID']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/Fields/Field[@Name='InterchangeControlNumber']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/Fields/Field[@Name='InterchangeDate']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/Fields/Field[@Name='InterchangeTime']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/Fields/Field[@Name='InterchangeTrailerControlNumber']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/Fields/Field[@Name='GroupControlNumber']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/Fields/Field[@Name='GroupDate']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/Fields/Field[@Name='GroupTime']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/Fields/Field[@Name='GroupTrailerControlNumber']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/GroupNode[@Name='Transaction']/Fields/Field[@Name='TransactionControlNumber']",


"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/GroupNode[@Name='Transaction']/Fields/Field[@Name='TransactionTrailerControlNumber']",

"/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/GroupNode[@Name='Group']/GroupNode[@Name='Transaction']/Field[@Name='TransactionSetDate']",


"/DataReport/ConfidentialStatement",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/namespace::*",

"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-ISA']/*[local-name()='Element-I08']",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-ISA']/*[local-name()='Element-I09']",

"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-GS']/*[local-name()='Element-373']",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-GS']/*[local-name()='Element-337']",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-GS']/*[local-name()='Element-28']",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-ST']/*[local-name()='Element-329']",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Segment-SE']/*[local-name()='Element-329']",

"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Loop-2000']/*[local-name()='Loop-2300']/*[local-name()='Loop-2310']/*[local-name()='Segment-LX']/*[local-name()='Element-554']",


"/*[starts-with(name(), 'Transaction-')]/*[local-name()='Loop-2000']/*[local-name()='Loop-LS']/*[local-name()='Loop-2700']/*[local-name()='Segment-LX_1']/*[local-name()='Element-554']",

}





 A comparison-configuration XML file


<comparisonConfigurations>


<x12Configuration overrideMergeMode="merge">


<ext>dat</ext>


<ext>txt</ext>


<ext>edi</ext>


<ext>x12</ext>


<ext/>


<ignore>ISA09</ignore>


<ignore>ISA10</ignore>


<ignore>ISA13</ignore>


<ignore>GS04</ignore>


<ignore>GS05</ignore>


<ignore>GS06</ignore>


<ignore>GE02</ignore>


<ignore>IEA02</ignore>


<ignore>ST02</ignore>


<ignore>SE02</ignore>


<ignore>BGN03</ignore>


<ignore>BGN04</ignore>


<ignore>BHT04</ignore>


<ignore>BHT05</ignore>


<ignore>HL01</ignore>


<ignore>HL02</ignore>


<ignore>LX01</ignore>


<ignore>IT101</ignore>


</x12Configuration>


<xmlConfiguration overrideMergeMode="merge">


<ext>xml</ext>


<ignore>//@GUID</ignore>


<ignore>//@Guid</ignore>


<ignore>//ExecutionDate</ignore>


<ignore>//ExecutionTime</ignore>


<ignore>//LastModified</ignore>


<ignore>//FilePath</ignore>


<ignore>//FileSize</ignore>


<ignore>//DataFile</ignore>


<ignore>//DataURL</ignore>


<ignore>//TaskID</ignore>


<ignore>/DataReport/Fields/Field[@Name='TaskID']</ignore>


<ignore>/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/Fields/Field[@Name='InterchangeControlNumber']</ignore>


<ignore>/DataReport/GroupNode[@Name='Interchange']/Fields/Field[@Name='InterchangeDate']</ignore>

Note When you copy from the code snippet above, ensure that the copied path is in the same line without breaks or spaces. The sample snippet has breaks to accommodate the lines for reading.









 Sample Matcher Configuration file


This configuration file allows you to define a strategy to match files (files within a folder) before making a comparison. If you do not provide a Matcher Configuration file, the Compare component uses the default matching strategy to compare the files.

 A matcher configuration file:


<matchers>


<fileMatcher>


<files>


<includeMasks>


<expected>


<masks>**/*</masks> <!-- comma separated file masks -> dir/**.txt,dir/**.dat -->


<extensions>


dat,txt,xml


</extensions> <!-- comma separated file extensions -> dat,txt,xml -->


</expected>


<actual>


<masks>**/*</masks> <!--comma separated file masks -> dir/**.txt,dir/**.dat -->


<extensions>


dat,txt,xml


</extensions> <!-- comma separated file extensions -> dat,txt,xml -->


</actual>


</includeMasks>


</files>


<!-- at least one matcher is required, if you use fileMatcher-->


<matchers>


<propertyMatcher>


<properties>


<property>ClaimID</property>


<property>GroupControlNumber</property>


</properties>


</propertyMatcher>


 


</matchers>


</fileMatcher>


<propertyMatcher>


<properties>


<property>ClaimID</property>


<property>GroupControlNumber</property>


</properties>


</propertyMatcher>


 


<fileNameMatcher>


<patterns>


<pattern>


<expected>^.*Bad_Claim.*$</expected>


<actual>^.*Bad_Claim.*$</actual>


</pattern>


</patterns>


</fileNameMatcher>


 


<defaultMatcher>


<threshold>medium</threshold> <!-- low,medium,strict,exact -->


</defaultMatcher>


</matchers>


Note When you copy from the code snippet above, ensure that the copied path is in the same line without breaks or spaces. The sample snippet has breaks to accommodate the lines for reading.






| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| matchers | The master tag that allows you to define the overall matching strategy before you make a comparison. You can use four types of matchers in any sequence and each matcher can be used as many times as required. You must specify at least one matcher in the configuration file. |
| <defaultMatcher> | <threshold>: Defines the level of similarity, based on how similar the file names (file names of files inside a folder) must be to consider them a match. Threshold is the minimum percentage required for a match. If the match percentage of a relative path is less than this threshold, then there is no match between the files. The possible values are the following:Exact: 100% matchStrict: 80% match Medium: 60% matchLenient: 40% match. This value is default.If the threshold is other than "Exact," that is, the match is less than 100%, then the following conditions are applicable:If the parent directories of files at expected and actual locations have same relative path and these files have no sibling files with a matching file type (x12/xml/properties), then it is considered as a match.If a file (file A) at an expected location and a file (File B) at an actual location have the same file type (x12/xml/properties) and the matching percentage of relative paths of expected and actual files is:greater than the specified threshold valuegreater than the matching percentage of any other file that matches with the above files (A and B) at the expected or actual locations.then it is a match. See Default Matcher and Threshold Examples. |
| <propertyMatcher> | Matches an actual and an expected file based on their corresponding .properties files. You can define as many properties as required, but the Compare component considers a match only if at least one set of <properties> matches from one file to another. The following attributes are available: <properties>: The set of properties based on which the component finds a match. <property>: A property that is present in the .properties file of the expected and actual files. |
| <fileNameMatcher> | Matches the expected and actual files based on the file name patterns. The following attributes are available:<pattern>: A pair of file naming pattern, one each for expected and actual file.<expected>: A regex naming pattern of the expected file.<actual>: A regex naming pattern of the actual file. |
| <fileMatcher> | Allows you to filter a set of input expected and actual files and match them according to the matchers provided under <fileMatcher>. The following attributes are available:<files>: Set of expected and actual files, and the filters/masks to pick up those files to find a match.<includeMasks>: Sets a filter/mask to include files that match your naming condition for the actual and the expected files.<masks>: Comma separated file masks to filter the files. You can also use macros. For example, path/**.txt, path/**.dat<extensions>: Comma separated file extensions to filter the files. You can also use macros. For example, dat, txt, xml<matchers>: You can use three types of matchers in any sequence and each matcher can be used as many times as required. You must use at least one <matchers> under <fileMatcher>, ensuring that you specify at least one matcher. The following matchers are available:<propertyMatcher><fileNameMatcher><defaultMatcher> |






 Default Matcher and Threshold Examples


Threshold determines the minimum match score needed for a file name (for files inside a folder) to be considered a match. The default matching logic includes four threshold values.

- Exact: 100% match
- Strict: 80% match of the file name
- Medium: 60% match
- Lenient: 40% match. This is the default value.


An exact match searches for strings that exactly match a pattern. Strict, Medium, and Lenient matches search for strings that approximately match a pattern, based on the set threshold. For example, if you have set up Medium threshold to match your files, the component matches the files that have at least 60% similar file names and relative paths.


The following table displays the examples for different threshold values:


| Expected Files | Actual Files |   |   | Match? |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|   |   | | | | |
| Exact (100%) | Strict (80%) | Medium (60%) | Lenient (40%) | | |
| 2_Good_Claim-0000004-1-Native Processing_split.bad.dat | 2_Bad_Claim-0000004-1-Native Processing_split.bad.dat | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| 837_Matching_Dental_only_claim.dat | 837_M_a_t_c_h_i_ng_Dental_claim_file.txt | No | No | No | Yes |
| Good270x279B2.dat | Bad270x279A1.dat | No | No | Yes | Yes |


 Sample reports


Exact match



Strict match



Medium match



Lenient match










 Comparison Reports

 Folder Comparison


A folder comparison report compares two folder structures/contents side-by-side and highlights the differences between the Expected and the Actual folders. You can view the following example of the folder comparison report:



When you hover over a file name, the name of the corresponding file with the same name in the other folder is displayed.



The following attributes are available:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Expected Files | The count of files/folders/sub-folders that are picked up first for a comparison. |
| Actual Files | The count of files/folders/sub-folders that are picked up next to be compared to Expected Files. |
| Files with no data difference | The count of **Expected Files** that have been compared with the corresponding **Actual Files** and have no data difference. |
| Files with data difference | The count of **Expected Files** that have been compared with the corresponding **Actual Files** and have data differences. |
| Expected files not found in Actual | The count of missing files/folders at the Actual location that are available at the Expected location. |
| Actual files not found in Expected | The count of missing files/folders at the Expected location that are available at the Actual location. |
| Data Not Compared | The count of files that have not been compared due to unsupported file formats. |






 File Data Comparison


A file data comparison report display uses color cues to highlight the data differences between two files or folders.

1. To view the file data comparison report in HTML, select the following filters: - **Show All**: When you select **Show All**, the comparison report displays the fields with no differences, the fields that have been ignored, and the data with differences. The following attributes are available: | Field | Description | | --- | --- | | Expected File | The name of the file that is picked up first for comparison. | | Actual File | The name of the file that is picked up next to be compared to Expected File. | | Lines with Differences | The count of lines with data mismatch. This does not include the lines that have differences as a result of ignored fields. If a line includes both an ignored field and a field with actual difference, then that line is included in this count. | - **Show Ignored Fields Only**: When you select Show Ignored Fields Only, the comparison report displays only those lines with fields that have been ignored. *[Image: Data Comparison Report with Ignored Fields]* - **Show Differences Only**: When you select **Show Differences Only**, the comparison report displays the file data with differences. *[Image: Comparison Report with Show Differences only]* When the size of the data comparison report exceeds 2 MB, then to prevent the report from taking a long time to open, the Regression Suite displays the file data with differences only under the filter option Show Differences Only. In this case, the filters**Show All** and **Show Ignored Fields Only** remain disabled. When the file size of the Show Differences Only report also exceeds 2 MB, then you can view the report in the PDF format.
2. To view the full comparison report in the PDF format, click **Show Data Comparison PDF**. For example: *[Image: Comparison Report Sample PDF]*

---

<a id="result-watcher"></a>

#### Result Watcher

> 📄 Source: [Result_Watcher.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Result_Watcher.htm)

##### Result Watcher


The Result Watcher component introduces a delay in the processing of a route and waits for a pre-defined number of files or time before the execution of a subsequent component within a route.


When you add the component to a route, you can view the following settings:



###### Component Settings


Apart from [generic](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Properties.htm) settings, you can configure the following component-specific settings.


###### Settings tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Exclude masks | Set a filter/mask to exclude only those expected files that match your naming condition. For more information, see [File Include and Exclude Masks.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm) |
| File Count* | Set the number of files that the Result Watcher component should wait for. The default value is 1. |
| Include masks | Set a filter/mask to include only those expected files that match your naming condition. For more information, see [File Include and Exclude Masks.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm) |
| Location* | Set a location to a local file system folder where the files are accumulated. |
| Timeout (ms)* | Set the duration (in milliseconds), after which the Result Watcher component encounters a timeout. The default value is 30000 milliseconds. |
| Timeout expiration action* | Set the action that the Result Watcher component should execute if a timeout is encountered. The possible values are:**Success** : The wait/delay is considered complete, even if the Result Watcher component encounters a timeout.**Error** : If a timeout is encountered, the Result Watcher component errors out. This value is default. |
| Reset timeout after new file* | Resets the Timeout duration. The possible values are:**Yes**: The timeout counter is reset each time a file is added in the file watch folder. For example, if the component has been set to wait for 100 files with a timeout of 30000 milliseconds, each time a file is added to the watch folder, the timeout is reset to 30000 milliseconds. If timeout occurs before the next file is dropped in the folder, the standard timeout action is triggered.**No**: The timeout is not reset, and in keeping with the standard functionality. This value is default. |



###### Outputs tab


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| original | This is the original input that is received by the component, that is, the component relays the same input message(s) that it receives as input. |
| error | This output channel relays any exceptions that occur during the execution of the component File Watcher. The following are the possible reasons for an error / exception:If an exception occurs during the execution of the Result Watcher component.If the Result Watcher component encounters a **Timeout**, and the **Timeout expiration action** is set to Error. |

---

<a id="file-include-and-exclude-masks"></a>

#### File Include and Exclude Masks

> 📄 Source: [File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_Include_and_Exclude_Masks.htm)

##### File Include and Exclude Masks


The following File Include and Exclude masks should be used for the following components: Compare and Result Watcher:


| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Include masks | Set a filter to pick up only those files that match your naming condition. To set a condition, use the following masks: ** : Resolved as any folder with all its subfolders. * : Resolved as any set of characters. ? : Resolved as any character. Note The Include masks field is case-sensitive. Example Mask: folder1/**/data?.dat Accepted files: <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/data1.dat, <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/<AnySubfolder>/dataX.dat, and so on. Mask: 5010* Accepted files: <SourceFolder>/5010_837P_x222_A1.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010_837D_x224_A2.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010.txt, and so on.   To use multiple masks at the same time, separate masks by using a comma or semicolon without the white space character. Example Masks: folder1/**/Test?.dat;5010* Accepted files: <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/Test1.dat, <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/<AnySubfolder>/TestN.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010_270_x279_A1.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010.txt, and so on. By default, the value is set to "**/*" that picks up all the files in all the folders of the source directory. |
| Exclude masks | Set a filter to exclude only those files that match your naming condition. To set a condition, use the following masks: ** : Resolved as any folder with all its subfolders. * : Resolved as any set of characters. ? : Resolved as any character. Note The Exclude masks field is case-sensitive. Example Mask: folder1/**/data?.dat Excluded files: <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/data1.dat, <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/<AnySubfolder>/dataX.dat, and so on. Mask: 5010* Excluded files: <SourceFolder>/5010_837P_x222_A1.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010_837D_x224_A2.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010.txt, and so on.   To use multiple masks simultaneously, separate masks by using a comma or semicolon without the white space character. Example Masks: folder1/**/Test?.dat;5010* Excluded files: <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/Test1.dat, <SourceFolder>/folder1/<AnySubfolder>/<AnySubfolder>/TestN.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010_270_x279_A1.dat, <SourceFolder>/5010.txt, and so on. |

---

<a id="regression-suite-sample-case-study"></a>

#### Regression Suite Sample Case Study

> 📄 Source: [Regression_Suite_Sample_Case_Study.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Regression_Suite_Sample_Case_Study.htm)

##### Regression Suite Sample Case Study


This sample case study demonstrates how you can use Regression Suite components to test your data. In this case study, you can perform regression testing on **Sample 270-271** profile using the **Sample Regression Test** profile.


###### Step 1: Create profiles


You should first create the Sample 270-271 profile, and then create the Sample Regression Test profile.


###### Sample 270-271 profile


To create the Sample 270-271 Profile:

- Open the EAM (64) application, and connect as a administrator.
- On the System tab, expand XEServer.
- Right-click **Profiles**, and then click **New**.
- In the Template list, under XEngine, select **Sample 270-271**, and then click **OK**.



###### Sample Regression Test profile


If you use EAM 9.0.0 or an earlier version, you can create a new profile by selecting the **Sample Regression Test** from Edifecs Library. You can follow the steps 1-3 as described earlier.



Alternatively, if you are using EAM 8.8.x, you can download the image Sample Regression Test profile from the Edifecs Cloud repository by following the steps:


To download the profile image:

1. Log into your Edifecs Smart Trading Cloud account. If you do not have an account, you can create the account on [https://www.edifecsfedcloud.com/](https://www.edifecsfedcloud.com/)
2. Navigate to **Cloud Repository > LIBRARY > ProfileImages**, and then download the **XEServer(8.8.1)_Sample Regression Test_20180726_192955_841.zip**



 



To create and deploy a Sample Regression Test profile:

1. Open the EAM (64) application.
2. Connect to the Edifecs Application Manager server as an administrator.
3. Under **System**, click **Configuration Manager > Images**.
4. Click **Upload** to create a profile.
5. Navigate to the path where you have downloaded the Sample Regression Test profile image, and then select the image.
6. Click **Open**.
7. To deploy the profile, click the profile name from the list of images, and then click **Deploy**.
8. On the pop-up that appears, select the image selected in the list option, and then click **Next**.
9. Click **Deploy**. The Sample Regression Test is created under the XEServer node of the System tab, and profile folder is created under the XEServer Profiles folder. For example, C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test.
10. Start the profile.


To start the profiles:

- On the EAM **System** tab, expand **XEServer**.
- Double-click **Admin Console**.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand **Profiles**, and then select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click **Start**.
- Verify that all profile's routes and services are started.


###### Step 2: Access and view a profile (Sample Regression Test) artifacts


Once you deploy the Sample Regression Test profile, you can view the sample artifacts at the following location:


C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\config\sampleArtifacts


The following image displays a sample folder structure:


- Reports: contains the results of the regression test. You can view this folder only after starting and executing the **Sample Regression Test** profile.
- TestData: contains the sample data that you can use for testing. The following data folders are available:
- ActualResult: contains multiple sub folders of the actual test data sets.
- ExpectedResult: contains multiple sub folders of the expected test data sets.
- InputTestFiles: contains multiple input test files.
- TestScripts: contains the sample test scripts to test the 270-271 profile. The following test script folders are available, each having a test scripts with multiple test cases:
- AAA Test Scripts
- Error270 Test Scripts
- Good270 Test Scripts
- **Sample_Exclusion_Fields_Config** contains the configuration for exclusion fields. To know more about the configuration file, see [Sample Configuration File](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Compare.htm#Sample_Content_Comparison_Configuration_File).
- **TriggerFile.dat.properties** contains the location of the test script and reports, and the execution mode as **RegressionTest**.
- **TriggerFile_ExpectedResult.dat.properties** contains the location of the test script and reports, and the execution mode as **CaptureExpectedResult**. If you only want to capture the expected results, you should first configure this file, and drop this file in the trigger folder, and execute it. Once the expected results are captured, you can execute the **TriggerFile.dat.properties** file for your regression testing.


###### Step 3: Capture expected results


To capture the expected results that can be used for test scripts:

- Navigate to C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\config\sampleArtifacts, and copy the TriggerFile_ExpectedResult.dat and TriggerFile_ExpectedResult.dat.properties files.
- Navigate C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\workspace\test-data\inbound, and paste the trigger files, from the above step, at this location.
- Once the processing is done, you can view the captured expected results in the ExpectedResult folder at C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\sampleArtifacts\test-data\ExpectedResult.


###### Step 4: Execute a regression test


To execute a regression test:

- Navigate to C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\config\sampleArtifacts, and copy the TriggerFile.dat and TriggerFile.dat.properties files.
- Navigate C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\workspace\test-data\inbound, and paste the trigger files from the above step, at this location.
- The Sample Regression Test profile picks up the test scripts for processing. Once the processing is done, you can view the regression test results in the **Reports** folder at C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\config\sampleArtifacts\Reports


###### Step 5: View results


To view the results:

1. Navigate to the **Reports** folder at C:\Edifecs\XEServer\profiles\Sample Regression Test\config\sampleArtifacts\Reports.
2. Open the index.html to view the results.




You can view two level of reports - folder comparison and file comparison. For more information on reports, see [Comparison Reports](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Compare.htm#Comparison_Reports).


###### How the Sample Regression Test Route Work


The Sample Regression Test profile contains the following routes:

- Eligibility Profile Testing
- Regression Testing
- Setup
- Teardown


###### Eligibility Profile Testing Route


This route picks up the input files, sends them to the 270-271 profile for processing, captures the output data, and does a comparison.



###### Regression Testing Route


This route contains several subroutes, and triggers different regression testing scenarios by processing test scripts and routing the output data to subroutes.



###### Setup Route


This route clears the data from the output folder of the Sample 270-271 profile (profile that is being tested), and sets up the testing environment.



###### Teardown Route


This route cleans up tracers from the profile location.

---

<a id="control-xeserver"></a>

## Control XEServer

> 📄 Source: [Book_Control_XEServer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Control_XEServer.htm)

### Control XEServer


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="admin-console-overview"></a>

### Admin Console Overview

> 📄 Source: [Admin_Console_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Admin_Console_Overview.htm)

#### Admin Console Overview


Admin Console is used to control and monitor XEServer profiles and manage XEServer licenses. The Admin Console has several views, depending on the item selected on the EAM Outline tab.


##### License View


For example, if you open the Admin Console and select the top-level XEServer item on the Outline tab, the Admin Console has the following view.


*[Image: Admin Console tab]*


In this view, you can view XEServer license information and [activate new licenses](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manage_XEServer_Licenses.htm).


##### Profiles View


If you open the Admin Console and select the Profiles expandable item on the Outline tab, the Admin Console has the following view.


*[Image: Admin Console Default View]*


This is the default Admin Console view that displays statistics on all XEServer profiles and allows you to control the selected profiles.


###### Edit profile ports


In this view, you can change a profile's host and port values for JMS, SSH, HTTP, HTTPS protocols. For all the four protocols, you can specify any port number that is within the range 1000 - 65535. If a port number you want to use is already occupied by another XEServer profile, you receive a warning.


##### Individual Profile View


If you open the Admin Console and select an individual profile on the Outline tab, the Admin Console has the following view:



This view displays statistics about the selected profile and allows you to control the profile.

---

<a id="view-web-console"></a>

### View Web Console

> 📄 Source: [View_Admin_Console.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/View_Admin_Console.htm)

#### View Web Console


You can use Web Console to view all the available services within the selected profile.


To view services:

1. On the System tab, expand XEServer, and double-click Admin Console.
2. On the Outline tab, expand Profiles, and double-click the profile you need.
3. On the Admin Console toolbar, click Start to start the selected profile.
4. Go to the Outline tab, expand the selected profile, and double-click Web Console.


*[Image: Outline tab]*


You can view the web console in the EAM or in your browser at http://localhost:9080/console/.The list of all available services is displayed. Click XEngine Server Web Console to view routes statuses within the current profile.


*[Image: Admin Console tab]*

---

<a id="start-xeserver"></a>

### Start XEServer

> 📄 Source: [Start_XEServer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Start_XEServer.htm)

#### Start XEServer


XEServer can be started in several ways:

- By using [Admin Console](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Admin_Console_Overview.htm)
- By using the [HTTPS endpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_Using_HTTP(S)_Endpoints.htm#PUT_state)
- As a [Windows Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_XEServer_as_Windows_Service.htm)(Windows only)
- By using the scripts in the directory ${XESRoot}\bin. The directory has two scripts to start XEServer: - start.bat(.sh) <profileName>: Starts a local XEServer profile. - start-cloud-profile.bat(.sh) <repository://path_to_profile_image.zip>: downloads a profile image from the [Smart Trading Cloud Repository](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Smart_Trading_Cloud_Repository_Overview.htm), and then starts the downloaded profile.


Note See XEServer Log Viewer or the log file (located in the directory ${XESProfile}\log\) for an explanation of any errors that might occur when attempting to start the server.

---

<a id="control-xeserver-using-ssh-client"></a>

### Control XEServer using SSH Client

> 📄 Source: [Book_Control_XEServer_using_SSH_Client.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Control_XEServer_using_SSH_Client.htm)

#### Control XEServer by using SSH Client


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="control-xeserver-using-ssh-client-overview"></a>

#### Control XEServer Using SSH Client Overview

> 📄 Source: [Control_XEServer_Using_SSH_Client_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_Using_SSH_Client_Overview.htm)

##### Control XEServer Using SSH Client Overview


There is an ability to control XEServer through .ssh console. Using this tool you can stop, start, reset specified processes, services or XEServer as a whole. There are two ways to control XEServer through the SSH protocol:

- [Using SSH Console](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_SSH_Console.htm)
- [Using PuTTY](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_Putty.htm)


You need to specify a port for SSH connection. When you run SSH console through the ssh.bat file the default port is set in this file. However, you may change it if you need to use another port. You can check the port to be used in Edifecs Application Manager.


To check the port to be used for SSH connection:

1. Start Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Double-click Admin Console.


See the port number in the SSH column on the Admin Console.



You also need a login and password to connect to XEServer through SSH. By default, XEServer contains one predefined SSH user xes identified by the password xes. You can add new console users by using [SSH Console commands](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SSH_Console_Parameters_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="control-xeserver-through-ssh-using-ssh-console"></a>

#### Control XEServer through SSH Using SSH Console

> 📄 Source: [Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_SSH_Console.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_SSH_Console.htm)

##### Control XEServer through SSH Using SSH Console


There is a .bat file provided with XEServer that runs SSH console. By default, all the necessary parameters like port number, login, and password are set in the file. However, you may change them if you need.


To run SSH console:

1. Make sure that the profile you are working with is running.
2. Go to ${XESRoot}\bin\ssh-client\ and run ssh.bat.
3. To override the default port number add the port option. For example, **ssh.bat -a 11001**.
4. Use available parameters to manage XEServer. For the list of available parameters and commands press the Tab key while your SSH session is active. For help with any of the commands, enter [command_name] --help.


For more information about the available parameters, see [SSH Console Parameters Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SSH_Console_Parameters_Overview.htm).

---

<a id="control-xeserver-through-ssh-using-putty"></a>

#### Control XEServer through SSH Using PuTTY

> 📄 Source: [Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_Putty.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_Putty.htm)

##### Control XEServer through SSH Using PuTTY


You can control services, processes and XEServer as a whole by using PuTTY SSH client.


To control XEServer by using PuTTY:

1. Run putty.exe.
2. Provide host name, SSH port, and session name on the PuTTY Configuration screen.
3. Click Open.


On the terminal screen provide the SSH user password and login to connect to XEServer.


Use available parameters to manage your XEServer. For the list of available parameters, see [SSH Console Parameters Overview.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SSH_Console_Parameters_Overview.htm)

---

<a id="ssh-console-parameters-overview"></a>

#### SSH Console Parameters Overview

> 📄 Source: [SSH_Console_Parameters_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SSH_Console_Parameters_Overview.htm)

##### SSH Console Parameters Overview


Below is the list of available parameters to be used when controlling XEServer through SSH console.


| Parameter | Description | Arguments |
| --- | --- | --- |
| --help | Displays this help message |   |
| adduser [user] [password] | Creates a new console user. | user: The name of the user. password: The password of the new user. |
| listusers | Displays the SSH console users list. |   |
| reset-server | Resets XEServer. |   |
| stat [command] | Route statistics trigger. | start: Starts the route statistics. stop: Stops the route statistics. dump: Dumps the route statistics. reset: Resets the route statistics. |
| terminate | Terminates XEServer. |   |
| bc-search [group] | Performs search in Balance Counters database. | group: The name of the Counter Group. |
| pid | Returns XEServer process ID. |   |
| scope | Changes the scope of commands. The default scope is xes. You can use commands without the xes prefix while you are in the xes scope. In this case, typing start is equal to xes: start. | scope: The scope of the command. |
| status | Returns the status of the XEServer. |   |
| dump-threads | Threads dump to the log folder. |   |
| removeuser [user] | Removes the SSH console user. | user: The name of the user that must be removed. |
| shutdown | Shuts down the XEServer. |   |
| start [target] | Starts XEServer's services. You can specify a service ID to start only this particular service. If no ID is specified, the command is applied to all available services. | target: Command target ID. |
| stop [target] | Stops XEServer's services. You can specify a service ID to stop only this particular service. If no ID is specified, the command is applied to all available services. | target: Command target ID. |
| gc | Requests garbage collection in JVM. |   |
| reset [target] | Resets XEServer's services. You can specify a service ID to reset only this particular service. If no ID is specified, the command is applied to all available services. | target: Command target ID. |
| start-server | Starts XEServer. |   |
| stop-server | Stops XEServer. |   |
| tracer [command] | Route tracer trigger. | start: Starts tracing all routes. XEServer uses the settings from the Tracer tab of the [route properties](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Route_Properties.htm#Tracer), but changes activation mode to Always. reset: Sets the tracer activation mode back. Empty command displays the current route tracer status. If you restart a route, it doesn't affect tracer settings. If you restart a profile, tracer settings are reset. |

---

<a id="reset-vs-shutdown"></a>

### Reset vs Shutdown

> 📄 Source: [Reset_vs_Shutdown.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Reset_vs_Shutdown.htm)

#### Reset vs. Shutdown


There are following XEServer commands that stop XEServer profiles:

- Stop. Stops the profile execution.
- Reset. Resets the profile execution.
- Shutdown. Stops the profile execution and terminates the corresponding process in the underlying operating system. If you change your profile configuration and want the new settings to be used by the profile, it is recommended that you use Shutdown + Start commands as it will guarantee that the profile picks up the latest configuration changes.


XEServer can be reset or shut down in four ways:

- [Using Admin Console](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Admin_Console_Overview.htm)
- [Using Windows Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Use_XEServer_as_Windows_Service.htm)
- [Using SSH Console](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_through_SSH_Using_SSH_Console.htm)
- [Using HTTPS Endpoints](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_XEServer_Using_HTTP(S)_Endpoints.htm)


Note When using the reset command in the Windows service, it stops the whole XEServer process and starts it again from scratch.

---

<a id="profile-samples"></a>

## Profile Samples

> 📄 Source: [Book_Profile_Samples.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Profile_Samples.htm)

### Profile Samples


The following sample profiles are available with the default XEServer installation.



Tip XEConnect sample profiles become available after you install XEConnect.


[Edifecs Library](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Edifecs_Library.htm) contains a set of preconfigured XEServer profiles that illustrate the best practices and reusable patterns when you use the XEServer functions for real-life EDI tasks. To use the sample profiles from the Edifecs Library, you have to:

1. Specify your Smart Trading Account credentials in EAM (XEServer Environment tab => Repository tab).
2. Select Edifecs Library template location when creating a new XEServer profile.


Check [Edifecs Library sample profiles](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/Library/Content/Home_Edifecs_Library_Profiles.htm) before you create an XEServer route from scratch. We regularly update and extend the list of sample profiles to help you meet your business requirements.
