<a id="sample-270271"></a>

#### Sample 270/271

> 📄 Source: [Book_270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm)

##### 270/271 Realtime with Timeout Scenario


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="270271-realtime-with-timeout-scenario"></a>

##### 270/271 Realtime with Timeout Scenario

> 📄 Source: [270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm)

###### 270/271 Realtime with Timeout Scenario


A submitting system sends a 270 request transaction. According to real-time processing rules, a 271 response must be sent back within a specified period of time. XEngine operates between the sender and the customer’s back-end system. You receive the 270 data file, send it to the back end where a 271 response is generated. If the back end does not respond within the specified period of time, the original 270 is mapped to a special timeout 271 response, which is sent back.


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In the Template list, click Sample 270-271.
5. Click Finish.


To simulate a timeout:

- On the System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin console.Note Disable any other XEServer processes.
- On the Admin Console tab, click the profile name, and then click Start.
- Copy the 5010_270_x279_A1_good.dat file from the sampleData folder to the inbound folder.
- Ensure that the accepted acknowledgments were generated.
- A timeout message should be created in the Out_271 folder.

---

<a id="270-to-back-end"></a>

##### 270 to Back End

> 📄 Source: [270_to_Back_End.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/270_to_Back_End.htm)

###### 270 to Back End


Route Components


In this scenario 270 transaction is received from a client. For more info, see [270/271 Realtime with Timeout Scenario](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm).


This route receives 270 transaction from a client, validates it, and creates acknowledgments. If the 270 transaction is accepted, it is sent to back-end system for the further processing. At the same time the 270 transaction is stored in the Data Storage. The JMS message that contains the arrival time of the 270 transaction is sent to the expiration queue.


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.

1. In Profile Name provide a name for the profile.
2. In the Template list, click Sample 270-271.
3. Click OK.
4. On the System tab, expand Profiles, and then expand the created profile.
5. Double-click Configuration.
6. On the Outline tab, expand Services, double-click Routes, expand Sample, and select 270_to_BackEnd from the list of sample routes.


 


*[Image: 270 to Back End route]*


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Inbound 270 | JMS Inbound | Original source for 270 data file. |
| Native Processing | Native Processing | Processes data and creates acknowledgments. |
| Ack | File System Outbound | Folder for acknowledgments. |
| Accepted 270 filter | Router | Passes along accepted 270 data. |
| Save 270 | Data Storage Outbound | Storage location for 270 data. |
| To Back-End 270 | JMS Outbound | Simulation of 271 from back-end system. |
| Remove Message Body | Velocity | Removes the message body according to the corresponding template. |
| Outbound 271 | JMS Outbound | Final 271 output file. |



To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started

---

<a id="271-from-back-end"></a>

##### 271 from Back End

> 📄 Source: [271_from_Back_End.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/271_from_Back_End.htm)

###### 271 from Back End


Route Components


In this scenario the 270 transaction message is send to back-end system for the further processing. For more info, see [270/271 Realtime with Timeout Scenario](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm).


This route receives 271 transaction response from the back-end system and validates it. If the 271 transaction response is valid and processing timeout is not exceeded, then the 271 transaction is sent to the client. If the 271 transaction response is valid but processing timeout is exceeded, then the 271 transaction from the back-end is saved as 'expired' to the file system. If the 271 transaction response is not valid, it is saved as 'invalid' to the file system.


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In Profile Name provide a name for the profile.
5. In the Template list, click Sample 270-271.
6. Click OK.
7. On the System tab, expand Profiles, and then expand the created profile.
8. Double-click Configuration.
9. On the Outline tab, expand Services.
10. Double-click Routes, expand Sample.
11. Select 271_from_BackEnd from the list of sample routes.


*[Image: 271 from Back End route]*


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| From Back End 271 | JMS Inbound | JMS message from the back end. |
| Validate 271 Response | Native Processing | Validates the result and processes it to the Router in original format. |
| Router | Router | Passes good files to the corresponding Data Storage. |
| Invalid 271 | File System Outbound | Invalid 271 transaction response is saved. |
| Remove original 270 | Data Storage Outbound | Removes original 270 data file. |
| Data removed check | Data Storage Inbound | Defines whether the 271 transaction response was on time or expired. |
| Outbound 271 | JMS Outbound | Final 271 output file is sent to the client. |
| Save Expired 271 | File System Outbound | Expired file is saved to the file system. |



To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started

---

<a id="expiration"></a>

##### Expiration

> 📄 Source: [Expiration.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Expiration.htm)

###### Expiration


Route Components


In this scenario the 270 transaction message is send to back-end system for the further processing.


For more info, see [270/271 Realtime with Timeout Scenario](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_270_271_Realtime_with_Timeout_Scenario.htm).


This route receives JMS message from the expiration queue and waits for the expiration timeout. If expiration timeout for the corresponding 270 transaction is elapsed and no 271 response is received from the back end, then XE Mapper generates 271 transaction from the 270 one. If expiration timeout for the corresponding 270 transaction is elapsed but 271 response from the back-end system has been already received, then no 271 transaction is generated.


Note The number of instances for this route should be greater than or equal to the number of instances for route '270_to_BackEnd'.


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In Profile Name, provide a name for the profile.
5. In the Template list, click Sample 270-271.
6. Click OK.
7. On the System tab, expand Profiles, and then expand the created profile.
8. Double-click Configuration.
9. On the Outline tab, expand Services.
10. Double-click Routes, expand Sample.
11. Select Expiration from the list of sample routes.


*[Image: Expiration route]*


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| JMS Inbound | JMS Inbound | Original source for data file. |
| Delay | Delay | Sets timer delay for the specified time. |
| Retrieve 270 | Data Storage Inbound | Data storage for 270 file with errors. |
| Translator | Translator | Maps 270 to 271. |
| Outbound 271 | JMS Outbound | Final 271 output file. |



To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started

---

<a id="xeserver-lessons"></a>

#### XEServer Lessons

> 📄 Source: [Book_XEServer_Lessons.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_XEServer_Lessons.htm)

##### XEServer Lessons


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="file-system-batching"></a>

##### File System Batching

> 📄 Source: [File_System_Batching.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_System_Batching.htm)

###### File System Batching


Route Components Folder Structure Test the Route


You receive multiple XData files during the day and want to batch them together into a single EDI file by using a control message as a triggering mechanism.


The control message is a trigger for the Inbound Endpoint to read all XData messages from the specified folder. They are then aggregated into a single multimessage exchange, and then batched by using the XData Processing route component. The resulting EDI file is sent to the Output folder on the file system.


*[Image: XEngine Server lessons route]*


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In Profile Name provide a name for the profile.
5. In the Template list, click XEServer Lessons.
6. Click OK.
7. Expand Profiles, expand the profile you need, and then double-click Configuration.
8. On the Outline tab, expand Service manager, and then expand Routes.
9. Click Lesson1_FileSystemBatching.


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Control Message | FS Inbound | Passes the control message. |
| Inbound | FS Inbound | Inbound folder for XData. |
| Aggregation | Aggregation | Collects all messages to one multimessage exchange. |
| XData Batching | XData Processing | XEngine batches all the message exchanges to one EDI file. |
| Outbound | FS Outbound | Outbound - outbound folder for the result EDI file. |



###### Folder Structure

- Control message - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson1-FileSystemBatching/Control
- FS Inbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson1-FileSystemBatching/In
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson1-FileSystemBatching/Out


###### Test the Route


To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started
- Go to ${XESRoot}/profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson1-FileSystemBatching.
- Copy all XData files from SampleData to In folder.
- Copy control message from SampleData folder to Control folder.
- Ensure all XData files are picked up from In folder.
- Outbound folder should contain .dat file with batched EDI result.

---

<a id="jms-batching"></a>

##### JMS Batching

> 📄 Source: [JMS_Batching.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_Batching.htm)

###### JMS Batching


You receive multiple XData files during the day and want to batch them together into a single XData file and send the file and the control message to a JMS queue.


*[Image: XEngine Server lessons route]*


This works the same as the File System Batching, except that the control message and data is read from the queue. Results are delivered to the outbound queue. To facilitate queue population there is a sub-route in the same route. Its main purpose is to transfer data in passthrough mode to and from a JMS queues.


*[Image: XEngine Server lessons route]*


Note ApacheMQ is used as a transport server, so it must be enabled by user in Services section of Profile configuration.


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In Profile Name provide a name for the profile.
5. In the Template list, click XEServer Lessons.
6. Click OK.
7. Expand Profiles, expand the profile you need, and double-click Configuration.
8. On the Outline tab, expand Service manager, and expand Routes.
9. Click Lesson2_JMSBatching.


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Control1 | JMS Inbound | Passes the control message. |
| Inbound1 | JMS Inbound | Incoming data folder. |
| Aggregation 1 | Aggregation | Collects all messages to one multimessage exchange. |
| XData Batching1 | XData Processing | XEngine batches all the message exchanges to one EDI file. |
| Outbound | JMS Outbound | Outbound folder for the result EDI file. |



###### Folder Structure

- Control message - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson1-FileSystemBatching/Control
- FS Inbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson2-JMSBatching/In
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson2-JMSBatching/Out


###### Start the Profile


To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started


###### Test the Profile

1. Copy all XData files from SampleData to In folder.
2. Copy control message from SampleData folder to Control folder.
3. Ensure all XData files are picked up from In folder.
4. Out folder should contain .dat file with batched EDI result.

---

<a id="simple-splitting"></a>

##### Simple Splitting

> 📄 Source: [Simple_Splitting.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Simple_Splitting.htm)

###### Simple Splitting


You receive an 837 Professional 4010A1 data file containing three transactions. Two of the transactions are good (no errors), and one is bad. You want to split good transactions from bad ones, sending 997 and TA1 acknowledgments and generate XML and HTML Audit and LDNS reports.


Data comes to the Inbound folder, and then the Native Processing component performs XEngine execution with validation, splitting and report generation. Results are routed to the appropriate folder.


*[Image: XEngine Server lessons route]*


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In Profile Name, provide a name for the profile.
5. In the Template list, click XEServer Lessons.
6. Click OK.
7. Expand Profiles, expand the profile you need, and then double-click Configuration.
8. On the Outline tab, expand Service manager, and expand Routes.
9. Click Lesson3_SimpleSplitting.


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Inbound1 | FS Inbound | Inbound folder that receives source 837 transaction. |
| Native Processing_1 | Native Processing | XEngine performs splitting and produces reports and acknowledgments. |
| Good Data1 | FS Outbound | Outgoing split good data results. |
| Bad Data1 | FS Outbound | Outgoing split bad data results. |
| Reports1 | FS Outbound | Outgoing reports. |
| Achs1 | FS Outbound | Outbound acknowledgments. |



###### Folder Structure

- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson3-SimpleSplitting/Acks
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson3-SimpleSplitting/BadData
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson3-SimpleSplitting/GoodData
- FS Inbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson3-SimpleSplitting/In
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson3-SimpleSplitting/Reports
- SampleData - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson3-SimpleSplitting/SampleData with sample data file (837 with 3 transactions)


###### Start the Route


To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started


###### Test the Profile

1. Drop the 837 file from SampleData to In folder.
2. Check if there are two Acks (TA1, 997), 4 reports, bad data with 1 transaction, good data with 2 transactions.

---

<a id="split-and-translate"></a>

##### Split and Translate

> 📄 Source: [Split_and_Translate.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Split_and_Translate.htm)

###### Split and Translate


You receive an 837 Professional 4010A1 data file containing three transactions. One transaction is bad, another is intended to go to the back-end system for Payer 1 who only accepts proprietary COBOL files. The third transaction goes to Payer 2 who expects XML in Edifecs XData format.


*[Image: XEngine Server lesson route]*


The source file is validated and split by using a custom split rule based on Payer Id. Bad data is sent to the Bad Data folder. Initial validation reports are stored in the Reports folder. Data for Payer 1 is translated from EDI to COBOL, and then send to the Payer 1 back-end system. Data for Payer 2 is changed from native to XData, and then sent to the corresponding folder.


To access this route:

1. Open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer.
3. Right-click Profiles, and then click New.
4. In Profile Name, provide a name for the profile.
5. In the Template list, click XEServer Lessons.
6. Click OK.
7. Expand Profiles, expand the profile you need, and then double-click Configuration.
8. On the Outline tab, expand Service manager, and then expand Routes.
9. Click Lesson4_SplittingAndMapping.


###### Route Components


| Component Name | Component Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Inbound | FS Inbound | Incoming data folder. |
| Native Processing | Native Processing | XEngine based on the performs splitting based on the custom splitting rules file. |
| Translator | Translator | Translator performs transformation from EDI to COBOL. |
| Native to XData | Native Processing | Good split result is transformed to XData. |
| Bad Data | FS Outbound | Bad splitting result. |
| Reports | FS Outbound | Audit and LDNS reports in both xml and html formats. |
| Good Data Payer 1 | FS Outbound | Good COBOL file. |
| Good Data Payer 2 | FS Outbound | XData result. |



###### Folder Structure

- FS Inbound -${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/In
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/BackEndPayer1-COBOL
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/BackEndPayer2-XML
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/BadData
- FS Outbound - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/Reports
- Config - ${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/Config - configuration (837 to COBOL map, COBOL ecs file, Data Split Rules XML file)
- SampleData -${XESRoot}profiles/[ProfileName]/config/_lessons/Lesson4-SplittingAndMapping/SampleData


###### Start the Profile


To start the profile:

- On the EAM System tab, expand XEServer.
- Double-click Admin Console.
- On the EAM Outline tab, expand Profiles, and select the profile you need.
- On the Admin Console tab, click Start.
- Verify that all the profile's routes and services have been started


###### Test the Profile

1. Drop the 837 file from SampleData to In folder.
2. Check that BackEndPayer1-COBOL folder was updated with the COBOL file.
3. Check that BackEndPayer2-XML folder contains XData XML file.
4. Ensure bad data got to the BadData folder and reports got to Reports folder.

---

<a id="performance"></a>

## Performance

> 📄 Source: [Book_Performance.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Performance.htm)

### Performance


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="route-instances-and-asynchronous-connection"></a>

### Route Instances and Asynchronous Connection

> 📄 Source: [Route_Instances_And_Asynchronous_Connection.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Route_Instances_And_Asynchronous_Connection.htm)

#### Route Instances and Asynchronous Connection


If the route processing takes too much time and there is a need to increase the route throughput, you can change the following parameters:

- Number of instances of the route. One route can have several instances which altogether are applied to the same tasks queue. They work simultaneously so the tasks assigned for the route are split between them. To set the number of route instances, select the corresponding route on the Outline tab, and on the Overview tab, set the needed value.


*[Image: Route Service Overview tab]*

- Number of threads for asynchronous connection. The number of tasks threads that can be transferred to next component simultaneously. To set the number of asynchronous connection threads, select the corresponding connection arrow on the Route editor, and on the Settings tab, set the needed value.


All the messages are sent one by one. As soon as the processing starts on the first message, the second message is picked up and sent to the queue. You can set maximum number of messages to be sent to one component. When the number of allowed threads is reached, processor waits for at least one message to be processed and then picks up the next message.


*[Image: Asynchronous Threads]*


Asynchronous connection threads work only for the next component on the route, whereas route instances work for all route components involved in the route processing. The number of route instances, and the number of connection threads, if equal, result in the same performance growth.

---

<a id="xeserver-statistics"></a>

### XEServer Statistics

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Statistics.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Statistics.htm)

#### XEServer Statistics


XEServer provides several statistics options. Statistics is used to troubleshoot performance issues and get detailed analysis of your routes. There are several sources of XEServer statistics:

- Real-time statistics in EAM Admin Console. This statistics shows the current system load including the amount of tasks processed and being processed, failures, average performance index, and so on.
- XEServer and Routes logging. This information is available in the form of standard logs of Edifecs Application Manager Log Viewer. These logs primarily show when a certain route was started, stopped, then the inbound data was received, and so on.
- Component Level Run-time Statistics. A new feature that is added in 8.0. It allows you to collect statistics from a live running system by turning it on and off and get results in a form of a special log file.
- Zabbix Statistics. Allows you to send XEServer statistics to a Zabbix server. To configure statistics parameters, see [Zabbix Statistics](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Zabbix_Statistics.htm).
- XEServer profile statistics. This type of statistics is internal and is used by XESManager to display the status of a monitored XEServer instance and to generate statistical alerts. For more information, see [Manage Statistics Gathering](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manage_Statistics_Gathering.htm).

---

<a id="manage-statistics-gathering"></a>

### Manage Statistics Gathering

> 📄 Source: [Manage_Statistics_Gathering.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manage_Statistics_Gathering.htm)

#### Manage Statistics Gathering


XEServer generates internal profile statistics that is used by XESManager to display the status of a monitored XEServer instance and to generate statistical alerts. By default, the profile statistics gathering is enabled. You can suppress gathering of statistical data for a profile, a service, or a route, and configure statistics purging. XEServer stores statistics in the file system at the ${XESRoot}/statistics/profiles/<ProfileName>/ directory.


If the statistics gathering is enabled, the default values of the statistics configuration properties for all profiles are the following:


| Property | Value |
| --- | --- |
| statistics | true |
| statistics.suppress.<all_types> | false |
| statistic.expiration.<default> | 2 months |
| statistic.expiration.component | 1 month |



To disable statistics gathering, set the statistics property to false.


You can use the following properties to manage statistics gathering:


| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| statistic=true/false | Enables/disables storing statistics to the File System. |
| statistic.suppress.<profile/service/route/ component/batcher/statreporter/dupcheck>=true/false | Enables/disables gathering of statistics for all types. |
| statistic.expiration.<default/profile/service/route /component/batcher/statreporter/dupcheck>=integer | Indicates the retention period of statistical data in months. The data older than the specified time frame is deleted. |



To add a new property to a profile:

1. On the workstation where you have the monitored XEServer installed, open Edifecs Application Manager.
2. On the System tab, expand XEServer, and then expand Profiles.
3. Expand the profile you need, and then double-click Configuration.
4. On the Profile Information tab, click Environment.
5. On the right side of the Profile Environment tab, click New, and then select Environment properties category.
6. In the New Environment properties category dialog box, enter a name for the new category, and then click OK.
7. On the right side of the Profile Environment tab, click New, and then select Environment property.
8. In the New Environment Property dialog box, in Name, enter a property from the table, in Value, enter a value from the table depending on your needs.
9. Click Finish.
10. Click Save() or press CTRL+S.


The statistical alert constructor searches for data in the generated statistics and triggers when alert conditions are met.

---

<a id="component-level-run-time-statistics"></a>

### Component Level Run-Time Statistics

> 📄 Source: [Component_Level_Run_Time_Statistics.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Level_Run_Time_Statistics.htm)

#### Component Level Run-Time Statistics


The purpose of this statistics is to get a raw log of events happening in the system as well as cumulative time metrics to understand what component from what route took which amount of time on the test data files.


Access to the statistics is provided through secure SSH protocol. You can use any SSH client to connect to XEServer remotely.


*[Image: Statistics Console]*


In order to start the statistics gathering, use the command xes:stat start, when the process is completed and reports needs to be generated, please use the command xes:stat dump. This leads to a creation of a new log with raw results collected during the statistics gathering session.


Here is an example of such log:


*[Image: Log example]*


Main information that this log shows:

- What components on what routes were invoked during this time
- Type of the component (just in case the name is not self-sufficient)
- Amount of times the component was invoked
- Total time component worked
- Average time
- Date/Time of the first invocation
- Date/Time of the last invocation


This log is a tab-separated and can be easily imported to any office application such as Microsoft Excel for further analysis.

---

<a id="zabbix-statistics"></a>

### Zabbix Statistics

> 📄 Source: [Zabbix_Statistics.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Zabbix_Statistics.htm)

#### Zabbix Statistics


By using a remote Zabbix server, you can track your XEServer statistics and receive notifications on predefined events. To integrate your XEServer with Zabbix, create an account at *http://www.zabbix.com* and configure statistics parameters. Configuring of statistics parameters includes managing a list of suppressed metric categories, specifying system and environment properties, and managing the metrics configuration file.


###### A list of suppressed metric categories


All metrics categories are stored in the *zabbix.properties* file, located at ${XESRoot}*/platform/resources/statistics/*. By using the metric categories list, you can define the statistics categories to be reported to a Zabbix server. Setting the value of a metric category to false, enables sending statistics information about this category to a Zabbix server. Set the value to true, to stop sending data about the corresponding category. The following metric categories are available:

- zabix.suppress.profile
- zabix.suppress.service
- zabix.suppress.route
- zabix.suppress.component
- zabix.suppress.batcher
- zabix.suppress.statreporter
- zabix.suppress.dupcheck
- zabix.suppress.dupcheck.service
- zabix.suppress.rest


For example, to track statistics on the Profile category, set the zabix.suppress.profile value to **FALSE**. To stop tracking the category, set its value to TRUE.


###### Setting system and environment properties


To establish a connection to a remote Zabbix server, configure the system and environment properties. The following environment properties need to be added:

- zabbixServer - the IP address of a Zabbix server.
- zabbixWebPort - the port number used for connection to Zabbix web interface.
- zabbixServerPort - the port number used for connection to a Zabbix server.
- zabbixUsername - the user name of your Zabbix account.
- zabbixPassword - the password to your Zabbix account.
- zabbixHost - Zabbix hosts you want to monitor. Typical Zabbix hosts are the devices you want to monitor, such as servers, workstations, and switches.
- zabbixHostGroup - a group of hosts you want to monitor. All Zabbix hosts are organized into groups.


To add a new environment property:

1. Expand your profile and double-click Configuration.
2. On the Environment tab, click New... and in the Environment properties category dialog box, type a property name, and then click Finish. The environment property category is created.
3. Click the created environment property category, and then click New..., select the Environment property wizard, and then click Next.
4. In Name, type zabbixServer and in Value, type the IP address of your Zabbix server.
5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 to provide name-value pairs for each environment property. All properties are displayed on the Environment tab. After you have specified all the environment properties, your Environment tab should look like the following:


*#### Metrics configuration file The metrics configuration file is used to define which metrics are to be reported to a Zabbix server for every statistics group. The file is located at ${XESRoot}/platform/resources/statistics/zabbix-metrics.json*.


By specifying key-value pairs in this file, you can define which metrics are reported to a Zabbix server. The following metrics are supported:

- MAX - tracks the maximum value of a category.
- MIN - tracks the minimum value of a category.
- AVERAGE - tracks the average value of a category.
- SUM - tracks the sum of value categories.
- COUNT - tracks the number of values reported for a period of time.
- LATEST - tracks the last reported value.


By default, metrics are tracked without any time limitations. By adding the prefix "-LASTMIN" to a metric, you can track metric values only within the last minute. For example, to report the CPU(%) parameter from the Profile group with the LATEST and MAX metrics, add the following to the configuration file:


Example


{


"name": "CPU (%)",


"type": "LATEST,MAX"


}

---

<a id="test-xeserver"></a>

## Test XEServer

> 📄 Source: [Book_Test_XEServer.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Test_XEServer.htm)

### Test XEServer


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="xeserver-test-tools"></a>

### XEServer Test Tools

> 📄 Source: [XEServer_Test_Tools.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XEServer_Test_Tools.htm)

#### XEServer Test Tools


There are several command-line tools distributed with XEServer.


| File Name | Location | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| start.bat | ${XESRoot}\bin\start.bat | Allows you to start any XEServer profile manually to test functionality. A profile name should be specified. For example: start.bat sample-profile. |
| ssh.bat | ${XESRoot}\bin\ssh-client\ssh.bat | Allows you to run a terminal that connects to a profile through a secure shell. You can start, stop, check status for routes, processes, and services. |
| JMS test files | ${XESRoot}\test\jms\ | Sample .bat files that allow you to send or receive messages to or from the following JMS-compliant providers: ActiveMQ WebSphere (IBMMQ) WebSphere Secure Sockets Layer (IBMMQ SSL) WebLogic |
| ConfigTool | ${EAMRoot}\Server\ConfigTool\exec\win | Command-line tools that allow you to create configuration images for XEngine, XEServer, and then deploy them. These images can be used to assist in moving XEngine or XEServer configurations from one computer to another or to migrate between different versions of the applications. It doesn’t support partial images. Partial images can be created or deployed by using the EAM Configuration Manager. For more information about EAM Configuration Manager, see [Configuration Manager Overview](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Configuration_Manager_Overview.htm). |
| ConfigTool | ${EAMRoot}\Server\ConfigTool\exec\win\deploy_xescfg.bat | A configuration tool that allows you to deploy a profile to XEServer. The possible return values are the following: 0 - A program finished successfully. 1 - A program failed with an exception. 2 - A wrong program usage. Use the -h option to see the list of command line parameters descriptions. 3 - A program failed with an exception but a target system was not changed. 4 - A program failed with an exception. It's probably that a target system was changed. You should analyze log files and correct installation directory in case of unrollbacked changes. 5 - A program is unable to process an image file. 6 - The deployment was rejected because a target profile is active and running now. You should stop the running profile and perform the image deployment one more time. |
| HTTP_Client.bat | ${XESRoot}test\http\HTTP_Client.bat | Tool for testing HTTP endpoint components. Use the -h option to display command line parameters descriptions. For more information about this tool, see [Test XEServer Using the HTTP Test Tool.](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Test_XEServer_Using_the_HTTP_Test_Tool.htm) |
| WS_Client.bat | ${XESRoot}\ws\WS_Client.bat | Tool for testing Web Service endpoint components. For more information about this tool, see [Test XEServer Using the Web Service Test Tool](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Test XEServer Using the Web Service Test Tool.htm). |
| fs-test.bat | ${XESRoot}\test\enviroment\fs-test\config | With this test tool, you can evaluate your current file system performance. For more information, see [File System Test Tool](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Test_Tool.htm). |
| jms-test.bat | ${XESRoot}\test\enviroment\jms-test\config | With this test tool, you can evaluate the performamce of your JMS servers. For more information, see [JMS System Test Tool](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_Test_Tool.htm). |

---

<a id="test-xeserver-using-the-jms-test-tool"></a>

### Test XEServer Using the JMS Test Tool

> 📄 Source: [Test_XEServer_Using _JMS_Test_Tool.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Test_XEServer_Using _JMS_Test_Tool.htm)

#### Test XEServer Using the JMS Test Tool


The XEServer JMS test tool is used to send JMS messages to a queue and have XEServer receive the data for processing by using JMS connectors. XEServer results are sent to a JMS queue to be retrieved by the receiver. The files for the test tool are located in the ${XESRoot}\test\jms folder and are organized by JMS-compliant messaging system type. They consist of a set of .bat, .conf, and .properties files that define the queues and other properties. The files can be modified to match the customer's configuration. The customer needs to configure inbound and outbound connectors and verify the queue names.


The following JMS-compliant providers have test tools:

- ActiveMQ
- WebSphere (IBMMQ)
- WebSphere Secure Sockets Layer (IBMMQ SSL)
- WebLogic


For more information about the queue type, see [Test the XEServer Install](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Installation_Instructions/Test_XEServer_Installation.htm).


To run the test tool:

1. Go to the ${XESRoot}\test folder.
2. Open the folder for your messaging system.
3. Verify the queue names in the sender and receiver batch files.
4. Run the batch files in the correct order.


For example, if you want to send and receive files by using WebLogic, run the batch files in the following order:

1. createQueues.bat
2. sender.bat (or any other sender file)
3. receiver.bat


Use the properties files to specify the queue name, connection factory, the source file to send, destination for receiving messages, for server credentials, and so on. The ${XESRoot}\test\data folder contains files to send.

---

<a id="test-xeserver-using-the-http-test-tool"></a>

### Test XEServer Using the HTTP Test Tool

> 📄 Source: [Test_XEServer_Using_the_HTTP_Test_Tool.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Test_XEServer_Using_the_HTTP_Test_Tool.htm)

#### Test XEServer Using the HTTP Test Tool


The XEServer HTTP tool is used to test HTTP endpoint components.


The .bat file that initiates the testing is located at ${XESRoot}\test\http.


Use the -h option to see the list of command line parameters. The following parameters are available:


| Parameter | Default value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| -url <url> |   | Specify the URL for the connection. |
| -i <payload_file_name> |   | Specify the name for the HTTP request body file. |
| -hdr <headers_file_name> |   | Specify the name for request headers and request body files. |
| -n <#_iteration(s)> | 1 | Specify how many times the request is sent to the XEServer. |
| -th <# of thread(s)> | 1 | Specify number of thread requests are sent to XEServer. |
| -sm <response_saving_mode> | 0 | Specify the saving mode for the response: 0 - save all data headers and bodies 1 - suppress response header saving 2 - suppress all data saving. |
| -p <response_prefix> |   | Specify the prefix for the response file name. |
| -d <destination folder> |   | Specify the destination folder where the results are stored. |
| -ks <key store> |   | Specify the path to the key store. |
| -ksp <key store password> |   | Specify the password for the key store. |
| -tks <trusted store> |   | Specify the path to the trusted store. |
| -tksp <trusted store password> |   | Specify the password for the trusted store. |

---

<a id="test-xeserver-using-the-web-service-test-tool"></a>

### Test XEServer Using the Web Service Test Tool

> 📄 Source: [Test XEServer Using the Web Service Test Tool.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Test XEServer Using the Web Service Test Tool.htm)

#### Test XEServer Using the Web Service Test Tool


The XEServer web service tool is used to test the web service endpoint components.

- Go to the corresponding test file location.


Note Sample command batch files are under ${XESRoot}\test\ws\TestProfile.

- Edit batch files to provide the correct HTTP port in the –wsdl-url parameter.


Example


-wsdl-url "http://localhost:9086/ws?wsdl"

- Configure the samples with a specific data file.
- Submit your own tests by using the WS_Client.bat file found at ${XESRoot}\test\ws.
- Run WS_Client.bat to get the list of parameters.


The following parameters are available:


| Parameter | Default value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| -wsdl-url <url> |   | Specify WSDL URL for connecting to the web service. |
| -payload <payload_file_name> |   | Specify the name for request body file. If this parameter points to a directory with some files, then the multi-message request is sent; each file is mapped to the message. |
| -headers <headers_file_name> |   | Specify the name for request headers files. |
| -n <#_iteration(s) | 1 | Specify how many times the request is sent to the XEServer. |
| -th <# of thread(s)> | 1 | Specify number of thread requests is sent to XEServer. |
| -d <destination folder> |   | Specify the destination folder where the results are stored. |
| -rfnp <result_file_name_property> |   | Specifies which property of response message to use as file name for storing response message. |
| -mtom <true/false> | false | Specify whether or not to enable MTOM on the client side. |
| -soap <SOAP version> | 12 | Specify the version of SOAP to be used (either 11 or 12). |
| -ks <key store> |   | Specify the path to the key store. |
| -ksp <key store password> |   | Specify the password for the key store. |
| -tks <trusted store> |   | Specify the path to the trusted store. |
| -tksp <trusted store password> |   | Specify the password for the trusted store. |
| -username <username> |   | Specify the username for UsernameToken authentication. |
| -password <password> |   | Specify the password for UsernameToken authentication. |

---

<a id="environment-test-tools"></a>

### Environment Test Tools

> 📄 Source: [Book_Environment_Test_Tools.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_Environment_Test_Tools.htm)

#### Environment Test Tools


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="file-system-test-tool"></a>

#### File System Test Tool

> 📄 Source: [FS_Test_Tool.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Test_Tool.htm)

##### File System Test Tool


With this test tool, you can check the READ, WRITE, DELETE, and LIST operations' speed rates of your file system.


This is useful when you observe that XEServer processes that involve reading or writing data to the file system have become slow. To ascertain the reason for performance degradation, use this test tool to benchmark speeds for your file system. Then, by comparing the test tool statistics with the XEServer performance, you will know whether your XEServer configuration or your file system needs to be fixed.


To run the test tool, run the fs-test.bat script that is located at: ${XESRoot}\XEServer\test\enviroment\fs-test\.


###### Test tool parameters


You can run the test tool with the following parameters:


| Parameter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| -h | Displays available parameters descriptions. If this parameter is provided, all others are ignored. |
| -path <path> | The parameter is mandatory. This specifies the path to the file system directory to be tested. |
| -i <iterations> | Specifies the number of test iterations to be performed in each thread. The default value is "1". |
| -th <number of threads> | Specifies the number of java threads to be run. The default value is "1". |
| -files <number of files> | Specifies the number of files to be used during the test. The default value is "500". |
| -file-size <file size in bytes> | Specifies the file size in bytes. Use "*" for testing with mixed file sizes. |
| -m | Specifies the test mode. The following modes are available: full: Performs all the steps one after another. prepare: Writes files with random content to the specified directory. list: Lists all the files in the directory. read: Reads file contents from the directory. Note All created files are deleted after the testing is complete. The default value is "full". |
| -t | Specifies intervals between iterations in seconds. The default value is "3". |



###### Running the Test Tool


The command below demonstrates a sample test:


Example:


fs-test.bat -path C:\Edifecs\XEServer\test\enviroment\fs-test\ -i 3 -files -1000 -file-size 1000 -t 3


The script runs the test tool without the -m parameter specified, so the default full mode is used. In this case, 1000 files (1000 bytes each) are written to the C:\Edifecs\XEServer\test\enviroment\fs-test\ directory. Then, the file system lists the created files, and reads the file contents. Then, the created files are removed.


The procedure is iterated three times with intervals of 3 seconds. The statistics for READ, WRITE, LIST, and DELETE operations are stored in a result .csv file in the ${XESRoot}\XEServer\test\enviroment\fs-test\ directory and looks as shown below:


| date, | t, |   | count, | max, | avg, | min |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2016-11-17 19:03, | Thread#0, | Listing, | 3, | 10, | 10.000000, | 10 |   |
| 2016-11-17 19:03, | Thread#0, | Reading, | 3, | 40, | 36.498994, | 30 |   |
| 2016-11-17 19:03, | Thread#0, | Removal, | 3, | 340, | 326.693906, | 320 |   |
| 2016-11-17 19:03, | Thread#0, | Writing, | 3, | 360, | 313.727395, | 250 |   |



The result .csv file includes statistics on particular operations. Each line consists of the following fields:


| Line Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| date | The date and time when the statistics were aggregated. |
| t | The java thread within which the operation was performed. |
| operation | The name of the operation performed. |
| count | The number of iteration for an operation. |
| min | The minimum time in milliseconds to perform a single iteration. |
| avg | The average time in milliseconds to perform a single iteration. |
| max | The maximum time in milliseconds to perform a single iteration. |



After you get the statistics on your file system performance, you can compare them to the XEServer performance indices and ascertain the reason for performance degradation. The results of the test tool should be close to the XEServer performance. However you should consider moderate XEServer performance overheads (validating, preprocessing, parsing efforts, and so on).

---

<a id="jms-test-tool"></a>

#### JMS Test Tool

> 📄 Source: [JMS_Test_Tool.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_Test_Tool.htm)

##### JMS Test Tool


With this test tool, you can check the performance of a JMS server. This is useful when you observe that XEServer processes that involve interaction with JMS servers have become slow. To ascertain the reason for performance degradation, use this test tool to benchmark the speeds of read/write operations of your JMS server. Then, by comparing the test tool statistics with XEServer performance results, you will know whether your XEServer configuration or your JMS server needs to be fixed.


The test tool is compatible with JMS servers that support Java JMS API, including the most popular Apache ActiveMQ and IBMMQ servers. In this section, the XEServer ActiveMQ Server service has been used to deploy a local JMS server for testing.


To run the test tool, run one of the scripts located at: ${XESRoot}\test\enviroment\jms-test\ . The folder contains three .bat scripts:

1. jms-test.bat: Use to evaluate your JMS server performance for both read and write operations in the non-transacted mode.
2. jms-transacted-read-test.bat: Use to evaluate reading speed from your JMS server in the transacted mode.
3. jms-transacted-write-test.bat: Use to evaluate writing speed to your JMS server in the transacted mode.


###### JMS Server Configuration


To allow the test tool to connect to your JMS server, specify the connection parameters in the corresponding configuration files which are located at ${XESRoot}\test\enviroment\jms-test\config. The directory contains the following files:

- activemq.properties: The configuration file used for connecting to Apache ActiveMQ JMS Servers. To make the jms-test.bat use this JMS configuration, in the jms-test.bat, set the -config parameter to "%~dp0config/activemq.properties".
- data.dat: The test data file used for writing to the JMS server.
- data.dat.properties: The test property file used for writing to the JMS server.
- ibm.properties: The configuration file used for connecting to IBM MQ JMS Servers. To make the jms-test.bat use this JMS configuration, in the jms-test.bat, set the -config parameter to "%~dp0config/ibm.properties".


###### Test Tool Parameters


You can run the test tool with the following parameters:


| Parameter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| -h | Displays available parameters descriptions. If this parameter is provided, all others are ignored. |
| -data <data_file> | Specifies the path to the data file that should be submitted to your JMS server during testing. The default value is config/data.dat. |
| -api <api_name> | Specifies the API to be used. Use jms for Apache Active MQ or any other server that supports ActiveMQ API, or use ibmmq when testing IBM MQ servers. The default value is "jms". |
| -config <path_to_config_file> | Specifies the path to the configuration to be used. |
| -m <mode> | Specifies the test mode. The following modes are available: read: Reads data from a JMS server. write: Writes data to a JMS server. The default value is "read". |
| -i <iterations> | Specifies the number of test iterations to be performed in each thread. The default value is "1". |
| -t <interval> | Specifies intervals between read/write operations in milliseconds. The default value is "100". |
| -transacted | Specifies whether the testing should be performed in the transacted mode. In this mode, data is batched and submitted to a JMS server. |
| -transaction-size <number_of_messages_per_transaction> | Specifies the number of messages in one transaction. The default value is "10". |



###### Running the Test Tool


Example 1


//Non-transacted mode


C:\Edifecs\XEServer\test\enviroment\jms-test>jms-test.bat -m write -i 3 -t 500


The script runs the test tool in the non-transacted mode and writes the default data file to the JMS server. The procedure iterates three times with intervals of 500 milliseconds. The -config parameter is not specified, and so the default configuration file is used. The statistics for the WRITE operation are stored in a result .csv file in the ${XESRoot}\XEServer\test\enviroment\fs-test\ directory and looks as shown below:


| date, | t, | count, | max, | avg, | min |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2016-11-18 17:54, | write, | 3, | 41, | 16.271513, | 4 |   |



| Line Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| date | The date and time when the statistics were aggregated. |
| t | The name of the performed operation. |
| count | The number of iterations for an operation. |
| max | The maximum time in milliseconds for performing a single iteration. |
| avg | The average time in milliseconds for performing a single iteration. |
| min | The minimum time in milliseconds for performing a single iteration. |



Example 2


//Transacted mode


C:\Edifecs\XEServer\test\enviroment\jms-test>jms-test.bat -m write -i 100 -transacted -transaction-size 10


-or-


C:\Edifecs\XEServer\test\enviroment\jms-test>jms-transacted-write-test.bat


Note These two example scripts run the test tool with identical parameters since the latter script is a modification of the first one.


The scripts run the test tool in the transacted mode that batches files into transactions (10 files each) and then writes them to a JMS server.


The procedure iterates 100 times with default intervals of 100 milliseconds. Once the batch is formed, it is sent to the JMS server. The statistics for the WRITE operation for a single batch and for a single message is stored to a result .csv file in the ${XESRoot}\XEServer\test\enviroment\fs-test\ directory and looks as shown below:


| date, | t, | count, | max, | avg, | min |   |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2016-11-18 17:54, | write, | 100, | 4, | 0.569684, | 0.1 |   |
| 2016-11-18 17:54, | write-transaction, | 10, | 76, | 20.960883, | 4 |   |



The second line of the result .csv file displays statistics on writing a single file to a JMS server. The third line displays the statistics for a transaction.


| Line Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| date | The date and time when the operation was started. |
| t | The name of the performed operation. |
| count | The number of iterations for an operation. |
| max | The maximum time in milliseconds for performing a single iteration. |
| avg | The average time in milliseconds for performing a single iteration. |
| min | The minimum time in milliseconds for performing a single iteration. |



After you get the statistics on your JMS server performance, you can compare them to the XEServer performance indices and ascertain the reason for performance degradation. The results of the test tool should be close to the XEServer performance. However you should consider moderate XEServer performance overheads (validating, preprocessing, parsing efforts, and so on).

---
