<a id="xeserver-configuration-update-requirements"></a>

### XEServer Configuration Update Requirements

> 📄 Source: [XES_Configuration_Update_Requirements.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XES_Configuration_Update_Requirements.htm)

#### XEServer Configuration Update Requirements


When you start an XEServer profile, XEServer loads the settings (configuration files, templates, scripts, property files) to the RAM. If you change such a configuration file while the profile is running, you have to restart the profile to allow XEServer to pick up the updated configuration. For some XEServer settings, you have to reset the profile (Reset option in Admin Console) for the changes to take effect, while for others, you do not have to perform any actions as XEServer detects the changes at runtime.


an action you have to perform for XEServer to pick up the changed configuration.


The following table lists XEServer settings and the actions you have to perform to allow XEServer to pickup the updated configuration:


| Setting / Configuration file | Required Action | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Groovy script](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_Component.htm#Script): in-memory. | Reset | With the Groovy component, you can execute custom groovy scripts within a route. |
| [Groovy script](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_Component.htm#Script): external file. | Reset | |
| XSLT/STX external stylesheet | None | The XML Transformer component allows you to transform XML data using transformation templates. |
| [Velocity component](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Velocity_Component.htm#Velocity): external template | Reset | The Velocity component allows you to transform data using Velocity Template Language. |
| [Business Rules](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Business_Rules_Component.htm) | Shutdown + Start | The Business Rules component allows you to apply Drools rules to your data processing flow. |
| Encryption/Decryption components: [keystore path](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Encryption.htm#Keystore) | Reset | Encryption and Decryption components allow you to encrypt and decrypt data using robust encryption mechanisms. |
| SSL: Truststore/Keystore settings on all components. | Reset | XEServer components and services that use the SSL mechanism require XEServer reset if an SSL configuration changes. |
| SOAP Call: [Soap Message Template](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/SOAP_Call.htm#SOAP_message_template) | None | The SOAP Call component allows you to send SOAP requests to web services. |
| [XML Batcher](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XML_Batcher.htm) | Reset | With XML Batcher, you can batch multiple XML documents of the same schema into one document. |
| [Process Call](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Process_Call.htm) | Reset | This component allows you to invoke an external process (application). |
| [Groovy Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Groovy_Service.htm) | None | With the Groovy component, you can execute custom groovy scripts.Note It is safe to change the script only if no XEServer message exchange is being currently processed. If a new message comes in as an input at the moment of editing the script, this leads to a script file access error. |
| JMS Generic Service: [External JNDI properties file](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_Generic_Service.htm#JNDI) | Reset | The JMS Generic service provides a universal interface to work with JMS servers. |
| Active MQ Service: [Broker file](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/ActiveMQ_Server_Service.htm#Broker_Configuration) | Reset | The ActiveMQ service allows you to set up a full-fledged Apache ActiveMQ JMS server within XEServer. |
| [Mock Component](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Mock_Velocity.htm) | Reset | This component allows you to emulate custom functionality using XML Velocity templates. |
| [Aggregation component](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Aggregation_Properties.htm#Groovy_script): Groovy Strategy | Reset | With the Aggregation component, you can accumulate data and release the data in one batch. |

---

<a id="rabbitmq-server"></a>

### RabbitMQ Server

> 📄 Source: [Book_RabbitMQ_Server.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_RabbitMQ_Server.htm)

#### RabbitMQ Server


This section has the following information:

---

<a id="edifecs-rabbitmq-server-overview"></a>

#### Edifecs RabbitMQ Server Overview

> 📄 Source: [Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server_Overview.htm)

##### RabbitMQ Server Overview


RabbitMQ Server is a full-featured and popular open-source lightweight message broker. You can use a preconfigured instance of RabbitMQ Server (broker version 3.8.0) to reliably transfer data between XEServer routes. The solution integrates with XEServer Rabbit MQ components effortlessly and its default configuration allows you to start transferring data between XEServer routes without any additional setup (assuming that RabbitMQ Server and XEServer are both running on the same computer). RabbitMQ Server runs inside the Erlang sandbox, which is installed by default and requires no explicit configuration. For more information, see [Set up XEServer and RabbitMQ Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Set_up_XEServer_to_Work_with_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm).


The RabbitMQ Server is available for Windows (.msi installer) and Linux (.tar.gz package) platforms. After you have installed XEServer 9.4, you can find the installers for Windows and Linux [here](https://github.com/rabbitmq/rabbitmq-server/releases/tag/v3.8.0). You can find the installers' source files here: for [Windows](https://github.com/rabbitmq/rabbitmq-server/archive/refs/tags/v3.8.0.zip) and [Linux](https://github.com/rabbitmq/rabbitmq-server/archive/refs/tags/v3.8.0.tar.gz). Click [here](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Install_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm) to see the installation instructions.

---

<a id="install-edifecs-rabbitmq-server"></a>

#### Install Edifecs RabbitMQ Server

> 📄 Source: [Install_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Install_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm)

##### Install RabbitMQ Server


The following software is required for RabbitMQ Server.


| Requirement | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Operating System | Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6.x, 7.x, 8.x (64-bit) SUSE Linux 12, 15 (64-bit) Microsoft Windows 10 (64-bit) Microsoft Windows Server 2016 (64-bit) Microsoft Windows Server 2019 (64-bit) |
| Java | OpenJDK 11 (11.0.11 or later). |



RabbitMQ Server is available for Windows and Linux platforms, and comprises the following steps.


###### Windows Installation

1. Run the RabbitMQServer-3.8.0.msi installer file.
2. In the installation wizard that opens, click Next.
3. Change the destination folder (if required), and then click Next.
4. On the Network Configuration page, specify the following parameters: - Node Port: This port number is used by the RabbitMQ client applications (XEServer [RabbitMQ](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RabbitMQ_Service.htm) service) to connect to the RabbitMQ server.Tip If you change the port number, make sure you use the same port for the XEServer [RabbitMQ service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RabbitMQ_Service.htm). - Distribution port: The port number through which multiple instances of RabbitMQ service can be arranged in a [cluster](https://www.rabbitmq.com/clustering.html). You can leave both port values unchanged unless the ports are already occupied.
5. Click Install, and then click Finish.
6. If you want to install RabbitMQ as a Windows service, go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service, and run install.bat.


###### Linux Installation

1. Extract the RabbitMQServer-3.8.0.tar.gz archive: tar -xvzf RabbitMQServer-3.8.0.tar.gz
2. Run the extracted executable file RabbitMQServerInstaller: ./RabbitMQServerInstaller
3. After the installation process completes, the 3rdParty/RabbitMQServer-3.8.0 directory appears. Note If you want to specify a different port number through which XEServer communicates with RabbitMQ Server (by default, it is 5672) on Linux, you have to edit the ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/setEnv.sh script. More specifically, you have to change the RABBITMQ_NODE_PORT property value. ## Install RabbitMQ Server as a service You can install RabbitMQ Server as Windows service or as a Linux service. This allows the server to automatically boot up when you start the operating system. To install and control the server service, use the .bat / .sh scripts (with root permissions) that are under the directory ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service.

---

<a id="control-edifecs-rabbitmq-server"></a>

#### Control Edifecs RabbitMQ Server

> 📄 Source: [Control_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm)

##### Control RabbitMQ Server


RabbitMQ Server contains a set of .bat/.sh scripts (located at ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin) that you can use to control the server.


Note All script files listed in this section must run with root permissions.


| To | Do this (Windows) | Do this (Linux) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Use RabbitMQ Server as a regular process: | | |
| Start RabbitMQ Server | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin and run start.bat. | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin and run start.sh. |
| Stop RabbitMQ Server | Close the terminal session. | Close the terminal session. |
| Use RabbitMQ Server as a Windows/Linux service: |   | |
| Install the service | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run install.bat. | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run install.sh. |
| Start the service | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run start.bat. | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run start.sh. |
| Stop the service | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run stop.bat. | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run stop.sh. |
| Uninstall the service | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run uninstall.bat. | Go to ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/service and run uninstall.sh |



###### Change RabbitMQ Server port number


To change the default port number (the one you specified during installation) through which RabbitMQ Server communicates with XEServer, you have to edit the setEnv.bat file located at ${RabbitMQServerRoot}/bin/. More specifically, you have to change the RABBITMQ_NODE_PORT property value.

---

<a id="set-up-xeserver-to-work-with-edifecs-rabbitmq-server"></a>

#### Set up XEServer to work with Edifecs RabbitMQ Server

> 📄 Source: [Set_up_XEServer_to_Work_with_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Set_up_XEServer_to_Work_with_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm)

##### Set up XEServer to Work with RabbitMQ Server


This section shows you how to set up a simple data pipe between two routes using RabbitMQ Server.

1. [Install RabbitMQ Server](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Install_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm) on the same computer where XEServer is running.
2. Create an XEServer profile with two routes that have the following structure: - Source route: - Destination route: The two RabbitMQ components should reference the same [RabbitMQ service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/RabbitMQ_Service.htm).
3. On the Source route, select the component RabbitMQ Outbound, and on the component's Settings tab, specify a value for Target Exchange (for example, test_exchange).
4. On the Target route, select the component RabbitMQ Inbound, and on the component's Settings tab, use the same value (test_exchange) for Source Queue.
5. Ensure that the XEServer RabbitMQ service uses the same port number as the RabbitMQ Server.Tip The default configuration of the XEServer RabbitMQ service and of RabbitMQ Server uses the same port number - 5672.
6. On the XEServer RabbitMQ service Configuration page, click Test Connection to verify the connection.
7. [Start](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Control_Edifecs_RabbitMQ_Server.htm#Start) RabbitMQ Server.
8. Start the XEServer profile.
9. Drop a test data file in the directory ${XESProfile}\workspace\test-data\inbound. A result data file appears in the ${XESProfile}\workspace\test-data\outbound. This indicates that the data has transitioned through RabbitMQ Server.

<a id="xprocess-management"></a>

### XProcess Management

> 📄 Source: [Book_XProcess_Management.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Book_XProcess_Management.htm)

#### XProcess Management
[Redirect TO: {XProcess_Management_Overview}](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XProcess_Management_Overview.htm)

---

<a id="xprocess-management-overview"></a>

#### XProcess Management Overview

> 📄 Source: [XProcess_Management_Overview.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XProcess_Management_Overview.htm)

##### XProcess Management Overview


Edifecs XProcess Management is a monitoring solution used to model real-life business processes and track their execution by receiving notifications (events) about the changes in the process state from an external system.


With XProcess Management, you can:

- Track the completion of your business processes
- Make queries to get the current state of a certain business object
- View statistics an business process execution


With XProcess Management, you can model complex business processes using an intuitive BPM notation:



Using XEServer, you can send events to XProcess Management API and notify XProcess Management that a certain monitored object has changed its state (for example, a claim payment has been succeeded). This way, you get a real-time image of your business process completion as well as the current state of individual business objects.


An XProcess Management event is a message that notifies XProcess Management that a certain monitored business object has changed its state. When XProcess Management receives an event with a new state, it assigns the new state to the corresponding business object, thereby moving the business object one step further from the Start towards one of the End points on the BPMN diagram.


XEServer provides an out-of-the-box integration with XProcess Management, so you do not have to spend time connecting the two products. The connection to XProcess Management is established using the XEServer [XProcess](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/XProcess_Service.htm) service. Using this service, any route component can send events to XProcess Management. You can configure event sending on the [Events](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Component_Events_Tab.htm) tab of route component settings.


For more information about XProcess Management, see [XProcess Management Help Center](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/xprocessmanagement/9.2/Content/Home.htm).

---

<a id="manual-database-initialization"></a>

### Manual Database Initialization

> 📄 Source: [Manual_DB_Initialization.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Manual_DB_Initialization.htm)

#### Manual Database Initialization


All XEServer components that use databases automatically create the database schema and perform all necessary DB initialization. However, XEServer can run CREATE and ALTER queries only as a highly privileged user. If this is not applicable for your environment, you might want to handle all database initialization manually (as a privileged user) for XEServer to use.


XEServer comes with a bundle of preconfigured SQL scripts that allow you to create component-specific databases for popular database engines (Oracle, MS SQL, H2, PostgreSQL).


Note All SQL scripts listed in this section require sysadmin and dbcreator roles.


###### XEServer components


The SQL scripts are available at ${XESRoot}\sql-scripts for the following XEServer components:

- Balance Counters (see implementation details)
- Batcher
- Data Storage
- CheckpointNote The *.sql files in ${XESRoot}\sql-scripts\checkpoint\ contain the string "${db.object.prefix}" that you must replace with the value of the DB object prefix parameter which is set on the [Checkpoint](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Checkpoint_Service.htm#DB_object_prefix) service and defaults to "" (empty string).
- Scheduled Jobs


The SQL scripts are available at ${XECRoot}\sql-scripts for the following XEConnect components:

- XEC Tracking (see implementation details)
- FTP Server
- OAuth ServiceTip XEConnect OAuth service does not support H2 databases.


##### Some Characteristics of Database Initialization


Preparing a database for some of the XEServer features is not as straightforward as running a static SQL script. By design, some modules create database tables dynamically, use random table naming, or bind table names to an external environment, and so on. Edifecs recommends that you use the database structure natively built by XEServer components and services. However, this section walks you through sophisticated cases of database initialization for some XEServer features, such as Balance Counters and XEC Tracking.


###### Balance Counters


By design, Balance Counters module dynamically creates database entities and some of the table names depend on the Balance Counters group names. So, to initialize a database for Balance Counters properly, you must have a zipped Balance Counter configuration image and perform the following steps:

1. Depending on the database type, execute the script bc-create-<db_type>.sql. This creates static group-independent tables.
2. Open the configuration image archive.
3. Go to \configuration\config and open GroupsConfiguration.zip. The archive contains files each representing a separate counter group.
4. For each group (for each file named <GroupID> in GroupsConfiguration.zip), do the following:For MS SQL - Run the following SQL:`INSERT INTO CounterGroups (GroupID, Configuration) VALUES ('<GroupID>','<ByteArray_Created_From_File_GroupID>'); SELECT ID FROM CounterGroups WHERE ID=@@IDENTITY`where: - `<GroupID>` is the name of the corresponding group file in GroupsConfiguration.zip, - `<ByteArray_Created_From_File_GroupID>` is the binary representation of the content of the file <GroupID>. The query returns an ID required for the next steps. - Edit the file bc-create-tableset-mssql.sql and replace <SUFFIX> with the ID returned by the query in Step A. - Execute the updated bc-create-tableset-mssql.sql. For Oracle - Execute the following SQL:`SELECT counter_sequence.nextval from dual;` - The query returns an ID required for the next steps. - Execute the following SQL:`INSERT INTO CounterGroups (ID, GroupID, Configuration) VALUES (<ID_From_Step_A>, '<GroupID>', '<ByteArray_Created_From_File_GroupID>');`where: - `<ID_From_Step_A>` is the ID returned in Step A, - `<GroupID>` is the name of the group (the corresponding file in GroupsConfiguration.zip), - `<ByteArray_Created_From_File_GroupID>` is the binary representation of the content of the file <GroupID>. - Edit the file bc-create-oracle-mssql.sql and replace <SUFFIX> with the ID returned by the query in Step A. - Execute the updated bc-create-oracle-mssql.sql. For PostgreSQL 1. Run the following SQL:`INSERT INTO "CounterGroups" ("GroupID", "Configuration") VALUES ('"<GroupID>"','<ByteArray_Created_From_File_GroupID>') RETURNING "ID";`where: - `"<GroupID>"` is the name (including characters " ") of the corresponding group file in GroupsConfiguration.zip, - `<ByteArray_Created_From_File_GroupID>` is the binary representation of the content of the file <GroupID>. The query returns an ID required for the next steps. 2. Edit the file bc-create-tableset-postgresql.sql and replace <SUFFIX> with the ID returned by the query in Step A. 3. Execute the updated bc-create-tableset-postgresql.sql. Note Once you have initialized the database manually, you should not deploy the same Balance Counter configuration using Configuration Manager. #### XEConnect Tracking Initializing a database for XEC Tracking is tricky as some table names must contain the receipt date (yyyyMMdd) of the corresponding CORE request. This means that for each day in the calendar, you have to create a set of tables. As an option, you can automate this step by implementing a custom scheduler tool that triggers SQL scripts on a regular basis.To initialize a database for XEC Tracking: - Depending on the database type, execute the script create-<db_type>.sql once. This creates generic static tables. - Execute the script create-tableset-<db_type>.sql every day where <SUFFIX> in the script file is replaced with the next day's date (to create tables in advance) in the format yyyyMMdd. Alternatively, instead of running this script every day, you can create a set of tables in advance, for example for the upcoming month. Tip The directory XEConnect\sql-scripts\tracking has drop-tableset-<db_type>.sql scripts to clear the database structure.

---

<a id="connectivity-with-apache-hive"></a>

### Connectivity with Apache Hive

> 📄 Source: [Connectivity_with_Apache_Hive.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Connectivity_with_Apache_Hive.htm)

#### Connectivity with Apache Hive


XEServer can connect to and operate with [Apache Hive 3.1.2](http://hive.apache.org/) that is a data warehouse system in an open Hadoop platform, which facilitates ad-hoc queries, data summarization, and data analysis of large datasets that reside in various distributed storages: databases and file systems through SQL.


When you connect to the Apache Hive data warehouse, you have to take the following limitations into consideration:

- The official driver distributed by Apache Hive does not implement all methods from the JDBC API, so use the following Cloudera JDBC Driver: com.cloudera.hive.jdbc4.HS2Driver that helps you access Hadoop data using various Business Intelligence (BI) applications with JDBC support.


Use the connection URL in the following format to supply connection information to the data source:


jdbc:hive2://${HOST_NAME}:${PORT}/${DB_NAME}

- DB Connection Service uses the default isolation level for test connection to database servers, which Apache Hive does not support (for more information, see [Limitations](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/display/Hive/Hive+Transactions#HiveTransactions-Limitations)), so you have to skip the test connection. To skip the test connection to the Apache Hive data warehouse server: 1. In [DB Connection Service](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/eam/9.4/Content/DB_Connection_Service.htm), go to the Configuration tab. 2. Set the property Connection check on start to false.
- Apache Hive supports only snapshot level isolation and does not support the default isolation level for all SQL Server databases, so you should change the level of isolation to Read_Uncommitted for your databases that handle transactions. To set the transaction isolation level: 1. In the [DB components](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/go/eam/9.4/Content/Book_DB_Components.htm), go to Settings. 2. On the Common tab, for Local, select the transaction isolation level: TRANSACTION_READ_UNCOMMITTED.

---

<a id="expression-language"></a>

### Expression Language

> 📄 Source: [Expression_Language.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Expression_Language.htm)

#### Expression Language


With Expression language, you can define conditional expressions for some XEServer settings that are evaluated at a route's run time. For example, you can use the expression language to:

- Specify filters for the components'(Native Processing, JData Processing, Translator, XData Processing,Router) outputs. When you set a filter on a component output, you define a rule on what sort of data should go through this output.
- Define conditions for the EAM Debugger breakpoints. With expression language, you can set a breakpoint to trigger only if a certain condition is met (for example an XEServer message exchange carries a specific property).
- Define startup conditions for a route or a service. You can configure a route (or a service) to start with the profile based on a certain condition.


You can use the following operations:

- Logical (OR, AND, NOT)
- Comparison (=, !=, >, <, >=, <=)
- Arithmetical (+, -, *, /)
- String comparison by a wildcard (LIKE)


For the operators above, the operands can be either constants (String, Integer, Floating point, Boolean), [macros](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Terms.htm#Macro), or expressions. The expression language also supports data type conversion (casting). You can perform the following data type conversions:


| Function | Description |
| --- | --- |
| String (string) | Returns the string representation of the `string` argument. |
| String (bool) | Returns the string representation of the Boolean argument `bool`. |
| String (int) | Returns the string representation of the Integer argument `int`. |
| String (float) | Returns the string representation of the Float argument `float`. |
| Bool (string) | Converts a string to a Boolean value. |
| Bool (bool) | Returns the Boolean argument. |
| Int (string) | Converts a string value to an integer. If the `string` argument represents an invalid integer, then the function throws an exception. |
| Int (string, defInt) | Converts a string value to an integer. If the `string` argument represents an invalid integer, then the function returns the integer `defInt`. |
| Int (int) | Returns the Integer `int` argument. |
| Int (float) | Converts the floating point argument `float` to an integer by dropping its fractional part. |
| Float (string) | Converts a string to a float. If the `string` argument represents an invalid float number, then the function throws an exception. |
| Float (string, defFloat) | Converts a string to a float. If the `string` argument represents an invalid float number, then the function returns the floating point number defFloat. |
| Float (int) | Converts an integer to a float. |
| Float (float) | Returns the `float` argument. |



Example


// The following expression casts a Boolean value to a String and compares it with another String


String(${testBoolProperty})='foo'


All the operations are executed based on their priority. The higher an operator's position is in the list below, the higher its priority.

- Parentheses ( )
- Unary plus '+' and minus '-'
- *, /
- +, -
- =, !=, >, <, >=, <=, LIKE
- NOT
- AND
- OR


Example


Expression1 OR Expression2 AND Expression3


// First, the interpreter compares `Expression2 AND Expression3`, and then executes Expression1 OR {comparisonResults2And3}


###### XEServer Route macros support


Expression language supports XEServer route macros. See the following example:


Example


{MD_XE_RPN} LIKE '*' AND {MD_XE_RT} LIKE '*Report*'


This example expression uses XEngine macros and string comparisons, where:


`{MD_XE_RPN} LIKE '*'` – any result processor name


`{MD_XE_RT} LIKE '*Report*'` – result type contains the word 'Report'


###### Supporting Data node dictionary


Data node dictionary can be used in expressions.


Example


Int({DN[dns.cnt.re]}) != 0


The previous example expression uses the DN macro (data node dictionary) and returns a count of rejected items when the count is greater than or equal to three.


 


{DN[dns.cnt.re]} = '0'


The rejected items counter is compared with string '0'


 


{DN[dns.fnm.Transaction.prp.TransactionID]}='270'


Property TransactionID located on Transaction level is compared with string '270'

---

<a id="jms-bind-job-parameters"></a>

### JMS Bind Job Parameters

> 📄 Source: [JMS_Bind_Job_Parameters.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/JMS_Bind_Job_Parameters.htm)

#### JMS Bind Job Parameters


Parameters specific for IBMMQ


| Parameter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| cfg.BindingJobName | Any job name |
| cfg.jms.Content | Specifies data that should be sent as JMS Message body. If Job is used as Binding Job and Result object is set on IJobContext then this property is ignored. |
| cfg.jms.CorrelationID | CorrelationID for the message to be sent. |
| cfg.jms.MessageFormat | JMS/MQ |
| cfg.jms.Password | The password which is passed to Connection Factory while creating new JMS Connection. This property is used in conjunction with "UserName" property. |
| cfg.jms.Persistent | Specifies if JMS Messages sent by Job should be persistent. |
| cfg.jms.Priority | 0-9. Default value is 4. |
| cfg.jms.ReplyTo | Specifies queue name where a reply to the message should be sent. |
| cfg.jms.SaveProperties | Specifies whether or no properties supplied along with content should be passed to JMS message as JMS Message properties. |
| cfg.jms.SkipIfNoContent | Used for specifying the job's behavior when the property 'cfg.jms.Content' is not specified (or set to null). If 'cfg.jms.SkipIfNoContent' property is set to 'false' or not specified then an empty string is used as message body. If 'cfg.jms.SkipIfNoContent' property is set to 'true' then JMS message is not sent at all. |
| cfg.jms.TimeToLive | Specifies message expiration timeout defined in milliseconds. Value 0 means that the message never expires. Default value is 4. |
| cfg.jms.Transacted | Specifies whether or not data should be transferred in scope of the transacted JMS Session. |
| cfg.jms.UserName | UserName which is passed to ConnectionFactory while creating new JMS Connection. |
| cfg.xes.JobFailureInstruction | Stop Process, Log warning, Stop Server |
| java.naming.security.credentials | The credentials of the principal for authenticating the caller to the service. The value of the property depends on the authentication scheme. For example, it could be a hashed password, clear-text password, key, certificate, and so on. |
| java.naming.security.principal | The principal for authenticating the caller to the service. |
| cfg.jms.java.naming.provider.url | JMS Provider URL. |
| cfg.jms.java.naming.factory.initial | Specifies which initial context factory to use when creating a new initial context object. |
| cfg.jms.ConnectionFactory.JNDIName | Specifies Connection Factory name. A JNDI Lookup is performed to get the Connection Factory. |



##### Parameters specific for IBMMQ


| Parameter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| cfg.jms.SSL.SocketFactory | Sets the SSLSocketFactory for use with SSL encryption. If a custom SSLSocketFactory is specified, the MQConnectionFactory cannot be bound into a JNDI namespace. Attempting to do so results in an exception. Parameter specifies - the full class name of SSLSocketFactory class. If not specified, the JSSE default SSLSocketFactory is used when SSL encryption is requested. This property is ignored if SSL.CipherSuite is not specify. Warning: DSA certificates are not supported because of the migration to Java 17. For SSL connections, you have to [use RSA certificates](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/DSA_Certificates_Support.htm) instead. |
| cfg.jms.SSL.ResetCount | Sets the SSL reset count. This must be an integer, with a value between 0 (disabled) and 999,999,999. |
| cfg.jms.SSL.PeerName | Sets a distinguished name (DN) pattern. If sslCipherSuite is set, this pattern can ensure that the correct queue manager is used. The connection attempt fails if the distinguished name provided by the queue manager does not match this pattern. |
| cfg.jms.SSL.FipsRequired | Sets whether sslFips (FIPS) is required. (Federal Information Processing Standards). |
| cfg.jms.SSL.CipherSuite | Sets the CipherSuite used for SSL encryption. Set this to the CipherSuite matching the CipherSpec set on the SVRCONN channel. cipherSuite - the CipherSuite used for SSL encryption. If set to null (the default), no SSL encryption is performed. |
| cfg.jms.SSL.CertStore | Specifies a list of LDAP servers used for certificate revocation list (CRL) checking. Each LDAP server is tried in turn until one is successfully used to verify the queue manager's certificate. This String must consist of a sequence of space-delimited LDAP URIs of the form ldap://host[:port]. If no port is specified, the LDAP default of 389 is assumed. If set to null (the default), no checking of the queue manager's certificate is performed. |
| cfg.jms.QueueManager | Queue Manager name |
| cfg.jms.channel | Only one property 'CCDTURL' or 'Channel' can be specified at a time. |
| cfg.jms.CCSID | The coded character set identifier |
| cfg.jms.CCDTURL | Client channel definition table URL. The CCDT URL specifies the file location where the CCDT resides. File and FTP protocols are supported. For example 'file:///C:/table/AMQCLCHL.TAB' or 'ftp://userName:password@myServer/path/AMQCLCHL.TAB'. Only one property 'CCDTURL' or 'Channel' can be specified at a time. If CCDTURL is specified the following configuration properties are ignored: 'hostname', 'port' and all properties starting with 'SSL.'. |

---

<a id="file-system-locking-considerations"></a>

### File System Locking Considerations

> 📄 Source: [File_System_Locking_Considerations.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/File_System_Locking_Considerations.htm)

#### File System Locking Considerations


If you use several XEServer profiles with File System endpoints that read/write to the same file(s), XEServer offers cluster mode that you must enable on all [FS Inbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Inbound.htm#Cluster_mode) and [FS Outbound](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/FS_Outbound.htm#Locking) components to prevent multiple XEServer instances from processing the same file concurrently and thus creating duplicates. In cluster mode, the first XEServer instance that begins processing of the file *fileName.** automatically creates the file *fileName.lock* that acts as an indicator for other XEServer instances that the file is currently processed.


##### Known issues and troubleshooting


If the XEServer process that created the fileName.lock file terminates unexpectedly, this file remains in the file system and prevents the *fileName.** file from being processed by other instances. To avoid such issues, you can apply an exclusive (write) lock on the file at the operating system level by setting the following property on the profile [Startup tab](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Profile_Startup_Tab.htm) for all profiles that use File System endpoints in cluster mode:


`-Dcom.edifecs.etools.xeserver.fs.cluster.extralock=true`


This lock binds the file to the parent process, so if the XEServer process terminates unexpectedly, the operating system automatically removes the .lock file allowing other XEServer instances (if any) to process the file.


The exclusive locking used by XEServer is allowed in almost all file systems that support either mandatory or advisory locks. However, for the extra lock functionality of XEServer to work properly, your file system must support mandatory locking. When mandatory lock is activated on a file, the operating system prevents other processes from reading or writing the file. In case of advisory locks, file locking might be ignored by another process if this process is not aware of this lock. Some file systems use mandatory file locks by design (for example, on Windows operating system), the others always use advisory locks (for example, AWS EFS). File locks on Unix operating systems are advisory in general, however in some systems you can control the kind of a lock to apply (mandatory or advisory).


If your file system supports advisory locks only, to let several XEServer instances read/write to the same file(s) without leaving orphaned files in case of process stoppage, consider the following options for profiles architecture:

- Use only one instance of a profile that reads the files and distributes these files among processing profiles using queues (for example, MQ connectivity, Kafka queue, and so on).
- Configure the process that writes to the folder to report the file path to a queue (Kafka queue, MQ connectivity, and so on) once the file is uploaded to the folder:

---

<a id="frequently-asked-questions"></a>

## Frequently Asked Questions

> 📄 Source: [Frequently_Asked_Questions.htm](https://help.edifecsfedcloud.com/svc/product/eam/9.4/Content/Frequently_Asked_Questions.htm)

### Frequently Asked Questions


###### Subroute questions

 Should all terminating endpoints return results to a parent route?


The best practice is to make all outbound results from a subroute return to the parent route. Using the subroute components, you can view all the associated sources and subroutes, which allows you to understand the organization of the entire route.


Also, when you only have JMS inbound and JMS outbound on a parent route, you can use the JMS shared transactions that are committed together on task completion.





 Can I use outbound JMS and Data Storage with subroutes?


You can use JMS and Data Storage outbound endpoints with subroutes, but Edifecs does not recommend their use with this type of routes.





 How are errors returned to the parent routes?


Subroute errors are reported on subroutes. A corresponding error is reported on the Subroute component of the parent route at the same time. This error indicates that the task could not be completed successfully on the subroute. However, this error does not include the details of the subroute error because there could be several errors.






###### Component outputs questions

 What is the order in which output data leaves a component?


The order in which a component returns processing results through several outputs depends on the component type. Typically, the sequence is the same as the order of outputs available on the component (from top to bottom).


However, there is one rule that works for all route components. The original message that is received by a component as an input is reported further down the route after the component's processing is complete. This message is reported through the original output only if no exception is thrown during the processing. If there is an error, the original message leaves the component through the error output.






###### Data Storage questions

 What is the best practice for using Data Storage with big data and what is its impact on the performance? Are there any alternatives to the processing of large volumes of data?


With Data Storage components, you can read from and write data to the database. By default, the feature uses an H2 database. But it also supports MS SQL and Oracle.


When you store data and retrieve the data within the same profile, Edifecs recommends the light-weight H2. Also, if you are sure that you are operating with a limited set of data, you can use the in-memory configuration for H2. However, when you store data on one server and want to retrieve the data from another, Edifecs recommends the MS SQL or Oracle databases.


Data Storage also gives you an expiration mechanism. With this function, you can be sure that the data in data storage does not grow excessively as a result of the accumulation of stale data items (those items that have been written to the storage, but failed to be extracted).


As an alternative, you can use the following:

- Shared file system location,
- Implement your own component that communicates with the storage






###### Aggregation questions

 How does the Task Completion strategy work?


Task Completion is an event-driven strategy to aggregate several messages. When an XEServer task completes, XEServer reports the corresponding event and the Aggregation component executes the aggregation strategy. An XEServer task is complete when all the messages with the same TaskID are processed.


The Aggregation component usually prepares the results within the scope of the same task, so to use this strategy, you must configure the Aggregation component to create a new task.





 How does the Last Message strategy work?


The strategy Last Message activates aggregation when a special XEServer exchange message (the message carries a specific message property that marks the message as "last") comes to the component. You can opt to include the last message into the aggregated result.
